2. **Quality Control Era (1930s–1950s):** Walter A. Shewhart introduced **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** at Bell Labs in 1924. Control charts and sampling inspection replaced 100% inspection. The cost per inspection dropped dramatically.

3. **Quality Assurance Era (1950s–1970s):** Quality became a *system-wide* responsibility. Pioneers:
   - **W. Edwards Deming** — proposed 14 Points for Management; emphasized the system, not the worker. His PDCA cycle is the foundation of all modern improvement methodologies.
   - **Joseph M. Juran** — defined Quality as "Fitness for Use" and proposed the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
   - **Armand V. Feigenbaum** — coined the term **Total Quality Control (TQC)**, advocating that quality is everyone's responsibility, not just the QC department's.

4. **Strategic QM / TQM Era (1970s–1990s):** Quality became a competitive differentiator. **Kaoru Ishikawa** introduced **Quality Circles** and the **Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram**. **Philip Crosby** popularized the **Zero Defects** concept and the mantra *"Quality is Free."*

5. **Six Sigma Era (1986–Present):** Engineer **Bill Smith** at Motorola developed Six Sigma in 1986. **Jack Welch** made it a corporate religion at GE in 1995. The **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology formalized data-driven quality improvement.

*(Refer to Diagram 4.3 from Section 4 for the timeline visualization.)*

> **[End of sub-part (a) — 7 Marks]**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] "A manufacturing company has the following quality-related annual cost data: Prevention Cost = ₹2,00,000; Appraisal Cost = ₹1,50,000; Internal Failure Cost = ₹3,00,000; External Failure Cost = ₹4,50,000. Calculate the Total Cost of Quality and classify it into Conformance and Non-Conformance categories. Comment on the company's quality strategy."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List the given values.**

$$C_P = ₹2{,}00{,}000, \quad C_A = ₹1{,}50{,}000$$

$$C_{IF} = ₹3{,}00{,}000, \quad C_{EF} = ₹4{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute Conformance Cost.**

$$\text{Conformance Cost} = C_P + C_A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹3{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute Non-Conformance Cost.**

$$\text{Non-Conformance Cost} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 = ₹7{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total COQ.**

$$\text{Total COQ} = 3{,}50{,}000 + 7{,}50{,}000 = ₹11{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Comment on the strategy.**

> The Non-Conformance Cost (₹7,50,000) is **more than double** the Conformance Cost (₹3,50,000). This is a classic **"fire-fighting"** quality strategy. The company is *detecting* and *correcting* defects after they occur, rather than *preventing* them at the source. Six Sigma recommendation: **increase Prevention Cost (training, process design, FMEA, root cause analysis)** so that Internal and External Failure costs fall. A healthy Six Sigma organization typically has **Conformance Cost > Non-Conformance Cost**, ideally in the ratio of **70:30 or even 80:20**.

> **[End of sub-part (b) — 7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] "Explain Deming's PDCA cycle. Why is it considered the foundation of continuous improvement in Six Sigma?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define PDCA.**

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, also known as the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is a four-stage iterative process for continuous improvement:

- **PLAN:** Identify the problem, define the goal, analyze the current process, and develop a hypothesis for improvement.
- **DO:** Implement the plan on a small scale (pilot run). Collect data carefully.
- **CHECK:** Compare the observed results against the expected outcomes. Use statistical tools to validate.
- **ACT:** If the improvement worked, **standardize** it across the process. If not, revise the hypothesis and loop back to PLAN.

**Step 2 — Why PDCA is the foundation of Six Sigma.**

Six Sigma's **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) is essentially an *industrial-scale* PDCA cycle:
- **Define** ≈ Plan (what are we trying to solve?)
- **Measure + Analyze** ≈ Do + Check (gather data, test hypothesis)
- **Improve + Control** ≈ Act (standardize the gain)

**Step 3 — Key properties of PDCA.**

1. It is **iterative** — every loop lifts the process to a higher performance level.
2. It is **scientific** — based on the hypothetico-deductive method.
3. It is **universal** — applicable to manufacturing, services, healthcare, software.

*(Refer to Diagram 4.4 from Section 4 for the cycle visualization.)*

> **[End of sub-part (a) — 7 Marks]**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] "Explain the concept of Cost of Quality. With a suitable diagram, describe the relationship between Prevention Cost, Failure Cost, and Total COQ. Why does the COQ model have an optimum point?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define COQ.**

Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total cost incurred to ensure product/service quality. It has four components: $C_P$, $C_A$, $C_{IF}$, $C_{EF}$.

**Step 2 — Describe the relationships.**

- **Prevention Cost ($C_P$)** *rises* as quality level increases (more training, better process design).
- **Appraisal Cost ($C_A$)** remains relatively *stable* or rises slowly.
- **Failure Cost ($C_{IF} + C_{EF}$)** *falls* sharply as quality improves.
- **Total COQ** = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ → forms a **U-shaped curve**.

**Step 3 — Locate the optimum.**

The minimum of the U-curve is the **Economic Quality Optimum ($Q^*$)**. Beyond this point, every additional rupee spent on prevention costs more than it saves in failure reduction.

**Step 4 — Why is the optimum critical?**

- Below $Q^*$: Company is *fire-fighting* — high scrap, warranty, returns.
- At $Q^*$: Company is *economically optimal*.
- Beyond $Q^*$ (e.g., 100% inspection): Diminishing returns; over-engineering.

**Step 5 — Six Sigma perspective.**

Six Sigma aims for $3.4$ DPMO, *beyond* the classical $Q^*$. The justification: the classical model ignores *intangible* failure costs (brand damage, lost customers, regulatory fines), which make pushing quality to extreme levels still economically justifiable.

*(Refer to Diagram 4.2 from Section 4 for the curve visualization.)*

> **[End of sub-part (b) — 7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing QM with QC:** Many students interchange "Quality Management" and "Quality Control." QM is strategic (top-down); QC is operational (inspection). Defining the wrong one in a 3-mark question = **0 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting all four COQ components:** A common mistake is writing only Prevention and Failure costs. You must mention **all four** ($C_P$, $C_A$, $C_{IF}$, $C_{EF}$) for full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the classification:** Always classify each cost as **Conformance vs Non-Conformance**. Examiners specifically check for this in the marking scheme.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark questions:** A 14-mark answer without a diagram is capped at 10–11 marks even if the text is excellent. **Always draw at least one labeled diagram.**
> 5. **Generic Guru definitions:** Don't write "Deming was a quality expert." Write his *contribution*: "Deming's 14 Points for Management, PDCA cycle, and the System of Profound Knowledge." Specificity = marks.
> 6. **Numerical errors in COQ:** Add wrong components, or forget the unit (₹). Always show the formula and the substitution step-by-step.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Quality** = Conformance to requirements + Fitness for use + Customer satisfaction.
- **QM** is the strategic umbrella; **QA** is process-focused and preventive; **QC** is product-focused and detective.
- **Seven Quality Gurus** to memorize: *Shewhart, Deming, Juran, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Crosby, Taguchi* — plus *Bill Smith* for Six Sigma.
- **COQ = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$**. Conformance = $C_P + C_A$. Non-Conformance = $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$.
- The **COQ curve** is U-shaped; the minimum defines the **Economic Quality Optimum ($Q^*$)**.
- Six Sigma (3.4 DPMO) operates *beyond* $Q^*$ because of intangible failure costs.
- **PDCA** = Plan-Do-Check-Act. DMAIC is industrial-scale PDCA.
- **RTY** = product of individual step yields. DPMO = $\dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$.
- **Evolution timeline**: Inspection → QC → QA → TQM → Six Sigma → Quality 4.0.
- **Always draw a labeled diagram** in any 7 or 14 mark answer — it is worth at least 2–3 marks.
- **Deming's mantra**: *95% of quality problems are system-related, not worker-related.*
- **Juran's trilogy**: Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- **Crosby's four absolutes**: Quality = conformance, prevention not inspection, zero defects, measure with the cost of non-conformance.
- **Taguchi's Loss Function**: Any deviation from target causes loss — to the customer and to society.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Board Mantra:** *"Define + Classify + Diagram + Compute + Comment."* Every quality answer should follow this 5-step structure for maximum marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Total Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Total Quality Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1: Fundamentals of Total Quality Management

## 1.1 Core Definition of Total Quality Management (TQM)

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a **comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy** that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving **all members** of the organization — from top management to front-line workers — while maintaining a **strong customer focus**.

**Authoritative Definitions (as per KTU syllabus references):**

- **Goetsch & Davis (2014):** "TQM is an *integrated* effort by *all* members of an organization directed at *continuously improving* the *quality* of products, services, and processes to *exceed customer expectations*."
- **ISO 8402 (1994):** "A management approach of an organization, centered on *quality*, based on the *participation* of all its members and aiming at *long-term success* through *customer satisfaction* and *benefits* to all members of the organization and to society."
- **ASQ (American Society for Quality):** "A *continuous improvement* philosophy that emphasizes the *systematic approach* to process and product improvement using *data-driven* decisions."

### 🌟 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of TQM as running a world-class hospital.**
>
> Imagine a hospital where:
> - The **patient (customer)** is the centre of everything.
> - The **reception staff, nurses, doctors, lab technicians, and even the cleaning crew** all collaborate to ensure the patient gets the best care.
> - Every small step — from registration to discharge — is monitored, measured, and improved.
> - If a patient catches an infection, the hospital doesn't blame the nurse; it fixes the *system* (handwash protocol, equipment sterilization, etc.).
> - The hospital **continuously learns** from feedback, near-misses, and successful recoveries.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Doctors** = Top Management (set vision)
> - **Nurses** = Middle Management (execute standards)
> - **Support staff** = Front-line workers (deliver service)
> - **Patients** = Customers
> - **Hospital protocols** = Standardized processes
> - **Patient feedback forms** = Quality measurement systems

This is **exactly** what TQM does inside an organization.

### 1.2 Key Terminology — "Vocabulary Anchor Box"

> [!NOTE]
> **MUST-KNOW SYLLABUS TERMS (Bold for emphasis)**
>
> - **Quality** — The degree to which a product/service meets customer requirements and expectations.
> - **Customer** — The ultimate judge of quality (internal or external).
> - **Process** — A set of interrelated work activities that transform inputs into outputs.
> - **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Ongoing effort to improve products, services, or processes.
> - **Customer Satisfaction** — A measure of how well a product/service fulfils customer expectations.
> - **Supplier** — Any entity (internal or external) that provides input to a process.
> - **Value Chain** — The entire series of activities that create value for the customer.
> - **Stakeholder** — Anyone affected by the organization's actions (employees, society, shareholders).

### 1.3 The Evolution of Quality (Historical Timeline)

| Era | Focus | Approach | Limitation |
|-----|-------|----------|------------|
| **Inspection Era (Pre-1900s)** | Detecting defects | Sorting good vs. bad | Reactive, no prevention |
| **Quality Control (1930s)** | Statistical control | Sampling & SPC charts | Narrow, shop-floor only |
| **Quality Assurance (1950s)** | System-wide planning | ISO, documentation | Bureaucratic, sometimes excessive |
| **Total Quality Management (1980s+)** | Customer satisfaction, system thinking | Organization-wide philosophy | Long-term cultural change required |
| **Six Sigma (1986+)** | Data-driven defect reduction | DMAIC methodology | Metric-intensive |

### 1.4 The Gurus of Quality (Foundational Pillars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Section — Remember the 5 Gurus and their core contribution.**

| **Guru** | **Core Philosophy** | **Key Contribution** |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | System thinking, variation | 14 Points, PDCA Cycle, System of Profound Knowledge |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality planning & control | Juran Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Zero defects, prevention | "Quality is Free", Four Absolutes of Quality |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total quality control | Concept of *total* — everyone responsible |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality tools, teamwork | Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram, Quality Circles |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔍 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Absolutes of Quality (Philip B. Crosby)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crosby's 4 Absolutes (Frequently asked in 3-mark questions):**
>
> 1. **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** — Not "goodness" or "elegance."
> 2. **Prevention, not Inspection** — Build quality in; don't inspect it in.
> 3. **Zero Defects Performance Standard** — The target is always zero.
> 4. **Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** — Cost of doing things wrong.

## 2.2 The Juran Trilogy (Joseph M. Juran)

| Step | Name | Activity | Typical % of Effort |
|------|------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Quality Planning** | Identify customers, establish goals, plan processes | 15–20% |
| 2 | **Quality Control** | Evaluate performance, compare to goals, act on differences | 60–70% |
| 3 | **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure, identify projects, implement changes | 15–20% |

## 2.3 Deming's 14 Points — High-Yield Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's philosophy emphasizes SYSTEM thinking, not blaming individuals. His 14 points (1986)** include:
>
> - Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
> - Adopt the new philosophy.
> - Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
> - End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
> - Improve constantly every process.
> - Institute training on the job.
> - Institute leadership.
> - Drive out fear.
> - Break down barriers between departments.
> - Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
> - Eliminate numerical quotas.
> - Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
> - Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
> - Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

## 2.4 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Core Quantitative Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the **TOTAL financial impact** of preventing, detecting, and correcting defects. It is the *sum* of all costs incurred because quality is *not 100% perfect*.

### 2.4.1 The Four Categories of COQ

| Category | Type | Examples | Behaviour |
|----------|------|----------|-----------|
| **Prevention Costs** | Cost of *avoiding* defects | Training, quality planning, supplier capability assessment, process design | **↑ when quality improves** |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Cost of *detecting* defects | Inspection, testing, audits, calibration | Stays roughly constant |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Cost of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, failure analysis | **↓ when quality improves** |
| **External Failure Costs** | Cost of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lost goodwill, lawsuits | **↓ drastically when quality improves** |

### 2.4.2 The Master COQ Formula

$$
\text{Total Cost of Quality} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

Where:
- $C_P$ = Prevention Costs
- $C_A$ = Appraisal Costs
- $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Costs
- $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Costs

### 2.4.3 Conformance vs. Non-Conformance Costs

$$
\text{Conformance Cost} = C_P + C_A
$$

$$
\text{Non-Conformance Cost} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal COQ Strategy (Juran's "Optimum Cost" Model):** As prevention spending increases, failure costs fall. The **minimum total COQ** occurs at a balance point. Investing more in prevention *always* reduces the much more expensive failure costs.

## 2.5 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Genichi Taguchi** (the father of modern Six Sigma) proposed that **any deviation from the target value** causes loss — *even if the product is within specification limits.*

$$
L(x) = k(x - T)^2
$$

Where:
- $L(x)$ = Loss in rupees (or any currency) due to deviation
- $x$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $T$ = Target value
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (a constant)

The constant $k$ is calculated as:

$$
k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta^2}
$$

Where:
- $A_0$ = Average loss per unit when the product is at the specification limit
- $\Delta$ = Tolerance (deviation from target to specification limit)

## 2.6 Process Capability Indices (Quantitative Bridge to Six Sigma)

$$
C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

Where:
- USL = Upper Specification Limit
- LSL = Lower Specification Limit
- $\mu$ = Process mean
- $\sigma$ = Process standard deviation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $C_p \geq 1.33$ is generally considered capable. $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ indicates the process is also **centred** between specification limits.

## 2.7 The PDCA Cycle (Deming / Shewhart Cycle)

> [!NOTE]
> **PDCA = Plan–Do–Check–Act** — the backbone of continuous improvement.

| Step | Activity | Tool Examples |
|------|----------|---------------|
| **Plan** | Identify problem, analyse, plan change | Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Why |
| **Do** | Implement the plan on a small scale | Pilot run, SOP |
| **Check** | Measure results, compare to expected | Control charts, hypothesis testing |
| **Act** | Standardize if successful; restart if not | Updated SOP, training |

## 2.8 🧠 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value symbols to keep tables safe.

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|------------|----------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Total COQ | $C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Currency (₹/\$/€) | Quality cost accounting |
| Conformance Cost | $C_{P} + C_{A}$ | Currency | Investment in quality |
| Non-Conformance Cost | $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Currency | Cost of poor quality |
| Process Yield | $\dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100\%$ | % | First-pass yield |
| Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | % | Multi-stage process yield |
| Defects Per Opportunity (DPO) | $\dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | — | Defect rate |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000$ | ppm-equivalent | Sigma-level metric |
| Process Sigma | $\text{NORMSINV}(1.5 / 1{,}000{,}000) + 1.5$ — table lookup | $\sigma$ | Six Sigma level |
| Process Capability $C_p$ | $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Process spread |
| Process Capability $C_{pk}$ | $\min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Centred capability |
| Taguchi Loss | $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$ | Currency | Loss to society |
| Taguchi Constant $k$ | $\dfrac{A_0}{\Delta^2}$ | Currency / unit² | Loss coefficient |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility of TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why engineers care about TQM:**
>
> - **Manufacturing:** Reduces rework, scrap, and warranty claims.
> - **Software Engineering:** CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) is built on TQM principles.
> - **Civil Engineering:** ISO 9001 quality systems in construction reduce structural failures.
> - **Healthcare:** Lean Six Sigma saves lives by reducing medical errors.
> - **Service Sectors:** Banks, airlines, and IT firms use TQM to improve customer satisfaction and reduce churn.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for a Multi-Stage Process

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a manufacturing process with **$n$ sequential stages**. Let the yield (fraction of good units) at stage $i$ be $Y_i$, where $0 \le Y_i \le 1$.

Each stage may pass some defective units to the next stage. The **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** measures the probability that a single unit passes through *all* stages defect-free.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $P(\text{pass stage } i) = Y_i$. Since the stages are independent:

$$
P(\text{unit passes all } n \text{ stages}) = P(\text{pass 1}) \times P(\text{pass 2}) \times \ldots \times P(\text{pass } n)
$$

$$
\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \cdot Y_2 \cdot Y_3 \cdots Y_n
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**
>
> A printed circuit board (PCB) goes through 3 stages: Soldering ($Y_1 = 0.96$), Testing ($Y_2 = 0.92$), Packaging ($Y_3 = 0.98$). Find RTY.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$\text{RTY} = 0.96 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 = 0.8655 = 86.55\%$$
>
> So **13.45%** of units will have at least one defect across all stages.

### 3.1.3 Single-Pass Yield (SPY) vs. RTY Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Pass Yield (SPY)** assumes defective units are *reworked* and re-introduced; **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** assumes they are *scrapped*. RTY is always **less than or equal to** SPY.

## 3.2 Derivation: DPMO and Process Sigma Level

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of DPMO

**Step 1:** Count the **number of defects** observed during a defined period.

**Step 2:** Count the **number of units** produced in that period.

**Step 3:** Count the **number of opportunities per unit** (opportunities for a defect to occur on one unit).

**Step 4:** Calculate **Defects Per Opportunity (DPO):**

$$
\text{DPO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO):**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

### 3.2.2 Conversion of DPMO to Process Sigma Level

The process sigma level is obtained by:

$$
\text{Sigma Level} = \text{NORMSINV}\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) + 1.5
$$

The "+1.5" is the **Six Sigma shift** (Motorola's empirical allowance for long-term process drift).

**Standard DPMO ↔ Sigma Level Conversion Table (KTU favourite):**

| DPMO | Sigma Level | Yield (%) |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| 690,000 | 1.0σ | 31.0% |
| 308,537 | 2.0σ | 69.1% |
| 66,807 | 3.0σ | 93.3% |
| 6,210 | 4.0σ | 99.38% |
| 233 | 5.0σ | 99.977% |
| 3.4 | 6.0σ | 99.9997% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma Goal:** **3.4 DPMO** = only **3.4 defects per million opportunities** = **99.9997% yield**.

### 3.2.3 Worked Example: DPMO Calculation

> A call centre processes 5,000 calls/day. Each call has 4 opportunities for an error (greeting, account verification, issue resolution, closure). Yesterday, there were 17 errors. Find DPMO and Sigma Level.

**Step 1:** Total opportunities = $5{,}000 \times 4 = 20{,}000$

**Step 2:** DPO = $\dfrac{17}{20{,}000} = 0.00085$

**Step 3:** DPMO = $0.00085 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 850$

**Step 4:** Sigma Level = $\text{NORMSINV}(1 - 0.00085) + 1.5 \approx 4.84\sigma$

**Interpretation:** The call centre operates at about **4.84 sigma**, well short of Six Sigma.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost of Quality (COQ) Optimisation

### 3.3.1 Setup

As prevention spending $C_P$ **increases**, failure costs $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ typically **decrease** (often non-linearly, in an inverse exponential fashion).

The **Total Cost of Quality** is:

$$
\text{Total COQ}(C_P) = C_P + C_A + C_{IF}(C_P) + C_{EF}(C_P)
$$

A simplified empirical model assumes:

$$
C_{IF}(C_P) + C_{EF}(C_P) \approx \dfrac{K}{C_P}
$$

where $K$ is a constant representing the inherent "fixability" of the process.

### 3.3.2 Optimisation

Differentiate Total COQ with respect to $C_P$ and set to zero:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dC_P}\left[ C_P + \dfrac{K}{C_P} \right] = 1 - \dfrac{K}{C_P^2} = 0
$$

$$
C_P^2 = K \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_P^{*} = \sqrt{K}
$$

The **optimal prevention cost** is $\sqrt{K}$, and the **minimum total COQ** is:

$$
\text{Total COQ}_{\min} = 2\sqrt{K} + C_A
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Doubling $C_P$ beyond the optimum *wastes* money, because failure-cost savings are smaller than the extra prevention spend. The optimum is at the **break-even** point.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Apply Each Quality Tool

| Situation | Recommended TQM Tool | Output |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| Frequent defects in incoming material | Acceptance Sampling Plan (AQL) | Go/No-go decision |
| Process drifting over time | SPC Control Charts (X-bar, R, p, c) | Stability of process |
| Identifying root cause of a problem | Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram | Categorised causes |
| Prioritising problems by frequency | Pareto Analysis (80/20 rule) | Top vital few issues |
| Customer complaints dominating | Voice of Customer (VOC), QFD | Specified requirements |
| High warranty claims | Taguchi Loss Function | Financial loss estimate |
| Multiple sequential defects | RTY Analysis | True first-pass yield |
| New process design | DFSS (DMADV) | Six-Sigma-capable process |

## 3.5 Sub-Derivation: Process Capability $C_{pk}$ in TQM Context

A process is "Six Sigma capable" when $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ (since $C_{pk} = 2.0 \Rightarrow$ the specification limits are $\pm 6\sigma$ from the mean).

$$
C_{pk} = 2.0 \quad\Leftrightarrow\quad \text{USL} - \mu = 6\sigma \text{ and } \mu - \text{LSL} = 6\sigma
$$

This is the **mathematical link** between classical SPC capability indices and Six Sigma metrics.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part A Format (KTU 2024):** Short-answer questions. Answer in **3–4 sentences**, **80–100 words**, and **two-thirds of a page**. Direct definitional recall.

### 🔹 Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> **Define Total Quality Management (TQM) and list any four of its key principles.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a customer-focused, integrated, system-wide management philosophy aimed at continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving **all levels** of the organization.
>
> Four key principles of TQM are:
> 1. **Customer focus** — Quality is judged by the customer.
> 2. **Continuous improvement (Kaizen)** — Small, ongoing improvements in every process.
> 3. **Employee involvement** — Every employee is responsible for quality.
> 4. **Process approach** — Manage activities as interrelated processes, not isolated tasks.
>
> TQM emphasizes that quality is a **strategic, long-term commitment** rather than a one-time project.

### 🔹 Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

> **Explain Philip Crosby's concept of "Quality is Free" and list his Four Absolutes of Quality.**

**Model Answer:**

> **"Quality is Free"** is Philip Crosby's landmark idea (1979) that the cost of preventing defects is always **less than** the cost of correcting them. Money saved by doing things right the first time **more than compensates** for the investment in prevention.
>
> **Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality** are:
> 1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness" or luxury).
> 2. **Prevention, not inspection** (build quality in).
> 3. **Zero Defects performance standard** (the target is always zero).
> 4. **Measure quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**.
>
> This philosophy shifted focus from detection to **prevention**, fundamentally changing modern quality management.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part B Format (KTU 2024):** Long-answer questions. Each question is **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means: *either* Question A *or* Question B must be answered. Provide a clear, structured, **point-by-point** answer with diagrams wherever possible.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Define Total Quality Management. Explain the contributions of **any three Quality Gurus** with their key philosophies. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A call centre processes 8,000 calls/day. Each call has 5 opportunities for an error. Yesterday, 24 errors were recorded.
> - (i) Calculate the **DPO** and **DPMO**.
> - (ii) Determine the **Process Sigma Level** (use standard DPMO-Sigma table).
> - (iii) Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, customer-focused, organization-wide management philosophy that seeks to continuously improve the quality of products, services, and processes through the active participation of all members, from top management to front-line workers, while maintaining strong customer focus and data-driven decision making (Goetsch & Davis, 2014).

**Three Quality Gurus:**

**1. W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993):**
- Emphasis on **system thinking** — most problems are system-caused, not worker-caused.
- **PDCA Cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) — the framework for continuous improvement.
- **14 Points for Management** — actionable guidelines for transforming an organization.
- Famous quote: *"In God we trust, all others must bring data."*

**2. Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008):**
- Defined quality as **fitness for use** (customer-focused).
- **Juran Trilogy** — Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- Pioneered the **Pareto Principle (80/20)** in quality — focus on the vital few.
- Emphasized that quality improvement should be a **measurable business goal**.

**3. Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001):**
- Author of *"Quality is Free"* (1979).
- **Four Absolutes of Quality** (Conformance, Prevention, Zero Defects, PONC).
- Advocated that quality is **conformance to requirements**, not elegance.
- Emphasized that **prevention** is cheaper than inspection or rework.

> 💡 **Optional 4th Guru (for extra credit):**
> - **Kaoru Ishikawa** — developed the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** and **Quality Circles** (team-based quality improvement at the shop-floor level).

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Calls processed per day, $N = 8{,}000$
- Opportunities per call, $O = 5$
- Number of defects, $D = 24$

**(i) Calculate DPO and DPMO:**

Total opportunities in a day:

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = N \times O = 8{,}000 \times 5 = 40{,}000
$$

DPO:

$$
\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{\text{Total Opportunities}} = \dfrac{24}{40{,}000} = 6 \times 10^{-4}
$$

DPMO:

$$
\text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6 = 600
$$

**(ii) Process Sigma Level:**

From the standard DPMO-Sigma conversion table:

| DPMO Range | Sigma Level |
|------------|-------------|
| 233 | 5.0σ |
| 3.4 | 6.0σ |

DPMO of 600 lies between 233 (5.0σ) and 6,210 (4.0σ). Using linear interpolation:

$$
\text{Sigma} \approx 4 + \dfrac{6{,}210 - 600}{6{,}210 - 233} \times 1.0 \approx 4 + \dfrac{5{,}610}{5{,}977} \approx 4.94\sigma
$$

So the process operates at approximately **4.94 sigma**.

**(iii) Comment on Process Capability:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 4.94σ process yields about **99.9965%** defect-free calls. This is **good**, but still well short of Six Sigma (6σ = 3.4 DPMO). The process has about **600 defects per million opportunities**, which is **177 times worse** than the Six Sigma benchmark. Management should launch a **DMAIC project** to identify root causes and reduce variation.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style — Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** concept. Discuss its **four categories** with suitable examples. Why is prevention cost considered an *investment* rather than an *expense*? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bolt-manufacturing process has the following parameters: Target diameter $T = 10.00$ mm, Tolerance $\Delta = 0.05$ mm, and the average loss when at the specification limit is $A_0 = \text{₹} 400$ per bolt.
> - (i) Calculate the **Taguchi quality loss coefficient** $k$.
> - (ii) Find the **loss to society** for a bolt with measured diameter $x = 10.03$ mm.
> - (iii) Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
**Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total financial impact of preventing, detecting, and correcting defects in a product or service. It quantifies the monetary consequence of quality-related activities and is essential for managerial decision-making.

**Four Categories of COQ:**

| Category | Definition | Examples |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Prevention Costs** | Costs of *avoiding* defects | Training, quality planning, supplier evaluation, process design, FMEA |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Costs of *detecting* defects | Incoming inspection, in-process testing, calibration, audits |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, defect analysis, downtime |
| **External Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill |

**Why Prevention is an *Investment*, not an *Expense*:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Expense** reduces current profit; **investment** generates future returns.
> - Investing ₹1 in prevention typically saves ₹10–₹100 in failure costs.
> - Prevention addresses the **root cause**; failure costs are merely symptoms.
> - Increased prevention costs **drive down** the much larger external failure costs (Juran's "Optimum COQ" model).
> - Prevention yields long-term benefits: **brand reputation, customer loyalty, market share** — all of which are not captured in short-term accounting.
> - Hence, prevention spending is a **strategic investment** with measurable ROI.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Target diameter $T = 10.00$ mm
- Tolerance $\Delta = 0.05$ mm
- Loss at specification limit $A_0 = \text{₹}400$ per bolt
- Measured diameter $x = 10.03$ mm

**(i) Calculate the Taguchi quality loss coefficient $k$:**

$$
k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta^2} = \dfrac{400}{(0.05)^2} = \dfrac{400}{0.0025} = 1{,}60{,}000\ \text{₹/mm}^2
$$

**(ii) Loss to society for $x = 10.03$ mm:**

Deviation from target:

$$
x - T = 10.03 - 10.00 = 0.03\ \text{mm}
$$

Loss:

$$
L(x) = k(x - T)^2 = 1{,}60{,}000 \times (0.03)^2 = 1{,}60{,}000 \times 0.0009 = \text{₹}144
$$

**(iii) Comment on the Result:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the bolt (10.03 mm) is **within** the specification limit of 10.05 mm, the **Taguchi loss** is still **₹144 per bolt**.
>
> This is a **critical insight** of TQM: **conformance to specifications does not equal conformance to quality**. A bolt that is "in spec" but **off-target** still causes loss (e.g., loose fit, reduced fatigue life, customer dissatisfaction).
>
> This finding motivates the Six Sigma goal: drive the process **mean exactly onto the target** and shrink the variance to **±6σ within specification**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners penalise heavily:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up PDCA with PDSA:** Some texts call it the **Deming Cycle**, others the **Shewhart Cycle**. Use "PDCA" (Plan–Do–Check–Act) consistently.
> 2. **Confusing Crosby's "Quality is Free" with "Quality costs nothing":** It means *the savings from prevention exceed the prevention costs*, not that quality is free in absolute terms.
> 3. **Wrong COQ formula:** Total COQ is the **sum of all four** categories, not a ratio or a difference.
> 4. **Taguchi loss at $x = T$:** $L(T) = 0$ (zero loss at target) — students often write $L = kT$ by mistake.
> 5. **DPMO vs. DPO confusion:** DPMO is **1,000,000 times DPO**. Forgetting the factor of $10^6$ is the most common error.
> 6. **DPMO–Sigma conversion:** Always cite the **+1.5 sigma shift** for long-term Six Sigma level. Examiners will mark down if the short-term and long-term sigmas are mixed.
> 7. **Omitting diagrams in Part B:** A **labelled PDCA / Cost of Quality / SIPOC diagram** is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 8. **Writing "Quality = Excellence":** This is the **incorrect** Crosby interpretation. Quality is **conformance to requirements**, not "luxury" or "excellence."
> 9. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ measures *spread only*; $C_{pk}$ measures *spread AND centring*. $C_p = 2$ does **not** mean a Six Sigma process.
> 10. **Answering Part A in 2 lines:** Part A requires **3–4 sentences minimum** (80–100 words). One-line answers get 1/3 marks.

---

> ✅ **TQM Definition** — Customer-focused, organization-wide, continuous improvement, all members involved.
>
> ✅ **5 Gurus & 1 Key Idea Each:**
> - **Deming** → System, PDCA, 14 Points, "In God we trust, all others bring data."
> - **Juran** → Fitness for use, Trilogy, Pareto (80/20).
> - **Crosby** → Conformance, Prevention, Zero Defects, PONC, "Quality is Free."
> - **Feigenbaum** → *Total* — quality is everyone's job.
> - **Ishikawa** → Fishbone diagram, Quality Circles.
>
> ✅ **Cost of Quality (COQ):** Total = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
> - Conformance = Prevention + Appraisal.
> - Non-Conformance = Internal + External Failure.
>
> ✅ **PDCA Cycle** — Plan → Do → Check → Act.
>
> ✅ **Taguchi Loss Function** — $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$, where $k = A_0 / \Delta^2$.
>
> ✅ **Process Capability:**
> - $C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL}) / 6\sigma$
> - $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$
> - Six Sigma target: $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$.
>
> ✅ **Yield Formulas:**
> - First Time Yield (FTY) = Good Units / Total Units.
> - Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) = $Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \ldots \times Y_n$.
>
> ✅ **Defect Metrics:**
> - DPO = Defects / (Units × Opportunities).
> - DPMO = DPO × 1,000,000.
> - Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.9997% yield.
>
> ✅ **Key Numerical Benchmarks to Memorise:**
> - 3σ → 66,807 DPMO → 93.3% yield.
> - 4σ → 6,210 DPMO → 99.38% yield.
> - 5σ → 233 DPMO → 99.977% yield.
> - 6σ → 3.4 DPMO → 99.9997% yield.
>
> ✅ **The 8 TQM Principles** (Universal, also ISO 9001:2015): Customer focus, Leadership, People involvement, Process approach, Improvement, Evidence-based decision making, Relationship management, **System thinking**.
>
> ✅ **Evolution of Quality** — Inspection → QC → QA → TQM → Six Sigma.
>
> ✅ **Why TQM matters** — Reduces waste, improves customer satisfaction, lowers long-run cost, builds brand equity, ensures regulatory compliance.

---

## 🎯 Final Examiner's Tip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> In any 14-mark question, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition (1 mark)** — crisp, textbook-style.
> 2. **Body (10–11 marks)** — bulleted points, sub-headings, equations.
> 3. **Diagram (2–3 marks)** — labelled Mermaid-style or hand-drawn block diagram.
> 4. **Conclusion (1 mark)** — 1-2 line summary, link to industry.
>
> Use **tables and equations liberally** — they break up the page, improve readability, and signal to the examiner that you know the syllabus keywords. Always cite the **Guru's name** when quoting a principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total financial outlay incurred by an organization to ensure that its products and services conform to the established quality standards and customer requirements. It is a comprehensive financial measure that quantifies the monetary impact of quality-related activities across the entire value chain.

The term "Cost of Quality" is, by definition, a **paradox** — it does **NOT** mean the cost of producing a high-quality product. Instead, it represents the total cost associated with:
- **Building** a high-quality product (prevention + appraisal)
- **Failing** to build a perfect product (internal + external failure)

As per the **American Society for Quality (ASQ)**, COQ is categorized into four distinct cost categories forming the **PAF Model**.

## 1.2 The PAF Model — Prevention-Appraisal-Failure Framework

| Category | Type | Nature | Timing |
|----------|------|--------|--------|
| **Prevention Costs (P)** | Conformance | Planned investment | Before defect occurs |
| **Appraisal Costs (A)** | Conformance | Planned investment | During detection |
| **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** | Non-conformance | Unplanned loss | Before delivery |
| **External Failure Costs (EFC)** | Non-conformance | Unplanned loss | After delivery |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Vocabulary:** Prevention + Appraisal = **Cost of Conformance (COC)**. Internal + External Failure = **Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)** or **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Leaky Bucket"

Imagine a manufacturing plant as a **leaky bucket** holding water (profits).

- **Prevention costs** = money spent **plugging the holes before they form** (training, quality planning, robust design). Cheap, proactive.
- **Appraisal costs** = money spent **measuring the bucket's water level and inspecting the holes** (inspection, testing). Moderate, detective.
- **Internal failure costs** = water lost **inside the factory** (rework, scrap, re-inspection). Expensive, internal.
- **External failure costs** = water lost **after the bucket has left the factory** (warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits). Catastrophically expensive, external.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule of COQ:** A **1% investment in prevention** typically saves **10–100% in failure costs** — this is the foundational economic logic of Six Sigma.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in KTU 2024 Curriculum

- **COQ as a % of Sales Revenue** — Industry benchmark: **2.5% – 4%** for manufacturing, **15%–40%** for software/services.
- **COPQ Ratio** = `Failure Costs / (Prevention + Appraisal Costs)` — Target: **< 1.0** (ideally 0.3–0.5).
- **Quality Cost Index (QCI)** = `Total COQ / Number of Units Produced`.

---

## 1.5 The Two Classical Schools of Thought

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept** — Know the difference between these two foundational models.

**Joseph Juran's "Gold Mine" Model (1951):** Quality costs are **mineable** — they hide in operations like gold in a mine. He categorized COQ into the PAF model and emphasized that **optimal quality is NOT zero defects**, but the level where the **marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal savings from reduced failure**.

**Philip Crosby's "Zero Defects / P-Z" Model (1979):** Crosby argued that **quality is free** because the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure. He introduced the **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** vs. **Price of Conformance (POC)** terminology. His formula:
- **Quality is Free** ⟹ `POC < PONC` always, so do it right the first time.

---

## 1.6 Hidden Costs of Quality

> [!WARNING]
> KTU boards frequently award marks for mentioning **hidden costs**, which are not captured in standard accounting ledgers.

Hidden costs include: **customer dissatisfaction, lost goodwill, employee morale decay, missed business opportunities, excessive inventory due to over-processing, warranty administration overhead, and opportunity costs from production line stoppages**.

---

## 1.7 Strategic Importance of COQ in Six Sigma

In Six Sigma, COQ is the **financial translator** of the DMAIC project. Without COQ quantification:
- Projects cannot be prioritized.
- Savings cannot be validated.
- The **"Black Belt" business case** cannot be approved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Six Sigma goal is to **shift the cost curve** — driving Prevention/Appraisal UP modestly while driving Internal/External Failure DOWN dramatically, achieving a **net reduction in total COQ** of **20–40%** per project.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Four Cost Categories

### A. Prevention Costs (P)
**Definition:** Costs incurred to **prevent the occurrence of defects** in the first place.

| Sub-Component | Industry Example | Typical Range |
|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| Quality planning & administration | Quality manuals, procedures | 5–10% of COQ |
| Training (Six Sigma, SPC, TQM) | Green/Black Belt programs | 5–15% of COQ |
| Product/process design reviews | DFMEA, PFMEA, APQP | 15–25% of COQ |
| Supplier capability development | Vendor audits, training | 5–10% of COQ |
| Process capability studies | Cpk, Ppk analysis | 3–8% of COQ |
| Quality improvement projects | DMAIC projects, Kaizens | 10–20% of COQ |

### B. Appraisal Costs (A)
**Definition:** Costs of **measuring, evaluating, and auditing** products/services to detect defects.

| Sub-Component | Example | Typical Range |
|---------------|---------|---------------|
| Incoming inspection | Material receiving tests | 10–20% of COQ |
| In-process inspection | Line checks, gaging | 20–30% of COQ |
| Final product testing | Functional, burn-in tests | 15–25% of COQ |
| Calibration of test equipment | Gage R&R studies | 5–10% of COQ |
| Quality audits (system/process) | ISO 9001, AS9100 audits | 5–10% of COQ |

### C. Internal Failure Costs (IFC)
**Definition:** Costs of defects discovered **before the product reaches the customer**.

| Sub-Component | Example | Typical Range |
|---------------|---------|---------------|
| Scrap (raw material wastage) | Rejected castings, boards | 15–25% of COQ |
| Rework / repair | Manual re-soldering, re-machining | 20–35% of COQ |
| Re-inspection of reworked items | Additional 100% check | 5–10% of COQ |
| Material review / disposition | MRB board activities | 3–8% of COQ |
| Downtime due to quality issues | Line stoppages, changeovers | 10–20% of COQ |
| Failure mode analysis (internal) | Root cause investigations | 3–5% of COQ |

### D. External Failure Costs (EFC)
**Definition:** Costs of defects discovered **after the product reaches the customer** — the **most damaging** category.

| Sub-Component | Example | Typical Range |
|---------------|---------|---------------|
| Warranty claims | Free parts, labor reimbursement | 20–30% of COQ |
| Field repairs / service | Technician dispatches | 10–20% of COQ |
| Product recalls | Massive, company-threatening | Variable, catastrophic |
| Returned materials (RMA) | Reverse logistics costs | 5–15% of COQ |
| Customer complaint handling | Call centers, investigation | 5–10% of COQ |
| Litigation / legal costs | Lawsuits, settlements | Variable, often hidden |
| Lost customer goodwill | Future revenue impact | Hard to quantify |
| Penalties / regulatory fines | Government fines | Sector-specific |

---

## 2.2 The Total COQ Equation

$$\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IFC + EFC$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kano's Strategic Insight:** The **optimum quality level is NOT 100% conformance**. It is the point where the **marginal cost of preventing one more defect** equals the **marginal savings from avoiding that defect**.

## 2.3 The Optimal COQ Curve (Feigenbaum Model)

> This is the most graphable concept in Module 1 — KTU frequently asks for a labeled diagram.

**Behavior of the four cost curves:**

1. **Prevention (P)** rises **monotonically and steeply** as quality level increases.
2. **Appraisal (A)** rises gradually, peaks at high quality, then drops slightly (less to inspect).
3. **Internal Failure (IFC)** falls steeply, then levels off.
4. **External Failure (EFC)** falls steeply, then levels off near zero.
5. **Total COQ** is **U-shaped** with a clear **minimum** at the **economic optimum (Q\*)**.

Mathematically, the optimum Q\* is found by:

$$\frac{d(\text{Total COQ})}{dQ} = \frac{dP}{dQ} + \frac{dA}{dQ} + \frac{dIFC}{dQ} + \frac{dEFC}{dQ} = 0$$

At this optimum, the **marginal prevention cost equals the marginal failure savings**:

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = -\left(\frac{dIFC}{dQ} + \frac{dEFC}{dQ}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of numerical KTU questions on COQ.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | `COQ = P + A + IFC + EFC` | Total cost of quality | ₹/year |
| 2 | `COPQ = IFC + EFC` | Cost of poor quality (non-conformance) | ₹/year |
| 3 | `COC = P + A` | Cost of conformance | ₹/year |
| 4 | `COQ% = (Total COQ / Sales) × 100` | COQ as percentage of sales | % |
| 5 | `COPQ Ratio = (IFC + EFC) / (P + A)` | Failure-to-prevention ratio | Dimensionless |
| 6 | `Savings = COQ_{before} − COQ_{after}` | Project savings | ₹/year |
| 7 | `QCI = Total COQ / Units Produced` | Quality Cost Index | ₹/unit |
| 8 | `COQ/GDP = Industry benchmark` | Sector benchmarking | % |
| 9 | `PONC = IFC + EFC` | Price of non-conformance (Crosby) | ₹/year |
| 10 | `POC = P + A` | Price of conformance (Crosby) | ₹/year |
| 11 | `Defect Cost / Defect = (IFC + EFC) / N_{defects}` | Cost per defect (DPMO calc) | ₹/defect |
| 12 | `Yield Multiplier = 1 / (1 − DPMO)` | Process yield equivalent | Ratio |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of COQ

| Industry | Primary Use of COQ |
|----------|--------------------|
| **Manufacturing** | Justify Six Sigma project capex, balance line quality |
| **Software/IT** | Quantify bug-fix cost vs. design prevention (CMMI Level 5) |
| **Healthcare** | Reduce hospital readmissions, surgical complications |
| **Service Sector** | Reduce customer churn, call-center rework |
| **Aerospace/Defence** | AS9100 compliance, supplier cost recovery |
| **Automotive** | IATF 16949 / APQP, warranty reduction programs |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Perspective:** Six Sigma projects without a COQ business case **will fail the project approval committee**. The CFO signs the project charter **only if** the projected COQ reduction exceeds the project cost within 12 months.

---

## 2.6 Activity-Based Costing (ABC) for Quality

Traditional accounting **fails** to capture true quality costs because:
- It pools overhead into a single cost center.
- It does not track **process-level** quality costs.
- It ignores **appraisal labor** that is buried in "indirect labor".

**ABC for Quality (Cooper-Kaplan Method)** assigns costs to:
- **Activities** (e.g., "rework an engine block") — measured in cost-driver units (e.g., hours).
- **Activity rates** = `Total Activity Cost / Total Cost Driver Volume`.
- **Product-level COQ** = `Σ (Activity Rate × Cost Driver Used by Product)`.

This allows COQ to be allocated per product, per customer, per process step — enabling **granular Six Sigma project targeting**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — COQ Composition Analysis

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style):** A manufacturing unit has the following annual quality-related data:
> - Quality training programs: ₹2,00,000
> - Inspection of incoming materials: ₹1,50,000
> - Scrap and waste: ₹3,00,000
> - Warranty claims from customers: ₹6,00,000
> - Process capability studies: ₹1,00,000
> - Rework labor: ₹2,50,000
> - Design reviews and DFMEA: ₹1,50,000
> - Product returns: ₹4,00,000
> - Calibration of measurement equipment: ₹50,000
>
> **Required:**
> (a) Classify into PAF categories, compute Total COQ, COC, COPQ, COQ as % of sales (assume sales = ₹50,00,000).
> (b) Comment on the cost structure and recommend a Six Sigma strategy.

### Step 1: Classify Each Item into PAF

| S.No. | Item | Amount (₹) | Category | Justification |
|-------|------|-----------:|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Quality training programs | 2,00,000 | **P** | Prevents defects via skill |
| 2 | Inspection of incoming materials | 1,50,000 | **A** | Detects defects |
| 3 | Scrap and waste | 3,00,000 | **IFC** | Defect found internally |
| 4 | Warranty claims | 6,00,000 | **EFC** | Defect reached customer |
| 5 | Process capability studies | 1,00,000 | **P** | Prevents defects |
| 6 | Rework labor | 2,50,000 | **IFC** | Internal correction |
| 7 | Design reviews and DFMEA | 1,50,000 | **P** | Prevents design defects |
| 8 | Product returns | 4,00,000 | **EFC** | Customer returned |
| 9 | Calibration of equipment | 50,000 | **A** | Maintains measurement |

### Step 2: Sum Each Category

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000 \\
A &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \\
IFC &= 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 \\
EFC &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute Aggregated Metrics

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total COQ} &= P + A + IFC + EFC \\
&= 4{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}50{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 22{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{COC} = P + A = 4{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{COPQ} = IFC + EFC = 5{,}50{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{COPQ Ratio} = \frac{IFC + EFC}{P + A} = \frac{15{,}50{,}000}{6{,}50{,}000} = 2.385$$

$$\text{COQ\% of Sales} = \frac{22{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 44\%$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The company spends ₹2.38 on failure for every ₹1 on prevention.** The benchmark is **< ₹1.00**. Total COQ at **44% of sales** is alarmingly high (benchmark: 2.5–4% for manufacturing).

**Six Sigma Recommendation:**
- Failure costs (₹15.5L) constitute **70.5%** of total COQ.
- **Strategy:** Aggressively increase prevention spend by **30–40%** (to ₹6–7L) through DMAIC projects focused on design robustness and supplier quality.
- **Expected outcome:** COPQ will drop to **< ₹1.00 ratio** within 18 months, yielding **savings of ₹6–9L per year**.

---

## 3.2 The Optimal COQ Derivation (Feigenbaum Model)

**Given:** The four cost components as functions of quality level $Q$ (where $Q \in [0, 1]$ represents proportion of good units).

**Assume** the following empirical functional forms (industry-standard):

$$\begin{aligned}
P(Q) &= a \cdot Q^2 \quad \text{(Prevention cost grows quadratically)} \\
A(Q) &= b \cdot Q \quad \text{(Appraisal grows linearly)} \\
IFC(Q) &= c \cdot (1 - Q)^2 \quad \text{(Quadratic decay)} \\
EFC(Q) &= d \cdot (1 - Q)^2 \quad \text{(Quadratic decay, higher coefficient)}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $a, b, c, d > 0$ are empirically determined coefficients.

**Total COQ:**

$$TC(Q) = aQ^2 + bQ + c(1-Q)^2 + d(1-Q)^2$$

**Simplify:**

$$TC(Q) = aQ^2 + bQ + (c+d)(1 - 2Q + Q^2)$$

$$TC(Q) = (a + c + d)Q^2 + (b - 2c - 2d)Q + (c + d)$$

**To find optimum**, differentiate w.r.t. $Q$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dTC}{dQ} = 2(a + c + d)Q + (b - 2c - 2d) = 0$$

**Solve for $Q^*$:**

$$Q^* = \frac{2(c + d) - b}{2(a + c + d)} = \frac{(c + d) - b/2}{a + c + d}$$

**Second derivative check** (for minimum):

$$\frac{d^2TC}{dQ^2} = 2(a + c + d) > 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (minimum confirmed)}$$

**Economic interpretation:** The optimum quality level is **NOT** 100% — it is the level at which the **incremental cost of further prevention is exactly offset by the incremental savings in failure costs**.

---

## 3.3 The Hidden COQ Multiplier Effect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Essay Concept:** Industry studies (Berger, 1997) have shown that **only 30–40% of true quality costs appear in the PAF accounts**. The remaining **60–70% is hidden** in overhead pools, lost productivity, and opportunity costs.

| Visible COQ (PAF accounts) | Hidden COQ (must be estimated) |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Inspection labor | Managerial time on firefighting |
| Scrap & rework | Lost machine capacity (utilization) |
| Warranty | Lost customer lifetime value |
| Calibration | Excess inventory from over-processing |
| Test equipment | Employee morale & turnover |
| Defect analysis | Engineering rework time |
| Training budget | Lost sales from reputation damage |

**Multiplier Rule of Thumb:** `True COQ ≈ 3 × to 4 × × (Visible PAF COQ)` in service industries, and **2× to 3×** in manufacturing.

---

## 3.4 Activity-Based Costing (ABC) for Quality — Step-by-Step

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Scenario:** A factory has 3 product lines (A, B, C). Quality-related activities and their cost drivers are:
>
> | Activity | Cost (₹) | Cost Driver | Total Driver Volume |
> |----------|---------:|-------------|--------------------:|
> | Incoming inspection | 3,00,000 | # of receipt lots | 500 |
> | In-process rework | 6,00,000 | Rework hours | 1,200 |
> | Warranty repair | 9,00,000 | Warranty claims | 300 |
> | Quality training | 1,50,000 | Training hours | 750 |

**Step 1: Compute activity rates:**

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{inspection}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{500} = 600 \text{ ₹/lot}$$

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{rework}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}200} = 500 \text{ ₹/hour}$$

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{warranty}} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{300} = 3{,}000 \text{ ₹/claim}$$

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{training}} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{750} = 200 \text{ ₹/hour}$$

**Step 2: Assign to Product Line A** (used 100 lots, 300 rework hrs, 50 claims, 200 training hrs):

$$\text{COQ}_A = (600)(100) + (500)(300) + (3{,}000)(50) + (200)(200)$$

$$\text{COQ}_A = 60{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

> **Insight for Six Sigma:** Product Line A consumes **40% of total COQ (₹4L of ₹19.5L)** despite unknown revenue share — making it the **#1 candidate for a DMAIC project**.

---

## 3.5 The COQ Reduction Roadmap (Six Sigma Logic)

The strategic shift in COQ distribution across project phases:

| Project Phase | P% | A% | IFC% | EFC% | Total COQ |
|---------------|---:|---:|----:|----:|----------:|
| **Baseline (Before Six Sigma)** | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 100% |
| **After 1 Six Sigma Project** | 18 | 22 | 25 | 35 | ~85% |
| **After Mature Six Sigma (3+ years)** | 30 | 30 | 20 | 20 | ~50% |

> **Validation:** Total COQ drops by **50%**, and the cost mix **inverts** — the company moves from a "**firefighting culture**" to a "**prevention culture**".

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAF Model Hierarchy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["COQ SYSTEM"] --> B["CONFORMANCE COSTS COC"]
    A --> C["NON CONFORMANCE COSTS COPQ"]
    
    B --> B1["PREVENTION COSTS P"]
    B --> B2["APPRAISAL COSTS A"]
    
    C --> C1["INTERNAL FAILURE COSTS IFC"]
    C --> C2["EXTERNAL FAILURE COSTS EFC"]
    
    B1 --> B1A["Quality Planning"]
    B1 --> B1B["Training and Education"]
    B1 --> B1C["Process Design"]
    B1 --> B1D["Supplier Capability"]
    B1 --> B1E["DFMEA and PFMEA"]
    
    B2 --> B2A["Incoming Inspection"]
    B2 --> B2B["In Process Inspection"]
    B2 --> B2C["Final Product Testing"]
    B2 --> B2D["Calibration"]
    B2 --> B2E["Quality Audits"]
    
    C1 --> C1A["Scrap and Waste"]
    C1 --> C1B["Rework and Repair"]
    C1 --> C1C["Re Inspection"]
    C1 --> C1D["Material Review Board"]
    C1 --> C1E["Internal Downtime"]
    
    C2 --> C2A["Warranty Claims"]
    C2 --> C2B["Field Service"]
    C2 --> C2C["Product Returns"]
    C2 --> C2D["Customer Complaints"]
    C2 --> C2E["Litigation and Recalls"]
    C2 --> C2F["Lost Goodwill"]
```

## 4.2 Six Sigma COQ Reduction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1 BASELINE COQ\nCapture PAF Costs"] --> S2["STEP 2 COQ CLASSIFICATION\nPAF Matrix"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 COPQ ANALYSIS\nIdentify Failure Hotspots"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 DMAIC PROJECT\nSelection of High COQ Areas"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 INCREASE P SPEND\nPrevention and Training"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 REDUCE IFC\nRoot Cause and Rework"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7 REDUCE EFC\nFMEA and Robust Design"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8 POST COQ\nMeasure Total Savings"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9 STANDARDIZE\nControl Phase and SOPs"]
    S9 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Hidden vs Visible COQ — Iceberg Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W["WATERLINE CUSTOMER VISIBILITY THRESHOLD"]
    
    W --> ABOVE["ABOVE WATERLINE VISIBLE COQ 30 to 40 PERCENT"]
    W --> BELOW["BELOW WATERLINE HIDDEN COQ 60 to 70 PERCENT"]
    
    ABOVE --> A1["Scrap and Rework"]
    ABOVE --> A2["Warranty Costs"]
    ABOVE --> A3["Inspection and Testing"]
    ABOVE --> A4["Calibration Costs"]
    ABOVE --> A5["Quality Training"]
    
    BELOW --> B1["Lost Customer Goodwill"]
    BELOW --> B2["Managerial Firefighting Time"]
    BELOW --> B3["Excess Inventory"]
    BELOW --> B4["Lost Machine Capacity"]
    BELOW --> B5["Employee Turnover"]
    BELOW --> B6["Engineering Rework Time"]
    BELOW --> B7["Opportunity Cost Lost Sales"]
    BELOW --> B8["Reputation Damage"]
```

## 4.4 Optimal COQ Curve — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["QUALITY LEVEL Q INCREASES LEFT TO RIGHT"]
    
    P["PREVENTION COST P\nMonotonically Rising\nQuadratic Slope"]
    A["APPRAISAL COST A\nSlowly Rising\nThen Declines"]
    IFC["INTERNAL FAILURE\nSteeply Falling\nThen Plateaus"]
    EFC["EXTERNAL FAILURE\nSteeply Falling\nApproaches Zero"]
    TC["TOTAL COQ CURVE\nU SHAPED\nHas Optimum Q STAR"]
    
    Q --> P
    Q --> A
    Q --> IFC
    Q --> EFC
    P --> TC
    A --> TC
    IFC --> TC
    EFC --> TC
    
    TC --> OPT["OPTIMUM Q STAR\nWhere Marginal P = Marginal Failure Savings\nNOT at 100 Percent Quality"]
```

## 4.5 Activity-Based Costing (ABC) for Quality — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["RESOURCE POOLS\nLabor Equipment Overhead"] --> A1["ACTIVITY 1\nIncoming Inspection\nCost 300000\nDriver Receipt Lots 500"]
    R1 --> A2["ACTIVITY 2\nIn Process Rework\nCost 600000\nDriver Rework Hours 1200"]
    R1 --> A3["ACTIVITY 3\nWarranty Service\nCost 900000\nDriver Warranty Claims 300"]
    R1 --> A4["ACTIVITY 4\nQuality Training\nCost 150000\nDriver Training Hours 750"]
    
    A1 --> AR1["ACTIVITY RATE\n600 per lot"]
    A2 --> AR2["ACTIVITY RATE\n500 per hour"]
    A3 --> AR3["ACTIVITY RATE\n3000 per claim"]
    A4 --> AR4["ACTIVITY RATE\n200 per hour"]
    
    AR1 --> P1["PRODUCT LINE A COQ\n100 lots 300 hrs\n50 claims 200 hrs\nTotal 400000"]
    AR2 --> P1
    AR3 --> P1
    AR4 --> P1
    
    AR1 --> P2["PRODUCT LINE B COQ"]
    AR2 --> P2
    AR3 --> P2
    AR4 --> P2
    
    AR1 --> P3["PRODUCT LINE C COQ"]
    AR2 --> P3
    AR3 --> P3
    AR4 --> P3
    
    P1 --> SEL["SIX SIGMA TARGET\nHighest COQ Product Selected\nfor DMAIC Project"]
    P2 --> SEL
    P3 --> SEL
```

---
