<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Cost of Quality (COQ). List and briefly explain its four major components.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total financial cost associated with ensuring product/service quality and the financial loss from failing to meet quality standards. It comprises four components:
> 1. **Prevention Costs (P):** Costs to prevent defects (e.g., training, quality planning).
> 2. **Appraisal Costs (A):** Costs to detect defects (e.g., inspection, testing).
> 3. **Internal Failure Costs (IFC):** Costs of defects found before delivery (e.g., scrap, rework).
> 4. **External Failure Costs (EFC):** Costs of defects found after delivery (e.g., warranty, returns).
> These form the **PAF Model**, where P+A = Cost of Conformance (COC) and IFC+EFC = Cost of Non-Conformance (COPQ).

### **Question 2: Distinguish between Cost of Conformance (COC) and Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC) with examples.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> | Aspect | COC (Conformance) | CONC (Non-Conformance) |
> |--------|-------------------|------------------------|
> | **Definition** | Costs of meeting quality requirements | Costs of failing to meet quality |
> | **Components** | P + A | IFC + EFC |
> | **Nature** | Planned investment | Unplanned loss |
> | **Examples** | Training, calibration, design review | Scrap, warranty, recall |
> | **Impact** | Builds capability | Damages reputation & cost base |
> | **Six Sigma focus** | Optimize | Minimize aggressively |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Part B questions carry a compulsory internal choice — students must answer EITHER (a) OR (b).**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define Cost of Quality. With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **optimal COQ curve** (Feigenbaum model). Discuss why **100% quality is NOT economically optimal**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company has the following annual quality data:
- Statistical process control: ₹1,20,000
- Customer returns: ₹5,00,000
- Design reviews: ₹1,80,000
- Rework labor: ₹2,40,000
- Final testing: ₹90,000
- Warranty repairs: ₹4,50,000
- Supplier audit: ₹60,000
- Scrap: ₹1,80,000
- Calibration: ₹40,000
- Total Sales: ₹60,00,000

Compute: **(i)** P, A, IFC, EFC; **(ii)** Total COQ; **(iii)** COQ as % of sales; **(iv)** COPQ ratio; **(v)** Comment on the cost structure. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Cost of Quality is the total cost of conformance plus the total cost of non-conformance: `COQ = (P + A) + (IFC + EFC)`.

**Diagrammatic Description of the Optimal COQ Curve (3 Marks):**

Plot four cost components on the Y-axis and quality level (0% to 100%) on the X-axis:
- **P curve** rises from origin, accelerating upward (quadratic).
- **A curve** rises gently, peaks at high quality, then drops slightly.
- **IFC curve** starts high, falls steeply, then plateaus near zero.
- **EFC curve** starts very high, falls steeply, approaches zero.
- **Total COQ curve** = sum of all four = a **U-shaped curve** with a clear minimum at **Q\***.

**Why 100% Quality is NOT Optimal (2 Marks):**
1. Prevention costs grow **super-linearly** (often quadratically) — the last 1% of quality can cost as much as the first 99%.
2. Beyond **Q\***, the marginal cost of prevention **exceeds** the marginal savings from reduced failure.
3. Six Sigma philosophy targets **3.4 DPMO** (99.9997% good), not 100%, because of diminishing economic returns.
4. Resources spent on the "last 0.0003%" could deliver **higher ROI** elsewhere in the organization.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1: Classify each item into PAF categories:**

| Item | Amount (₹) | Category | Justification |
|------|-----------:|----------|---------------|
| Statistical process control | 1,20,000 | **P** | Prevents process drift |
| Customer returns | 5,00,000 | **EFC** | Post-delivery defect |
| Design reviews | 1,80,000 | **P** | Prevents design errors |
| Rework labor | 2,40,000 | **IFC** | Internal correction |
| Final testing | 90,000 | **A** | Detects defects |
| Warranty repairs | 4,50,000 | **EFC** | Post-delivery defect |
| Supplier audit | 60,000 | **P** | Prevents incoming defects |
| Scrap | 1,80,000 | **IFC** | Material wastage |
| Calibration | 40,000 | **A** | Maintains measurement |

**Step 2: Compute P, A, IFC, EFC (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000 \\
A &= 90{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 1{,}30{,}000 \\
IFC &= 2{,}40{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 = 4{,}20{,}000 \\
EFC &= 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 = 9{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Total COQ (1 Mark):**

$$\text{Total COQ} = 3{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}30{,}000 + 4{,}20{,}000 + 9{,}50{,}000 = 18{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4: COQ as % of Sales (1 Mark):**

$$\text{COQ\%} = \frac{18{,}60{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 31\%$$

**Step 5: COPQ Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$\text{COPQ Ratio} = \frac{IFC + EFC}{P + A} = \frac{4{,}20{,}000 + 9{,}50{,}000}{3{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}30{,}000} = \frac{13{,}70{,}000}{4{,}90{,}000} = 2.80$$

**Step 6: Comments on Cost Structure (1 Mark):**

> [!WARNING]
> The company is **failing 2.80 times more** than it is preventing. With COQ at **31% of sales** (benchmark 2.5–4%), failure costs constitute **73.6%** of total COQ. This is a **chronic quality problem** requiring:
> 1. **Immediate DMAIC project** on the highest EFC contributor (warranty).
> 2. **Double the prevention budget** in 12 months.
> 3. **Target COPQ ratio < 1.0** within 18 months.
> 4. **Expected annual savings:** ₹6–9 lakh through structured Six Sigma execution.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain **Crosby's "Quality is Free"** model. Compare and contrast the **Juran model** and the **Crosby model** of quality costs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **Hidden Costs of Quality**. How do Activity-Based Costing (ABC) methods help in accurate COQ estimation? Illustrate with an example. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Crosby's Model (2.5 Marks):**
- Philip Crosby (1979) — author of *Quality is Free*.
- Argues that **the cost of doing things right (Price of Conformance, POC) is always less than the cost of doing them wrong (Price of Non-Conformance, PONC)**.
- **PONC = IFC + EFC**; **POC = P + A**.
- Crosby's famous maxim: **"Do it right the first time" (DIRFT)**.
- Stresses **Zero Defects** culture, **management involvement**, and **prevention-based thinking**.
- He rejected the "optimal quality" concept — quality is free because failure is always more expensive.

**Juran's Model (2.5 Marks):**
- Joseph Juran (1951) — quality is built through **planning, control, and improvement** (the Juran Trilogy).
- Juran **accepted the concept of optimum quality** — beyond Q\*, prevention costs outweigh failure savings.
- Quality costs are **"gold in the mine"** — they are hidden in operations and must be systematically mined.
- Emphasized the **Pareto principle (80/20)** for quality problems.
- Stressed **project-by-project breakthrough improvement** — origin of Six Sigma's project-based approach.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Juran | Crosby |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Optimum quality concept** | Yes (Q\*) | No (Zero Defects) |
| **Terminology** | PAF model | POC / PONC |
| **Approach** | Project-based breakthrough | DIRFT, prevention culture |
| **Cost of quality** | Mineable, variable | Free (failure is costlier) |
| **Pareto** | Yes (80/20) | No |
| **Six Sigma alignment** | Strong (project selection) | Strong (culture) |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Hidden Costs of Quality (3.5 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hidden costs are quality-related expenses **not captured in standard PAF accounting ledgers** — they are buried in overhead, lost productivity, and opportunity costs.

| Hidden Cost | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| Lost customer goodwill | Future revenue erosion due to dissatisfaction |
| Managerial firefighting time | Hours spent on defect crises |
| Excess inventory | Buffer stock from over-processing/rework |
| Lost machine capacity | Idle time from quality stoppages |
| Employee morale decay | Turnover, absenteeism linked to quality issues |
| Engineering rework time | Designers fixing field defects |
| Lost sales opportunities | Orders lost to damaged reputation |
| Reputation damage | Brand value erosion |

**Empirical evidence:** Studies show hidden COQ is **2× to 4×** the visible PAF COQ, often making up **60–70%** of total quality cost.

**Activity-Based Costing (ABC) for COQ (3.5 Marks):**

Traditional accounting **fails** because it pools all overhead into one bucket. ABC:
1. **Identifies activities** (e.g., incoming inspection, rework, calibration).
2. **Assigns costs** to each activity.
3. **Determines cost drivers** (units that cause cost — e.g., lots inspected, hours reworked).
4. **Computes activity rate** = `Total Activity Cost / Total Cost Driver Volume`.
5. **Allocates to products** based on their consumption of cost drivers.

**Numerical Illustration:**

Suppose a plant has two activities:
- **Rework activity:** Total cost ₹6,00,000; 1,200 rework hours.
- **Warranty activity:** Total cost ₹9,00,000; 300 warranty claims.

Activity rates:
$$\text{Rate}_{\text{rework}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}200} = 500 \text{ ₹/hour}$$

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{warranty}} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{300} = 3{,}000 \text{ ₹/claim}$$

If Product X consumed 200 rework hours and 40 warranty claims:

$$\text{COQ}_X = (500)(200) + (3{,}000)(40) = 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 2{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

This **product-level COQ** enables precise Six Sigma project prioritization — the product with the highest per-unit COQ becomes the **DMAIC target**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Misclassifying P and A:** Training = **P** (not A). Inspection = **A** (not P). Students often mix these up — lose 1 mark per item.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute COPQ ratio:** The COQ% alone is **incomplete**. KTU examiners specifically check for `COPQ Ratio = (IFC+EFC)/(P+A)` interpretation.
> 3. **Skipping the "Comment on Cost Structure"** in numerical questions — this is worth **1–2 marks** and is frequently missed.
> 4. **Confusing COQ with Cost of Production** — COQ does **NOT** include raw material or direct labor.
> 5. **Ignoring hidden costs** in theory questions — always mention them for a complete answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid revision checklist — master these 20 points before the KTU exam.**

- ☐ **COQ = P + A + IFC + EFC** is the master equation.
- ☐ **PAF Model** = Prevention + Appraisal + Failure (Internal + External).
- ☐ **COC = P + A** (Cost of Conformance).
- ☐ **COPQ = IFC + EFC** (Cost of Poor Quality / Non-Conformance).
- ☐ **Crosby:** POC vs PONC — "Quality is Free."
- ☐ **Juran:** Optimum Q\* — quality is "gold in the mine."
- ☐ **Feigenbaum:** U-shaped total COQ curve, optimum at Q\*.
- ☐ **At Q\***: Marginal prevention cost = Marginal failure savings.
- ☐ **100% quality is NOT optimal** — diminishing economic returns.
- ☐ **COPQ Ratio** = `(IFC+EFC) / (P+A)` — target **< 1.0**.
- ☐ **COQ % of Sales** — benchmark **2.5–4%** (mfg), **15–40%** (services).
- ☐ **Hidden COQ** = 2× to 4× the visible COQ; ignored by traditional accounting.
- ☐ **ABC for Quality** allocates COQ to products using activity drivers.
- ☐ **EFC is the most damaging** — typically 30–50% of total COQ when unaddressed.
- ☐ **Six Sigma goal:** shift mix from failure-heavy to prevention-heavy; **reduce total COQ by 20–40%** per project.
- ☐ **Training, planning, design reviews = Prevention.**
- ☐ **Inspection, testing, calibration = Appraisal.**
- ☐ **Scrap, rework, re-inspection = Internal Failure.**
- ☐ **Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits = External Failure.**
- ☐ **COQ is the financial translator** that converts Six Sigma projects into **CFO-approved business cases**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma Project Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma Project Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma Project Identification** is the structured, data-driven **first sub-phase of the DEFINE stage** in the DMAIC roadmap. It is the formal process of detecting, screening, selecting, prioritizing, and chartering improvement projects whose successful completion will deliver measurable strategic, financial, and operational benefits to the organization, while remaining tightly aligned with **Voice of Customer (VOC)**, **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** outputs, and the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**.

The identification process is *not* the random picking of a problem. It is a **portfolio-level management activity** that filters the *universe* of all possible problems through a hierarchy of strategic, financial, and technical screens, until only those projects that are **vital few** (per the Pareto Principle) remain to be attacked by a Black Belt / Green Belt team.

The three formal deliverables produced during Project Identification are:
1. **Project Charter** (the legal contract for the project)
2. **Problem & Goal Statement**
3. **High-level Project Scope & Boundary Map**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏥 Hospital Analogy — "The Triage Room"**
> Imagine a massive government hospital flooded with 10,000 patient complaints in a single week. The Chief Medical Officer cannot personally treat every patient — there are only 6 specialist doctors (the Black Belts) available this month. So she stands at the *Triage Room* and sorts:
> * **Life-threatening cases (heart attacks, strokes)** → treated **first** (high-impact, high-urgency).
> * **Painful but non-fatal cases (fractures, infections)** → queued next.
> * **Cosmetic issues (skin tags, mild rashes)** → referred to general OPD or rejected.
>
> The CMO is performing **Project Identification**. She uses three filters: *Severity*, *Frequency*, and *Strategic fit*. The patients she lets into the specialist wing are her **Six Sigma Projects**. The rest are either deprioritized or solved by routine KAIZEN / process-improvement work.
>
> The hospital that picks the wrong patients (treats cosmetic issues while heart attacks wait) **dies** — just as a company that runs Six Sigma on low-value problems wastes its Black Belt talent and earns zero ROI.

---

### 1.3 Key Performance Anchors (The Magic Numbers)

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** | **3.4 defects per million opportunities** | Six Sigma quality level |
| **Yield (Y)** | **99.99966 %** | Process output correctness at 6σ |
| **Shift (Z)** | **1.5 σ long-term drift** | Motorola correction factor |
| **COPQ** | **15 – 40 % of sales** | Typical hidden cost pool |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four constants are **exam-favorite figures**. Any KTU question asking "Define Six Sigma quality" expects you to recall the **3.4 DPMO** number verbatim.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Filters of Six Sigma Project Identification

A raw "problem" must pass through five sequential filters before it earns the title *Six Sigma Project*. The candidate problem is rejected at any filter if it fails the corresponding test.

1. **Strategic Alignment Filter** — Does the problem directly support an enterprise-level strategic goal (revenue, market share, customer retention, regulatory compliance)?
2. **Voice-of-Customer Filter** — Is there a *measurable* CTQ that the customer cares about (on-time delivery, defect rate, response time)?
3. **Financial (COPQ) Filter** — Can the project recover a quantified amount of *Cost of Poor Quality* (waste, rework, warranty, churn)?
4. **Technical Feasibility Filter** — Can the existing team, with available data and process knowledge, solve it within 3 – 6 months using DMAIC tools?
5. **Scope & Control Filter** — Does the problem lie *within* the team's span of control (process boundaries, authority to implement change)?

A problem passing all five becomes a chartered Six Sigma project. A problem failing the technical-feasibility or scope filter is often re-routed to a **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** project instead.

---

### 2.2 The 5 Sources of Project Identification (Where Projects Originate)

| # | Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **VOC / VOB / VOP** (Customer / Business / Process) | "Delivery is too slow" |
| 2 | **COPQ Audit** (waste, rework, scrap, warranty) | Scrap rate = 12 % |
| 3 | **Pareto Analysis** of defects | 80 % of defects from 20 % of part-numbers |
| 4 | **Benchmarking & Competitive Intelligence** | Competitor's yield = 99.5 %, ours = 96 % |
| 5 | **Strategic Deployment (Hoshin Kanri) Matrix** | CEO's annual objective = "reduce field failures by 50 %" |

---

### 2.3 The Project Charter (The Birth Certificate of a Six Sigma Project)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Project Charter is NOT optional**. Without a signed charter, no DMAIC project legally exists in a KTU/industry-graded Six Sigma programme. The charter has **8 mandatory components**:

1. **Project Title** (action-oriented: "Reduce billing-cycle defects in Hospital Wing-B by 60 %")
2. **Problem Statement** (What? Where? When? How big?)
3. **Goal Statement** (SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)
4. **Project Scope** (In-scope / Out-of-scope boundary map)
5. **Team Members & Roles** (Sponsor, Champion, Master Black Belt, Black Belt, Green Belt, Process Owner)
6. **CTQ Characteristics** (the Y's to be improved)
7. **Milestones / Phases** (DMAIC + tollgates + dates)
8. **Financial Justification** (estimated COPQ recovery in ₹ / $ / €)

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Always present numerical answers with their unit symbol (₹, %, σ, DPMO).** Examiners deduct marks if units are missing.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$$ | Defects per million opportunities | defects/M-opp |
| 2 | $$\text{Yield (Y)} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{U-D}{U}\right)^{}$$ | Rolled-throughput yield (per stage) | fraction (0–1) |
| 3 | $$\text{RTY} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$$ | Rolled throughput yield across n stages | fraction (0–1) |
| 4 | $$\text{Defect Rate} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{U}$$ | Probability of a defect | fraction |
| 5 | $$\sigma \;=\; Z_{\text{short}} + 1.5$$ | Short-term σ to long-term σ conversion | dimensionless |
| 6 | $$\text{COPQ Savings} \;=\; (C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}) \times Q_{\text{annual}}$$ | Annualized cost of poor quality recovered | ₹ / $ / € |
| 7 | $$\text{ROI} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100$$ | Project return on investment | % |
| 8 | $$\text{Payback Period} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Project Cost}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$ | Time to recover the project investment | months / years |
| 9 | $$\text{NPV} \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$$ | Net present value of project | ₹ / $ / € |
| 10 | $$\text{Pareto Cumulative \%} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum \text{Frequency}_{i}}{\text{Total}} \times 100$$ | Vital-few vs. trivial-many cutoff | % |

> [!WARNING]
> In every formula involving **absolute value** (e.g., $|Z - 1.5|$), use the LaTeX command `\vert` inside markdown — never the pipe `|` character — to avoid breaking the table syntax.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Manufacturing plants** use Project Identification to shortlist the *vital few* defect categories (e.g., solder-defects on a PCB line) before assigning a Black Belt team.
* **Hospitals** use it to pick projects like "reduce patient-wait time in the emergency department from 47 min to under 15 min."
* **IT service desks** use it to charter projects on "first-call resolution rate" or "ticket-reopen percentage."
* **Banks** use it for "loan-approval cycle time" or "ATM cash-out incidents per month."
* The output of Project Identification is the **only document the Champion signs** before DMAIC execution begins; therefore its quality decides the entire project's success.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — From "Complaint" to "Signed Charter"

**Scenario (Hospital Wing-B):**
The CEO's strategic objective is *"Reduce outpatient complaints by 50 % in FY 2024-25."* A customer-survey shows that 2,340 complaints were filed last year, of which:
* 1,150 — long waiting time
* 562 — billing errors
* 380 — staff attitude
* 248 — others

The Champion asks the Black Belt to **identify** the Six Sigma project.

---

#### **Step 1 — Apply the Pareto Filter**

Sort categories in descending order, compute cumulative %.

| Rank | Complaint | Frequency | Cumulative Freq. | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Waiting time | 1150 | 1150 | 49.1 % |
| 2 | Billing errors | 562 | 1712 | 73.2 % |
| 3 | Staff attitude | 380 | 2092 | 89.4 % |
| 4 | Others | 248 | 2340 | 100 % |

The 80/20 cutoff is crossed *between* Rank 1 and Rank 2 (73.2 % < 80 % < 89.4 %). The **vital few** = categories 1 & 2.

---

#### **Step 2 — Convert VOC into a CTQ Tree**

The customer says: *"I waited too long."* This must be converted to a measurable Y.

> [!NOTE]
> **CTQ Tree (CTQ Drill-Down):**
> * **Customer Need (N1):** "Short waiting time"
> * **Driver (D1):** Time from token-issue to consultation
> * **CTQ (C1):** Average waiting time ≤ 15 minutes
> * **Measure (M1):** (Token-time − Consultation-start-time), in minutes, recorded for 100 % of patients

---

#### **Step 3 — Quantify the COPQ**

* Average revenue lost per long-wait patient (re-booking, walk-out) = **₹ 850**.
* Annual long-wait patient count = **1,150**.
* $$\text{COPQ}_{\text{waiting}} \;=\; 850 \times 1150 \;=\; ₹\,977{,}500$$

* Average cost per billing-error (rework + penalty) = **₹ 1,200**.
* Annual billing-error count = **562**.
* $$\text{COPQ}_{\text{billing}} \;=\; 1200 \times 562 \;=\; ₹\,674{,}400$$

* **Total combined COPQ of the vital-few = ₹ 16,51,900 per year.**

---

#### **Step 4 — Draft the SMART Goal Statement**

* *S*pecific — Reduce outpatient waiting time **and** billing errors in Wing-B.
* *M*easurable — From 49 min → ≤ 15 min; from 562 → ≤ 200 errors/yr.
* *A*chievable — 60 % reduction is feasible per benchmark data.
* *R*elevant — Directly supports CEO's "−50 % complaints" goal.
* *T*ime-bound — By **31-Mar-2025** (5 months from project start).

> **Goal Statement:** *"Reduce average outpatient waiting time in Wing-B from 49 min to ≤ 15 min AND reduce billing errors from 562 to ≤ 200 per year by 31 March 2025, saving an estimated ₹ 10 L+ in COPQ."*

---

#### **Step 5 — Build the Project Charter Skeleton**

| Charter Field | Content |
|---|---|
| **Title** | "Reduce Wing-B Outpatient Wait Time & Billing Errors" |
| **Problem** | 49 % of complaints are wait-time; 24 % are billing errors |
| **Goal** | 60 % reduction in both CTQs by 31-Mar-2025 |
| **Scope (In)** | Wing-B outpatients, Mon–Sat, 9 am – 5 pm |
| **Scope (Out)** | In-patients, Wing-A, emergency cases |
| **Team** | Champion (COO), MBB, BB, GB-2, Process Owner |
| **Milestones** | Define: 2 wks; Measure: 4 wks; Analyze: 4 wks; Improve: 4 wks; Control: 2 wks |
| **Financials** | COPQ recovery ≈ ₹ 10 L; project cost ≈ ₹ 3 L; **ROI ≈ 233 %** |

---

#### **Step 6 — Compute the Long-Term σ of the Current Process (using DPMO)**

* Total **opportunities per patient** = 2 (wait-time exceeds target + billing error) → *O = 2*
* Annual patients = **28,000** (U)
* Defects (D) = complaints used as proxy = 1,150 + 562 = **1,712**

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}712 \times 10^{6}}{28{,}000 \times 2}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{1.712 \times 10^{9}}{5.6 \times 10^{4}}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; 30{,}571 \;\text{defects per million opportunities}
$$

Now look up the standard **Z-table** (without 1.5 σ shift):

* DPMO = 30,571 corresponds to **short-term Z ≈ 3.45 σ**
* Long-term Z = 3.45 + 1.5 = **4.95 σ**

> **Interpretation:** The current process operates at roughly **5 σ** quality — *not* Six Sigma yet. A successful project should push it to **6 σ** (≤ 3.4 DPMO).

---

#### **Step 7 — Project Prioritization Score (BOCR-Style Weighted Matrix)**

Three candidate projects A, B, C are scored on a 1-10 scale across four criteria, each weighted.

| Criterion | Weight | Project A (Wing-B) | Project B (IT Helpdesk) | Project C (Canteen Hygiene) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic fit | 0.30 | 9 | 6 | 4 |
| COPQ (₹ recovered) | 0.30 | 8 | 5 | 2 |
| Feasibility (≤ 6 mo) | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 10 |
| Customer impact | 0.20 | 9 | 7 | 5 |
| **Weighted Score** | **1.00** | **8.3** | **6.4** | **4.7** |

**Project A wins.** This is the formal **Identification** decision.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the Identification Workflow

The following matrix summarizes *what is produced* at each sub-step, *which tool is used*, and *who is responsible*. This format is the **highest-scoring layout** for 14-mark KTU essay answers.

| Sub-step | Tool Used | Deliverable | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Gather VOC / VOB | Interviews, surveys, CTQ tree | Customer-needs list | Black Belt |
| 2. Quantify COPQ | Pareto, cost-attribute matrix | ₹ value of problems | Champion + Finance |
| 3. Strategic filter | Hoshin Kanri matrix | Aligned-problem list | Champion |
| 4. Prioritize | BOCR, Pugh, weighted matrix | Ranked candidate projects | Steering Committee |
| 5. Scope & boundaries | SIPOC, process map | Scope statement | Black Belt + Process Owner |
| 6. Draft charter | Project-charter template | Signed charter | Champion signs |
| 7. Tollgate review | Define-stage tollgate | Approval to enter MEASURE | Champion / Sponsor |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Six Sigma Project Identification — End-to-End Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the *complete identification workflow* with tollgates, owners, and decision diamonds. It is safe to compile under KTU's strict node-identifier rules (all IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved words used as labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Problem Universe: All Complaints]) --> A1[Capture VOC / VOB / VOP]
    A1 --> A2[Translate VOC to CTQ Tree]
    A2 --> A3{Pareto Filter 80/20}
    A3 -- Trivial Many --> A4[Reject or Route to Kaizen]
    A3 -- Vital Few --> A5[Quantify COPQ in Rupees]
    A5 --> A6{Strategic Fit Yes or No}
    A6 -- No --> A4
    A6 -- Yes --> A7{Technical Feasibility 3 to 6 Months}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Re-route to DFSS Project]
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Apply BOCR Weighted Matrix]
    A9 --> A10[Select Top Ranked Project]
    A10 --> A11[Define SMART Goal Statement]
    A11 --> A12[Build SIPOC and Scope Map]
    A12 --> A13[Draft Project Charter]
    A13 --> A14{Champion Tollgate Review}
    A14 -- Reject --> A15[Return to Step A9]
    A14 -- Approve --> A16([Charter Signed: Enter DMAIC Measure Phase])
    A14 -- Hold --> A17[Project Parked for Future Cycle]

    classDef gate fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#B7E1A1,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#CFE2FF,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    class A14,A3,A6,A7 gate
    class A0,A16,A17 out
    class A1,A2,A5,A9,A10,A11,A12,A13,A15,A4,A8 step
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The 8 Elements of a Project Charter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHARTER[Project Charter Document]
        direction TB
        F1[01 Project Title]
        F2[02 Problem Statement]
        F3[03 SMART Goal Statement]
        F4[04 Scope In and Out]
        F5[05 Team and Roles]
        F6[06 CTQ Characteristics Y]
        F7[07 Milestones and Tollgates]
        F8[08 Financial Justification COPQ and ROI]
    end
    SPONSOR[Champion / Sponsor] -- Signs --> CHARTER
    CHARTER -- Becomes the Legal Contract for --> DMAIC([DMAIC Execution Begins])

    classDef input fill:#FFD8A8,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000
    classDef arch fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#5B2C6F,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#A9DFBF,stroke:#1E8449,color:#000
    class SPONSOR input
    class F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F6,F7,F8 arch
    class DMAIC out
```

---

### 4.3 CTQ Drill-Down Tree (Block-Style Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1([Customer Need: Short Waiting Time]) --> D1[Driver: Total Time in Outpatient Wing]
    D1 --> C1[CTQ: Average Wait Time 15 Min or Less]
    C1 --> M1[Measure: Token Time Minus Consultation Start Time in Minutes]
    C1 --> M2[Measure: Percent of Patients Seen Within 15 Min Target]
    C1 --> M3[Measure: 95th Percentile Wait Time]

    N2([Customer Need: Accurate Billing]) --> D2[Driver: Zero Billing Errors]
    D2 --> C2[CTQ: Billing Error Rate 0.5 Percent or Less]
    C2 --> M4[Measure: Errors per 1000 Bills]
    C2 --> M5[Measure: Reimbursement Reversal Count]

    classDef need fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#CD853F,color:#000
    classDef ctq fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef meas fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    class N1,N2 need
    class D1,D2 drv
    class C1,C2 ctq
    class M1,M2,M3,M4,M5 meas
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the Mermaid diagrams above, the developer-style border colors and shapes are produced by `classDef` blocks. They are valid Mermaid syntax and pass the KTU compile-safeguard rules (no reserved keywords `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` used as node IDs, all node IDs alphanumeric, and labels are clean uppercase text without bold-markdown inside quotes).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the term "Cost of Poor Quality" (COPQ). List any four components of COPQ with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** *Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) is the total cost incurred by an organization due to defects, errors, rework, warranty claims, and customer dissatisfaction — i.e., all costs that would disappear if the process were perfect (Six Sigma).*

**Four Components (2 marks):**

| Component | Example |
|---|---|
| **Internal Failure Cost** | Rework, scrap, re-inspection |
| **External Failure Cost** | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service |
| **Appraisal Cost** | Inspection, testing, calibration of gauges |
| **Prevention Cost** | Training, FMEA, supplier qualification, poka-yoke installation |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series-1]**
*What is a "Project Charter" in Six Sigma? State any five elements that must be present in a valid Project Charter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** *A Project Charter is a formal, signed document that authorizes the existence of a Six Sigma project, defines its purpose, scope, team, deliverables, timeline, and financial justification. It is the contract between the project team and the Champion/Sponsor.*

**Five Mandatory Elements (2 marks — list any 5):**
1. Project Title
2. Problem Statement (with baseline data)
3. SMART Goal Statement
4. Project Scope (In/Out boundaries)
5. Team members and roles
6. CTQ characteristics to be improved
7. Milestone plan with tollgates
8. Financial justification (COPQ & ROI)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU's 2024 scheme, every Part-B question offers the student a **choice between two 14-mark sub-options**. Below we present both **Question A** *and* **Question B** so the student can revise either path.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focused on Tools & Workflow*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *"Explain with a suitable example the step-by-step process of Six Sigma Project Identification in an organization. Use the Pareto Principle in your explanation."*

#### Model Solution:

1. **Define the Universe of Problems (1 mark):** Collect all complaints, defects, and improvement ideas from VOC, VOB, and VOP. Example: 2,340 outpatient complaints in a hospital.
2. **Categorize & Count (1 mark):** Group into categories — Wait-time, Billing errors, Staff attitude, Others.
3. **Sort Descending & Compute Cumulative % (1 mark):** Apply the **80/20 Pareto rule**. The vital-few (Wait-time + Billing errors) account for 73.2 %; with Staff attitude they cover 89.4 %.
4. **Quantify COPQ (1 mark):** Convert counts to ₹. Wait-time waste = ₹ 9.77 L; Billing waste = ₹ 6.74 L; total = ₹ 16.5 L/year.
5. **Strategic & Feasibility Filter (1 mark):** Verify the vital-few aligns with CEO's goal ("−50 % complaints") and is solvable in 3–6 months.
6. **Prioritize using BOCR Matrix (1 mark):** Score the candidate projects on weighted criteria; select the top-ranked project.
7. **Draft Charter & Gain Approval (1 mark):** Title, problem, SMART goal, scope, team, milestones, financials; Champion signs → project is officially **identified**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"Calculate the current long-term sigma level of a process given the following data. Annual units = 50,000; Opportunities per unit = 4; Defects recorded = 1,800. Comment on whether the process qualifies as Six Sigma."*

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO (2 marks):**

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}800 \times 10^{6}}{50{,}000 \times 4}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{9}}{2.0 \times 10^{5}}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; 9{,}000 \;\text{defects per million opportunities}
$$

**Step 2 — Look up the σ from the standard Six Sigma conversion table (2 marks):**
* DPMO = 9,000 corresponds to **Short-term Z = 4.13 σ** (per Motorola's table)
* Long-term Z = 4.13 + 1.5 = **5.63 σ**

**Step 3 — Convert to Yield (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Roll Yield} \;\approx\; 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} \;=\; 1 - 0.009 \;=\; 99.10\%
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The process currently operates at **5.63 σ**, which is *not* Six Sigma. To reach **6 σ** it must reduce DPMO from 9,000 to **≤ 3.4**, a 99.96 % relative reduction — a clearly chartered DMAIC project opportunity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Focused on Charter & Financial Justification*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *"Draft a complete Project Charter for reducing customer-complaint resolution time in an IT helpdesk from 36 hours to under 6 hours. Show all eight components and justify the financial case using COPQ."*

#### Model Solution:

| # | Charter Element | Content (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Title** | "Reduce IT-Helpdesk Complaint Resolution Time from 36 h to ≤ 6 h" |
| 2 | **Problem Statement** | 12,000 tickets/year; avg resolution = 36 h; SLA breach = 38 % of tickets; top 20 % of complaint-types drive 78 % of breaches (Pareto). |
| 3 | **SMART Goal** | Reduce avg resolution time to ≤ 6 h AND reduce SLA breach from 38 % to ≤ 5 % by 30-Sep-2024. |
| 4 | **Scope (In)** | L1 & L2 tickets, weekdays 9 am – 9 pm, North-India region. |
| 5 | **Scope (Out)** | L3 hardware-failure tickets, weekend/holiday shifts. |
| 6 | **Team & Roles** | Sponsor (CIO), Champion (Head of IT Ops), MBB, BB, 2 × GB, Process Owner (Helpdesk Manager). |
| 7 | **Milestones** | Define: 2 wks; Measure: 3 wks; Analyze: 3 wks; Improve: 4 wks; Control: 2 wks. |
| 8 | **Financials** | COPQ — SLA penalty = ₹ 4,200/ticket × 4,560 breaches = **₹ 1.91 Cr/yr**; project cost = ₹ 35 L; **ROI = 446 %**; **Payback = 2.2 months**. |

**Why this is a strong Charter (1 mark bonus point for clarity):** The problem is quantified, the goal is SMART, the financial case is robust (> 4× ROI in year 1), and the scope is bounded — all eight required elements are present.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"Compare any three project-prioritization tools used in Six Sigma Project Identification. Which is most suitable for a beginner Black Belt, and why?"*

#### Model Solution:

| # | Tool | How it works (1.5 marks each) | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pareto Chart** | Sorts categories by frequency; vital-few 80/20. | Simple, visual. | Ignores financial value. |
| 2 | **BOCR / Weighted Scoring Matrix** | Scores candidates on multiple weighted criteria (Strategic, COPQ, Feasibility, Customer impact). | Holistic, financial + strategic. | Subjective weights. |
| 3 | **Pugh Matrix (Decision Matrix)** | Compares each candidate against a *baseline* candidate using + / 0 / −. | Quick consensus. | Sensitive to baseline choice. |
| 4 | **NPV / Payback Analysis** | Pure financial valuation. | Most objective. | Ignores strategic/qualitative factors. |

**Best for a beginner Black Belt (1.5 marks):** The **Pareto Chart** combined with a simple **Weighted Scoring Matrix** is best. Pareto gives a quick visual shortlist; the Weighted Matrix forces the team to consider strategic and financial criteria. Together they are simple, require no advanced statistics, and produce a defensible selection — exactly what a first-time Black Belt needs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for:**
> 1. **Writing the Goal Statement without baseline data** — examiners look for *"from X to Y by when."* A bare goal like *"improve quality"* scores **0/3**.
> 2. **Confusing COPQ with "Total Quality Cost only"** — COPQ includes *both* the cost of *not* having quality (failure) *and* the cost of *creating* quality (appraisal + prevention). Many students forget the prevention side.
> 3. **Skipping the 1.5 σ shift** in DPMO-to-σ conversions — every KTU paper expects **long-term σ = short-term Z + 1.5**.
> 4. **Mixing up CTQ and COPQ** — CTQ is a *measure*, COPQ is a *cost*. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Failing to sign / date the charter** — a charter without a Champion signature is treated as **incomplete** and loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Writing DPMO without the "per million opportunities" unit** — examiners are strict; "30,571" alone is *wrong*; "30,571 DPMO" is *correct*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* 📌 **Project Identification** is the *first sub-phase* of the **DEFINE** stage, *not* a separate stage.
* 📌 The **three deliverables** are: (i) Project Charter, (ii) Problem & Goal Statement, (iii) Scope/Boundary Map.
* 📌 The **five filters** in order: Strategic → VOC/CTQ → Financial (COPQ) → Technical Feasibility → Scope/Control.
* 📌 The **80/20 Pareto rule** is the *first* statistical tool applied during identification.
* 📌 A **SMART goal** must contain *all five* letters — S, M, A, R, T — partial SMARTs get partial marks.
* 📌 **COPQ = Internal Failure + External Failure + Appraisal + Prevention costs.**
* 📌 **DPMO formula:** $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ — never forget the $10^6$ factor.
* 📌 **Long-term σ = Short-term Z + 1.5** (Motorola's shift) — apply in *every* DPMO → σ conversion.
* 📌 **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966 % yield.**
* 📌 The **Project Charter must be signed by the Champion** — unsigned charters are *not* valid DMAIC projects.
* 📌 Tools used in identification: **VOC → CTQ tree → Pareto → COPQ matrix → BOCR / Pugh matrix → SIPOC → Charter.**
* 📌 Typical Six Sigma project duration: **3 – 6 months** (anything longer is re-scoped or re-routed to DFSS).
* 📌 If a candidate project **fails the financial filter** (i.e., COPQ is not quantifiable), it is *rejected* — not re-worded.
* 📌 KTU 14-mark questions expect: **definition + tool + numerical worked example + interpretation**, in that order.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection and Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)** roadmap of Six Sigma, **Selection and Definition** constitutes the foundational **Define Phase (D)**. It is the structured process of identifying, screening, prioritizing, and formally launching the right business problem (project) that aligns with organizational strategy, and then creating a rigorous, binding document (the **Project Charter**) that clearly articulates the problem, goal, scope, deliverables, timeline, and team.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Selection and Definition* is the first stage of DMAIC where a Six Sigma project is chosen based on strategic alignment, financial impact, and feasibility, and is then formally defined using a Project Charter, Problem Statement, Goal Statement, SIPOC, VOC, and CTQ tree.

**Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary):**

- **Project Selection:** The act of choosing a problem or opportunity that warrants a Six Sigma intervention.
- **Project Definition:** The formal documentation of the project's boundaries, objectives, and constraints.
- **Project Charter:** A contract between the project team and management that authorizes the project.
- **Problem Statement:** A clear, concise, measurable description of the gap between current and desired state.
- **Goal Statement:** The SMART target (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) the project intends to achieve.
- **SIPOC:** Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers — a high-level process map used in the Define phase.
- **VOC (Voice of Customer):** The captured requirements, expectations, and pain points of the customer.
- **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality):** The measurable characteristics of a product or process that directly satisfy the customer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Battlefield Analogy** — Think of Six Sigma as a military campaign. Before a General commits troops, he must (a) **select** which enemy outpost is most strategically valuable to attack, and (b) **define** the mission: where, when, with whom, using what resources, and what success looks like. Selecting the wrong target wastes the entire army. Defining the mission poorly causes friendly fire and mission creep. Selection and Definition is exactly this — picking the **right battle** and writing the **mission briefing (Charter)**.

A simpler analogy: when you visit a doctor, the first step is *not* treatment. The doctor **selects** your ailment (diagnosis from many possibilities) and **defines** the treatment plan (dosage, duration, target outcome). A wrong selection (misdiagnosis) makes all subsequent medical efforts useless. Similarly, Six Sigma's Define phase ensures the team is solving the **right problem**, not just any problem.

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold

The following **standard Six Sigma metrics** are used throughout the Selection and Definition phase:

- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality):** The cost associated with providing poor quality products or services, measured in monetary terms (₹, $, €).
- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities):** A normalized metric of process capability, dimensionless.
- **Sigma Level (σ):** A statistical measure of process performance, ranging typically from **1σ to 6σ** where **6σ = 3.4 DPMO**.
- **Yield (Y):** The percentage of products or services that are defect-free, dimensionless.
- **RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield):** The probability that a single unit can pass through an entire process with zero defects, dimensionless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A Sigma Level of **6σ** corresponds to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, and a process yield of **99.99966%**. The higher the sigma level, the better the process capability and the lower the COPQ.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Stage Logic of Selection and Definition

#### Stage A — Project Selection (The "What" and "Why")

The selection stage answers the question: *"Which problem deserves our limited Six Sigma resources?"* The structured logic follows:

1. **Identify candidate problems** through VOC, internal data, brainstorming, customer complaints, warranty claims, and benchmarking.
2. **Apply screening criteria** to filter out non-viable candidates (must be measurable, bounded, align with strategy).
3. **Score and prioritize** surviving candidates using a weighted scoring matrix.
4. **Develop a Business Case** quantifying the financial impact (COPQ, savings, ROI).
5. **Obtain executive approval and sponsorship (Champion)**.

#### Stage B — Project Definition (The "How" and "Who")

The definition stage answers: *"Now that we have a problem, what exactly is the boundary of our work, and what does success look like?"* The structured logic follows:

1. **Create a Project Charter** — the master authorization document.
2. **Write the Problem Statement** — uses the *5W1H* approach (What, Where, When, Who, Why, How much).
3. **Write the Goal Statement** — must obey the **SMART** criteria.
4. **Define the Scope** — explicit boundaries (in-scope and out-of-scope).
5. **Develop the SIPOC diagram** — a top-down view of the process.
6. **Capture the VOC** — interviews, surveys, focus groups, complaint data.
7. **Derive the CTQ Tree** — translating customer needs into measurable specifications.
8. **Identify the Process Owner, Champion, Black Belt, Green Belt, and team members**.

### 2.2 The SMART Criteria (Goal Statement)

A KTU-mandated evaluation tool for any Six Sigma Goal Statement:

- **S** — Specific: clearly stated, no ambiguity.
- **M** — Measurable: quantifiable metric (e.g., reduce defects from X to Y).
- **A** — Achievable: realistic given resources and capability.
- **R** — Relevant: aligned with business strategy.
- **T** — Time-bound: has a deadline.

> [!WARNING]
> A Goal Statement that is *not measurable* is **automatically rejected** by a KTU examiner. Always quantify the target.

### 2.3 The CTQ Tree (Customer-Centric Decomposition)

A hierarchical tool that converts VOC into actionable metrics:

$$
\text{VOC} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Customer Need} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Performance Specification} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Target Value}
$$

**Example:** For an online food delivery platform,
- VOC: *"I want my food hot when it arrives."*
- Customer Need: Timely and hot delivery
- CTQ: Delivery temperature at doorstep
- Specification: Temperature in degrees Celsius
- Target: $\geq 60^\circ\text{C}$ at the time of delivery

### 2.4 The SIPOC Diagram

A high-level process map built **before** detailed process mapping. SIPOC is built top-down at the **process level** (not step level):

| Element | Symbol | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Suppliers | Who provides inputs to the process? |
| **I** | Inputs | What materials, data, or information go into the process? |
| **P** | Process | The high-level sequence of 4–7 major steps that transform inputs to outputs. |
| **O** | Outputs | The products, services, or information produced. |
| **C** | Customers | Who receives the outputs? (Internal and external) |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains all high-yield formulas and quantitative tools used in Selection and Definition:

| # | Tool / Concept | Formula or Rule | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Quantifies the defect rate of a process |
| 2 | **Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%$ | First-pass yield measure |
| 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Yield across $n$ serial process steps |
| 4 | **Process Sigma Level (σ)** | $\sigma = Z + 1.5$ (long-term shift correction) | Maps Z-score to sigma level |
| 5 | **COPQ** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}$ | Justifies project selection financially |
| 6 | **Project Priority Score** | $\text{Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ where $w_i$ is weight, $s_i$ is rating (1–5) | Prioritizes candidate projects |
| 7 | **ROI of Six Sigma Project** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Justifies investment to management |
| 8 | **Pareto (80/20 Rule)** | Top 20% of causes account for 80% of defects | Helps select high-impact projects |
| 9 | **Defects (D)** | $D = \text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit} \times \text{DPMO} / 10^6$ | Converts DPMO into absolute defect count |
| 10 | **Normalized Yield** | $Y_{\text{norm}} = e^{-DPU}$ where $DPU = D/\text{Units}$ | Theoretical yield (Poisson model) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Always state the **unit** of any computed metric in the exam. For example, *DPMO* is dimensionless (defects per million opportunities), while *COPQ* must be in monetary units (₹ or $).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Selection and Definition phase is **the single highest-leverage activity** in any Six Sigma deployment:

- **Manufacturing:** Selecting a project to reduce solder defects in a PCB assembly line. Definition includes the SIPOC of the SMT line.
- **Healthcare:** Selecting a project to reduce patient wait time in the emergency department. Definition includes the CTQ tree for patient satisfaction.
- **Software/IT:** Selecting a project to reduce ticket resolution time. Definition includes the SIPOC of the ITIL process.
- **Banking/Finance:** Selecting a project to reduce loan-processing turnaround time. Definition includes CTQs of accuracy and speed.

> A wrong selection in any of these domains can waste **months of effort** and **lakhs of rupees**. Definition prevents *scope creep* and *goal drift*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing Project Priority Score Using the Weighted Matrix

A Six Sigma Steering Committee at a B.Tech project-lab of an engineering college identifies **3 candidate projects** for Black Belt deployment. The criteria and weights are:

| Criterion | Weight $(w_i)$ |
|---|---|
| Strategic Alignment | 0.30 |
| Financial Impact (COPQ) | 0.35 |
| Customer Impact | 0.20 |
| Feasibility | 0.15 |

Each project is rated on a **1–5 scale** (5 = best). The team leader must compute the priority score and rank the projects.

**Given Data:**

| Criterion | Weight | Project A | Project B | Project C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic Alignment | 0.30 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Financial Impact | 0.35 | 5 | 4 | 4 |
| Customer Impact | 0.20 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Feasibility | 0.15 | 4 | 3 | 5 |

**Step 1 — State the general weighted-sum formula:**

$$
\text{Priority Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

**Step 2 — Validate the weights sum to 1 (mandatory for KTU):**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.30 + 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00
$$

The sum equals **1.00**, which is consistent. If the weights did not sum to 1, we would have to **normalize** them by dividing each weight by the sum.

**Step 3 — Compute Score for Project A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_A &= (0.30 \times 4) + (0.35 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 4) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.75 + 0.60 + 0.60 \\
&= 4.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Score for Project B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_B &= (0.30 \times 5) + (0.35 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 3) \\
&= 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.00 + 0.45 \\
&= 4.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Score for Project C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_C &= (0.30 \times 3) + (0.35 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 5) \\
&= 0.90 + 1.40 + 0.80 + 0.75 \\
&= 3.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Rank the projects by descending score:**

$$
\text{Score}_B \;(4.35) \;>\; \text{Score}_A \;(4.15) \;>\; \text{Score}_C \;(3.85)
$$

**Step 7 — Final ranking and selection decision:**

| Rank | Project | Priority Score | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Project B** | **4.35** | **SELECT** |
| 2 | Project A | 4.15 | Hold for next cycle |
| 3 | Project C | 3.85 | Reject / rework scope |

> **Project B is selected** for Six Sigma deployment because it has the highest weighted priority score.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing DPMO and Sigma Level (Selection Justification)

A manufacturing line produces **2,000 mobile phone displays per week**. Each display has **15 critical-to-quality opportunities** for defects. Quality inspection in the past week recorded **180 defects**. The Champion needs to determine the current Sigma level to justify a Define-phase project.

**Step 1 — State the DPMO formula:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{180}{2{,}000 \times 15} \times 10^6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
2{,}000 \times 15 = 30{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{180}{30{,}000} = 0.006
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $10^6$:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.006 \times 10^6 = 6{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Convert DPMO to Yield (First Pass):**

$$
Y = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - 0.006 = 0.994 = 99.40\%
$$

**Step 7 — Convert Yield to Z-score (using standard normal table):**

For $Y = 0.994$, $Z \approx 2.51$ (from the standard normal table, one-tailed).

**Step 8 — Apply the 1.5σ short-term shift correction for long-term sigma level:**

$$
\sigma = Z + 1.5 = 2.51 + 1.5 = 4.01
$$

> **Conclusion:** The display line operates at **4.01σ** (≈ 6,000 DPMO), which is significantly below the world-class **6σ** benchmark. This DPMO value is the **Selection Justification** — the data-driven reason a Six Sigma project is warranted.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Building the CTQ Tree (Definition Tool)

**VOC Statement (raw customer voice):**
*"I want my online order to arrive without any damage and on the same day I place it."*

**Step 1 — Identify the customer need (CN):**
- CN1: Product must arrive undamaged
- CN2: Product must arrive same-day

**Step 2 — Translate to Critical-to-Quality (CTQ):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CN1} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{CTQ}_1: \text{Percentage of orders delivered without damage} \\
\text{CN2} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{CTQ}_2: \text{Percentage of orders delivered same-day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Performance Specifications:**

| CTQ | Specification | Unit | Target | Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Damage-free delivery | $Y_1$ | % of orders | $\geq 99.5$ | $\leq 0.5\%$ damage rate |
| Same-day delivery | $Y_2$ | % of orders | $\geq 95$ | $\leq 5\%$ late rate |

**Step 4 — Form the complete CTQ Tree:**

$$
\text{VOC} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CN1, CN2} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CTQ}_1, \text{CTQ}_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Specs (99.5\%, 95\%)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Measurable target}
$$

This CTQ tree is now part of the **Project Charter** and the **Problem Statement**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Building a SIPOC (Definition Tool)

Suppose the process under study is *Order Fulfillment* in an e-commerce company.

**Step 1 — Identify Customers (C):**

External: End consumer, retail partner.
Internal: Warehouse, shipping team.

**Step 2 — Identify Outputs (O):**

Packed order, shipping confirmation email, tracking ID.

**Step 3 — Identify the Process (P) — high level, 4–7 steps:**

1. Receive order
2. Pick items
3. Pack order
4. Quality check
5. Ship order

**Step 4 — Identify Inputs (I):**

Order data, inventory, packaging material, shipping label.

**Step 5 — Identify Suppliers (S):**

IT system (ERP), warehouse staff, inventory database, courier service.

**Step 6 — Construct the SIPOC table:**

| Suppliers (S) | Inputs (I) | Process (P) | Outputs (O) | Customers (C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ERP System | Order data | 1. Receive order | Order entry | Warehouse |
| Warehouse staff | Inventory items | 2. Pick items | Picked order | Packing team |
| Packaging vendor | Boxes, tape | 3. Pack order | Packed order | Quality team |
| Quality team | Checklist | 4. Quality check | Verified order | Shipping team |
| Courier | Shipping label | 5. Ship order | Shipment + tracking | End customer |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Charter Validation Checklist

The following KTU 2024 evaluation matrix must be satisfied for a Project Charter to be deemed complete:

| Charter Element | Validation Question | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Problem Statement | Is it quantified? Does it state the gap? | 20% |
| Goal Statement | Is it SMART? | 20% |
| Scope | Are in/out boundaries explicit? | 15% |
| Team | Are roles (Champion, BB, GB, Owner) named? | 10% |
| Timeline | Are milestones and end date set? | 15% |
| Benefits | Is the financial benefit quantified? | 15% |
| Constraints | Are assumptions and risks listed? | 5% |

> A charter missing any of the first three elements will **fail** the KTU exam question on Project Definition.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — DMAIC Framework with Selection and Definition Positioned

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DMAIC_D[Define Phase: Selection and Definition] --> DMAIC_M[Measure Phase: Data Collection]
    DMAIC_M --> DMAIC_A[Analyze Phase: Root Cause]
    DMAIC_A --> DMAIC_I[Improve Phase: Solution Pilot]
    DMAIC_I --> DMAIC_C[Control Phase: Sustain Gains]

    subgraph DEFINE_SUB[Define Phase Components]
      A1[Identify VOC] --> A2[Build CTQ Tree]
      A2 --> A3[Write Problem Statement]
      A3 --> A4[Write Goal Statement SMART]
      A4 --> A5[Define Scope In and Out]
      A5 --> A6[Build SIPOC]
      A6 --> A7[Develop Project Charter]
      A7 --> A8[Approve and Launch]
    end

    DMAIC_D -.-> DEFINE_SUB
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Project Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify Candidate Problems from VOC Data, Complaints, COPQ] --> P2[Step 2: Apply Screening Filter: Measurable, Bounded, Strategically Aligned]
    P2 --> P3{Step 3: Surviving Candidates Exist?}
    P3 -- No --> P4[Return to Step 1]
    P3 -- Yes --> P5[Step 4: Apply Weighted Scoring Matrix 1 to 5]
    P5 --> P6[Step 5: Compute Priority Score Sum of w times s]
    P6 --> P7[Step 6: Rank by Descending Score]
    P7 --> P8[Step 7: Develop Business Case for Top Project]
    P8 --> P9[Step 8: Secure Executive Sponsor and Champion]
    P9 --> P10[Step 9: Project Selected and Ready for Define]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Project Charter Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CHARTER[Project Charter Master Document]

    CHARTER --> PS[Problem Statement 5W1H]
    CHARTER --> GS[Goal Statement SMART]
    CHARTER --> SC[Scope Definition In and Out]
    CHARTER --> TM[Team Roles: Champion, Master Black Belt, Black Belt, Green Belt, Process Owner, Team Members]
    CHARTER --> TL[Timeline and Milestones]
    CHARTER --> BN[Quantified Business Benefits and COPQ]
    CHARTER --> CT[CTQ Tree and SIPOC Reference]
    CHARTER --> RK[Risks, Assumptions, and Constraints]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — CTQ Tree Construction (Hierarchical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VOC[Voice of Customer VOC Raw Statement] --> CN[Customer Needs CN]
    CN --> CTQ[Critical to Quality CTQ Measurable]
    CTQ --> PS[Performance Specification with Unit]
    PS --> TV[Target Value with Limit]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — VOC to CTQ Conversion Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Surveys] --> VOCP[VOC Pool]
    S2[Interviews] --> VOCP
    S3[Focus Groups] --> VOCP
    S4[Complaint Data] --> VOCP
    S5[Field Observations] --> VOCP

    VOCP --> AF[Affinity Diagram Grouping]
    AF --> TT[Translate to CTQ Language]
    TT --> CTQT[CTQ Tree Final]
```
