<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Define phase of DMAIC. List any four elements of a Project Charter.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Define phase** is the first stage of the DMAIC methodology, focused on the *Selection* and *Definition* of a Six Sigma project. It identifies the right problem to solve and creates a formal Project Charter that authorizes the work.

**Four elements of a Project Charter:**

1. **Problem Statement** — a quantified description of the gap between current and desired performance (uses 5W1H).
2. **Goal Statement** — a SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) target for the project.
3. **Scope** — explicit in-scope and out-of-scope boundaries.
4. **Team** — identified roles: Champion, Master Black Belt, Black Belt, Green Belt, Process Owner, and team members.

*(Other valid elements: Timeline, Business Benefits, SIPOC, CTQ Tree, Risks and Assumptions.)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is VOC? Explain the concept of a CTQ Tree with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**VOC (Voice of Customer)** is the captured set of customer requirements, expectations, preferences, and pain points regarding a product or service. It is obtained through surveys, interviews, focus groups, complaint logs, warranty data, and field observation.

A **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) Tree** is a hierarchical tool that converts raw VOC into measurable, actionable quality characteristics by breaking it down into:

- VOC → Customer Need → CTQ → Performance Specification → Target Value

**Example:** For a bank ATM,
- VOC: *"I want cash quickly."*
- Customer Need: Fast cash withdrawal
- CTQ: Time to dispense cash
- Specification: Seconds
- Target: $\leq 15$ seconds

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the structured process of *Project Selection* in Six Sigma with reference to the Weighted Scoring Matrix. A company has identified four candidate projects and three selection criteria as given below. Compute the priority scores, rank the projects, and recommend the project to be selected.

| Criterion | Weight | Project P | Project Q | Project R | Project S |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic Fit | 0.40 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Financial Impact | 0.40 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Feasibility | 0.20 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 4 |

**Marks: 7**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Project Selection:** Project Selection is the screening, scoring, and prioritization of candidate problems to identify the one that offers the highest strategic, financial, and operational value. The **Weighted Scoring Matrix** is the standard Six Sigma tool for this purpose.

**Step 2 — Validate weights:** $0.40 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓ (weights sum to 1).

**Step 3 — Compute Score for Project P:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_P &= (0.40 \times 3) + (0.40 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 1.20 + 2.00 + 0.80 = 4.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Score for Project Q:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_Q &= (0.40 \times 4) + (0.40 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 5) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.60 + 1.00 = 4.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Score for Project R:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_R &= (0.40 \times 5) + (0.40 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 3) \\
&= 2.00 + 1.20 + 0.60 = 3.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Score for Project S:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_S &= (0.40 \times 3) + (0.40 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 1.20 + 2.00 + 0.80 = 4.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Ranking:**

| Rank | Project | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Q** | **4.20** |
| 2 | P (tie) | 4.00 |
| 2 | S (tie) | 4.00 |
| 4 | R | 3.80 |

**Recommendation:** **Project Q is selected** for Six Sigma deployment.

---

**Q: (b)** Explain the components of a Project Charter in detail. Develop a sample Problem Statement and Goal Statement for a project aimed at reducing customer complaint resolution time at a telecom service center.

**Marks: 7**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of a Project Charter:**

1. **Problem Statement** (uses 5W1H: What, Where, When, Who, Why, How much).
2. **Goal Statement** (SMART criteria).
3. **Scope** (In-scope and Out-of-scope).
4. **Team** (Champion, MBB, BB, GB, Process Owner, members).
5. **Timeline & Milestones.**
6. **Business Benefits & Financial Justification.**
7. **CTQ Tree & SIPOC Reference.**
8. **Risks, Assumptions, Constraints.**

**Sample Problem Statement:**
*"Currently, the average time taken to resolve a customer complaint at the Kerala regional telecom service center is **9.2 days** (What), affecting all postpaid customers (Who) in the Trivandrum district (Where), during the period Jan–Dec 2024 (When). This delay is caused by repeated hand-offs between the support and technical teams (Why), and the current process operates at **2.8σ** (How much). The target industry benchmark is 3 days."*

**Sample Goal Statement (SMART):**
*"Reduce the average customer complaint resolution time at the Trivandrum regional service center from **9.2 days to ≤ 3 days** (S, M) by **31st March 2025** (T), without increasing the cost-per-resolution above ₹350 (A), thereby improving the customer satisfaction index from 6.4 to 8.0 (R)."*

This satisfies **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound** criteria.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** What is a SIPOC diagram? Construct a SIPOC diagram for the process *Admission Process in a KTU-affiliated Engineering College* and explain why SIPOC is built at the *process level* (not step level).

**Marks: 7**

**Model Answer:**

**SIPOC Definition:** SIPOC stands for **Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers**. It is a high-level process map created in the Define phase to give the Six Sigma team a shared understanding of the boundaries of the process under study. It is a top-down map covering 4–7 major process steps.

**SIPOC for College Admission Process:**

| Suppliers (S) | Inputs (I) | Process (P) | Outputs (O) | Customers (C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| KEAM Office | Rank list | 1. Receive application | Allotted seat | Student |
| Applicant | Application form, certificates | 2. Verify documents | Verified record | Admission cell |
| University | Fee structure | 3. Collect fee | Fee receipt | Accounts dept. |
| Admission cell | Allotment letter | 4. Issue admission | Admission letter | Student |
| IT system | Student database | 5. Enroll in ERP | Enrolled student | Department |

**Why process level and not step level:** SIPOC is built at the process level to provide a **bird's-eye view** that is **manageable in a single meeting**, **avoids premature detail**, and **forces alignment** between the team and process owner. Detailed step-level maps are built later in the Measure phase using flowcharts and value-stream maps.

---

**Q: (b)** Define VOC and CTQ. For a *food delivery app* in Kerala, derive a CTQ Tree starting from the VOC statement: *"Customers want their food to be hot, fresh, and delivered on time."* Also compute the DPMO and Sigma Level of the app, given: 5,000 daily orders, 8 critical opportunities per order, and 120 defects recorded last week.

**Marks: 7**

**Model Answer:**

**VOC:** The Voice of Customer is the set of requirements, expectations, and complaints captured directly from customers through surveys, interviews, social media, ratings, and reviews.

**CTQ:** Critical-to-Quality characteristics are the measurable attributes of a product or service that are most important to customer satisfaction.

**CTQ Tree Derivation:**

| Level | Item | Description |
|---|---|---|
| VOC | "Hot, fresh, on-time delivery" | Raw customer voice |
| Customer Need (CN) | CN1: Hot food, CN2: Fresh food, CN3: On-time delivery | Translated needs |
| CTQ | CTQ1: Temperature at delivery, CTQ2: Shelf life remaining, CTQ3: Delivery time | Measurable |
| Specification | °C, Hours, Minutes | Unit-bearing |
| Target | $\geq 60^\circ\text{C}$, $\geq 80\%$ shelf life, $\leq 30$ min | Quantitative |

**DPMO Computation:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{120}{5{,}000 \times 8} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{120}{40{,}000} \times 10^6 = 0.003 \times 10^6 = 3{,}000
$$

**Yield:**

$$
Y = 1 - \dfrac{3{,}000}{10^6} = 0.997 = 99.70\%
$$

**Z-score for Y = 0.997:** $Z \approx 2.75$

**Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift):**

$$
\sigma = Z + 1.5 = 2.75 + 1.5 = 4.25\sigma
$$

> **Conclusion:** The food delivery app operates at **4.25σ** with **3,000 DPMO**, which is below the world-class benchmark of **6σ (3.4 DPMO)**, justifying a Six Sigma project in the Define phase.

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Selection and Definition" Questions:**
> 1. **Writing a vague Problem Statement** (e.g., "Service is slow") — examiner deducts up to **2 marks** for not quantifying.
> 2. **Goal Statement that is not SMART** — especially missing the *Time-bound* and *Measurable* parts; expect **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to validate that weights sum to 1** in the priority score question — KTU explicitly tests this; deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing SIPOC level (process-level) with detailed flowcharts (step-level)** — SIPOC must show **4–7 major steps**, not micro-steps.
> 5. **In DPMO problems, omitting the $\times 10^6$ factor** — students often write 0.006 instead of 6,000 DPMO, losing **1 mark**.
> 6. **Forgetting the +1.5σ long-term shift correction** when computing Sigma Level from DPMO — this is a KTU 2024 favorite; lose **1 mark**.
> 7. **Listing CTQ elements without units (°C, mm, seconds)** — CTQ is invalid without a unit; deduct **0.5–1 mark**.

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Bullets for KTU Exam:**
> - The **Define phase = Selection + Definition**, the foundation of DMAIC.
> - **Selection** uses the Weighted Scoring Matrix: $\text{Score} = \sum w_i s_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
> - **Definition** uses the **Project Charter** containing Problem Statement, Goal Statement, Scope, Team, Timeline, Benefits.
> - **Problem Statement** uses **5W1H** and must be **quantified**.
> - **Goal Statement** must obey **SMART** rules.
> - **VOC** is captured via surveys, interviews, complaint data, focus groups, observations.
> - **CTQ Tree**: VOC → CN → CTQ → Spec → Target (with units!).
> - **SIPOC** is built at the **process level** (4–7 major steps), Suppliers–Inputs–Process–Outputs–Customers.
> - **DPMO** = (Defects ÷ Units × Opportunities) × $10^6$.
> - **Sigma Level** = Z + **1.5** (long-term shift correction).
> - **6σ benchmark = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield**.
> - **COPQ** = Internal + External Failure + Appraisal + Prevention costs (used to justify project selection).
> - **Roles**: Champion (sponsor), Master Black Belt (mentor), Black Belt (leader), Green Belt (part-time), Process Owner (accountable for the process).
> - **Pareto (80/20)** helps identify which problems have the largest impact and should be selected.
> - A poorly chosen project wastes the entire team’s effort — **selection is the highest-leverage decision** in DMAIC.
> - A Project Charter missing the *Problem Statement*, *Goal Statement*, or *Scope* is **automatically rejected** by a KTU examiner.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Charter and Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Project Charter and Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Project Charter — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Charter (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A **Project Charter** is a formal, short document (typically 5–6 pages) that authoritatively defines a Six Sigma project's scope, objectives, stakeholders, timeline, boundaries, and key metrics. It serves as the **official contract** between the project team and the project sponsor, and acts as the foundational reference document for the entire **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) lifecycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Project Charter is the **single most critical artifact of the DEFINE phase**. Without a signed charter, no DMAIC project is considered officially authorized.

## 1.2 Project Monitoring — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Monitoring (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> **Project Monitoring** is the continuous, systematic process of tracking, reviewing, and regulating the progress of a Six Sigma project against the **baseline metrics, milestones, and deliverables** defined in the Project Charter. It uses statistical, financial, and schedule-tracking tools to detect deviations early and trigger corrective action.

Monitoring is the **lifeline of execution** — it converts the static charter into a dynamic, controllable system.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy 1: The Charter as a "Pilot's Flight Plan"

Imagine a pilot preparing to fly from Kerala to Delhi. Before takeoff, the pilot signs a **Flight Plan** (Charter) that says:
- **Where** we are going (Objective)
- **Why** we are going (Business Case)
- **Who** is on board (Team)
- **What** fuel, time, and route are allowed (Scope & Constraints)
- **When** we must land (Timeline)
- **How** we know we are on track (Instruments = Metrics)

The **Project Charter** is exactly this — a **pre-flight authorization document** for a Six Sigma journey.

### Analogy 2: Monitoring as a Car's Dashboard

While driving, you don't stare at the road map (Charter) alone — you constantly watch the **speedometer, fuel gauge, and engine temperature** (Monitoring metrics). If the engine temperature rises, you stop the car (corrective action). Project monitoring is the **dashboard of the project vehicle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand that the **Charter = Planning** and **Monitoring = Control**. Both belong to the DEFINE phase and the Control-Measure-Analyze phases respectively, but together they span the entire DMAIC lifecycle.

---

## 1.4 Why These Concepts Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask:
> - "List the components of a Project Charter" (3 marks direct)
> - "Differentiate between Project Charter and Project Scope" (7–14 marks)
> - "Explain any four tools of project monitoring with diagrams" (14 marks)
> - "Prepare a sample Project Charter for a given case study" (14 marks case study)
>
> Mastery of this topic = **guaranteed 14-mark question** in the end-semester exam.

---

## 1.5 Standard KTU Terminology Glossary

| Term | Full Form | Meaning in Six Sigma Context |
|------|-----------|------------------------------|
| **VOC** | Voice of Customer | Customer needs/wants identified from market data |
| **CTQ** | Critical to Quality | Measurable characteristics that define quality |
| **SIPOC** | Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers | High-level process map |
| **RACI** | Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed | Role assignment matrix |
| **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Six Sigma quality metric |
| **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality | Financial loss from defects |
| **Champion** | Project Sponsor | Senior leader who owns and funds the project |
| **Master Black Belt** | MBB | Senior Six Sigma expert mentoring Black Belts |
| **Black Belt** | BB | Full-time project leader of Six Sigma projects |
| **Green Belt** | GB | Part-time team member supporting the project |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Project Charter (The 9 Pillars)

A KTU-compliant Project Charter contains **nine mandatory components**. Each must be present for full marks.

### Pillar 1: Problem Statement
A concise, **data-driven** description of the issue. It must answer:
- **What** is wrong?
- **Where** is it occurring (process, location, department)?
- **When** is it observed (time, frequency)?
- **How much** (magnitude quantified)?

> [!NOTE]
> A weak problem statement says: *"Quality is poor."*
> A strong KTU-style statement says: *"The defect rate in the PCB soldering process at Plant A is 18.5%, which is 3.7× higher than the industry benchmark of 5%, causing a monthly revenue loss of ₹12.4 lakhs."*

### Pillar 2: Business Case
Justifies **WHY** the project is worth doing. Must show:
- **Financial impact** (₹ savings, ROI)
- **Strategic alignment** (link to organizational goals)
- **Risk of inaction** (what happens if we don't fix it)

### Pillar 3: Goal Statement / Project Objective
A **SMART** objective:
- **S**pecific
- **M**easurable
- **A**chievable
- **R**elevant
- **T**ime-bound

Example: *"Reduce the customer complaint rate from 14% to below 4% within 6 months."*

### Pillar 4: Scope (In-Scope and Out-of-Scope)
Defines **boundaries** to prevent scope creep.

| In-Scope | Out-of-Scope |
|----------|--------------|
| Soldering defects in Line 3 | Defects in Line 1 and 2 |
| Period: Jan–June 2025 | Pre-2024 historical data |
| Manual inspection process | Automated AOI systems |

### Pillar 5: Team Members and Roles (RACI Matrix)
Clearly assigns **who does what**.

| Role | Name | Responsibility |
|------|------|----------------|
| Champion | Mr. X (Plant Head) | Approves budget, removes roadblocks |
| Black Belt | Ms. Y | Leads DMAIC, statistical analysis |
| Green Belt | Mr. Z | Data collection, pilot implementation |
| Process Owner | Mr. W | Implements final solutions |
| Team Members | 3 engineers | Day-to-day execution |

### Pillar 6: Timeline / Milestones
A **Gantt-chart-style** schedule showing each DMAIC phase with start and end dates.

### Pillar 7: Key Metrics (CTQs)
The **measurable quality characteristics** that define success.
Examples: Defect rate (%), Cycle time (min), Customer satisfaction score (NPS), DPMO.

### Pillar 8: Expected Benefits / Savings
Quantified in **financial terms** (₹) and operational terms (time, quality).

### Pillar 9: Charter Sign-off
Formal **signatures** of Champion, Process Owner, and Black Belt — this converts the document from a draft into an authorized contract.

---

## 2.2 The Project Monitoring Framework

Project monitoring operates on a **closed-loop feedback principle**:

$$
\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act}
$$

This is the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)** applied to project execution.

### 2.2.1 The Four Pillars of Project Monitoring

1. **Schedule Monitoring** — Are we on time?
2. **Cost Monitoring** — Are we within budget?
3. **Quality Monitoring** — Are the CTQs improving?
4. **Risk Monitoring** — Are new risks emerging?

### 2.2.2 The RACI Matrix (Monitoring Role Assignment)

| Activity | Champion | Black Belt | GB | Process Owner |
|----------|----------|------------|----|--------------| 
| Approve changes | A | C | I | C |
| Collect data | I | A | R | C |
| Run analysis | I | R | C | I |
| Report status | I | R | C | I |

Where R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the HIGH-YIELD formula bank for KTU 2024 Six Sigma examinations.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables Explained | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Defect Rate** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{D}{U} \times 100\%$ | $D$ = Defects, $U$ = Units produced | % |
| 2 | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | $O$ = Opportunities per unit | defects/million |
| 3 | **Sigma Level** | $\text{Sigma} = Z_{\text{short-term}} + 1.5\,\sigma$ | $1.5\,\sigma$ = long-term shift | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | USL/LSL = spec limits, $\sigma$ = st. dev. | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Process Capability (shifted)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ = process mean | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Cost of Poor Quality** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{internal}} + C_{\text{external}}$ | Internal + External failure costs | ₹ |
| 7 | **Project Schedule Variance** | $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$ | EV = Earned Value, PV = Planned Value | ₹ |
| 8 | **Project Cost Variance** | $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ | AC = Actual Cost | ₹ |
| 9 | **Schedule Performance Index** | $\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}$ | SPI > 1 = ahead, < 1 = behind | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Cost Performance Index** | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}$ | CPI > 1 = under budget | dimensionless |
| 11 | **Earned Value** | $\text{EV} = \% \text{Complete} \times \text{BAC}$ | BAC = Budget at Completion | ₹ |
| 12 | **Yield (First Pass)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{U_{\text{no rework}}}{U_{\text{total}}}$ | First-pass yield | dimensionless |
| 13 | **Rolled Throughput Yield** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$ | Product of all step yields | dimensionless |
| 14 | **Project Completion %** | $\%\text{Done} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks Closed}}{\text{Tasks Planned}} \times 100$ | Schedule tracking | % |
| 15 | **Estimated Time to Complete** | $\text{ETC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI}}$ | Revised estimate | days/weeks |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never use** the vertical pipe `|` inside formulas. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead (e.g., $C_{pk} = \min(\text{USL} - \mu, \mu - \text{LSL})$ divided by $3\sigma$).

---

| Industry | Application of Charter & Monitoring |
|----------|-------------------------------------|
| **Manufacturing** | Reducing defect rate in assembly lines |
| **Healthcare** | Reducing patient waiting time in hospitals |
| **IT / Software** | Reducing bug density in software releases |
| **Banking** | Reducing loan processing cycle time |

Project Charters in industry are **legally binding documents** that link Six Sigma projects to **annual operating plans (AOP)** and **balanced scorecards (BSC)** of the organization.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Earned Value Analysis for Six Sigma Project Monitoring

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU examinations.

### Step 1: Define the Three Baseline Values

$$
\text{PV} = \text{Planned Value} = \text{Budget allocated for work scheduled to date}
$$

$$
\text{EV} = \text{Earned Value} = \text{Budget for work actually completed}
$$

$$
\text{AC} = \text{Actual Cost} = \text{Money actually spent}
$$

### Step 2: Derive Schedule Variance (SV)

Schedule Variance measures **how far ahead or behind schedule** the project is in financial terms.

$$
\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{SV} > 0$: Ahead of schedule
- $\text{SV} = 0$: Exactly on schedule
- $\text{SV} < 0$: Behind schedule

### Step 3: Derive Cost Variance (CV)

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{CV} > 0$: Under budget
- $\text{CV} = 0$: On budget
- $\text{CV} < 0$: Over budget

### Step 4: Derive Performance Indices

To get a **ratio-based** measure (dimensionless), we normalize:

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}
$$

$$
\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}
$$

**Combined Health Indicator:**

$$
\text{Project Health} = \text{SPI} \times \text{CPI}
$$

### Step 5: Forecast Completion (EAC)

Estimate at Completion uses CPI to predict the final cost:

$$
\text{EAC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}
$$

where **BAC** is the Budget at Completion (total sanctioned budget).

### Step 6: Variance at Completion

$$
\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC}
$$

This is the **predicted final savings or overrun** in monetary terms.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Six Sigma project has the following data after 3 months:
- Total sanctioned budget (BAC) = **₹ 10,00,000**
- Planned work value for 3 months (PV) = **₹ 4,00,000**
- Work actually completed value (EV) = **₹ 3,50,000**
- Actual money spent (AC) = **₹ 4,20,000**

**Calculate SV, CV, SPI, CPI, and predict final EAC.**

### Solution:

**Step 1: Schedule Variance**

$$
\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 3{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = -50{,}000
$$

The negative SV indicates the project is **₹ 50,000 behind schedule**.

**Step 2: Cost Variance**

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 3{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}20{,}000 = -70{,}000
$$

The negative CV indicates the project is **₹ 70,000 over budget**.

**Step 3: Schedule Performance Index**

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.875
$$

Since $\text{SPI} < 1$, the project is **delayed**.

**Step 4: Cost Performance Index**

$$
\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{4{,}20{,}000} = 0.833
$$

Since $\text{CPI} < 1$, the project is **over-spending**.

**Step 5: Estimate at Completion**

$$
\text{EAC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.833} = ₹\,12{,}00{,}480
$$

**Step 6: Variance at Completion**

$$
\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}480 = -₹\,2{,}00{,}480
$$

**Conclusion:** If current trends continue, the project will overrun the budget by **₹ 2,00,480**. The project team must take **corrective action** — typically by either reducing scope, adding resources, or re-estimating remaining work.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2: Process Capability Linkage to Charter Metrics

When the Charter defines a CTQ (e.g., *"Reduce defect rate to below 4%"*), monitoring tracks it via $C_{pk}$.

### Step 1: Compute Process Mean

$$
\mu = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Process Standard Deviation

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i} - \mu)^{2}}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $C_{pk}$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Map $C_{pk}$ to Sigma Level

$$
\text{Sigma Level} \approx C_{pk} \times 3 + 1.5
$$

A Six Sigma quality level corresponds to $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ (in long-term, with $1.5\sigma$ shift).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"How do you monitor CTQ progress?"*, the answer is: *"By computing $C_{pk}$ at each project milestone and comparing it against the Charter target."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Charter vs. Monitoring

| Aspect | Project Charter | Project Monitoring |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Phase in DMAIC** | DEFINE Phase | All 5 phases |
| **Nature** | Static (created once, updated rarely) | Dynamic (continuous) |
| **Purpose** | Authorize and define the project | Track and control the project |
| **Time Horizon** | Beginning of project | Throughout project |
| **Output** | Signed document | Status reports, dashboards |
| **Owner** | Champion (with Black Belt input) | Black Belt (with team input) |
| **Frequency of Update** | Once or twice (major revisions only) | Weekly / bi-weekly |
| **Key Tools** | SIPOC, VOC, CTQ Tree | Gantt Chart, Earned Value, Dashboards |
| **Failure Mode** | Vague scope, missing sign-off | Schedule slip, cost overrun, scope creep |
| **KTU Mark Weightage** | 7 marks typical | 7–14 marks typical |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Project Charter. List any four of its components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Project Charter** is a formal, short document that authoritatively defines a Six Sigma project's scope, objectives, stakeholders, timeline, and key metrics, and is signed off by the project Champion to officially authorize the DMAIC project.

**Four Components:**
1. **Problem Statement** — concise data-driven description of the issue.
2. **Goal Statement** — SMART objective with measurable target.
3. **Scope** — defines in-scope and out-of-scope boundaries.
4. **Team and Roles** — RACI matrix assigning responsibilities.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark for listing = 2 marks; 1 mark for explanation of any one = 3 marks total.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is Project Monitoring? Name any two tools used for it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Project Monitoring** is the continuous, systematic process of tracking, reviewing, and regulating a Six Sigma project's progress against the Charter-defined baselines (schedule, cost, quality) to detect deviations and trigger corrective action.

**Two Tools:**
1. **Gantt Chart** — visualizes tasks vs timeline.
2. **Earned Value Analysis (EVA)** — quantifies schedule and cost performance using SPI and CPI.

*(1.5 marks definition + 1.5 marks tools = 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **nine essential components** of a Six Sigma Project Charter with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Earned Value Analysis (EVA)** method for project monitoring. For a project with BAC = ₹ 8,00,000, PV = ₹ 3,00,000, EV = ₹ 2,70,000, AC = ₹ 3,10,000, compute SV, CV, SPI, CPI, and EAC. Comment on the project health. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The nine components of a Six Sigma Project Charter are:

**1. Problem Statement (1 mark)**
A clear, data-driven description of *what is wrong, where, when, and how much*.
*Example:* "The defect rate in the CNC machining process of Plant B is 12%, which is 2.4× higher than the industry benchmark of 5%, leading to a monthly rejection loss of ₹ 8 lakhs."

**2. Business Case (1 mark)**
The *financial and strategic justification* for the project.
*Example:* "Reducing defects will save ₹ 96 lakhs annually and improve customer retention by 15%."

**3. Goal Statement (1 mark)**
A **SMART** objective.
*Example:* "Reduce the CNC defect rate from 12% to below 4% within 5 months."

**4. Scope (1 mark)**
Defines boundaries — what is included and what is excluded.
*Example:* *In-scope:* CNC machines M1–M5, period Jan–May 2025. *Out-of-scope:* Manual lathe operations, pre-2024 data.

**5. Team Members and Roles (0.5 mark)**
Lists Champion, Black Belt, Green Belt, Process Owner, and Members with **RACI** matrix.

**6. Timeline / Milestones (0.5 mark)**
Phase-wise Gantt schedule (Define: 2 weeks, Measure: 4 weeks, Analyze: 4 weeks, Improve: 6 weeks, Control: 4 weeks).

**7. Key Metrics — CTQs (0.5 mark)**
The measurable quality characteristics.
*Example:* Defect rate (%), Cycle time (min), DPMO, Customer satisfaction score (NPS).

**8. Expected Benefits (0.5 mark)**
Quantified in ₹ and operational units.
*Example:* "₹ 96 lakhs annual savings, 20% reduction in cycle time, sigma level improvement from 3.2 to 4.5."

**9. Sign-off (1 mark)**
Formal signatures of Champion, Black Belt, and Process Owner to authorize the project.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Theory (3 marks):** Earned Value Analysis integrates scope, schedule, and cost to provide an objective measure of project performance.

**Key metrics:**
- **PV** (Planned Value): Budgeted cost of work scheduled.
- **EV** (Earned Value): Budgeted cost of work performed.
- **AC** (Actual Cost): Real cost incurred.

**Variances:**
- $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$
- $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$

**Indices:**
- $\text{SPI} = \text{EV} / \text{PV}$
- $\text{CPI} = \text{EV} / \text{AC}$

**Forecast:**
- $\text{EAC} = \text{BAC} / \text{CPI}$

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

Given: $\text{BAC} = ₹\,8{,}00{,}000$, $\text{PV} = ₹\,3{,}00{,}000$, $\text{EV} = ₹\,2{,}70{,}000$, $\text{AC} = ₹\,3{,}10{,}000$.

**Step 1: Schedule Variance**

$$
\text{SV} = 2{,}70{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = -30{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Cost Variance**

$$
\text{CV} = 2{,}70{,}000 - 3{,}10{,}000 = -40{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Schedule Performance Index**

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{2{,}70{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 0.90
$$

**Step 4: Cost Performance Index**

$$
\text{CPI} = \dfrac{2{,}70{,}000}{3{,}10{,}000} = 0.871
$$

**Step 5: Estimate at Completion**

$$
\text{EAC} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.871} = ₹\,9{,}18{,}484
$$

**Comment:** Since $\text{SV} < 0$ and $\text{CV} < 0$, the project is **both behind schedule and over budget**. The predicted overrun is approximately **₹ 1,18,484**. The team should take immediate corrective action (reduce scope, add resources, or revise estimates).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Project Charter** and **Project Monitoring**. Why are both essential for a Six Sigma DMAIC project? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **four tools of project monitoring** with neat diagrams and their application in a manufacturing Six Sigma project. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Project Charter | Project Monitoring |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| Phase | DEFINE phase | All 5 DMAIC phases |
| Nature | Static document | Dynamic process |
| Purpose | Authorize project | Track and control project |
| Frequency | Created once | Continuous (weekly/bi-weekly) |
| Output | Signed charter | Status reports, dashboards |
| Owner | Champion | Black Belt |

**Why both are essential (2 marks):**

1. **Charter without monitoring** leads to a project that is *authorized but uncontrolled* — it may go off-track without anyone noticing until the end.
2. **Monitoring without charter** leads to *tracking without direction* — the team may collect data but not know whether they are aligned with the original objective.
3. Together, they form the **"plan-execute-control"** triad of any successful Six Sigma project.
4. The Charter sets the **destination**; Monitoring is the **GPS** that ensures you reach it.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Tool 1: Gantt Chart (1.75 marks)**
A horizontal bar chart that visualizes the project schedule, showing start and end dates of each task, milestones, and dependencies. In a manufacturing project, it tracks DMAIC phase progress against planned dates.

**Tool 2: Earned Value Analysis (1.75 marks)**
Quantifies schedule and cost performance using PV, EV, AC, SPI, and CPI. Provides an early warning system when $\text{SPI} < 1$ or $\text{CPI} < 1$.

**Tool 3: Control Charts (1.75 marks)**
Statistical charts (X-bar R, p-chart, CUSUM) that monitor whether a process remains in statistical control. In manufacturing, they track defect rate, dimension tolerances, etc., after improvements are implemented (Control phase).

**Tool 4: Status Reports and Dashboards (1.75 marks)**
Weekly written reports and visual dashboards summarizing progress, risks, and key metrics. Used in steering committee meetings to keep stakeholders informed and aligned.

> Each tool deserves (1 mark for explanation + 0.75 mark for diagram/application) = 1.75 marks × 4 = 7 marks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Charter" with "Plan"** — The Charter is a *short authorization document*, not a detailed project plan. A 5-page detailed plan ≠ Charter. (Lose 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Missing the Business Case** — Many students list only problem and goal, forgetting the financial justification. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 3. **Vague Problem Statement** — Writing "Quality is bad" instead of a quantified statement with data. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 4. **Not Computing EAC** — When asked about project health, students compute SV and CV but **forget EAC** (the most important forecast metric). (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 5. **Mixing up SPI and CPI interpretation** — SPI < 1 means *behind schedule*, CPI < 1 means *over budget*. Students often swap these. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 6. **Not drawing diagrams** — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for neat Gantt chart or process flow diagrams in monitoring questions. Always include a diagram.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the SMART framework** — When defining goal statements, students write vague goals. KTU strictly checks for SMART components.
>
> 8. **Ignoring the 1.5σ shift** — When converting $C_{pk}$ to sigma level, students forget to add the 1.5σ long-term shift. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Project Charter and Monitoring**

### Project Charter
- ☐ **Definition:** 5–6 page formal authorization document for a DMAIC project.
- ☐ **Phase:** Created in the **DEFINE phase**.
- ☐ **9 Components:** Problem Statement, Business Case, Goal (SMART), Scope, Team/RACI, Timeline, CTQs, Benefits, Sign-off.
- ☐ **Problem Statement** must be data-driven with the 4 W's (What, Where, When, How Much).
- ☐ **SMART Goal** = Specific + Measurable + Achievable + Relevant + Time-bound.
- ☐ **RACI Matrix** = Responsible + Accountable + Consulted + Informed.
- ☐ **Sign-off** by Champion, Black Belt, and Process Owner is mandatory.

### Project Monitoring
- ☐ **Definition:** Continuous tracking of project progress against charter baselines.
- ☐ **Closed Loop:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (Deming Cycle).
- ☐ **4 Pillars:** Schedule + Cost + Quality + Risk monitoring.
- ☐ **Gantt Chart** for schedule tracking.
- ☐ **Earned Value Analysis (EVA)** for cost-schedule integration.
- ☐ **Control Charts** for quality monitoring (X-bar R, p, CUSUM).
- ☐ **Status Reports** for stakeholder communication.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$
- ☐ $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$
- ☐ $\text{SPI} = \text{EV} / \text{PV}$
- ☐ $\text{CPI} = \text{EV} / \text{AC}$
- ☐ $\text{EAC} = \text{BAC} / \text{CPI}$
- ☐ $\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC}$
- ☐ $C_{pk} = \min\bigl((\text{USL} - \mu)/(3\sigma),\ (\mu - \text{LSL})/(3\sigma)\bigr)$
- ☐ $\text{Sigma Level} = C_{pk} \times 3 + 1.5$

### Key Interpretations
- ☐ $\text{SPI} > 1$ → Ahead of schedule.
- ☐ $\text{SPI} < 1$ → Behind schedule.
- ☐ $\text{CPI} > 1$ → Under budget.
- ☐ $\text{CPI} < 1$ → Over budget.
- ☐ $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ → Six Sigma quality level.
- ☐ $C_{pk} = 1.0$ → Three Sigma quality level.

### Roles to Remember
- ☐ **Champion** = Senior sponsor, funds the project.
- ☐ **Black Belt** = Full-time project leader, statistical expert.
- ☐ **Green Belt** = Part-time team member.
- ☐ **Master Black Belt** = Senior mentor of Black Belts.
- ☐ **Process Owner** = Implements and sustains the solution.

### Common Tools Quick Reference
- ☐ **SIPOC** = Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers.
- ☐ **VOC** = Voice of Customer.
- ☐ **CTQ** = Critical to Quality.
- ☐ **DPMO** = Defects Per Million Opportunities.
- ☐ **COPQ** = Cost of Poor Quality.
- ☐ **PDCA** = Plan-Do-Check-Act (Deming Cycle).
- ☐ **BAC** = Budget at Completion.

### Exam Strategy
- ☐ Always include a **diagram** (Gantt chart, flowchart, or RACI matrix) for 7–14 mark questions.
- ☐ For numerical problems, show **all 5 steps** explicitly: SV → CV → SPI → CPI → EAC.
- ☐ For definition questions, give the **definition (1.5 marks) + 2–4 components/items (1.5 marks)**.
- ☐ Use **₹ symbol** and **quantified examples** — examiners love numbers.
- ☐ Mention **KTU 2024 keywords**: SMART, RACI, DMAIC, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ, DPMO, COPQ, EVA, SPI, CPI.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Characteristics and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Characteristics and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Process? (Formal KTU Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process:** A process is a structured, repeatable sequence of interconnected activities, tasks, or operations that transforms a set of well-defined **inputs** (materials, methods, manpower, machines, measurements, and environment — the 6M's) into measurable **outputs** (products, services, or information) that hold value for an internal or external customer.

In the strict context of **Six Sigma (DMAIC framework)**, a process is the fundamental unit of analysis. Every process, no matter how complex, is a transformation function:

$$Y = f(X)$$

Where **Y** is the Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) output characteristic, and **X** represents the process inputs (the **Xs**). The Six Sigma philosophy aims to mathematically link, control, and reduce variation in Y by understanding, controlling, and optimizing the Xs.

## 1.2 Process Characteristics — The Three Pillars

Every KTU-evaluable process analysis revolves around three quantifiable pillars:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3 Pillars of Process Characteristics:**
> 1. **Capability** — Can the process meet specifications? (Cp, Cpk)
> 2. **Stability** — Is the process predictable over time? (Control Charts)
> 3. **Performance** — How well is the process actually doing? (Pp, Ppk, DPMO, Sigma Level)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tap Analogy"

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** in your kitchen sink:
- **Inputs (Xs):** Water pressure, tap opening angle, pipe cleanliness, temperature.
- **Process:** The tap-and-pipe system.
- **Output (Y):** The flow rate of water into your glass.

If you want exactly **250 ml of water** (the specification), the process must be:
- **Centered:** On average, deliver 250 ml.
- **Stable:** If you open it 10 times, the 10th time shouldn't suddenly gush 1 liter.
- **Capable:** Even under normal variation, it should never overflow the glass (USL) nor leave the glass half-empty (LSL).

This is precisely what Six Sigma tries to engineer — a process that is **centered, stable, and capable**.

## 1.4 Common vs. Special Cause Variation

| Variation Type | Definition | Source | Six Sigma Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Common Cause** | Inherent, random variation within the system's design | Built into the process | Improve the **system** (DMAIC) |
| **Special Cause** | Non-random, assignable variation due to external shocks | Specific, identifiable events | Remove the **cause**, then improve system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:** Deming's famous quote — *"Management is responsible for ~94% of common-cause variation; operators can only act on special-cause variation."* This is a frequently tested 3-mark conceptual question.

## 1.5 The 6M's of Process Inputs

In Six Sigma, every process input is classified under the **6M framework** (often extended to **Ishikawa Fishbone** categories):
- **Man** (workforce skill/training)
- **Machine** (equipment, tooling)
- **Material** (raw inputs)
- **Method** (procedure, SOP)
- **Measurement** (instrumentation, gauge)
- **Mother Nature / Environment** (temperature, humidity)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Process — The SIPOC Lens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIPOC (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers)** is the highest-level process map mandated by KTU in the **Define** phase of DMAIC. It forces a boundary definition before any statistical analysis.

A process in steady state is best described by the **statistical distribution** of its output Y. For nearly all Six Sigma applications, this is modeled as a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**:

$$f(Y) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{Y-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = process mean (location of the bell curve)
- $\sigma$ = process standard deviation (spread — the enemy of Six Sigma)

## 2.2 Process Variability — The Source of All Defects

**Total Variation** in a process is the sum of two independent variance components:

$$\sigma^2_{Total} = \sigma^2_{Common\ Cause} + \sigma^2_{Special\ Cause}$$

In a **stable process** (statistical control), only common-cause variation exists, allowing us to **predict** future performance. In an **unstable process**, special causes inflate $\sigma_{Total}$ and make prediction impossible.

## 2.3 Process Capability Indices — The KTU Formula Sheet

This is the **single most high-yield section** for KTU board exams. Master these formulas:

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Capability Indices (Killer Formulas for KTU 2024)**

| Index | Formula | Interpretation | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cp** (Process Capability) | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Measures *potential* capability (spread only) | When process is **centered** |
| **Cpk** (Process Capability Index) | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Measures *actual* capability (spread + centering) | **Most-used** index in KTU problems |
| **Cpu** (Upper Capability) | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Distance from mean to USL | Always computed alongside Cpl |
| **Cpl** (Lower Capability) | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Distance from LSL to mean | Always computed alongside Cpu |
| **Cpm** (Taguchi Index) | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$ | Penalizes off-target variation from target T | Less common in KTU, but definitional |
| **Pp** (Process Performance) | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Uses **overall** std dev $s$ | Long-term performance |
| **Ppk** (Process Performance Index) | $P_{pk} = \min(P_{pu}, P_{pl})$ | Long-term, includes centering | Final certification metric |
| **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \min(Z_{USL}, Z_{LSL})$ | Distance to nearest spec in sigma units | Used in DPMO conversion |
| **DPMO** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Direct Six Sigma metric |
| **Yield** | $Yield = 1 - DPMO$ | Proportion conforming | Reported in process validation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Cp vs Pp**. **Cp/Cpk** use the **within-subgroup** standard deviation $\sigma$ (short-term, assuming stability). **Pp/Ppk** use the **overall** standard deviation $s$ (long-term, including drift). For a stable process: $C_p \approx P_p$. For an unstable process: $P_p < C_p$.

## 2.4 Sigma Level, DPMO, and Yield — The Conversion Table

This is **memorization-mandatory** for KTU 2024:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Normal Tail Areas (One-Sided) — Memorize This Table**

| Sigma Level (Z) | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.15% |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32% |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.379% |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.9767% |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** |

Note: The famous **3.4 DPMO at 6σ** includes a **1.5σ shift correction** (Motorola's convention), accounting for long-term process drift.

## 2.5 Process Mapping Tools (Theory Layer)

Six Sigma uses a hierarchy of process maps:

1. **SIPOC** — Macro view (entire value chain)
2. **Top-Down Flowchart** — 8–12 major steps
3. **Detailed Process Flowchart** — Every decision, loop, and sub-step
4. **Value Stream Map (VSM)** — Adds time, inventory, and information flow data
5. **Process FMEA** — Failure Mode & Effects Analysis (RPN = S × O × D)

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Process Analysis

- **Manufacturing:** Ensure shaft diameters are within tolerance.
- **Software:** Reduce defects per million lines of code.
- **Healthcare:** Reduce patient wait time variability.
- **Finance:** Reduce transaction processing error rates.
- **Service:** Improve on-time delivery consistency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Full Process Capability Calculation (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]:**
A CNC lathe is producing a shaft with a specification of **24.50 mm ± 0.10 mm**. The process has been monitored with 25 subgroups of size 5, yielding the following summary statistics:
- Overall Mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.53$ mm
- Average Range: $\bar{R} = 0.04$ mm
- Sample Standard Deviation (overall): $s = 0.022$ mm
- Target Value: $T = 24.50$ mm

Calculate: **(a)** Cp, **(b)** Cpk, **(c)** Pp, **(d)** Ppk, and **(e)** the Sigma Level. Comment on process capability.

### Step 1: Define the Specification Limits

Given the nominal specification $24.50 \pm 0.10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
USL &= 24.50 + 0.10 = 24.60 \text{ mm} \\
LSL &= 24.50 - 0.10 = 24.40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Estimate the Within-Subgroup Standard Deviation

The d2 constant for subgroup size $n = 5$ is **$d_2 = 2.326$**. The within-subgroup standard deviation is estimated from the average range:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.04}{2.326} \\
\sigma &\approx 0.0172 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $\sigma$ is the short-term, common-cause variability (used in **Cp/Cpk**).

### Step 3 (a): Calculate Cp

Cp measures the ratio of the specification width to the 6-sigma process width, **ignoring centering**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
C_p &= \frac{24.60 - 24.40}{6 \times 0.0172} \\
C_p &= \frac{0.20}{0.1032} \\
C_p &\approx 1.938
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The specification width is ~1.94 times wider than the natural 6σ spread of the process. The process has **good potential capability**.

### Step 4 (b): Calculate Cpk

Cpk is the minimum of the upper and lower capability ratios. First, compute the two components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.60 - 24.53}{3 \times 0.0172} = \frac{0.07}{0.0516} \approx 1.357 \\
C_{pl} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.53 - 24.40}{3 \times 0.0172} = \frac{0.13}{0.0516} \approx 2.519
\end{aligned}
$$

Now take the minimum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.357, 2.519) \\
C_{pk} &\approx 1.357
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Because $\mu = 24.53$ is **closer to the USL** than the LSL, the upper side is the limiting factor. The process is **off-center** by 0.03 mm, and the actual capability is only 1.357 — a significant drop from Cp.

### Step 5 (c): Calculate Pp

Pp uses the **overall** sample standard deviation $s = 0.022$ mm (includes both common and special cause variation observed historically):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6s} \\
P_p &= \frac{0.20}{6 \times 0.022} \\
P_p &= \frac{0.20}{0.132} \\
P_p &\approx 1.515
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 (d): Calculate Ppk

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pu} &= \frac{24.60 - 24.53}{3 \times 0.022} = \frac{0.07}{0.066} \approx 1.061 \\
P_{pl} &= \frac{24.53 - 24.40}{3 \times 0.022} = \frac{0.13}{0.066} \approx 1.970 \\
P_{pk} &= \min(1.061, 1.970) \\
P_{pk} &\approx 1.061
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 (e): Calculate the Sigma Level (Z)

The Z-score represents the distance from the process mean to the nearest specification limit, in sigma units. Using the **short-term** within σ for the rolling 1.5σ correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{USL} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{0.07}{0.0172} \approx 4.07\sigma \\
Z_{LSL} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma} = \frac{0.13}{0.0172} \approx 7.56\sigma \\
Z &= \min(4.07, 7.56) = 4.07\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

Including the **Motorola 1.5σ long-term shift correction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{LT} &= Z - 1.5 = 4.07 - 1.5 = 2.57\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Engineering Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** With $C_{pk} = 1.357$ and $P_{pk} = 1.061$, the process is **capable** (industry standard requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$) but **not centered**. The gap between Cp and Cpk (1.938 vs 1.357) clearly indicates a **centering problem** that should be addressed first by re-tuning the CNC offset to bring $\mu$ from 24.53 mm down toward the target of 24.50 mm.

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — DPMO to Sigma Conversion (KTU 7-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** In a loan processing department, 450 applications were processed last month. A total of 27 defects were observed across 5 defect categories (opportunities per unit). Calculate DPMO and the corresponding Sigma Level.

### Step 1: Calculate DPMO

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= \frac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \\
DPMO &= \frac{27 \times 10^6}{450 \times 5} \\
DPMO &= \frac{27 \times 10^6}{2250} \\
DPMO &= 12{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
Yield &= 1 - \frac{DPMO}{10^6} \\
Yield &= 1 - 0.012 \\
Yield &= 0.988 = 98.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Sigma Level

For DPMO = 12,000, the one-sided tail area is 0.012. Looking up the standard normal table (or using the inverse CDF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Z > z) &= 0.012 \\
z &\approx 2.257
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **short-term** sigma level. With the **1.5σ shift** convention:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{level} &= z + 1.5 \\
\sigma_{level} &= 2.257 + 1.5 \\
\sigma_{level} &\approx 3.76\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The process operates at approximately 3.76σ**, far below the Six Sigma benchmark of 6σ. The department needs a major DMAIC project to address root causes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Transformation Model (Y = f(X))

This is the foundational block diagram that KTU frequently tests. Every process is a black box transforming Xs into Ys, with Noise (Z) factors interfering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input X1: Material] --> P1
    X2[Input X2: Method] --> P1
    X3[Input X3: Manpower] --> P1
    X4[Input X4: Machine] --> P1
    X5[Input X5: Measurement] --> P1
    X6[Input X6: Environment] --> P1
    Z1[Noise Z: Ambient Temp] -.-> P1
    Z2[Noise Z: Humidity] -.-> P1
    P1{{Process f: Transformation Function}} --> Y[Output Y: CTQ Characteristic]
    Y --> Spec{Within Specification Limits?}
    Spec -- Yes --> CONFORM[Conforming Product]
    Spec -- No --> DEFECT[Defect Nonconforming]
    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style Y fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style DEFECT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    style CONFORM fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style Z1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#FF69B4,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    style Z2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#FF69B4,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

## 4.2 Process Capability Analysis Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

Since the physical appearance of a normal distribution overlaid with specification limits is best rendered mathematically (which KTU expects you to sketch), here is the **analytical architecture** of a full process capability study:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Subgroup Data] --> B[Verify Normality]
    B --> B1{Anderson Darling p greater than 0.05}
    B1 -- Yes --> C[Compute X-double-bar and R-bar]
    B1 -- No --> B2[Apply Box-Cox Transformation]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Estimate Within Sigma]
    D --> D1[sigma equals R-bar divided by d2]
    C --> E[Compute Overall Std Dev s]
    E --> F[Capability Indices]
    D --> F
    F --> F1[Cp equals USL minus LSL divided by 6 sigma]
    F --> F2[Cpk equals min of Cpu and Cpl]
    E --> F3[Pp equals USL minus LSL divided by 6s]
    E --> F4[Ppk equals min of Ppu and Ppl]
    F --> G[Decision Matrix]
    G --> G1{Cpk greater than 1.33 AND Cpk equals Cp}
    G --> G2{1.0 less than Cpk less than 1.33}
    G --> G3{Cpk less than 1.0}
    G1 --> OUT1[Capable and Centered]
    G2 --> OUT2[Capable but Off-Center Recenter]
    G3 --> OUT3[Not Capable Redesign]
    style A fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C
    style F fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style OUT2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style OUT3 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
```

## 4.3 DMAIC Phase Map Showing Process Analysis Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Define: SIPOC and VOC] --> M[Measure: Data Collection Plan]
    M --> A[Analyze: Process Capability and Root Cause]
    A --> I[Improve: Pilot and Optimize]
    I --> C[Control: SPC and FMEA]
    C --> SUSTAIN[Standardize and Sustain]
    style A fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:4px
    style SUSTAIN fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Anchor for KTU Exam:** When you draw the bell curve, always label (1) the spec limits, (2) the process mean, (3) the shaded area beyond spec limits, and (4) the Z-scores to both USL and LSL. This is the most common 7-mark sketch question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Common Cause and Special Cause variation. Who is responsible for each?**

**Model Answer:**
Common cause variation is the inherent, random variability built into a process due to its design, methods, materials, and environment. It is predictable and represents the **voice of the system**. Special cause variation is non-random, assignable variation arising from specific, identifiable external shocks such as a broken tool, a new operator, or a power surge.

According to **W. Edwards Deming**, management is responsible for **~94% of common-cause variation** (since it stems from the system they designed), while operators are responsible for detecting and removing **special causes** (and then alerting management to underlying system flaws).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly assign special-cause responsibility to operators and common-cause to management. The correct framing is that **operators can act on special causes, but only management can improve the system** to reduce common causes.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q: Define Process Capability (Cp) and Process Capability Index (Cpk). Why is Cpk always less than or equal to Cp?**

**Model Answer:**
$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ measures the potential capability of a process, considering only the **spread** of the process relative to the specification width.

$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ measures the **actual** capability, accounting for both spread **and centering** of the process mean.

Cpk is always $\leq$ Cp because Cpk is essentially Cp multiplied by a centering factor (the ratio of the smaller distance from the mean to a spec limit, divided by half the spec width). When the process is **perfectly centered** ($\mu = T$), $C_{pk} = C_p$. Any deviation from the target causes $C_{pk} < C_p$.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A pharmaceutical company fills injection vials. The target fill volume is **10.00 ml** with specification limits of **USL = 10.20 ml** and **LSL = 9.80 ml**. From 30 subgroups of size 4, the following statistics are computed: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 10.05$ ml, $\bar{R} = 0.08$ ml, and overall $s = 0.045$ ml. (Note: $d_2$ for $n=4$ is 2.059.) **(7 Marks)**

**Compute Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk, and the Process Sigma Level. State whether the process is capable.**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Within-subgroup standard deviation.**

$$
\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.08}{2.059} \approx 0.0388 \text{ ml}
$$

**Step 2: Cp calculation.**

$$
C_p = \frac{10.20 - 9.80}{6 \times 0.0388} = \frac{0.40}{0.2328} \approx 1.718
$$

**Step 3: Cpk calculation.**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{10.20 - 10.05}{3 \times 0.0388} = \frac{0.15}{0.1164} \approx 1.289
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{10.05 - 9.80}{3 \times 0.0388} = \frac{0.25}{0.1164} \approx 2.148
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.289, 2.148) = 1.289
$$

**Step 4: Pp calculation.**

$$
P_p = \frac{10.20 - 9.80}{6 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.40}{0.270} \approx 1.481
$$

**Step 5: Ppk calculation.**

$$
P_{pu} = \frac{10.20 - 10.05}{3 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.15}{0.135} \approx 1.111
$$

$$
P_{pl} = \frac{10.05 - 9.80}{3 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.25}{0.135} \approx 1.852
$$

$$
P_{pk} = \min(1.111, 1.852) = 1.111
$$

**Step 6: Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift).**

$$
Z_{ST} = \min\left(\frac{0.15}{0.0388}, \frac{0.25}{0.0388}\right) = \min(3.866, 6.443) = 3.866\sigma
$$

$$
Z_{LT} = 3.866 - 1.5 = 2.366\sigma
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $C_{pk} = 1.289 \geq 1.33$ is **borderline** (just below the 1.33 industry benchmark) and $P_{pk} = 1.111$ is **below 1.33**, the process is **marginally capable short-term** but **not capable long-term**. The fill volume is over-target (10.05 vs 10.00), so the process is **off-center**, drifting toward the USL. **Recommendation:** Recalibrate the filling nozzle to bring the mean down to 10.00 ml.

---

**(b)** Explain the **SIPOC diagram** with a suitable example from a service industry. Why is SIPOC considered the first step in the **Define** phase of DMAIC? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**SIPOC** stands for **Suppliers – Inputs – Process – Outputs – Customers**. It is a high-level process map that provides a one-page snapshot of any process.

**Example: A Hospital Out-Patient Department (OPD)**

| Element | Content |
|---|---|
| **Suppliers** | Insurance companies, diagnostic labs, pharmaceutical vendors, ambulance services |
| **Inputs** | Patient records, insurance forms, test results, prescribed drugs, doctor's time |
| **Process** | Registration → Triage → Consultation → Diagnosis → Pharmacy → Billing → Discharge |
| **Outputs** | Diagnosed patient, prescription, medical report, bill, follow-up date |
| **Customers** | Patients, family members, referring doctors, insurance providers |

**Why SIPOC is the first step in Define:**
1. It forces a **boundary definition** of the project scope (where the process starts and ends).
2. It identifies the **internal and external customers** whose Voice of Customer (VOC) drives CTQ requirements.
3. It clarifies **supplier-customer interfaces** — critical for finding handoff defects.
4. It serves as a **communication tool** to align cross-functional team members before detailed data collection begins.
5. It prevents **scope creep** by documenting exactly which sub-processes are in/out of the project.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Students often describe SIPOC in excessive detail but forget the "Why" part. Marks are reserved for explaining *why* it is used in Define (alignment, scoping, customer identification) — not just listing what SIPOC stands for.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** A call center logs the following performance over one quarter: **12,000 calls handled, 96 defects observed, 4 opportunities per call.** Calculate DPMO, Yield, and the corresponding **Process Sigma Level** (with 1.5σ shift). Comment on the quality performance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: DPMO calculation.**

$$
DPMO = \frac{96 \times 10^6}{12000 \times 4} = \frac{96 \times 10^6}{48000} = 2000
$$

**Step 2: Yield calculation.**

$$
Yield = 1 - \frac{2000}{10^6} = 1 - 0.002 = 0.998 = 99.8\%
$$

**Step 3: Sigma Level (Short-Term).**
For DPMO = 2000, the one-sided tail probability is 0.002. From the standard normal table:

$$
P(Z > z) = 0.002 \implies z \approx 2.878
$$

**Step 4: Long-Term Sigma (with 1.5σ shift).**

$$
\sigma_{level} = 2.878 + 1.5 = 4.378 \approx 4.38\sigma
$$

**Conclusion:** The call center operates at **4.38σ**, with **99.8% yield**. While this is much better than 3σ (93.32%), it still produces **2,000 defects per million** — far from the 3.4 DPMO benchmark of 6σ. A **DMAIC project** is recommended to target the dominant defect categories.

---

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of **Process Capability** using the **Normal Distribution curve**. Clearly indicate USL, LSL, the process mean $\mu$, and the shaded non-conforming regions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

The output of a stable, in-control process typically follows a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution**, characterized by its mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Sketch Description (to be drawn by student on answer sheet):**

1. Draw a symmetric bell curve.
2. Mark the horizontal axis as the measurement scale.
3. Draw two vertical lines: **LSL** on the left and **USL** on the right.
4. Mark the **process mean $\mu$** in the center of the bell (if centered).
5. Shade the **left tail** (area where $X < LSL$) — this represents defects.
6. Shade the **right tail** (area where $X > USL$) — this represents defects.
7. Label $6\sigma$ as the total natural spread ($\mu \pm 3\sigma$).
8. Indicate $Z_{USL}$ and $Z_{LSL}$ as the sigma-distance from $\mu$ to the spec limits.

**Key Relationships to Annotate on the Sketch:**

$$
\text{Yield} = P(LSL \leq X \leq USL) = \Phi(Z_{USL}) - \Phi(Z_{LSL})
$$

$$
DPMO = (1 - \text{Yield}) \times 10^6
$$

**Interpretation of Capability Values:**

| Cpk Value | Process Status | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | Excellent (6σ capable) | Maintain |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | Capable | Monitor and improve |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 33$ | Marginally capable | Improve urgently |
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | Not capable | Complete redesign |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** When drawing the bell curve, students often place the spec limits **inside the curve** without labeling the **shaded tail areas**. Marks are explicitly allocated for **clearly shaded non-conforming regions** and the **Z-score annotations** on both sides of the mean.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Process** = Transformation function $Y = f(X)$ with 6M inputs.
- **Three Pillars of Process Analysis:** Capability, Stability, Performance.
- **Common cause** = system-level, management's responsibility. **Special cause** = assignable, operator-actionable.
- **Cp** uses *spread only*; **Cpk** uses *spread + centering*. Always $C_{pk} \leq C_p$.
- **Cp/Cpk** use $\sigma$ (within, short-term). **Pp/Ppk** use $s$ (overall, long-term).
- **d2 constants** to memorize: $n=2 \to 1.128$, $n=3 \to 1.693$, $n=4 \to 2.059$, $n=5 \to 2.326$, $n=6 \to 2.534$.
- **Sigma level conversion** — memorize the 3σ, 4σ, 5σ, 6σ DPMO values: 66,807 / 6,210 / 233 / 3.4.
- **Always apply the 1.5σ long-term shift** to compute the rolled sigma level: $\sigma_{LT} = z_{ST} + 1.5$.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the industry benchmark for a capable process.
- **DPMO** is **Defects × 10⁶ / (Units × Opportunities)** — note it is **defects**, not defective units.
- **SIPOC** is the mandatory first step of the **Define** phase — Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers.
- **Process Analysis** sits in the **Measure → Analyze** phases of DMAIC.
- **Z-score** = (USL − μ) / σ (upper) or (μ − LSL) / σ (lower); take the minimum for capability.
- **Deming's principle:** Management owns common-cause variation; operators detect special-cause variation.
- **6σ quality** = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield — the world-class benchmark.
- **For a stable process:** $C_p \approx P_p$. For an unstable process: $P_p < C_p$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Mapping SIPOC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Mapping SIPOC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — PROCESS MAPPING & SIPOC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SIPOC** is a high-level process mapping technique used in the **Define phase** of the **DMAIC** (Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control) methodology of Six Sigma. The acronym expands to:

$$\text{SIPOC} = \text{Suppliers} \rightarrow \text{Inputs} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Outputs} \rightarrow \text{Customers}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *SIPOC is a one-page process map that identifies a process by listing its Suppliers, Inputs, Process steps, Outputs, and Customers. It defines the boundaries of a project and provides a macroscopic view before detailed process mapping is performed.*

Formally, SIPOC is also known as the **COPIS diagram** (Customers–Outputs–Process–Inputs–Suppliers) when constructed in reverse order, emphasizing the **customer-first** philosophy of Six Sigma.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and order a **Masala Dosa**:

| SIPOC Element | Restaurant Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Suppliers** | Vegetable vendor, dairy supplier, gas agency |
| **Inputs** | Rice batter, potatoes, onions, oil, spices, cook's effort |
| **Process** | Mixing batter → Preparing masala → Tawa roasting → Serving |
| **Outputs** | Hot Masala Dosa, side sambhar, chutney |
| **Customers** | You (the diner) and fellow table-mates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** A SIPOC diagram works exactly like a chef's **"before-you-cook" checklist**. Before firing up the stove, a good chef confirms: *Who is eating? What must I produce? What steps will I use? What ingredients do I need? Where do I get them?* SIPOC forces a Six Sigma team to answer these five questions *before* diving into problem-solving.

### 1.3 Why SIPOC Was Created

SIPOC was popularized by **Sir John Dalgleish** at Motorola (Six Sigma's birthplace) and later refined at **GE** under Jack Welch. Its purpose is to:

1. **Define project boundaries** explicitly (where the process starts and ends).
2. **Identify the Voice of the Customer (VOC)** before measuring anything.
3. **Avoid scope creep** in DMAIC projects.
4. **Provide a common visual language** for cross-functional teams.
5. **Link suppliers and customers to a single integrated value stream.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Term:** SIPOC is a **scope-setting tool**, not a problem-solving tool. It is constructed *before* the project charter and SIPOC ensures everyone in the team agrees on **what is inside** and **what is outside** the project boundary.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of SIPOC — Structured Breakdown

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Suppliers (S)
- **Definition:** Any person, department, organization, or system that **provides the inputs** required by the process.
- **Categories:**
  - *Internal Suppliers:* Colleague departments, in-house IT, internal logistics.
  - *External Suppliers:* Vendors, third-party vendors, government agencies.
- **Key Question:** *"Who provides what we need to start the process?"*
- **High-Yield Insight:** Identify the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** characteristics of every supplier because defective supplier inputs propagate downstream as the **1-10-100 Rule** (cost of fixing a defect: ₹1 at supplier, ₹10 at process, ₹100 at customer).

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Inputs (I)
- **Definition:** All **materials, information, data, and resources** that are transformed or consumed by the process.
- **Types of Inputs:**
  - *Physical:* Raw materials, components, hardware.
  - *Informational:* Customer orders, specifications, approval forms.
  - *Human:* Skill, knowledge, experience, effort.
- **Key Question:** *"What do we need, in what quantity and form?"*

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Process (P)
- **Definition:** The **sequence of 4 to 7 high-level steps** that transform inputs into outputs.
- **Standard SIPOC Rule:** *Never exceed 7 process steps.* If more are needed, the scope is too broad and must be subdivided.
- **Key Question:** *"What major activities convert the inputs into outputs?"*
- **Verb Convention:** Process steps are always written in **active verbs** — *Receive → Verify → Process → Deliver.*

### 🔹 Pillar 4: Outputs (O)
- **Definition:** The **products, services, or information** that result from the process.
- **Critical Link:** Each output must trace back to at least one **Customer** and at least one **Customer Requirement (CTQ)**.
- **Key Question:** *"What tangible or intangible products emerge?"*

### 🔹 Pillar 5: Customers (C)
- **Definition:** Any person, department, or entity that **receives or uses the outputs** of the process.
- **Customer Pyramid:**
  - *External Customers:* End users, paying clients.
  - *Internal Customers:* Next department in the value chain.
- **Key Question:** *"Who benefits from the outputs and how do they judge quality?"*

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> SIPOC is largely a **qualitative/framework tool**, so it does not have heavy mathematical formulas. However, KTU examiners often test the **defect-propagation metric** and the **process complexity index** alongside it.

| Concept | Expression / Description | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIPOC Acronym** | Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers | Logical flow | Define phase scoping |
| **COPIS Reverse View** | Customers → Outputs → Process → Inputs → Suppliers | Logical flow | Customer-centric view |
| **Process Step Limit** | $4 \le N_{steps} \le 7$ | Count | Scope boundary rule |
| **1-10-100 Rule (Defect Cost)** | $C_{fix}(x) = 10^{(x-1)}$ where $x$ is the stage | Currency | Justifies early defect detection |
| **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** | $PCE = \dfrac{t_{value\,added}}{t_{total\,cycle}} \times 100\,\%$ | Percent | Used after SIPOC to find waste |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good\,units}}{N_{total\,units}} \times 100\,\%$ | Percent | Output quality measure |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Decimal | Cumulative process yield |
| **DPMO (Defects per Million Opportunities)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | ppm | Sigma level calculation |

Where:
- $D$ = total defects observed
- $N$ = total units produced
- $O$ = opportunities per unit
- $t_{value\,added}$ = time spent on value-adding activities
- $t_{total\,cycle}$ = total elapsed time of the process

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

SIPOC is the **foundational scoping tool** across industries:

| Industry | Typical SIPOC Application |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Mapping the production line from raw material intake to finished goods dispatch |
| **Software (IT Services)** | Mapping a bug-resolution workflow from ticket creation to closure |
| **Healthcare** | Mapping patient admission: Reception → Triage → Diagnosis → Treatment → Discharge |
| **Banking** | Loan processing: Application → Verification → Approval → Disbursal → Customer Notification |
| **Education (KTU B.Tech context)** | Course registration: Login → Elective selection → Fee payment → Confirmation → Student record |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** When a KTU question gives you a process scenario, the first thing to construct is the **SIPOC** *before* the detailed flowchart. Examiners award marks for identifying the **correct boundary** of the process.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction of a SIPOC Diagram

## 3.1 The 7-Step SIPOC Construction Methodology

Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step procedure** that KTU examiners expect students to follow when constructing a SIPOC for any given process.

### **STEP 1 — Identify the Process Name and Boundaries**
Clearly name the process (e.g., "KTU B.Tech Course Registration Process") and decide where it **starts** and **ends**.

$$\text{Boundary} = [\text{Start Event}, \text{End Event}]$$

For course registration: *Start = Student logs into KTU portal; End = Course confirmation is emailed to student.*

### **STEP 2 — Identify the Customers (C) — Work Backwards**
Start with the **Outputs first** because outputs define customers. Ask:
- *"Who consumes the result of this process?"*
- *"What is their primary need?"*

For course registration:
- **C1:** Students (need confirmed course slot).
- **C2:** Department (need updated roll list).
- **C3:** University Examination Cell (need registration data for hall tickets).

### **STEP 3 — Identify the Outputs (O)**
List the tangible and intangible products produced.

For course registration:
- **O1:** Confirmed course registration record.
- **O2:** Updated student database.
- **O3:** Fee receipt.
- **O4:** Email/SMS confirmation.

### **STEP 4 — Identify the High-Level Process Steps (P)**
List **4 to 7** major activities using **active verbs** in the **present tense**.

For course registration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &: \text{Student logs in to KTU portal} \\
P_2 &: \text{Student selects electives and verifies prerequisites} \\
P_3 &: \text{Student makes online fee payment} \\
P_4 &: \text{System validates payment and generates registration entry} \\
P_5 &: \text{HOD approves the registered list} \\
P_6 &: \text{University server issues confirmation email and receipt}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **STEP 5 — Identify the Inputs (I)**
List everything that the process consumes or transforms.

For course registration:
- **I1:** Student credentials and login ID.
- **I2:** Course catalogue and elective options.
- **I3:** Fee amount and payment details.
- **I4:** Prerequisite approval forms (if any).
- **I5:** Internet connectivity and system availability.

### **STEP 6 — Identify the Suppliers (S)**
For each input, identify its source.

For course registration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &\to S_1 : \text{KTU IT Cell (issues login credentials)} \\
I_2 &\to S_2 : \text{Academic Office (publishes course catalogue)} \\
I_3 &\to S_3 : \text{Bank/Payment Gateway (processes fee)} \\
I_4 &\to S_4 : \text{Department Faculty Advisor} \\
I_5 &\to S_5 : \text{Network Infrastructure Provider}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **STEP 7 — Validate, Review, and Publish**
Cross-check that:
- Every **Input** has at least one **Supplier**.
- Every **Output** has at least one **Customer**.
- Every **Process step** is verb-led.
- The number of process steps is between **4 and 7**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation Criterion:** A correctly drawn SIPOC always has the chain:
> $$\text{Supplier} \rightarrow \text{Input} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Output} \rightarrow \text{Customer}$$
> Any **break in this chain** is a deduction-worthy mistake in the exam.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing SIPOC Variants

| Variant | Order of Construction | Orientation | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIPOC** | Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers | Process-centric | Project scoping from internal view |
| **COPIS** | Customers → Outputs → Process → Inputs → Suppliers | Customer-centric | VOC-driven projects, QFD linkage |
| **IPO (Input–Process–Output)** | Inputs → Process → Outputs | Simplified | Quick classroom exercises |
| **Detailed Process Map** | All decisions, branches, sub-processes | Microscopic | After SIPOC, during Measure phase |

## 3.3 Derived Metrics from SIPOC Boundaries

Once SIPOC defines the boundaries, the team can compute the following quality metrics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PCE &= \frac{t_{value\,added}}{t_{total\,cycle}} \times 100\% \\
\\
FPY &= \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% \\
\\
RTY &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i \quad \text{(cumulative yield across all process steps)} \\
\\
DPMO &= \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O} \\
\\
\sigma_{level} &= 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times \ln(DPMO)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use Case:** In a semiconductor fabrication plant, after SIPOC defines the wafer-etching process boundary, the engineers compute **RTY** across 6 high-level steps. If $RTY = 0.98^6 = 0.8859$, then only **88.59%** of wafers pass through all steps without rework — a 11.4% loss that justifies Six Sigma intervention.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic SIPOC Architecture Flow

The diagram below shows the **standard left-to-right flow** of a SIPOC diagram. Suppliers feed Inputs into the Process Steps, which generate Outputs delivered to Customers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Supplier A]
    S2[Supplier B]
    S3[Supplier C]
    I1[Input X]
    I2[Input Y]
    I3[Input Z]
    P1[Step 1 Receive]
```
