> 3. **Confusing block with treatment.** Once a block, the row/column interpretation must be fixed. Board examiners strictly check the alignment of the ANOVA table.
> 4. **Failing to state hypotheses explicitly** before computing F. Always write $H_0$ and $H_1$ — 1 mark is reserved for this.
> 5. **Not verifying additivity.** When $F_B$ is significant, RBD's additivity assumption is suspect; this should be flagged.
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion sentence** ("Since $F_{Tr} = 32.0 > F_{0.05, 3, 12} = 3.49$, we reject $H_0$ at 5% level"). Marks are deducted for incomplete inference.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \varepsilon_{ij}$, with $\sum \tau_i = 0$ and $\sum \beta_j = 0$ (fixed-effect constraints).
- **Total runs:** $N = t \times b$ (one replicate per treatment per block).
- **Three-way partitioning:** $SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_B + SS_E$ (exact, not approximate).
- **Degrees of freedom:** $tb - 1 = (t - 1) + (b - 1) + (t-1)(b-1)$.
- **Key F-ratios:** $F_{Tr} = MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ and $F_B = MS_B/MS_E$.
- **Decision rule:** Reject $H_0: \tau_i = 0$ if $F_{Tr} > F_{\alpha, t-1, (t-1)(b-1)}$.
- **Blocking is local control**, not replication — it removes **one** source of systematic variability.
- **Use RBD when:** the experimental units have a *known* source of heterogeneity (a single nuisance factor).
- **Avoid RBD when:** units are completely homogeneous (use CRD) or there are ≥2 nuisance factors (use LSD).
- **Efficiency criterion:** RBD is worth using if relative efficiency $> 1$ (Kempthorne's formula).
- **Assumption of additivity:** No treatment × block interaction; if $F_B$ is significant, suspect violation.
- **Practical Six Sigma use:** RBD is a workhorse in the **Analyze** phase to identify critical process inputs after controlling environmental noise.
- **Always state hypotheses** before the F-test; always end with a **clear English conclusion sentence**.
- **Missing observations:** For KTU boards, the standard RBD assumes complete data; if a value is missing, use the formula $\hat{Y} = \frac{b \cdot T_i + t \cdot B_j - G}{(t-1)(b-1)}$ for the missing observation (advanced — only if asked).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factorial Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Factorial Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Factorial Design** is a strategic Design of Experiments (DOE) methodology in which **two or more independent variables (factors)** are varied *simultaneously* in a structured, pre-defined pattern across all possible combinations of their levels. The primary objective is to estimate not only the **main effects** of each factor on the response, but also the **interaction effects** between factors, in a single, statistically efficient experimental campaign. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEPET721 - Module 3):** The $2^k$ Factorial Design is the most extensively tested factorial model in the KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation). Students are expected to compute **main effects, two-factor interactions, perform ANOVA, and interpret normal probability / interaction plots**. ## 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Masterchef Optimization" Analogy Imagine you are a chef in a Michelin-star kitchen trying to perfect a new sauce. You suspect the *taste score* depends on two variables: **Salt (A)** and **Cooking Time (B)**. If you test salt first (varying it while keeping time constant) and then test time (varying it while keeping salt constant), you are doing a **One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT)** approach. The flaw: you might discover that salt only *dramatically* improves taste *when* the cooking time is long (a hidden **interaction**). OFAT will miss this completely. A **Factorial Design** instead says: bake **all 4 combinations** of (Low Salt / High Salt) × (Short Time / Long Time) in one shot. By doing this: - You measure the **average effect** of salt across both times (main effect A). - You measure the **average effect** of time across both salt levels (main effect B). - You measure the **synergy** — does salt behave differently at different times? (interaction A×B). This is exactly what a $2^2$ factorial design (4 runs) accomplishes, and it generalises elegantly to $2^k$ designs. ## 1.2 Core Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Factor** | An independent input variable under the experimenter's control (e.g., Temperature, Pressure). | | **Level** | The specific setting a factor takes. In a $2^k$ design, every factor has exactly **2 levels** (low "−" and high "+"). | | **Run** | A single experimental trial at a specified combination of factor levels. | | **Replicate** | Repeated execution of the same run to estimate pure experimental error. | | **Main Effect** | The average change in response when one factor moves from low to high, *averaged* over the other factors. | | **Interaction Effect** | The change in the effect of one factor as the level of another factor changes. | | **Treatment Combination** | A unique combination of factor levels, denoted by lowercase letters (e.g., *a*, *b*, *ab*). | | **Confounding** | Inability to separate (alias) the effect of one factor/interaction from another. | > [!TIP] > **Standard Notation:** In $2^k$ design literature (Montgomery, Box & Hunter), a run is labelled by the *lowercase letters* of the factors set to **HIGH**. So `ab` means A = high, B = high; the symbol `(1)` means all factors are at LOW. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anatomy of a $2^k$ Design For $k$ factors, each at 2 levels, the total number of treatment combinations is: $$N_{\text{total}} = 2^k$$ With $n$ replicates, total experimental runs: $$N = n \cdot 2^k$$ | $k$ (factors) | Runs (no rep.) | Reasonable? | |---|---|---| | 2 | 4 | Perfect for learning | | 3 | 8 | Most popular textbook case | | 4 | 16 | Industry workhorse | | 5 | 32 | Common in Six Sigma Black Belt projects | ## 2.2 Why Use Factorial Design? (The "Why") 1. **Efficiency (Orthogonality):** Every main effect and interaction is estimated independently, with maximum statistical power per run. 2. **Interaction Detection:** OFAT *cannot* detect interactions. Factorial designs can. 3. **Validity Over Wide Region:** The full factorial spans the *entire* factor space, giving results valid over a wide operating window. 4. **Sequential Building:** Significant factors can be carried forward to refined follow-up designs (e.g., Fractional Factorial, Response Surface). ## 2.3 Effect Estimation Logic For any effect (main or interaction) in a $2^k$ design with $n$ replicates: **Step 1 — Sign Table:** Assign `+1` and `−1` to the run according to the factor column. **Step 2 — Contrast:** $$\text{Contrast}_{\text{effect}} = \sum_{i=1}^{2^k} (\text{sign}_{i} \cdot \bar{y}_{i})$$ where $\bar{y}_{i}$ is the average of the $n$ replicate responses at run $i$. **Step 3 — Effect Magnitude:** $$\text{Effect} = \frac{\text{Contrast}_{\text{effect}}}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}}$$ **Step 4 — Sum of Squares:** $$SS_{\text{effect}} = \frac{(\text{Contrast}_{\text{effect}})^2}{n \cdot 2^{k}}$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are non-negotiable for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme exams.** Memorise the structure of the ANOVA table — it is asked every cycle. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total runs | $N = n \cdot 2^k$ | $n$ = replicates, $k$ = factors | | 2 | Correction Factor | $CF = \dfrac{(\sum y_{ij})^2}{N}$ | Used in Total SS | | 3 | Total Sum of Squares | $SS_T = \sum y_{ij}^2 - CF$ | $\text{df}_T = N-1$ | | 4 | Effect (Main or Interaction) | $E = \dfrac{\text{Contrast}}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}}$ | Sign of effect gives direction | | 5 | Sum of Squares (effect) | $SS = \dfrac{(\text{Contrast})^2}{n \cdot 2^{k}}$ | $\text{df} = 1$ for each effect | | 6 | Error SS | $SS_E = SS_T - \sum SS_{\text{effects}}$ | $\text{df}_E = 2^k(n-1)$ | | 7 | Mean Square | $MS = \dfrac{SS}{\text{df}}$ | | | 8 | F-statistic | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{\text{effect}}}{MS_E}$ | Compare with $F_{\alpha,1,df_E}$ | | 9 | Replicate sum at a corner | $T_i = \sum_{r=1}^{n} y_{ir}$ | Needed in contrast calc. | | 10 | Estimate of $\sigma^2$ | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$ | From pooled replicates | > **Engineering Utility:** In Six Sigma DMAIC, factorial designs are the engine of the **IMPROVE** phase. They help pinpoint the **Vital Few X's** (Critical Process Parameters) that drive the **Vital Few Y's** (CTQs) in manufacturing, semiconductors, pharma, and software. ## 2.5 The ANOVA Table Skeleton (KTU Favourite) | Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | $SS_A$ | 1 | $MS_A$ | $MS_A / MS_E$ | | B | $SS_B$ | 1 | $MS_B$ | $MS_B / MS_E$ | | AB | $SS_{AB}$ | 1 | $MS_{AB}$ | $MS_{AB} / MS_E$ | | Error | $SS_E$ | $2^k(n-1)$ | $MS_E$ | — | | **Total** | $SS_T$ | $N-1$ | | | > Decision rule: Reject $H_0$ (effect is zero) if $F_0 > F_{\alpha, 1, df_E}$. A significant interaction (AB) often means the main effects are *less interpretable in isolation* — interpret cautiously. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — $2^2$ Design with 2 Replicates > A Six Sigma Black Belt is optimising the **Yield (%)** of a chemical process. Two factors are studied: **A = Temperature** (Low = 80°C, High = 90°C) and **B = Catalyst Concentration** (Low = 1%, High = 2%). Each treatment combination is replicated **twice** ($n=2$, $k=2$, $N=8$). ### 3.1.1 Raw Data & Treatment Totals | Run | Notation | A | B | Rep 1 ($y_1$) | Rep 2 ($y_2$) | $T_i = y_1+y_2$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | (1) | − | − | 28 | 30 | **58** | | 2 | a | + | − | 36 | 38 | **74** | | 3 | b | − | + | 18 | 20 | **38** | | 4 | ab | + | + | 46 | 48 | **94** | Grand total: $G = 58 + 74 + 38 + 94 = 264$. ### 3.1.2 Contrasts Using sign columns [A : −,+ ,−,+ ] and [B : −,−,+,+ ] and [AB : +,−,−,+ ]: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Contrast}_A &= -(58) + (74) - (38) + (94) = -58 + 74 - 38 + 94 = 72 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_B &= -(58) - (74) + (38) + (94) = -58 - 74 + 38 + 94 = 0 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_{AB} &= +(58) - (74) - (38) + (94) = 58 - 74 - 38 + 94 = 40 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Effects (Magnitudes) $$ \begin{aligned} E_A &= \frac{72}{2 \cdot 2^{2-1}} = \frac{72}{4} = 18.00 \\[4pt] E_B &= \frac{0}{4} = 0.00 \\[4pt] E_{AB} &= \frac{40}{4} = 10.00 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Increasing A (temperature) raises yield by **18 percentage points** on average. B alone has zero main effect, but it *modifies* A's effect (interaction of magnitude 10). ### 3.1.4 Sum of Squares $$ \begin{aligned} SS_A &= \frac{(72)^2}{2 \cdot 2^2} = \frac{5184}{8} = 648.00 \\[4pt] SS_B &= \frac{(0)^2}{8} = 0.00 \\[4pt] SS_{AB} &= \frac{(40)^2}{8} = \frac{1600}{8} = 200.00 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.5 Total SS $$ \begin{aligned} \sum y_{ij}^2 &= 28^2+36^2+18^2+46^2+30^2+38^2+20^2+48^2 \\ &= 784+1296+324+2116+900+1444+400+2304 = 9568 \\[4pt] CF &= \frac{(264)^2}{8} = \frac{69696}{8} = 8712 \\[4pt] SS_T &= 9568 - 8712 = 856 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.6 Error SS $$ SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_B - SS_{AB} = 856 - 648 - 0 - 200 = 8 $$ ### 3.1.7 ANOVA Table Assembly | Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A (Temp) | 648.00 | 1 | 648.00 | $648/2 = 324.00$ | | B (Catalyst) | 0.00 | 1 | 0.00 | $0/2 = 0.00$ | | AB | 200.00 | 1 | 200.00 | $200/2 = 100.00$ | | Error | 8.00 | 4 | **2.00** | — | | **Total** | **856.00** | **7** | | | Critical value: $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$. - $F_0(A) = 324 \gg 7.71$ → **A is highly significant**. - $F_0(B) = 0$ → **B alone is insignificant**. - $F_0(AB) = 100 \gg 7.71$ → **AB interaction is highly significant**. ### 3.1.8 Interaction Plot (Qualitative Reasoning) Because AB is significant, we examine how A's effect depends on B: - At B = − (low catalyst): $E(A \vert B^-) = \bar{y}(a) - \bar{y}((1)) = 37 - 29 = 8$ - At B = + (high catalyst): $E(A \vert B^+) = \bar{y}(ab) - \bar{y}(b) = 47 - 19 = 28$ > A's effect *quadruples* when catalyst is increased — classic **synergistic interaction**. Hence both A=high and B=high are recommended. --- ## 3.2 Extended Case — $2^3$ Design (Sketch for Reference) For $k=3$, the design has $2^3 = 8$ corner runs. The effect hierarchy (in decreasing importance in most engineering systems) is: $$\text{3-way interaction (ABC)} < \text{2-way interactions (AB, AC, BC)} < \text{Main effects (A, B, C)}$$ The effect/SS formulas remain identical; only the **number of treatment combinations** changes. Yates' algorithm (a tabular shortcut to compute effects from the run-order data) is the standard KTU-accepted fast-computation tool for $2^k$ designs when $k \ge 3$. > [!NOTE] > **Yates' Algorithm in 4 Steps:** > 1. Write responses in standard order. > 2. Pair the first two, second two, etc.; compute **Col 1** = (top + bottom) and (top − bottom). > 3. Repeat pairing on Col 1 to get Col 2, then again to get Col 3. > 4. Divide Col 3 by $n \cdot 2^{k-1}$ for effects; square and divide by $n \cdot 2^k$ for SS. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Factorial Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([Define Problem
CTQ Y and Process Xs]) --> B0 B0[List candidate factors
and their 2 levels] --> C0 C0{Select 2^k
design size} --> D0[Construct Standard
Order Run Matrix] D0 --> E0[Randomise Run Order
and Replicate n times] E0 --> F0[Execute Experiments
Record y values] F0 --> G0[Compute Treatment
Totals T_i] G0 --> H0[Calculate Contrasts
for Main + Interactions] H0 --> I0[Compute Effects
and Sum of Squares] I0 --> J0[Construct ANOVA
Table with F-test] J0 --> K0{Significant
Effects?} K0 -- Yes --> L0[Plot Interaction
and Main Effect Plots] L0 --> M0[Identify Vital Few Xs
Set Optimal Levels] M0 --> N0[Confirmation Runs
+ Process Control] K0 -- No --> O0[Re-examine factors
or levels] O0 --> B0 N0 --> P0([IMPROVE Phase
Closed]) style A0 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style P0 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style K0 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00 style J0 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a $2^2$ Factorial Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Factor Space] FA[Factor A: Temp
Low minus, High plus] FB[Factor B: Catalyst
Low minus, High plus] end subgraph RUNMATRIX[Run Matrix 2k = 4] R1((1): A-, B-) R2((a): A+, B-) R3((b): A-, B+) R4((ab): A+, B+) end subgraph REPLIC[Replicate n=2] REP1[Rep 1 y] REP2[Rep 2 y] end subgraph ENGINE[Effect Engine] C1[Contrast_A] C2[Contrast_B] C3[Contrast_AB] EFX[Effects E_A, E_B, E_AB] SSS[Sum of Squares SS] end subgraph STAT[Statistical Inference] AOV[ANOVA Table] FTEST[F-test vs F alpha 1 dfE] end subgraph DECIDE[Decision Layer] SIG{Significant?} OPT[Optimal Settings] end FA --> R1 FA --> R2 FA --> R3 FA --> R4 FB --> R1 FB --> R2 FB --> R3 FB --> R4 R1 --> REP1 R1 --> REP2 R2 --> REP1 R2 --> REP2 R3 --> REP1 R3 --> REP2 R4 --> REP1 R4 --> REP2 REP1 --> C1 REP1 --> C2 REP1 --> C3 REP2 --> C1 REP2 --> C2 REP2 --> C3 C1 --> EFX C2 --> EFX C3 --> EFX EFX --> SSS SSS --> AOV AOV --> FTEST FTEST --> SIG SIG -- Yes --> OPT style RUNMATRIX fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593 style ENGINE fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00 style STAT fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style DECIDE fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#558b2f ``` > [!TIP] > **How to read the Mermaid diagrams in KTU exam context:** The block diagram conveys the *flow of information* — from factor definition to run matrix to effect engine to inference. Always label (1), a, b, ab to satisfy KTU's structure-and-labelling criteria. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Define a $2^k$ factorial design. State the number of treatment combinations and the number of effects (main + interactions) that can be estimated.** **Model Answer:** A **$2^k$ factorial design** is a Design of Experiments (DOE) strategy in which $k$ factors are each investigated at exactly 2 levels, and the experiment is run at *every* possible combination of these levels. - Number of treatment combinations: $\mathbf{2^k}$ - Number of effects that can be estimated (including the mean): $\mathbf{2^k}$ — comprising **1 mean, $k$ main effects, $\binom{k}{2}$ two-factor interactions, $\binom{k}{3}$ three-factor interactions, …, and 1 $k$-factor interaction**. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Distinguish between a *main effect* and an *interaction effect* in the context of factorial design.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Main Effect | Interaction Effect | |---|---|---| | Definition | Average change in response when **one** factor moves from low to high, averaged over the levels of other factors. | Change in the effect of one factor as the level of **another** factor changes. | | Order | 1st-order | 2nd-order or higher | | OFAT Detection | Yes (partially) | **No** — only factorial designs can detect it. | | Interpretation | A→↑Y by $E_A$ units. | $E_{AB} \ne 0$ means A's effect depends on B's level. | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A $2^3$ factorial experiment is conducted to study the effect of three factors — A (Speed), B (Feed), and C (Depth of Cut) — on the surface roughness (Ra, in $\mu$m) of a machined component. Each combination is replicated **twice**. The data (lower values = better quality) are given below: | Run | A | B | C | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | − | − | − | 22 | 24 | | 2 | + | − | − | 30 | 32 | | 3 | − | + | − | 18 | 20 | | 4 | + | + | − | 38 | 40 | | 5 | − | − | + | 14 | 16 | | 6 | + | − | + | 26 | 28 | | 7 | − | + | + | 10 | 12 | | 8 | + | + | + | 34 | 36 | **Compute all main effects and two-factor interaction effects. Identify the factor(s) most influential in reducing surface roughness.** **(b) [7 Marks]** Construct the full ANOVA table for the data above and determine, at $\alpha = 0.05$, which effects are statistically significant. ($F_{0.05, 1, 8} = 5.32$). --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (a): **Step 1 — Treatment Totals $T_i$:** | Run | (1) | a | b | ab | c | ac | bc | abc | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $T_i$ | 46 | 62 | 38 | 78 | 30 | 54 | 22 | 70 | Grand total: $G = 46+62+38+78+30+54+22+70 = 400$. $N=16, n=2, k=3$. **Step 2 — Sign columns (Montgomery standard) and Contrasts:** Using sign tables: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Contrast}_A &= -(46)+(62)-(38)+(78)-(30)+(54)-(22)+(70) = 128 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_B &= -(46)-(62)+(38)+(78)-(30)-(54)+(22)+(70) = 16 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_C &= -(46)-(62)-(38)-(78)+(30)+(54)+(22)+(70) = -48 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_{AB} &= +(46)-(62)-(38)+(78)+(30)-(54)-(22)+(70) = 48 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_{AC} &= +(46)-(62)+(38)-(78)-(30)+(54)-(22)+(70) = 16 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_{BC} &= +(46)+(62)-(38)-(78)-(30)-(54)+(22)+(70) = 0 \\[4pt] \text{Contrast}_{ABC} &= -(46)+(62)+(38)-(78)+(30)-(54)-(22)+(70) = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Effects:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_A &= \frac{128}{2 \cdot 2^{2}} = \frac{128}{8} = 16.00 \\[4pt] E_B &= \frac{16}{8} = 2.00 \\[4pt] E_C &= \frac{-48}{8} = -6.00 \\[4pt] E_{AB} &= \frac{48}{8} = 6.00 \\[4pt] E_{AC} &= \frac{16}{8} = 2.00 \\[4pt] E_{BC} &= 0 \\[4pt] E_{ABC} &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Identify the most influential factor for *reducing* roughness:** Since *lower* Ra is desirable, look at the factor with the **largest negative** effect. Here, $E_C = -6$ is the most beneficial; the strong $E_{AB}=6$ and $E_A=+16$ signal strong worsening trends to be **avoided** (i.e., keep A at LOW, keep B at LOW, and set C at HIGH). The optimal combination from the corner analysis is **A−, B−, C+** (Run 5), which matches the minimum data point. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b): **Step 1 — Total SS:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum y^2 &= 22^2+24^2+30^2+32^2+18^2+20^2+38^2+40^2+14^2+16^2+26^2+28^2+10^2+12^2+34^2+36^2 \\ &= 484+576+900+1024+324+400+1444+1600+196+256+676+784+100+144+1156+1296 \\ &= 11360 \\[4pt] CF &= \frac{(400)^2}{16} = \frac{160000}{16} = 10000 \\[4pt] SS_T &= 11360 - 10000 = 1360 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Sum of Squares for each effect** ($SS = \text{Contrast}^2 / (n \cdot 2^k) = \text{Contrast}^2/16$): $$ \begin{aligned} SS_A &= 128^2/16 = 16384/16 = 1024.00 \\[4pt] SS_B &= 16^2/16 = 256/16 = 16.00 \\[4pt] SS_C &= 48^2/16 = 2304/16 = 144.00 \\[4pt] SS_{AB} &= 48^2/16 = 144.00 \\[4pt] SS_{AC} &= 16^2/16 = 16.00 \\[4pt] SS_{BC} &= 0 \\[4pt] SS_{ABC} &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Error SS:** $$ SS_E = 1360 - (1024+16+144+144+16+0+0) = 1360 - 1344 = 16 $$ **Step 4 — ANOVA Table:** | Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | 1024.00 | 1 | 1024.00 | **512.00** | | B | 16.00 | 1 | 16.00 | 8.00 | | C | 144.00 | 1 | 144.00 | **72.00** | | AB | 144.00 | 1 | 144.00 | **72.00** | | AC | 16.00 | 1 | 16.00 | 8.00 | | BC | 0.00 | 1 | 0.00 | 0.00 | | ABC | 0.00 | 1 | 0.00 | 0.00 | | Error | 16.00 | 8 | **2.00** | — | | **Total** | **1360.00** | **15** | | | $F_{0.05, 1, 8} = 5.32$. **Step 5 — Significance Decision:** | Effect | $F_0$ | $F_{\text{crit}}$ | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | A | 512.00 | 5.32 | **Significant** | | B | 8.00 | 5.32 | **Significant** | | C | 72.00 | 5.32 | **Significant** | | AB | 72.00 | 5.32 | **Significant** | | AC | 8.00 | 5.32 | **Significant** | | BC | 0.00 | 5.32 | Not significant | | ABC | 0.00 | 5.32 | Not significant | **Final Recommendation (KTU 7-mark level):** Factor A is the **most dominant** (largest $F$ value), followed by the AB interaction and C. The optimal setpoints are **A = LOW, B = LOW, C = HIGH** (run 5), confirming the corner-wise optimum. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Yates' algorithm** for computing effects in a $2^k$ factorial design. Apply it to the following $2^3$ design (single replicate) and obtain all the effects. | Run | (1) | a | b | ab | c | ac | bc | abc | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Response | 12 | 18 | 14 | 22 | 10 | 16 | 12 | 20 | **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the sum of squares for all effects and the total sum of squares. Given an estimated $\sigma^2 = 1.5$ from prior replication, perform the F-test at $\alpha = 0.05$ for the largest effect. ($F_{0.05, 1, 8} = 5.32$). --- #### Model Solution Outline for Q.B (a) — Yates' Algorithm: **Step 1 — Col 0:** Write responses in standard order: 12, 18, 14, 22, 10, 16, 12, 20. **Step 2 — Col 1 (Successive pairs of Col 0):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{T1: } 12+18=30, \quad 14+22=36, \quad 10+16=26, \quad 12+20=32 \\ &\text{B1: } 18-12=6, \quad 22-14=8, \quad 16-10=6, \quad 20-12=8 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Col 2 (Pairs of Col 1):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{T2: } 30+36=66, \quad 26+32=58, \quad 6+8=14, \quad 6+8=14 \\ &\text{B2: } 36-30=6, \quad 32-26=6, \quad 8-6=2, \quad 8-6=2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Col 3 (Pairs of Col 2):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{T3: } 66+58=124, \quad 14+14=28, \quad 6+6=12, \quad 2+2=4 \\ &\text{B3: } 58-66=-8, \quad 14-14=0, \quad 6-6=0, \quad 2-2=0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Yates table summary:** | Col 0 | Col 1 (T1, B1) | Col 2 (T2, B2) | Col 3 (T3, B3) | Effect = B3 / 4 | |---|---|---|---|---| | 12 | 30 | 66 | 124 | (mean × 8) | | 18 | 36 | 58 | −8 | **A = −2.00** | | 14 | 26 | 14 | 28 | **B = 1.00** | | 22 | 32 | 14 | 0 | **AB = 0.00** | | 10 | 6 | 6 | 12 | **C = 0.50** | | 16 | 6 | 6 | 0 | **AC = 0.00** | | 12 | 8 | 2 | 4 | **BC = 0.25** | | 20 | 8 | 2 | 0 | **ABC = 0.00** | **Interpretation:** A has the largest effect (magnitude 2, negative — moving A to LOW increases response). B, C are weak; all interactions are negligible. The dominant factor is **A**. --- #### Model Solution Outline for Q.B (b): **Step 1 — SS computation:** For $k=3$, $n=1$: $SS = (\text{B3})^2 / 2^3 = (\text{B3})^2 / 8$. | Effect | B3 | $(\text{B3})^2$ | $SS = (\text{B3})^2/8$ | |---|---|---|---| | A | −8 | 64 | **8.000** | | B | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | | AB | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | | C | 4 | 16 | 2.000 | | AC | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | | BC | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | | ABC | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | **Step 2 — Total SS:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum y^2 &= 144+324+196+484+100+256+144+400 = 2048 \\[4pt] CF &= (98)^2/8 = 9604/8 = 1200.5 \\[4pt] SS_T &= 2048 - 1200.5 = 847.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — F-test for the largest effect (A):** $$ F_0 = \frac{SS_A/1}{\hat{\sigma}^2} = \frac{8.0}{1.5} \approx 5.33 $$ Compare with $F_{0.05, 1, 8} = 5.32$. > **Decision:** $F_0 = 5.33 > 5.32$ — A is **just barely significant** at $\alpha = 0.05$. (In practice, at $\alpha=0.01$ it would not be significant.) This is a borderline case warranting replication. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on Factorial Design questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the divisor.** Effect = Contrast / $(n \cdot 2^{k-1})$; SS = Contrast² / $(n \cdot 2^{k})$. Mixing these two formulas is the #1 error. *Memorise both with the multiplier and denominator swapped.* > 2. **Confusing the response direction.** A negative effect doesn't mean "insignificant" — it just means *reducing* the response. Always pair effect sign with the engineering objective. > 3. **Mis-assigning sign columns.** Sign for the AB column is the *element-wise product* of A and B signs, not a re-do of the (1)→a→b→ab sequence. > 4. **Omitting the Error row in ANOVA.** With $n \ge 2$ replicates, you *must* report $SS_E = SS_T - \sum SS_{\text{effects}}$ with $\text{df}_E = 2^k(n-1)$. Forgetting it gives zero F-values — full marks lost. > 5. **Yates' algorithm sign errors.** When computing Col $i+1$, the *second* pair is always (top − bottom), not (bottom − top). A systematic sign slip propagates and corrupts all effects. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Last 24 Hours** - ☐ **$2^k$ design = 2 levels per factor, $k$ factors, $2^k$ corners.** - ☐ **Replication $n$ → Total runs $N = n \cdot 2^k$.** - ☐ **Effect (main or interaction) = Contrast / $(n \cdot 2^{k-1})$.** - ☐ **SS for an effect = Contrast² / $(n \cdot 2^{k})$.** - ☐ **df for each effect (excluding mean) = 1.** - ☐ **Total df = $N - 1$; Error df = $2^k(n-1)$.** - ☐ **Correction Factor (CF) = $(\sum y)^2 / N$.** - ☐ **Total SS = $\sum y^2 - CF$.** - ☐ **Error SS = $SS_T - \sum SS_{\text{effects}}$ (computed only when $n \ge 2$).** - ☐ **F-test: $F_0 = MS_{\text{effect}} / MS_E$; compare to $F_{\alpha, 1, df_E}$.** - ☐ **Yates' algorithm is the fastest path for $k \ge 3$; 3 columns of pair sums/differences give all 8 effect estimates in one sweep.** - ☐ **Significant interaction ⇒ re-examine main effects carefully**; the "best" level of A may differ at different B levels. - ☐ **Negative effect ≠ unimportant**; it only means the response decreases as the factor moves from low to high. - ☐ **Engineering optimum ≠ statistical significance.** Significant effects must be interpreted in the context of the project's CTQ direction. - ☐ **Always randomise run order** to protect against lurking variables / time-based drift. - ☐ **Confirm with a confirmation run** at the chosen optimal corner — never trust a DOE conclusion without verification (Six Sigma DMADV/IMPROVE closure). > **One-line take-away:** A well-executed $2^k$ factorial design transforms Six Sigma's *IMPROVE* phase from guesswork into a precise, statistically-defensible optimisation engine — it is the single most important tool a Black Belt has for separating the **Vital Few X's** from the **Trivial Many**. ---

topics.title: Voice of Customer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Voice of Customer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voice of Customer (VOC)** is a Six Sigma terminology used to describe the **stated, unstated, and anticipated needs, expectations, preferences, and requirements** of the customer regarding a product, service, or process. It is the structured process of capturing customer feedback and translating it into actionable, measurable Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) characteristics that drive process improvement within the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) framework.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Six Sigma pedagogy, VOC is positioned as the foundational step of the **Define Phase**, acting as the bridge between qualitative customer sentiment and quantitative process metrics. It represents the customer's perception of value and is the *primary input* for the project's **Project Charter** and **SIPOC** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) diagram.

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology Breakdown

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **VOC** | Voice of Customer — raw, unfiltered customer requirements |
| **VOB** | Voice of Business — strategic, financial, and operational needs of the organization |
| **CTQ** | Critical-to-Quality — specific, measurable characteristics derived from VOC |
| **VOC Tree** | Hierarchical tool converting VOC data into measurable CTQ drivers |
| **Kano Model** | Framework categorizing customer needs into Basic, Performance, and Excitement |
| **Affected Customers** | Direct, indirect, and end-users impacted by the process |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Critic" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are the owner of a restaurant in Kerala.** You want to improve your business, but you don't just rely on your own assumptions about food quality. Instead, you actively **listen** to what customers say (the *Voice of Customer*).

- Some customers **explicitly say**: *"The fish curry was too salty."* — this is the **stated** need.
- Some customers **never complain**, but you observe they **always order the same dish** — this hints at **unstated** preferences.
- A few customers mention: *"I wish you had a drive-through."* — even though you don't offer one, this is a **latent/anticipated** need.
- An experienced restaurant owner would translate *"too salty"* into a measurable target: **"Reduce salt content by 15% and ensure consistency across batches."** — this is a **CTQ** tree conversion.

Similarly, in Six Sigma, VOC is the art of **listening systematically**, **decoding emotional and functional feedback**, and **converting it into engineering-grade metrics** that a process can be optimized against.

---

## 1.3 Why VOC is Indispensable in Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without VOC, a Six Sigma project is *"solving the wrong problem perfectly."* The DMAIC framework's success hinges on accurate VOC capture during the **Define Phase**, because every subsequent step (SIPOC mapping, CTQ selection, statistical analysis) is downstream of this critical translation.

**Three Pillars of VOC Importance:**
1. **Alignment** — Ensures project focus matches genuine customer pain points.
2. **Measurability** — Converts soft qualitative feedback into hard metrics.
3. **Strategic ROI** — Links process improvement directly to revenue and retention impact.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The VOC Capture Framework — Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes a **5-Stage VOC Translation Pipeline**. Mastering these stages is high-yield for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) exam questions.

### Stage 1: Customer Segmentation
- **Logic:** Not all customers are equal. A B2B industrial client and a retail consumer have radically different VOC profiles.
- **Action:** Segment by:
  - **Demographics** (age, income, region)
  - **Psychographics** (lifestyle, values)
  - **Behavioral patterns** (usage frequency, loyalty tier)
  - **Channel** (online vs. offline buyers)

### Stage 2: Data Collection (Listening Posts)
Multiple listening posts are deployed in parallel to capture VOC from different angles:

| Listening Post | Type of Data | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Surveys** (Likert Scale, NPS) | Quantitative + Qualitative | Large sample sizes, statistical significance required |
| **In-depth Interviews** | Qualitative, deep | Small N, exploratory research |
| **Focus Groups** | Qualitative, interactive | New product ideation, concept testing |
| **Observational Studies (Gemba Walks)** | Behavioral, unstated | Service processes, retail environments |
| **Complaint Logs / CRM Data** | Transactional, reactive | Mining existing pain points |
| **Social Media / Online Reviews** | Sentiment, unfiltered | Real-time sentiment analysis |
| **Voice of Process (VOP)** | Internal, cross-functional | Aligning VOB with VOC |

### Stage 3: Data Organization — The Affinity Diagram
Raw VOC data is often messy. The **Affinity Diagram** (a KJ Method tool from quality management) clusters similar customer statements into logical groupings.

> **Example:** Comments like *"the app crashes,"* *"login takes forever,"* and *"page won't load"* are grouped under a single affinity label: **"System Reliability Issues."**

### Stage 4: Translation via VOC/CTQ Tree
This is the *engineering heart* of VOC. The tree converts broad customer needs into measurable drivers:

```
                    ┌── CTQ₁: Login Time ≤ 3 seconds
                    │
VOC: "App is slow" ─┼── CTQ₂: Crash Rate ≤ 0.1%
                    │
                    └── CTQ₃: Page Load Time ≤ 2 seconds
```

### Stage 5: Prioritization — Kano Model + Pareto
Once CTQs are extracted, they must be **prioritized**. The **Kano Model** is the gold standard for this classification.

---

## 2.2 The Kano Model — Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Kano Model** (developed by Professor Noriaki Kano, 1984) classifies customer requirements into five categories based on how their presence/absence affects customer satisfaction. This is a **frequently tested KTU concept** — expect 3-mark or 14-mark questions.

### 2.2.1 Kano Categories

| Category | If Feature Present | If Feature Absent | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic (Must-Be)** | Neutral (not delighted) | **Extremely dissatisfied** | Car brakes work; ATM dispenses cash |
| **Performance (One-Dimensional)** | More satisfaction | More dissatisfaction | Fuel efficiency; bank interest rate |
| **Excitement (Attractive)** | **Highly delighted** | Neutral | Free upgrades; surprise rewards |
| **Indifferent** | No effect | No effect | Color of internal screw |
| **Reverse** | Dissatisfied | Satisfied | Complicated features some users hate |

### 2.2.2 Kano Classification Matrix

For each customer requirement, two questions are asked:
- **Functional (Positive):** *"If the product HAS this feature, how do you feel?"*
- **Dysfunctional (Negative):** *"If the product LACKS this feature, how do you feel?"*

|  | Dysfunctional: Like | Dysfunctional: Must-be | Dysfunctional: Neutral | Dysfunctional: Live-with | Dysfunctional: Dislike |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Functional: Like** | Q | A | A | A | O |
| **Functional: Must-be** | R | I | I | I | M |
| **Functional: Neutral** | R | I | I | I | M |
| **Functional: Live-with** | R | I | I | I | M |
| **Functional: Dislike** | R | R | R | R | Q |

**Legend:** A = Attractive · M = Must-be · O = One-dimensional · I = Indifferent · R = Reverse · Q = Questionable

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** Six Sigma's primary focus is to **secure all "Must-be" requirements first** (eliminating dissatisfaction), then optimize "One-dimensional" features, and finally innovate on "Attractive" features for competitive differentiation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — VOC Quantitative Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While VOC is largely qualitative, several **quantitative metrics** must be calculated. KTU 2024 Scheme expects familiarity with these formulas.

| Metric | Formula | Purpose | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | Measures customer loyalty | Score ($-100$ to $+100$) |
| **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** | $\text{CSAT} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Satisfaction Ratings}}{N \times \text{Max Rating}} \times 100$ | Measures satisfaction per interaction | Percentage (0–100%) |
| **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | $\text{CES} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Effort Ratings}}{N}$ | Measures ease of doing business | Score (1–7 typical) |
| **CTQ Tree Depth** | $\text{Depth} = \log_{2}(N_{\text{CTQs}})$ | Hierarchy levels of decomposition | Levels |
| **Pareto 80/20** | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{i} \geq 0.80$ | Top $k$% issues drive 80% complaints | Cumulative % |
| **Voice of Customer Response Rate** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{responses}}}{N_{\text{sent}}} \times 100$ | Survey effectiveness | Percentage |
| **VOC Reliability (Cronbach's α)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_{Y_{i}}^{2}}{\sigma_{X}^{2}}\right)$ | Internal consistency of survey | Unitless (0–1) |

> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - NPS ranges from **$-100$** (all detractors) to **$+100$** (all promoters).
> - Cronbach's $\alpha \geq 0.7$ is **acceptable reliability**; $\alpha \geq 0.9$ is **excellent**.
> - CSAT is bounded between **$0\%$** and **$100\%$**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industries aligned with KTU's B.Tech streams, VOC is applied as follows:

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Capturing customer feedback on product durability, noise, vibration, harshness (NVH).
- **Civil Engineering:** VOC for smart building occupants (thermal comfort, indoor air quality, accessibility).
- **Computer Science / IT:** VOC for software usability (SUS scores, task completion time, error rates).
- **Electronics:** VOC for IoT device reliability and battery life expectations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: NPS and CSAT from Raw VOC Data

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **worked numerical example** modeled on KTU's analytical problem style. Every step is shown explicitly to maximize partial-mark recovery during valuation.

### Problem Statement
A mobile phone manufacturer surveyed **$N = 500$** customers about their overall satisfaction. On a 0–10 satisfaction scale:
- **$N_1 = 250$** gave ratings 9 or 10 (Promoters)
- **$N_2 = 150$** gave ratings 7 or 8 (Passives)
- **$N_3 = 100$** gave ratings 0 to 6 (Detractors)

Each customer's individual satisfaction rating $R_i$ is recorded. Sample ratings from the 500 customers are:
- Group 1 (Promoters): $R_i = \{10, 9, 10, 9, 10, ...\}$
- Group 2 (Passives): $R_i = \{8, 7, 8, 7, 8, ...\}$
- Group 3 (Detractors): $R_i = \{5, 3, 6, 2, 4, ...\}$

Calculate the **NPS** and the **CSAT** (assuming max rating = 10).

---

### Step 1: Compute Promoter and Detractor Percentages

$$
\%\text{Promoters} = \frac{N_1}{N} \times 100 = \frac{250}{500} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

$$
\%\text{Detractors} = \frac{N_3}{N} \times 100 = \frac{100}{500} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

> **Logic:** Passives are excluded from NPS by design — they are *satisfied but unenthusiastic*, contributing neither to advocacy nor to churn risk.

---

### Step 2: Compute NPS

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} \\
&= 50\% - 20\% \\
&= +30
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** An NPS of $+30$ is **Good** (industry average is around $+30$ to $+40$ for top-quartile companies). Anything above 0 is acceptable; above $+50$ is world-class.

---

### Step 3: Compute Sum of All Ratings

Assuming the average rating within each group:
- Promoters average $\bar{R}_1 = 9.5$
- Passives average $\bar{R}_2 = 7.5$
- Detractors average $\bar{R}_3 = 4.0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i &= (N_1 \times \bar{R}_1) + (N_2 \times \bar{R}_2) + (N_3 \times \bar{R}_3) \\
&= (250 \times 9.5) + (150 \times 7.5) + (100 \times 4.0) \\
&= 2375 + 1125 + 400 \\
&= 3900
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The total sum of all 500 individual satisfaction scores is the sum of each group's contribution (group count × group average).

---

### Step 4: Compute CSAT

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSAT} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i}{N \times R_{\max}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3900}{500 \times 10} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3900}{5000} \times 100 \\
&= 78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A CSAT of 78% indicates a **healthy satisfaction level** but reveals room for improvement, especially in converting Passives into Promoters.

---

### Step 5: Compute Cronbach's α for Survey Reliability

Assume the survey has $k = 5$ questions measuring satisfaction, with:
- Variance of each item: $\sigma_{Y_1}^{2} = 1.2$, $\sigma_{Y_2}^{2} = 1.5$, $\sigma_{Y_3}^{2} = 1.3$, $\sigma_{Y_4}^{2} = 1.4$, $\sigma_{Y_5}^{2} = 1.1$
- Total score variance: $\sigma_{X}^{2} = 6.5$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_{Y_i}^{2} &= 1.2 + 1.5 + 1.3 + 1.4 + 1.1 = 6.5 \\
\alpha &= \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_{Y_i}^{2}}{\sigma_{X}^{2}}\right) \\
&= \frac{5}{4}\left(1 - \frac{6.5}{6.5}\right) \\
&= \frac{5}{4}(1 - 1) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Diagnostic:** This result signals a problem — the total variance equals the sum of item variances, meaning the items are **uncorrelated**. The survey needs redesign (add correlated items or increase $k$) to achieve $\alpha \geq 0.7$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Many students skip showing the $\sum$ step-by-step summation for Cronbach's α. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for the explicit sum of variances. Do not compress it.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Kano Classification Example

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix-style problem is a KTU favorite. You are given customer responses and must classify requirements.

### Scenario
A software firm collects Kano questionnaire responses from 200 customers. For the requirement *"Auto-save every 30 seconds"*:

| | Dysfunctional Response | Count |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional: Like** | Must-be | 30 |
| **Functional: Must-be** | Live-with | 80 |
| **Functional: Neutral** | Dislike | 40 |
| **Functional: Live-with** | Dislike | 30 |
| **Functional: Dislike** | Dislike | 20 |

### Step 1: Map Each Cell to a Kano Code

Using the standard Kano evaluation table:
- (Like, Must-be) → **A** (Attractive) → 30 customers
- (Must-be, Live-with) → **I** (Indifferent) → 80 customers
- (Neutral, Dislike) → **M** (Must-be) → 40 customers
- (Live-with, Dislike) → **M** (Must-be) → 30 customers
- (Dislike, Dislike) → **Q** (Questionable) → 20 customers

### Step 2: Count Each Category

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 30 \\
M &= 40 + 30 = 70 \\
I &= 80 \\
Q &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Identify Dominant Category

The category with the **highest frequency** is **M (Must-be) = 70 customers**.

### Step 4: Engineering Implication

> **Conclusion:** *"Auto-save every 30 seconds"* is a **Must-be requirement**. Customers will be **severely dissatisfied** if absent, but offering it will not delight them — it's a hygiene factor. The firm must **guarantee** this feature as a baseline before investing in attractive features.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VOC-to-CTQ Translation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw VOC Data Collection] --> B[Customer Segmentation]
    B --> C[Multi-Channel Listening Posts]
    C --> D1[Surveys]
    C --> D2[Interviews]
    C --> D3[Focus Groups]
    C --> D4[CRM Complaint Logs]
    C --> D5[Social Media Mining]
    D1 --> E[Affinity Diagram Clustering]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Thematic VOC Categories]
    F --> G[VOC/CTQ Tree Construction]
    G --> H1[Must-be CTQs]
    G --> H2[One-Dimensional CTQs]
    G --> H3[Attractive CTQs]
    H1 --> I[Pareto Prioritization]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Final CTQ Selection for DMAIC]
    J --> K[Project Charter & SIPOC]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This block-level functional flow maps how unstructured customer feedback is progressively refined into a measurable, prioritized set of project drivers. Each stage acts as a *filter*, increasing specificity and reducing ambiguity.

---

## 4.2 Kano Model — Customer Satisfaction Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A[Customer Satisfaction Spectrum]
        direction LR
        K1[Basic / Must-be]
        K2[Performance / One-Dimensional]
        K3[Excitement / Attractive]
    end

    A1[Dissatisfaction Zone] --> K1
    K1 --> A2[Neutral Baseline]
    K2 --> A3[Proportional Satisfaction]
    K3 --> A4[Wow Factor Zone]

    K1 -.->|Hygiene Factor| Note1[Must always be present to avoid complaints]
    K2 -.->|Linear Driver| Note2[More is better; competitive parity metric]
    K3 -.->|Innovation Lever| Note3[Delighters; competitive differentiation]

    style A1 fill:#ffcccc
    style A2 fill:#ffffcc
    style A3 fill:#ccffcc
    style A4 fill:#ccccff
    style K1 fill:#ff9999
    style K2 fill:#99ff99
    style K3 fill:#9999ff
```

> **Visual Reading Guide:** The color gradient from red (dissatisfaction) to blue (delight) maps directly to the customer's emotional state as feature fulfillment moves from *must-be* → *one-dimensional* → *attractive*.

---

## 4.3 SIPOC + VOC Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S[Suppliers]
        S1[Material Vendors]
        S2[Information Sources]
        S3[Regulatory Bodies]
    end

    subgraph I[Inputs]
        I1[Raw Materials]
        I2[VOC Data Streams]
        I3[Process Specifications]
    end

    subgraph P[Process]
        P1[Receive VOC]
        P2[CTQ Translation]
        P3[Statistical Analysis]
        P4[Improvement Actions]
    end

    subgraph O[Outputs]
        O1[Improved Product]
        O2[VOC Reports]
        O3[KPI Dashboards]
    end

    subgraph C[Customers]
        C1[End Users]
        C2[Internal Users]
        C3[Regulators]
    end

    S --> I
    I --> P
    P --> O
    O --> C
    C -.Feedback Loop.-> I2
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The dashed feedback line from **Customers → VOC Data Streams** represents the **closed-loop nature** of Six Sigma — every customer experience becomes *input* for the next DMAIC cycle, ensuring continuous improvement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Voice of Customer (VOC). List any four techniques used for capturing VOC data.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Voice of Customer (VOC) is the structured process of gathering customer's stated, unstated, and anticipated needs, expectations, and preferences regarding a product or service, and translating them into measurable Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) requirements for process improvement in Six Sigma.

**Four VOC Capture Techniques (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Surveys / Questionnaires** — Quantitative data collection using Likert scales, NPS, CSAT.
2. **In-depth Interviews** — Qualitative, one-on-one exploration of customer pain points.
3. **Focus Groups** — Interactive group sessions for idea generation and concept validation.
4. **Observational Studies (Gemba Walks)** — Direct observation of customer behavior in real environments.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Complaint log mining, Social media sentiment analysis, Voice of Process workshops.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between VOC and VOB with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 Marks):**

| Aspect | VOC (Voice of Customer) | VOB (Voice of Business) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | External & internal customers | Organization's strategy, finance, operations |
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction, loyalty, experience | Profitability, market share, scalability |
| **Metric Type** | CSAT, NPS, CES | ROI, CAGR, market share % |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-to-medium term feedback | Long-term strategic goals |
| **Example** | *"Reduce delivery time to 24 hours"* (customer) | *"Reduce logistics cost by 12% in FY25"* (business) |

> **Synthesis:** A Six Sigma project must reconcile VOC and VOB — the *intersection* of customer delight and business viability defines the **project sweet spot**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Module pattern requires **internal choice** in Part B. Two alternative 14-mark questions are provided below — examiners may set either.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Kano Model** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe its **five categories** of customer requirements and how they relate to customer satisfaction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hospital surveyed **400 patients** to assess satisfaction. On a 1–10 scale:
- **180 patients** rated 9 or 10
- **120 patients** rated 7 or 8
- **100 patients** rated below 7

Calculate the **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** and comment on its significance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**1. Kano Model Definition (1 Mark):**
The Kano Model, developed by Professor Noriaki Kano in 1984, is a theory of product development and customer satisfaction that classifies customer preferences into distinct categories based on how their presence or absence affects satisfaction.

**2. Five Categories (4 Marks — 1 each with example):**

| Category | Effect of Presence | Effect of Absence | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Must-be (Basic)** | No delight | Severe dissatisfaction | Hospital hygiene; clean drinking water |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | Increases satisfaction proportionally | Decreases satisfaction proportionally | Hospital room cleanliness; doctor expertise |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | Strong delight | No dissatisfaction | Free Wi-Fi for patients; complimentary meals |
| **Indifferent** | No effect | No effect | Color of hospital ceiling tiles |
| **Reverse** | Dissatisfaction | Satisfaction | Religious symbols in a multi-faith ward |

**3. Kano Satisfaction Curve (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Satisfaction}(F) &= f_{M}(F) + f_{O}(F) + f_{A}(F) \\
\text{where } f_{M} &\approx 0 \text{ (must-be plateaus quickly)} \\
f_{O} &\propto F \text{ (linear)} \\
f_{A} &\to \infty \text{ asymptotically} \text{ (excitement surges)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Feature Fulfillment F] --> Y[Customer Satisfaction S]
    Y1[Must-be: Flat then Plateau] --> Y
    Y2[One-Dimensional: Linear Growth] --> Y
    Y3[Attractive: Exponential Surprise] --> Y

    style Y1 fill:#ff9999
    style Y2 fill:#99ff99
    style Y3 fill:#9999ff
```

**Conclusion (within 7 marks):** The Kano Model guides Six Sigma teams to *first* secure all Must-be requirements (eliminate dissatisfaction), *then* optimize One-dimensional drivers, and *finally* invest in Attractive features for competitive differentiation.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1: Calculate Percentages**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Promoters} &= \frac{180}{400} \times 100 = 45\% \\
\%\text{Detractors} &= \frac{100}{400} \times 100 = 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply NPS Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} \\
&= 45\% - 25\% \\
&= +20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Significance Commentary (3 Marks):**

- An NPS of **$+20$** is **acceptable but below top-quartile benchmarks** (industry leaders score $+50$ or higher).
- **45% Promoters** indicate strong loyalty in nearly half the patient base.
- **25% Detractors** signal an urgent need for **service recovery interventions** — root cause analysis of complaints is warranted.
- **30% Passives** represent the **conversion opportunity** — targeted engagement (post-discharge follow-ups, satisfaction surveys) could shift them into Promoters, potentially raising NPS to $+50$.

> **Conclusion:** The hospital should launch a Six Sigma DMAIC project focused on the top 3 complaint categories driving Detractor sentiment, with a target NPS of $\geq +40$ within 2 quarters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE)

**(a)** What is a **VOC/CTQ Tree**? Construct a hypothetical VOC/CTQ tree for an **online food delivery app** based on the customer need: *"I want my food delivered hot and fresh."* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **five listening posts** used in VOC data collection, with one strength and one limitation of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A **VOC/CTQ Tree** is a hierarchical decomposition tool that converts broad, qualitative customer statements (VOC) into specific, measurable, actionable process-level requirements (CTQs). It has three levels:
- **Level 1 — VOC:** The original customer need.
- **Level 2 — CTQ Drivers:** Mid-level categories that influence the VOC.
- **Level 3 — CTQ Metrics:** Quantitative, measurable specifications.

**Hypothetical Tree (3 Marks):**

```
VOC: "I want my food delivered hot and fresh"
│
├── CTQ Driver 1: Delivery Speed
│   ├── CTQ₁: Average delivery time ≤ 30 minutes
│   ├── CTQ₂: 95th percentile delivery time ≤ 45 minutes
│   └── CTQ₃: On-time delivery rate ≥ 95%
│
├── CTQ Driver 2: Food Temperature on Arrival
│   ├── CTQ₄: Food temperature ≥ 60°C on delivery
│   ├── CTQ₅: Use of insulated delivery bags (100% compliance)
│   └── CTQ₆: Maximum travel time from kitchen to customer ≤ 25 min
│
└── CTQ Driver 3: Food Quality Preservation
    ├── CTQ₇: Packaging integrity score ≥ 9/10
    ├── CTQ₈: Spillage complaint rate ≤ 1%
    └── CTQ₉: Customer freshness rating ≥ 4.5/5
```

**Engineering Rationale (2 Marks):**
Each CTQ must satisfy the **SMART** criteria — **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound. The tree enables the DMAIC team to map each CTQ to a process input that can be statistically controlled.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

| # | Listening Post | Description | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Surveys** | Structured questionnaires (Likert, NPS) | Large sample, statistical validity | Low response rates; superficial |
| 2 | **In-depth Interviews** | One-on-one conversational deep dives | Rich qualitative insights | Time-intensive; small N |
| 3 | **Focus Groups** | 6–10 participants, moderated discussion | Interactive idea generation | Groupthink; dominant voices |
| 4 | **Observational Studies** | Direct watching of customer behavior | Captures unstated needs | Hawthorne effect; observer bias |
| 5 | **Complaint / CRM Data** | Mining existing feedback logs | Authentic, reactive truth | Negative bias (silent majority) |

> **Synthesis:** A robust VOC strategy **combines at least three listening posts** to triangulate truth — quantitative surveys for breadth, interviews for depth, and observations for unstated behavior.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in VOC Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating VOC with VOB** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you use them interchangeably. Always clarify *who* is speaking.
> 2. **Skipping the Kano Table** — When asked to classify, you MUST show the **functional × dysfunctional** matrix mapping, not just state the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting SMART CTQs** — A VOC/CTQ tree with vague CTQs (e.g., *"fast delivery"*) loses 2 marks. Always quantify.
> 4. **Missing Passives in NPS** — Many students incorrectly include Passives in Promoter %. They are excluded by definition.
> 5. **No Actionable Recommendation** — A 14-mark answer without a *next step* (DMAIC suggestion, control plan hint) loses the final 1–2 marks allocated to "Application."

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Voice of Customer:**

### 🎯 Core Concepts
- ✅ **VOC** = customer's stated + unstated + anticipated needs, captured systematically.
- ✅ **VOB** = business's strategic/financial/operational voice.
- ✅ **CTQ** = Critical-to-Quality — measurable driver derived from VOC.
- ✅ **VOC/CTQ Tree** = 3-level hierarchical decomposition tool.
- ✅ **Affected Customers** = direct, indirect, end-users.
- ✅ **SIPOC** = Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers — VOC feeds its *Customers* and *Outputs* blocks.

### 🎯 Kano Model Categories (Mnemonic: **"MBA-IR"**)
- ✅ **M**ust-be (Basic) — absence causes severe dissatisfaction.
- ✅ **B**asic = same as Must-be.
- ✅ **A**ttractive (Excitement) — presence causes delight.
- ✅ **I**ndifferent — no effect either way.
- ✅ **R**everse — presence causes dissatisfaction.

### 🎯 Quantitative Metrics
- ✅ **NPS** = %Promoters $-$ %Detractors (range: $-100$ to $+100$).
- ✅ **CSAT** = $\dfrac{\sum R_i}{N \cdot R_{\max}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **CES** = average effort score (lower is better).
- ✅ **Cronbach's α** $\geq 0.7$ acceptable, $\geq 0.9$ excellent.

### 🎯 VOC Capture Techniques (5+2 Formula)
- ✅ **Surveys** (quantitative breadth)
- ✅ **Interviews** (qualitative depth)
- ✅ **Focus Groups** (interactive ideation)
- ✅ **Observations** (unstated behavior)
- ✅ **Complaint/CRM mining** (reactive authenticity)
- ➕ **Social media sentiment** (real-time pulse)
- ➕ **Voice of Process** (internal alignment)

### 🎯 DMAIC Placement
- ✅ VOC belongs to the **Define Phase** of DMAIC.
- ✅ It feeds into the **Project Charter**, **SIPOC**, and **CTQ selection**.
- ✅ A VOC-driven project charter is **5× more likely** to achieve measurable ROI.

### 🎯 Common Exam Hooks
- ✅ *"Convert VOC into CTQ"* — always use a tree.
- ✅ *"Classify the requirement"* — always use Kano's 5-category framework.
- ✅ *"Calculate NPS/CSAT"* — show all intermediate percentages.
- ✅ *"Compare VOC and VOB"* — use a 5-row comparison table.

> 🏁 **Final Note:** Mastering VOC is the *gateway* to the entire DMAIC methodology — every Six Sigma project's success is rooted in how well you listened to the customer before measuring anything.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, customer-driven, cross-functional planning and product development methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific, measurable **technical engineering requirements** at every stage of product or service design, production, and delivery. It is the systematic translation of *what the customer wants* into *how the organization will deliver it*.

The term was coined by **Dr. Yoji Akao** in **1972** at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Japan, and was popularized in the West by **John Hauser and Don Clausing** in their landmark 1988 *Harvard Business Review* article *"The House of Quality."*

Mathematically, the philosophy of QFD can be expressed as:

$$\text{QFD} = f(\text{VOC}, \text{Technical Requirements}, \text{Relationships}, \text{Benchmarks}, \text{Trade-offs})$$

Where the central deliverable is the **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a visual planning matrix that maps customer desires against engineering characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of QFD as a **translator in a foreign restaurant**.
>
> - The **Customer (Diner)** speaks *Cuisine-Language*: "I want spicy, hot, creamy, and cheap food."
> - The **Chef (Engineer)** speaks *Kitchen-Language*: oil temperature, spice concentration, simmering time, ingredient cost.
> - The **QFD (Translator/Waiter)** converts the customer's fuzzy desires into the chef's exact technical specifications.
> - Without the translator, the chef might deliver a $5 gourmet dish when the customer wanted a $2 spicy snack — **waste, rework, and customer dissatisfaction**.
> - The **House of Quality** is the translator's notebook that records every conversation and ensures nothing is lost in translation.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU Standard)

| Term | Full Form | Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **VOC** | Voice of the Customer | Raw customer statements, complaints, surveys |
| **Whats** | Customer Requirements (CRs) | What the customer wants (e.g., "comfortable seat") |
| **Hows** | Technical Requirements (TRs) | Engineering metrics (e.g., "seat foam density = 45 kg/m³") |
| **HOQ** | House of Quality | The master planning matrix |
| **Roof** | Correlation Matrix | Shows how Hows interact (synergies/conflicts) |
| **CTCs** | Critical-to-Customer | Highest priority Whats |
| **CTQs** | Critical-to-Quality | Highest priority Hows |
| **Benchmarking** | Competitive Comparison | Performance vs. competitors |

## 1.4 Why QFD Matters in Six Sigma (DMAIC & DMADV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) framework, QFD is primarily deployed in the **Define** and **Measure** phases to ensure the project scope is grounded in genuine customer needs. In **DMADV** (Design for Six Sigma), QFD becomes the *backbone of the Design phase*, guiding new product/service development from concept to production.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Houses of QFD (Cascading Deployment)

QFD is not a single matrix — it is a **cascade of four interconnected houses**, each translating the output of the previous one:

1. **House 1 — Product Planning:** Customer Requirements → Technical Requirements
2. **House 2 — Parts Deployment:** Technical Requirements → Part Characteristics
3. **House 3 — Process Planning:** Part Characteristics → Manufacturing Operations
4. **House 4 — Production Planning:** Manufacturing Operations → Production Controls

> [!NOTE]
> KTU focus: **House 1 (Product Planning / The Classic House of Quality)** carries the maximum weight in board examinations.

## 2.2 The Six Logical Construction Steps of the House of Quality

1. **Identify the Whats (Customer Requirements):** Gather VOC through interviews, surveys, focus groups, complaint logs, and Kano model analysis.
2. **Identify the Hows (Technical Requirements):** Translate customer language into measurable engineering metrics chosen by the cross-functional team.
3. **Build the Relationship Matrix:** A central matrix quantifying the strength of the link between each What and each How using standard symbols: **Strong = 9, Medium = 3, Weak = 1, None = 0**.
4. **Build the Roof (Correlation Matrix):** A triangular matrix above the Hows showing whether engineering requirements *support* each other (positive correlation) or *conflict* with each other (negative correlation).
5. **Assign Customer Importance Ratings (CIR):** Rate each What on a 1–5 scale based on customer surveys.
6. **Compute Technical Importance Ratings & Target Values:** Calculate weighted scores and set engineering targets.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam favorites**. Memorize the symbols, not just the equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Marks |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $$\text{Raw Weight}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{ij} \times W_i$$ | Weighted score of How-j; $R_{ij}$ = relationship symbol, $W_i$ = customer weight | 3–4 |
| 2 | $$\text{Normalized Importance}_j = \frac{\text{Raw Weight}_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{Raw Weight}_j} \times 100$$ | Percentage priority of each How | 3 |
| 3 | $$\text{Target Value} = f(\text{Benchmark, Direction, Goal})$$ | Engineering target setting using competitive benchmarks | 2 |
| 4 | $$\text{Sales Point} = \text{Importance} \times \text{Competitive Advantage}$$ | Used in prioritizing Whats | 2 |
| 5 | $$\text{Row Sum} = \sum (\text{Strong} \times 9) + \sum (\text{Medium} \times 3) + \sum (\text{Weak} \times 1)$$ | Row-wise weighted aggregation | 4 |
| 6 | $$\text{Technical Correlation Direction} = \begin{cases} \uparrow & \text{Positive} \\ \downarrow & \text{Negative} \end{cases}$$ | Direction symbol for Hows in the roof | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` for absolute-value or conditional notation. In KTU exam markdown rendering, the `|` symbol breaks table syntax — always use the LaTeX vertical bar alternatives.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Toyota, Ford, and BMW use HOQ to align vehicle features (fuel efficiency, comfort, safety) with engineering specs (engine CC, suspension damping, airbag sensors).
- **Software Industry:** Translates user stories (Whats) into system architecture and code modules (Hows) in Agile-QFD hybrid frameworks.
- **Healthcare:** Maps patient experience requirements (low waiting time, clean facilities) into clinical workflow parameters.
- **Manufacturing Six Sigma:** Anchors the **CTQ tree** in the Define phase of DMAIC, ensuring project Y metrics are genuinely customer-valued.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (House of Quality Worked Example)

## 3.1 The House of Quality (HOQ) — Component Map

The HOQ is built from **eight interconnected blocks**, derived as follows:

| Block | Position | Content |
|-------|----------|---------|
| A | Left Wall | Customer Requirements (Whats) + Importance Ratings |
| B | Ceiling | Technical Requirements (Hows) + Target Values |
| C | Central Matrix | Relationship Matrix (Whats × Hows) |
| D | Roof | Correlation Matrix (Hows × Hows) |
| E | Right Wall | Competitive Benchmarking (Customer perception) |
| F | Floor | Technical Importance Ratings + Sales Points |
| G | Foundation | Technical Targets / Specifications |
| H | Weight columns | Derived importance, normalized scores |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A laptop manufacturer is building a House of Quality with **3 Customer Requirements (Whats)** and **3 Technical Requirements (Hows)**. The cross-functional team has collected the following data.

**Customer Requirements (Whats):**
- W1: Long Battery Life
- W2: Lightweight
- W3: Low Cost

**Technical Requirements (Hows):**
- H1: Battery Capacity (Wh)
- H2: Weight of Laptop (kg)
- H3: Production Cost (₹)

**Customer Importance Weights (1–5 scale, higher = more important):**
- $W_1 = 5$ (Battery)
- $W_2 = 4$ (Weight)
- $W_3 = 5$ (Cost)

**Relationship Matrix (Symbols & Numerical Weights: Strong = 9, Medium = 3, Weak = 1):**

| What \ How | H1 (Battery) | H2 (Weight) | H3 (Cost) |
|------------|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| **W1: Battery Life** | **Strong (9)** | Medium (3) | Weak (1) |
| **W2: Lightweight** | Medium (3) | **Strong (9)** | Weak (1) |
| **W3: Low Cost** | Weak (1) | Medium (3) | **Strong (9)** |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Technical Importance Ratings

### Step 1: Compute the Raw Weight of each How (column-wise weighted sum)

**Raw Weight of H1 (Battery Capacity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawWeight}_{H1} &= (W_1 \times R_{1,1}) + (W_2 \times R_{2,1}) + (W_3 \times R_{3,1}) \\
&= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 3) + (5 \times 1) \\
&= 45 + 12 + 5 \\
&= 62
\end{aligned}
$$

**Raw Weight of H2 (Weight of Laptop):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawWeight}_{H2} &= (W_1 \times R_{1,2}) + (W_2 \times R_{2,2}) + (W_3 \times R_{3,2}) \\
&= (5 \times 3) + (4 \times 9) + (5 \times 3) \\
&= 15 + 36 + 15 \\
&= 66
\end{aligned}
$$

**Raw Weight of H3 (Production Cost):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawWeight}_{H3} &= (W_1 \times R_{1,3}) + (W_2 \times R_{2,3}) + (W_3 \times R_{3,3}) \\
&= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 1) + (5 \times 9) \\
&= 5 + 4 + 45 \\
&= 54
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Total Raw Weight (Denominator)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Raw Weight} &= \text{RawWeight}_{H1} + \text{RawWeight}_{H2} + \text{RawWeight}_{H3} \\
&= 62 + 66 + 54 \\
&= 182
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Normalized Importance (Percentage)

**Normalized Importance of H1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Norm}_{H1} &= \left(\frac{62}{182}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 0.3407 \times 100 \\
&= 34.07\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Normalized Importance of H2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Norm}_{H2} &= \left(\frac{66}{182}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 0.3626 \times 100 \\
&= 36.26\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Normalized Importance of H3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Norm}_{H3} &= \left(\frac{54}{182}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 0.2967 \times 100 \\
&= 29.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Verification and Engineering Interpretation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum Check: } 34.07 + 36.26 + 29.67 &= 100.00 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** Since H2 (Weight) carries the highest normalized importance (36.26%), the design team must prioritize lightweight material selection. However, the **Roof Correlation Matrix** (next section) must be consulted to ensure that improving H2 does not negatively impact H1 or H3.

## 3.5 The Roof (Correlation Matrix) — Construction Logic

The roof of the HOQ uses **symbols** to show inter-How relationships:

| Symbol | Meaning | Numerical Value (optional) |
|--------|---------|---------------------------|
| **⊕ Strong Positive** | Increasing one How strongly increases the other | +9 |
| **○ Positive** | Mild positive correlation | +3 |
| **Blank** | Independent | 0 |
| **△ Negative** | Mild trade-off | −3 |
| **⊗ Strong Negative** | Severe conflict (red flag) | −9 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A student who draws the roof but **fails to interpret the negative correlations in design trade-offs** loses 2–3 marks. Always state *what the team must do* when a conflict is identified (e.g., "redesign chassis using carbon-fiber composite to reduce weight without reducing battery capacity").

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (House of Quality Visual Architecture)

## 4.1 The House of Quality — Master Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CUSTOMER REQUIREMENTS - WHATS - Left Wall"]:::wall
    B["TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS - HOWS - Ceiling"]:::ceiling
    C["RELATIONSHIP MATRIX - Strong 9 Medium 3 Weak 1 - Central"]:::matrix
    D["ROOF - CORRELATION MATRIX - How vs How - Triangular"]:::roof
    E["COMPETITIVE BENCHMARKING - Right Wall"]:::bench
    F["TECHNICAL IMPORTANCE RATINGS - Floor"]:::floor
    G["TECHNICAL TARGET VALUES - Foundation"]:::target

    A --> C
    B --> C
    B --> D
    C --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    C --> G

    classDef wall fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ceiling fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef matrix fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef roof fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bench fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef floor fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 The Four-Step Construction Process — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: GATHER VOC - Customer Surveys and Interviews"]:::step
    S2["STEP 2: LIST WHATS - 3 to 8 Customer Requirements"]:::step
    S3["STEP 3: LIST HOWS - 4 to 12 Engineering Metrics"]:::step
    S4["STEP 4: BUILD RELATIONSHIP AND ROOF MATRICES"]:::step
    S5["STEP 5: COMPUTE WEIGHTS - Importance Ratings"]:::step
    S6["STEP 6: SET TARGETS - Engineering Specifications"]:::step
    S7["STEP 7: BENCHMARK - Compare to Competitors"]:::step
    S8["STEP 8: INTERPRET AND DEPLOY TO NEXT HOUSE"]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8

    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 QFD Cascading Four-House Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1["HOUSE 1: PRODUCT PLANNING - Customer to Technical Reqs"]:::h1
    H2["HOUSE 2: PARTS DEPLOYMENT - Technical to Part Chars"]:::h2
    H3["HOUSE 3: PROCESS PLANNING - Parts to Operations"]:::h3
    H4["HOUSE 4: PRODUCTION PLANNING - Operations to Controls"]:::h4

    H1 -->|"Critical to Quality"| H2
    H2 -->|"Critical to Process"| H3
    H3 -->|"Critical to Operator"| H4

    classDef h1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef h2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef h3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef h4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Worked Laptop Example — Visualizing the HOQ Result

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W1["W1: Battery Life - Weight 5"]:::what
    W2["W2: Lightweight - Weight 4"]:::what
    W3["W3: Low Cost - Weight 5"]:::what

    H1["H1: Battery Wh - Norm 34.07 percent"]:::how
    H2["H2: Weight kg - Norm 36.26 percent"]:::how
    H3["H3: Cost Rs - Norm 29.67 percent"]:::how

    W1 -->|"Strong 9"| H1
    W1 -->|"Medium 3"| H2
    W1 -->|"Weak 1"| H3
    W2 -->|"Medium 3"| H1
    W2 -->|"Strong 9"| H2
    W2 -->|"Weak 1"| H3
    W3 -->|"Weak 1"| H1
    W3 -->|"Medium 3"| H2
    W3 -->|"Strong 9"| H3

    H2 -.->|"Negative Correlation - Tradeoff"| H1
    H1 -.->|"Positive Correlation - Synergy"| H3

    classDef what fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef how fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). List any four benefits of QFD.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** QFD is a customer-driven planning methodology that translates the Voice of the Customer (VOC) into specific technical engineering requirements using a structured matrix called the House of Quality (HOQ).

**Benefits (1.5 marks — any four):**
1. Ensures customer focus throughout the design process.
2. Reduces late-stage design changes and rework costs.
3. Improves cross-functional communication among marketing, design, and production.
4. Shortens product development cycle time.
5. Provides documentation of design decisions and trade-offs.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is the "House of Quality"? Name its six main components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The House of Quality (HOQ) is a visual planning matrix that maps customer requirements (Whats) against technical engineering requirements (Hows) to ensure product design is customer-driven.

**Six Main Components (2 marks — name any six):**
1. Customer Requirements (Whats) — Left Wall
2. Technical Requirements (Hows) — Ceiling
3. Relationship Matrix — Center
4. Correlation Matrix (Roof) — Top
5. Competitive Benchmarking — Right Wall
6. Technical Importance Ratings and Targets — Floor

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for constructing a House of Quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A scooter manufacturer identifies four customer requirements and three technical requirements with the following data. Construct the House of Quality and compute the normalized importance of each technical requirement. **(7 marks)**

| Customer Req. (Whats) | Importance | How1: Engine CC | How2: Fuel Efficiency | How3: Price ₹ |
|----------------------|------------|-----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| W1: High Pickup | 5 | Strong (9) | Weak (1) | Medium (3) |
| W2: Good Mileage | 4 | Weak (1) | Strong (9) | Weak (1) |
| W3: Low Noise | 3 | Medium (3) | Weak (1) | Weak (1) |
| W4: Affordable | 5 | Weak (1) | Weak (1) | Strong (9) |

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — HOQ Construction Steps (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify Customer Requirements (Whats):** Gather VOC using surveys, interviews, complaint analysis, and Kano model. List 5–8 prioritized Whats on the left wall with importance ratings on a 1–5 scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 (1 mark) — Identify Technical Requirements (Hows):** Cross-functional team (engineering, manufacturing, quality) translates customer language into 6–12 measurable engineering metrics, placed on the ceiling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Build the Relationship Matrix:** For each What–How pair, assign symbol: **Strong = 9, Medium = 3, Weak = 1, None = 0**. Strong is denoted by a solid circle, medium by a half-circle, weak by an open triangle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 (1 mark) — Build the Roof (Correlation Matrix):** Triangular matrix above the Hows indicating whether engineering requirements are synergistic (positive) or conflicting (negative). Use ⊕, ○, △, ⊗ symbols.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 (1 mark) — Competitive Benchmarking:** On the right wall, rate the company's product vs. competitors on each customer requirement (1–5 scale), and identify the technical performance gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 6 (1 mark) — Compute Technical Importance Ratings & Targets:** Multiply importance by relationship symbols, sum columns, normalize to percentages, and set engineering target values for the highest-priority Hows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 7 (0.5 marks) — Interpret and Deploy:** Discuss trade-offs from the roof, finalize targets, and feed outputs into House 2 (Parts Deployment).

**Part (b) — Normalized Importance Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Raw Weight for each How**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawWeight}_{H1} &= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 3) + (5 \times 1) \\
&= 45 + 4 + 9 + 5 = 63
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawWeight}_{H2} &= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 1) + (5 \times 1) \\
&= 5 + 36 + 3 + 5 = 49
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawWeight}_{H3} &= (5 \times 3) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 1) + (5 \times 9) \\
&= 15 + 4 + 3 + 45 = 67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Raw Weight**

$$
\text{Total} = 63 + 49 + 67 = 179
$$

**Step 3: Normalized Importance (%):**

$$
\text{Norm}_{H1} = \left(\frac{63}{179}\right) \times 100 = 35.20\%
$$

$$
\text{Norm}_{H2} = \left(\frac{49}{179}\right) \times 100 = 27.37\%
$$

$$
\text{Norm}_{H3} = \left(\frac{67}{179}\right) \times 100 = 37.43\%
$$

**Interpretation:** H3 (Price) has the highest priority (37.43%), followed by H1 (Engine CC) at 35.20%. The design team must prioritize cost-control measures and engine capacity optimization.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): 14 Marks**

**(a)** Discuss the role of QFD in the **DMAIC** and **DMADV** frameworks of Six Sigma. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the **Relationship Matrix** and the **Correlation Matrix (Roof)** of a House of Quality with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — QFD in DMAIC and DMADV (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **In DMAIC (3.5 marks):** QFD is deployed in the **Define** phase to capture VOC and identify the project's CTQs (Critical-to-Quality characteristics). In the **Measure** phase, QFD output guides the selection of measurable Y metrics. It ensures that the Six Sigma project is solving a problem the customer actually cares about, not an internally convenient metric.

> [!NOTE]
> **In DMADV (3.5 marks):** QFD becomes the **structural backbone** of the Design phase. The House of Quality is the *first* deliverable, translating VOC into a complete set of engineering specifications.
