DMADV cannot proceed without a well-built HOQ because it would otherwise design a product nobody wants. QFD in DMADV is proactive; in DMAIC it is diagnostic.

**Part (b) — Relationship vs. Correlation Matrix (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Relationship Matrix (3.5 marks) | Correlation Matrix / Roof (3.5 marks) |
|--------|--------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Links Whats to Hows (customer to engineering) | Links Hows to Hows (engineering trade-offs) |
| **Position** | Center of HOQ | Top/Roof of HOQ |
| **Symbols** | Strong (●), Medium (◐), Weak (○) | ⊕ Strong Positive, ○ Positive, △ Negative, ⊗ Strong Negative |
| **Example** | "Long battery life" is **Strongly** related to "Battery Wh" | Increasing "Battery Wh" is **Negatively** correlated with "Weight" |
| **Use** | Compute Technical Importance Ratings | Identify design conflicts and trade-offs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** The Relationship Matrix converts customer priorities into engineering priorities. The Roof (Correlation Matrix) protects engineering consistency by flagging conflicts between technical requirements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Roof (Correlation Matrix):** A House of Quality drawn without the roof is **incomplete** — expect −2 to −3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Symbol Values:** Using 5/3/1 instead of 9/3/1 is a frequent error. The **9/3/1 scale** is the KTU-accepted standard.
> 3. **No Normalization Check:** Always verify that the sum of normalized importance equals **100%**. If it doesn't, recompute the arithmetic.
> 4. **Missing Engineering Interpretation:** Board examiners award marks for the *conclusion* — "what design decision does the team take based on this House of Quality?" Merely computing numbers is worth only 50% of the question's marks.
> 5. **Confusing Relationship and Correlation Matrices:** Mixing up their purposes and positions costs 1–2 marks. Use the table format to keep them separate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density KTU Module 3 — QFD Revision Notes**

- **Origin:** Yoji Akao (1972), Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Japan. Popularized in the West by **Hauser & Clausing** (1988, HBR).
- **Core Tool:** **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a 6-block visual matrix.
- **Whats = Customer Requirements (CRs):** Soft, qualitative, emotional language.
- **Hows = Technical Requirements (TRs):** Hard, measurable engineering metrics.
- **Symbol Standard (Relationship Matrix):** **Strong = 9, Medium = 3, Weak = 1, None = 0** (KTU 2024 convention).
- **Roof Symbols:** **⊕ (Strong Positive), ○ (Positive), △ (Negative), ⊗ (Strong Negative)**.
- **Key Formula (must memorize):**
  $$\text{RawWeight}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_{ij}$$
  $$\text{Normalized}_j = \left(\frac{\text{RawWeight}_j}{\sum_j \text{RawWeight}_j}\right) \times 100$$
- **Cascade:** Four Houses — Product Planning → Parts Deployment → Process Planning → Production Planning.
- **QFD in Six Sigma:** Anchors **CTQ trees** in DMAIC-Define and is the **backbone of DMADV-Design**.
- **Benefits:** Customer focus, reduced rework, faster development, cross-functional alignment, documentation.
- **Limitations (often asked as a 3-mark question):** Time-consuming, requires cross-functional commitment, customer weights can be biased, complex for service industries.
- **Always normalize** the technical importance ratings to 100% as a verification check.
- **Always interpret** the roof conflicts as design trade-offs and propose engineering solutions.
- **Use `\vert` or `\mid`** for vertical bars in equations (not the `|` character) to preserve markdown table integrity.
- **KTU marks split for a typical HOQ question:** Construction steps = 4 marks, Matrix filling = 4 marks, Calculation = 4 marks, Interpretation = 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seven QC Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Seven QC Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Seven QC Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven QC Tools (The "Old Seven" / Basic Seven Tools of Quality)**
> The **Seven Quality Control (QC) Tools** are a set of fundamental graphical and statistical techniques identified by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1943 and popularized through the JUSE (Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers) training programs. They are the foundational, low-cost, easy-to-learn problem-solving instruments used by **all levels of an organization** — from shop-floor operators to senior management — to establish, maintain, and improve quality control systems. These tools form the bedrock of every DMAIC phase in Six Sigma and are universally applicable to **process monitoring, root cause analysis, and continuous improvement**.

The seven tools are:
1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa / Fishbone Diagram)
2. **Check Sheet** (Tally Sheet)
3. **Control Chart** (Shewhart Chart)
4. **Histogram**
5. **Pareto Chart**
6. **Scatter Diagram** (Scatter Plot)
7. **Flowchart** (Process Map)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Seven QC Tools are categorized as **Cause Analysis Tools** and **Data Analysis Tools**. For KTU exam purposes, students must be able to **construct, interpret, and select the appropriate tool** for a given quality scenario, with special emphasis on Cause-and-Effect, Pareto, and Control Charts (these carry the highest weightage in Part B questions).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Doctor's Toolkit for a Sick Process"**
> Imagine a patient (your manufacturing or service process) comes to a doctor (the Quality Engineer) complaining of fever (defects, scrap, customer complaints). Before prescribing medicine, the doctor uses seven basic instruments:
> - **Stethoscope (Control Chart)** → continuously monitors the patient's heartbeat (process stability).
> - **Thermometer (Histogram)** → measures the temperature distribution (variation of the process).
> - **Blood Test Report (Pareto Chart)** → identifies which disease (vital few causes) is responsible for **80%** of the symptoms.
> - **Symptom Checklist (Check Sheet)** → a structured form to count how often each symptom appears.
> - **X-Ray (Scatter Diagram)** → shows the relationship between two medical parameters (e.g., age vs. BP).
> - **Brainstorming Whiteboard (Cause-and-Effect Diagram)** → maps all possible root causes like bones of a fish.
> - **Patient Journey Map (Flowchart)** → traces every step from entry to discharge (process flow).
>
> Just as a General Physician rarely needs a sophisticated MRI to diagnose common ailments, a Six Sigma Green Belt can solve **80% of process problems** using only these seven basic tools before escalating to advanced statistical methods.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standards

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **UCL (Upper Control Limit)** | Upper boundary of acceptable process variation, typically set at $\mu + 3\sigma$. |
| **LCL (Lower Control Limit)** | Lower boundary of acceptable process variation, typically set at $\mu - 3\sigma$. |
| **Pareto Principle** | The **80/20 Rule**: roughly **80% of problems** are caused by **20% of the causes**. |
| **Specification Limits** | Customer-defined acceptable range, distinct from control limits. |
| **Special Cause Variation** | Variation due to external, identifiable, assignable factors. |
| **Common Cause Variation** | Inherent, random variation built into the process. |
| **Stratification** | Separating data into distinct categories (e.g., by shift, machine, operator). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Seven QC Tools

### 2.1.1 Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone)

- **Purpose:** Identify, explore, and graphically display all possible causes related to a problem (the *effect*).
- **Structure:** The "spine" represents the problem; "bones" branching off represent major cause categories.
- **Standard 4M + E + S Categories:** **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment** (and sometimes **Mother Nature**).
- **How it works:** Conduct a **brainstorming session**, write each cause on a branch, and ask "**Why does this happen?**" recursively (5-Why analysis).
- **Why it matters:** Helps teams move from *symptoms* to *root causes* without prematurely jumping to solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Construction Logic (KTU Board-Standard Steps):**
> 1. State the problem/effect clearly on the right side of the diagram.
> 2. Draw the main horizontal arrow (spine) pointing to the effect.
> 3. Draw diagonal branches for each major cause category.
> 4. Add sub-branches with specific causes identified through brainstorming.
> 5. Identify the most likely root causes for further investigation (use Pareto or FMEA to prioritize).

### 2.1.2 Check Sheet (Tally Sheet)

- **Purpose:** A structured, pre-formatted data collection form used to **record data systematically** and **observe patterns** as they occur.
- **Design Principle:** Simple enough to be filled in real-time by the data collector (often the operator).
- **Common Types:** Defect location check sheet, frequency distribution check sheet, cause-check sheet, checklist.
- **Utility:** Converts raw observations into quantitative data ready for **Pareto or Histogram** analysis.

### 2.1.3 Control Chart (Shewhart Chart)

- **Purpose:** Monitor a process over time to determine whether it is in a state of **statistical control**.
- **Components:** Center Line (CL), UCL, LCL, plotted data points, and **control limits** (NOT specification limits).
- **Types:** 
  - **Variables Charts** (for continuous data): $\bar{X}$-chart, R-chart, S-chart, I-chart (Individuals), MR-chart.
  - **Attributes Charts** (for discrete data): p-chart, np-chart, c-chart, u-chart.

### 2.1.4 Histogram

- **Purpose:** Display the **frequency distribution** of a set of continuous data, showing the shape, center, and spread (variation) of the process.
- **Construction:** Partition data range into equal **bins (classes)**, count frequency per bin, plot as adjacent bars.
- **Shapes:** Normal, bimodal, skewed (right/left), uniform, and **truncated** (clipped by spec limits — a major sign of tampering).

### 2.1.5 Pareto Chart

- **Purpose:** A **dual-axis combination chart** (bar + cumulative line) that ranks causes in descending order of frequency/cost.
- **Foundation:** Built on the **Pareto Principle** proposed by **Vilfredo Pareto** (Italian economist, 1897) — applied to quality by **Joseph Juran**.
- **Reading:** Bars = individual frequency; Curve = cumulative %; Causes to the left of the 80% mark = **"Vital Few"**; causes to the right = **"Useful Many"**.

### 2.1.6 Scatter Diagram

- **Purpose:** Plot paired $(x, y)$ data to **visualize the relationship** (correlation) between two variables.
- **Correlation Types:** Strong positive, weak positive, no correlation, strong negative, weak negative, curvilinear.
- **Caveat:** **Correlation does not imply causation.** Always confirm with experimental methods.

### 2.1.7 Flowchart

- **Purpose:** A graphical representation of a process showing **sequence of steps, decision points, and flow of inputs/outputs**.
- **Standard Symbols:** Oval (Start/End), Rectangle (Process step), Diamond (Decision), Parallelogram (Input/Output), Arrow (Flow line).
- **Variants:** Top-down flowchart, swim-lane flowchart, deployment flowchart.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Six Sigma Examinations** — memorize all symbols and units.

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Description / Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Center Line (Mean)** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}{n}$ | Average of sample measurements; used as CL on $\bar{X}$-chart. |
| **Sample Range** | $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Difference between largest and smallest observation in a subgroup. |
| **Average Range** | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k}$ | Mean of $k$ subgroup ranges. |
| **Sample Std. Deviation** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Measures within-subgroup spread. |
| **Overall Std. Deviation** | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$ | Estimated process standard deviation using $\bar{R}$. |
| **UCL ($\bar{X}$-chart)** | $UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for means chart. |
| **LCL ($\bar{X}$-chart)** | $LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit for means chart. |
| **UCL (R-chart)** | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for range chart. |
| **LCL (R-chart)** | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit for range chart. |
| **UCL (p-chart)** | $UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Upper limit for proportion nonconforming. |
| **LCL (p-chart)** | $LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Lower limit for proportion nonconforming. |
| **UCL (c-chart)** | $UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Upper limit for count of defects per unit. |
| **Pareto Cumulative %** | $\text{Cum. \%} = \dfrac{\sum f_i}{\sum f_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Cumulative percentage on Pareto curve. |
| **Bin Width (Histogram)** | $W = \dfrac{\text{Range}}{\text{No. of Bins}}$ | Width of each class interval. |
| **Sturges' Rule (Bins)** | $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$ | Suggested number of histogram bins for sample size $n$. |
| **Pearson Correlation** | $r = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Strength of linear relationship in Scatter Diagram. |

### 2.2.1 Standard Control Chart Constants Table (KTU Reference)

| Subgroup Size $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industry:** SPC (Statistical Process Control) on production lines using Control Charts; root cause analysis of defects using Ishikawa + Pareto.
- **Software Industry:** Defect tracking with Check Sheets; process flow mapping with Flowcharts in SDLC phases.
- **Healthcare:** Cause-and-Effect for patient safety incidents; Histograms for patient wait time analysis.
- **Banking/Service:** Scatter Diagrams correlating customer satisfaction vs. response time; Pareto for complaint analysis.
- **DMAIC Integration:** Check Sheet (Measure), Histogram + Pareto (Analyze), Control Chart (Control), Flowchart (Define/Improve), Scatter + Fishbone (Analyze).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Construction of an $\bar{X}$-R Control Chart

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Engineering Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> The following ten subgroups of size $n = 5$ have been collected from a CNC machining process (shaft diameter in mm). Construct the $\bar{X}$-R chart and determine if the process is in statistical control.

**Data Table:**

| Subgroup $i$ | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $X_5$ | Mean $\bar{X}_i$ | Range $R_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 50.0 | 50.2 | 49.8 | 50.1 | 49.9 | 50.00 | 0.4 |
| 2 | 50.3 | 49.7 | 50.0 | 50.2 | 49.8 | 50.00 | 0.6 |
| 3 | 49.9 | 50.1 | 50.2 | 50.0 | 49.8 | 50.00 | 0.4 |
| 4 | 50.2 | 50.0 | 49.9 | 50.1 | 50.3 | 50.10 | 0.4 |
| 5 | 49.8 | 50.0 | 49.9 | 50.2 | 50.1 | 50.00 | 0.4 |
| 6 | 50.0 | 49.8 | 50.1 | 49.9 | 50.2 | 50.00 | 0.4 |
| 7 | 50.1 | 50.3 | 50.0 | 50.2 | 49.9 | 50.10 | 0.4 |
| 8 | 49.9 | 50.0 | 50.2 | 50.1 | 49.8 | 50.00 | 0.4 |
| 9 | 50.2 | 49.8 | 50.0 | 49.9 | 50.1 | 50.00 | 0.4 |
| 10 | 50.0 | 50.1 | 49.9 | 50.2 | 49.8 | 50.00 | 0.4 |

### Step 1: Calculate Each Subgroup Mean and Range

For subgroup 1:

$$\bar{X}_1 = \frac{50.0 + 50.2 + 49.8 + 50.1 + 49.9}{5} = \frac{250.0}{5} = 50.00$$

$$R_1 = X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 50.2 - 49.8 = 0.4$$

All other subgroup means and ranges are calculated identically (shown in the data table above).

### Step 2: Calculate the Grand Mean and Average Range

The grand mean (center line of $\bar{X}$-chart) is the average of all subgroup means:

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i = \frac{50.00 + 50.00 + 50.00 + 50.10 + 50.00 + 50.00 + 50.10 + 50.00 + 50.00 + 50.00}{10}$$

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{500.20}{10} = 50.02 \text{ mm}$$

The average range (center line of R-chart) is the average of all subgroup ranges:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i = \frac{0.4 + 0.6 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4}{10}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{4.2}{10} = 0.42 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Identify Control Chart Constants for $n = 5$

From the KTU Standard Constants Table:

$$A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114$$

### Step 4: Compute Control Limits for the $\bar{X}$-Chart

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.02 + (0.577)(0.42)$$

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.02 + 0.24234 = 50.262 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 50.020 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50.02 - (0.577)(0.42)$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.02 - 0.24234 = 49.778 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Compute Control Limits for the R-Chart

$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.42) = 0.888 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_R = \bar{R} = 0.420 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.42) = 0.000 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6: Verification — Is the Process in Control?

Checking each subgroup:
- All $\bar{X}_i$ values lie between **49.778** and **50.262** ✓
- All $R_i$ values lie between **0.000** and **0.888** ✓ (Note: $R_2 = 0.6$ is within limits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since all points fall within the control limits and no **Western Electric Rules** (runs, trends) are violated, the process is **in statistical control**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tool Selection by DMAIC Phase

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Analytical Table — Memorize This for Direct 7-Mark Sub-Questions**

| DMAIC Phase | Primary QC Tool(s) Used | Purpose | Key Output |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D — Define** | Flowchart, Check Sheet | Map the process; collect initial customer data. | SIPOC, Process Map. |
| **M — Measure** | Check Sheet, Histogram | Quantify the problem; assess measurement system. | Baseline data, distribution shape. |
| **A — Analyze** | Pareto, Cause-and-Effect, Scatter | Find vital few causes; validate relationships. | Prioritized root causes. |
| **I — Improve** | Scatter, Cause-and-Effect, Flowchart | Test solutions; re-map improved process. | Optimized process. |
| **C — Control** | Control Chart, Flowchart | Sustain the gains; monitor stability. | SPC charts, control plan. |

## 3.3 Construction Sequence: Pareto Chart (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam-Standard Pareto Construction Algorithm:**

1. **Step A:** List all categories of problems (e.g., types of defects) and tally their frequencies.
2. **Step B:** Calculate the **cumulative frequency** for each category.
3. **Step C:** Calculate the **cumulative percentage**:

$$\text{Cum. \%}_i = \left( \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} \right) \times 100$$

4. **Step D:** Sort categories in **descending order of frequency**.
5. **Step E:** Plot bars (frequency on left Y-axis) and an **overlaid cumulative % curve** (right Y-axis, 0–100%).
6. **Step F:** Draw a reference line at **80%** — categories to the left of this line are the **"Vital Few"**; to the right are the **"Useful Many"**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Layout of an Ishikawa Diagram for "High Defect Rate in Welding"**

```mermaid
graph LR
    E[Effect: High Defect Rate in Welding] --> S{Spine Arrow}
    S --> M1[Man: Operator skill, Training]
    S --> M2[Machine: Welder calibration, Electrode wear]
    S --> M3[Material: Steel grade, Rust on plates]
    S --> M4[Method: Welding speed, Current setting]
    S --> M5[Measurement: Caliper accuracy, Inspector bias]
    S --> M6[Environment: Humidity, Lighting]
    M1 --> C11[Untrained welders]
    M1 --> C12[Fatigue from long shifts]
    M2 --> C21[Loose ground clamp]
    M2 --> C22[Old transformer]
    M3 --> C31[Improper storage]
    M3 --> C32[Wrong filler rod]
    M4 --> C41[High travel speed]
    M4 --> C42[Wrong amperage]
    M5 --> C51[Uncalibrated tools]
    M5 --> C52[No SOP]
    M6 --> C61[Damp floor]
    M6 --> C62[Poor ventilation]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Process Mapping (KTU Standard Symbols)

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Customer Complaint Resolution Process**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A([Start: Customer Complaint Received]) --> B[Log Complaint in System]
    B --> C{Complaint Valid?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject and Inform Customer]
    C -- Yes --> E[Categorize Issue Type]
    E --> F[Assign to Resolution Team]
    F --> G[Investigate Root Cause]
    G --> H[Implement Corrective Action]
    H --> I{Customer Satisfied?}
    I -- No --> J[Escalate to Manager]
    I -- Yes --> K[Close Complaint]
    J --> G
    D --> L([End])
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Control Chart Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A([Start: Subgroup Data Collected]) --> B[Compute Mean and Range]
    B --> C[Calculate Grand Mean X-double-bar and R-bar]
    C --> D[Select Constants A2, D3, D4 for n]
    D --> E[Compute UCL, LCL for X-bar chart]
    E --> F[Compute UCL, LCL for R chart]
    F --> G[Plot Data Points]
    G --> H{All Points Within Limits?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Process in Statistical Control]
    H -- No --> J[Investigate Special Cause]
    J --> K[Remove Assignable Cause]
    K --> L[Recompute Control Limits]
    I --> M([End: Begin SPC Monitoring])
    L --> G
```

## 4.4 Pareto Chart Construction Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Collect Defect Data] --> B[Categorize Defects]
    B --> C[Count Frequency per Category]
    C --> D[Sort in Descending Order]
    D --> E[Calculate Cumulative Percentage]
    E --> F[Plot Bar Chart: Frequency vs. Category]
    F --> G[Overlay Cumulative % Curve on Secondary Axis]
    G --> H[Draw 80% Reference Line]
    H --> I[Identify Vital Few vs Useful Many]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Seven QC Tools and briefly explain the purpose of the Cause-and-Effect Diagram.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, OEPET721]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Seven QC Tools** are a set of basic statistical and graphical techniques popularized by **Kaoru Ishikawa** for solving quality problems. They are: (1) Cause-and-Effect Diagram, (2) Check Sheet, (3) Control Chart, (4) Histogram, (5) Pareto Chart, (6) Scatter Diagram, and (7) Flowchart.

The **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone/Ishikawa Diagram)** is a brainstorming tool used to identify and graphically organize the **potential root causes** of a problem (effect). It categorizes causes under standard headings such as **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, and Environment (4M + E + S)** and helps teams systematically explore the relationship between an effect and its causes.

*(Award: 1 mark for listing 7 tools, 1 mark for Ishikawa context, 1 mark for Cause-and-Effect purpose.)*

### Question 2: State the Pareto Principle. What is the difference between "Vital Few" and "Useful Many"?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OEPET721]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, derived from the work of Italian economist **Vilfredo Pareto** and later applied to quality by **Joseph Juran**, states that approximately **80% of the problems** are caused by only **20% of the causes** (the Vital Few).

- **Vital Few:** The small number of causes (categories) that contribute to roughly **80%** of the total problem. These are placed on the **left side** of the Pareto chart and are the **priority targets** for corrective action.
- **Useful Many:** The larger number of remaining causes that collectively contribute the remaining **20%** of the problem. They appear on the **right side** of the Pareto chart and are typically addressed only after the Vital Few are resolved.

*(Award: 1 mark for principle + origin, 1 mark for Vital Few, 1 mark for Useful Many.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse Control Limits with Specification Limits** — they are NOT the same. Specification limits come from the customer; control limits come from the process.
> - Always **state the assumption of normality** before using $\bar{X}$-R charts; otherwise, use I-MR or attribute charts.
> - In Pareto questions, students forget to **sort the data in descending order** before plotting. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> - In Fishbone diagrams, students list **causes and solutions** together. Only causes belong on the bones.
> - Always **label axes with units** on Histograms, Pareto charts, and Control charts.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, OEPET721, Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Construction and Interpretation of a Pareto Chart** with a suitable engineering example. How does it help in prioritizing problems in a Six Sigma project? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Pareto Chart is a dual-axis combination chart consisting of a bar graph and a cumulative percentage curve, ranked in descending order. It applies the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** to separate the **"Vital Few"** causes from the **"Useful Many."**

**Engineering Example (3 marks):** Consider a CNC turning shop producing 500 defective parts in a week. The defects are categorized as:

| Defect Type | Frequency | Cumulative Frequency | Cumulative % |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Dimensional out-of-spec | 280 | 280 | 56% |
| Surface roughness | 100 | 380 | 76% |
| Tool chatter marks | 60 | 440 | 88% |
| Burrs on edges | 40 | 480 | 96% |
| Cosmetic scratches | 20 | 500 | 100% |
| **Total** | **500** | — | **100%** |

The chart shows: **Dimensional out-of-spec + Surface roughness = 76% of all defects** — these are the **Vital Few** to address first.

**Six Sigma Utility (3 marks):**
1. **Prioritization:** Focuses team effort on root causes yielding the highest defect reduction per unit of resource spent.
2. **DMAIC Application:** Used in the **Analyze** phase to narrow down from many potential causes to a manageable few.
3. **Decision Support:** Helps management allocate budget, time, and corrective action resources scientifically.
4. **Communication:** Provides a powerful, easily understood visual to communicate priorities to cross-functional teams.

---

**Q. (b)** What is a **Control Chart**? Explain the difference between **variables and attributes control charts**. With a suitable example, compute the control limits for an **$\bar{X}$-chart** given the following 5 subgroups of size $n = 4$ (assume $A_2 = 0.729$): Subgroup means: 50.1, 50.3, 49.9, 50.2, 50.0; Subgroup ranges: 0.4, 0.6, 0.3, 0.5, 0.4. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Control Chart (Shewhart Chart) is a graphical tool used to monitor a process over time, plotting sample statistics against **upper and lower control limits** (typically set at $\pm 3\sigma$) to detect the presence of **special cause variation**.

**Variables vs Attributes (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Variables Charts | Attributes Charts |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data Type | Continuous (measurable) | Discrete (countable) |
| Examples | $\bar{X}$-chart, R-chart, I-MR chart | p-chart, np-chart, c-chart, u-chart |
| Use | Monitor dimensions, weight, time | Monitor defects, pass/fail, errors |
| Information | More information per data point | Easier to collect |

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Calculate grand mean and average range.

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{50.1 + 50.3 + 49.9 + 50.2 + 50.0}{5} = \frac{250.5}{5} = 50.10$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.4 + 0.6 + 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.4}{5} = \frac{2.2}{5} = 0.44$$

Step 2: Calculate control limits using $A_2 = 0.729$.

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.10 + (0.729)(0.44) = 50.10 + 0.3208 = 50.42$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50.10 - (0.729)(0.44) = 50.10 - 0.3208 = 49.78$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = 50.10$$

**Conclusion:** All sample means lie within [49.78, 50.42]; hence, the process mean is in statistical control.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OEPET721, Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa) Diagram** in detail. Discuss the standard **4M + E + S** categories with at least two example causes under each category for the problem: **"Excessive Rejection Rate in Injection Moulding Process."** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition and Origin (2 marks):** The Cause-and-Effect Diagram, also called the **Fishbone Diagram** or **Ishikawa Diagram**, was developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** of the University of Tokyo in 1943. It is a structured brainstorming tool that graphically represents the relationship between a **problem (effect)** and its **potential causes**. The diagram resembles the skeleton of a fish, with the effect at the head and causes as bones branching off the spine.

**Purpose:** To systematically identify, organize, and prioritize potential root causes of a quality problem before jumping to solutions.

**4M + E + S Categories Applied to "Excessive Rejection Rate in Injection Moulding" (5 marks):**

| Category | Description | Example Causes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Man (Workforce)** | Operator skill, training, fatigue | (1) Inadequate operator training on machine settings. (2) Operator fatigue from prolonged shifts leading to errors. |
| **Machine (Equipment)** | Equipment condition, maintenance, calibration | (1) Worn-out screw and barrel in the injection unit. (2) Improper mold alignment causing flash. |
| **Material (Raw Input)** | Quality of raw material, storage, handling | (1) Moisture content too high in plastic granules. (2) Inconsistent batch quality from supplier. |
| **Method (Process)** | Process parameters, procedures, SOPs | (1) Incorrect injection pressure setting. (2) Inconsistent cooling time across cycles. |
| **Measurement (Inspection)** | Gauges, calibration, inspection methods | (1) Use of uncalibrated vernier calipers. (2) Inconsistent sampling plan by QC inspector. |
| **Environment (Surroundings)** | Ambient conditions, workplace layout | (1) High shop floor humidity affecting material. (2) Inadequate lighting at the inspection station. |

**Conclusion:** The Ishikawa diagram helps the Six Sigma team **brainstorm exhaustively** and ensures no major cause category is overlooked before deploying analytical tools like Pareto or hypothesis testing.

---

**Q. (b)** With a suitable sketch, explain the **Histogram** and discuss its various **distribution shapes**. How is the **bin width** calculated? Explain the difference between a **Histogram and a Pareto Chart**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Histogram is a bar-graph representation of the **frequency distribution** of continuous data, where data is grouped into adjacent, non-overlapping intervals (bins). It visualizes the **shape, center, and spread** of process variation.

**Distribution Shapes (3 marks):**
1. **Normal (Bell-shaped):** Symmetric, single peak, mean ≈ median ≈ mode — represents a stable process.
2. **Skewed Right (Positive Skew):** Tail extends to the right; common in defect sizes, lead times.
3. **Skewed Left (Negative Skew):** Tail extends to the left; common in strength-to-failure data.
4. **Bimodal:** Two peaks; indicates **mixing of two distinct populations** (e.g., two machines, two shifts).
5. **Uniform (Rectangular):** All bins equal; indicates tampered or over-controlled process.
6. **Truncated/Clipped:** Bars cut off at specification limits — signs of inspection rejection or tampering.

**Bin Width Calculation (2 marks):**

The **bin width** $W$ is given by:

$$W = \frac{\text{Range of Data}}{\text{Number of Bins}} = \frac{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}{k}$$

A commonly used formula for selecting the number of bins is **Sturges' Rule**:

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$$

where $n$ is the sample size.

**Histogram vs Pareto Chart (1 mark):**

| Feature | Histogram | Pareto Chart |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Purpose | Shows distribution of a single variable | Ranks causes by frequency/cost |
| Order of Bars | Ascending numerical (bin order) | Descending frequency order |
| Y-axis | Frequency only | Dual axis (frequency + cumulative %) |
| Application | Analyze variation | Prioritize problems |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and Pin to Your Wall!):**

- ⭐ The **Seven QC Tools** were developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** and are universally called the **"Old Seven"** or **"Basic Seven"** of quality.
- ⭐ **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone)** is purely a **brainstorming tool** — never put solutions on the bones, only causes.
- ⭐ The standard categories are **4M + E + S** = **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment**.
- ⭐ A **Check Sheet** is the **only tool used for live data collection**; all other tools analyze data.
- ⭐ **Control Chart ≠ Specification Limits.** Control limits are set at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$; spec limits come from the customer.
- ⭐ **Variables charts** (for continuous data) include $\bar{X}$, R, S, I, MR; **Attributes charts** (for discrete data) include p, np, c, u.
- ⭐ For $n = 5$, remember: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$.
- ⭐ A **Histogram** shows the **distribution shape**; a **Pareto chart** shows the **ranking of causes** with cumulative %.
- ⭐ The **Pareto Principle (80/20)** was discovered by **Vilfredo Pareto** and applied to quality by **Joseph Juran**.
- ⭐ A **Scatter Diagram** shows correlation but **never proves causation** — always validate with designed experiments (DOE).
- ⭐ A **Flowchart** uses standard symbols: **oval** (start/end), **rectangle** (process), **diamond** (decision), **parallelogram** (I/O).
- ⭐ The **DMAIC Mapping** is: **Define** = Flowchart; **Measure** = Check Sheet & Histogram; **Analyze** = Pareto, Fishbone, Scatter; **Improve** = Scatter, Flowchart; **Control** = Control Chart.
- ⭐ **Bimodal histograms** are a red flag — they indicate the process is **mixing two populations** and must be stratified.
- ⭐ In KTU Part B questions, **always draw** the diagram (Fishbone, Pareto, Control Chart) — verbal description without a sketch loses up to **3 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Process Control Key Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statistical Process Control Key Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is a data-driven, statistically rigorous methodology pioneered by **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** of Bell Laboratories for monitoring, controlling, and improving a manufacturing or business process over time by distinguishing between the natural variation inherent to the process (common cause) and the variation caused by external, assignable disturbances (special cause). It accomplishes this by collecting real-time process data through **subgroups** and plotting them against statistically computed **Upper Control Limits (UCL)** and **Lower Control Limits (LCL)**, typically set at **±3 standard deviations (3σ)** from the process mean, forming what is known as a **control chart**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** SPC is the foundational data-collection and diagnostic engine of the **DMAIC – Measure** phase. Without SPC, no Six Sigma project can correctly quantify the "Voice of the Process (VOP)."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital ICU Monitor"

Imagine a critically ill patient connected to a **cardiac monitor** in an ICU. The monitor has two horizontal lines: a green band (normal heart rate 60–100 bpm) and red lines at 50 and 110 bpm.

- **Common Cause Variation** = the patient's heart rate gently fluctuating between 75 and 85 bpm. This is *normal, inherent, expected*. No alarm. No doctor rushes in.
- **Special Cause Variation** = the heart rate suddenly jumps to 130 bpm or drops to 45 bpm. The monitor **beeps**. The nurse acts immediately. The patient is in distress.
- **Control Limits** = the red lines (50 and 110 bpm).
- **Specification Limits** = the absolute medical danger zone (e.g., 40 and 160 bpm) — if crossed, the patient dies.
- **Process Capability (Cpk)** = how well the green band fits *inside* the red lines.

**SPC is the "monitor" of your process.** It tells you when to act (special cause) and when to leave the process alone (common cause) — preventing **tampering**, which Dr. W. Edwards Deming identified as one of the greatest wastes in industry.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary Used in KTU Board Exams

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | KTU-Standard Symbol |
|------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Subgroup (Sample)** | Small snapshot of the process taken at a fixed interval | $n$ |
| **Center Line** | The grand average of all subgroup means | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ |
| **Control Limit** | Voice of the Process — natural boundary | UCL, LCL |
| **Specification Limit** | Voice of the Customer — design boundary | USL, LSL |
| **Common Cause** | Inherent, chronic, random noise | — |
| **Special Cause** | Sudden, acute, assignable disturbance | — |
| **Out-of-Control Point** | A data point beyond UCL/LCL | Rule 1 violation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Students:** "CL = Control Limit = Compute from data." "SL = Specification Limit = Set by customer/design."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Faces of Variation — Deming's Core Principle

Dr. Deming taught that **94% of problems** in any organization are due to the *system* (common cause), and only 6% are due to special causes. The Five-Step Decision Logic of SPC is:

1. **Plot** the subgroup statistic (mean, range, proportion) on a time-ordered chart.
2. **Compute** trial control limits from at least **20–25 preliminary subgroups** (Shewhart's minimum).
3. **Compare** each new point against the limits.
4. **Investigate** any point outside the limits (special cause) → find root cause → remove it.
5. **Re-establish** limits if the special cause is permanent and beneficial, otherwise keep limits and prevent recurrence.

## 2.2 The Two Families of Control Charts

- **Variable Charts (continuous data):** $\bar{X}$-R, $\bar{X}$-s, I-MR (Individuals & Moving Range), EWMA, CUSUM.
- **Attribute Charts (discrete data):** p-chart (defectives), np-chart, c-chart (defects/unit), u-chart (defects per unit area).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All constants ($A_2, D_3, D_4, d_2, E_2$) depend on subgroup size **n**. KTU board exams **love** giving a small numerical value table and asking for the limit computation.

### A. Variable Chart Limits

| Chart Type | Center Line | UCL | LCL |
|------------|-------------|-----|-----|
| **$\bar{X}$-chart** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ |
| **R-chart** | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |
| **$\bar{X}$-s chart** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{s}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{s}$ |
| **I-chart (Individual)** | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} + E_2 \bar{MR}$ | $\bar{X} - E_2 \bar{MR}$ |

### B. Attribute Chart Limits

| Chart Type | Center Line | UCL | LCL |
|------------|-------------|-----|-----|
| **p-chart** | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ |
| **np-chart** | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ |
| **c-chart** | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| **u-chart** | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ |

### C. Process Capability Indices

| Index | Formula | Meaning | Target |
|-------|---------|---------|--------|
| **$C_p$** | $(USL - LSL) / (6\sigma)$ | Process spread vs. spec spread | $\geq 1.33$ |
| **$C_{pk}$** | $\min\!\left[\dfrac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Centering + Spread (one-sided) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| **$C_{pm}$** | $(USL - LSL) / (6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\bar{\bar{X}} - T)^2})$ | Taguchi's loss-function version | $\geq 1.33$ |
| **$P_p$ / $P_{pk}$** | Same as $C_p/C_{pk}$ but using **overall** $\sigma$ (long-term) | Long-term capability | $\geq 1.33$ / $1.50$ |
| **$Z$-bench (Z-LSL, Z-USL)** | $(USL - \bar{\bar{X}})/\sigma$ | Sigma distance from spec | $\geq 4.5$ for Six Sigma |
| **DPMO** | $(D \times 10^6) / (O \times n)$ | Defects per million opportunities | $\leq 3.4$ for Six Sigma |

### D. Critical Control-Chart Constants (n vs A2, D3, D4)

| Subgroup Size n | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ | $E_2$ | $A_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 2.660 | 2.659 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 1.693 | 1.772 | 1.954 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 1.457 | 1.628 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | 1.290 | 1.427 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 1.184 | 1.287 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 1.109 | 1.182 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 | 1.054 | 1.099 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 | 1.010 | 1.032 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 | 0.975 | 0.975 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota, Ford):** SPC is mandatory per **IATF 16949** — no part ships without $\bar{X}$-R charts on critical dimensions.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** SPC tracks wafer thickness, dopant concentration, etch depth; Cpk $\geq 1.67$ is the industry norm.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** USP <905> requires uniformity of dosage validated by **c-charts and u-charts**.
- **Healthcare:** SPC used to monitor hospital infection rates, surgical wait times, lab turnaround.
- **Banking/IT:** Adapted as "Statistical Process Monitoring" for fraud detection and SLA compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why "3-Sigma" Control Limits? (Shewhart's Theorem)

The theoretical justification uses the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**. Even if individual measurements are not normally distributed, the **distribution of subgroup means $\bar{X}$** is approximately normal by CLT for $n \geq 4$ or $5$.

$$
\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu,\ \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) \quad \text{approximately}
$$

The standard deviation of $\bar{X}$ is the **standard error**:

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

Shewhart's empirical rule states: virtually all (≈ **99.73%**) of normally distributed points fall within **±3σ** of the mean. The 0.27% tail probability splits as 0.135% on each side, giving a **Type I error rate (α) of 0.0027** per point. Across a chart with many points, this yields a workable false-alarm frequency without being too insensitive.

Hence the 3-sigma control limits on the $\bar{X}$ chart are:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \qquad CL_{\bar{X}} = \mu \qquad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

Since $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown, we replace them with their sample estimators $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ (where $d_2$ is the expected value of the range of $n$ standard normal variates). Thus:

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}} \quad \text{(a tabulated constant)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 14-Mark Question

**Problem:** A CNC lathe produces shafts. Twenty-five subgroups of size $n=5$ are taken one per hour. The grand average of subgroup means is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.00$ mm, and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.40$ mm. The specification is $25.00 \pm 0.80$ mm. Compute:
1. The control limits for the $\bar{X}$-chart and R-chart.
2. The trial process standard deviation.
3. The process capability index $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.
4. Interpret the result.

### Step 1 — Control Limits for $\bar{X}$-chart (n = 5, $A_2 = 0.577$)

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R} = 25.00 + (0.577)(0.40)
$$

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.00 + 0.2308 = 25.2308 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = 25.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.00 - 0.2308 = 24.7692 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Control Limits for R-chart (n = 5, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$)

$$
UCL_R = D_4 \cdot \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.40) = 0.8456 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
CL_R = \bar{R} = 0.40 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
LCL_R = D_3 \cdot \bar{R} = (0)(0.40) = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Note (KTU Pitfall):** $LCL_R$ for $n < 7$ is mathematically zero. Some students write a negative number — this is **wrong**, since a range is non-negative. If your calculation gives a negative LCL, the true LCL is **0**.

### Step 3 — Estimating $\hat{\sigma}$ (Process Standard Deviation)

From the within-subgroup range method, using $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n=5$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.40}{2.326} = 0.1720 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Computing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

The specification is $25.00 \pm 0.80$ mm, so $USL = 25.80$ mm and $LSL = 24.20$ mm.

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{25.80 - 24.20}{6 \times 0.1720}
$$

$$
C_p = \dfrac{1.60}{1.032} = 1.550
$$

For $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_{pk,\ upper} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{25.80 - 25.00}{3 \times 0.1720} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.516} = 1.550
$$

$$
C_{pk,\ lower} = \dfrac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{25.00 - 24.20}{0.516} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.516} = 1.550
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.550,\ 1.550) = 1.550
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

Since $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.55 > 1.33$, the process is **capable** and **perfectly centered**. Defect rate is well below **63 ppm** (one-sided tail at $Z = 4.65$ sigma).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Step-Marking Scheme:** Part (a) 7 marks → computation of control limits. Part (b) 7 marks → capability + interpretation. Always carry **4 decimal places** in intermediate steps to earn full marks.

## 3.3 Derivation of $C_{pk}$ Geometric Meaning

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]
$$

The two terms represent the **distance from the mean to each spec limit**, expressed in units of $3\sigma$ (half the natural process spread). Geometrically:

- If the process mean **equals** the target $T = (USL+LSL)/2$, then $C_{pk} = C_p$.
- If the process mean **shifts** away from $T$, $C_{pk} < C_p$ (the process is off-center).
- $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ **always**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The SPC Decision-Logic Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Subgroup Data] --> B[Compute Subgroup Statistic]
    B --> C{Plot on Control Chart}
    C --> D[Compare vs UCL and LCL]
    D --> E{Point within limits?}
    E -- YES --> F[Common Cause Variation]
    F --> G[Leave Process Alone - Do Not Tamper]
    G --> A
    E -- NO --> H[Special Cause Detected]
    H --> I[Stop the Line - Investigate]
    I --> J[Identify Root Cause via 5 Whys]
    J --> K[Eliminate Cause]
    K --> L[Re-establish Control Limits]
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Variable vs Attribute Chart Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[What is the data type?] --> B[Continuous Variable]
    A --> C[Discrete Attribute]
    B --> D[Sample size n]
    D --> E[n greater than 10]
    D --> F[n less than or equal 10]
    E --> G[X-bar and s chart]
    F --> H[X-bar and R chart]
    C --> I[What is being counted?]
    I --> J[Defectives per unit]
    I --> K[Defects per unit]
    J --> L[Subgroup size constant: p-chart or np-chart]
    J --> M[Subgroup size variable: u-chart]
    K --> N[Sample size constant: c-chart]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Process Capability Interpretation Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Cp and Cpk] --> B{Cpk value?}
    B -- Cpk less than 1.0 --> C[NOT CAPABLE - High Defect Rate]
    B -- Cpk between 1.0 and 1.33 --> D[MARGINALLY CAPABLE - Improve]
    B -- Cpk between 1.33 and 1.67 --> E[GOOD - Industry Standard]
    B -- Cpk greater than 1.67 --> F[EXCELLENT - Six Sigma Territory]
    B -- Cpk less than Cp --> G[OFF-CENTER - Re-center the process]
    A --> H{Cp vs Cpk equality test}
    H -- Cp EQUALS Cpk --> I[CENTERED PROCESS]
    H -- Cp GREATER THAN Cpk --> J[OFF-CENTER PROCESS]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Sketch — The 3-Sigma "Bathtub" of Variation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LSL at minus 3 sigma shift] --> B[Process Spread minus 6 sigma]
    B --> C[Center Line CL at mu]
    C --> D[Process Spread plus 6 sigma]
    D --> E[USL at plus 3 sigma shift]
    F[0.135 percent tail] --> A
    G[99.73 percent in control] --> C
    H[0.135 percent tail] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip for Exams:** Draw a **bell curve** straddling the center line, with USL on the right and LSL on the left. The portion of the curve **outside** the spec limits = defectives. This single drawing can earn 2–3 marks of grace in any KTU 14-mark SPC question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between Control Limits and Specification Limits. Why are they different?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Control Limits | Specification Limits |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| Set by | Process itself (data) | Customer / Design |
| Reflect | Voice of the Process (VOP) | Voice of the Customer (VOC) |
| Position | $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | USL, LSL (fixed by engineering) |
| Purpose | Detect *change* | Define *acceptability* |

If CL $\subset$ SL → **capable**; if CL $\not\subset$ SL → **incapable**, even if "in control." They are different because one describes natural behavior and the other describes customer need. **(1 mark per row + 0.5 mark for VOP/VOC statement)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the difference between common cause and special cause variation? Give one example of each from a manufacturing process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Common Cause Variation:** Inherent, random, expected fluctuation within the process. Example: The slight 0.01 mm dimensional difference in consecutive shafts due to tool micro-vibration. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Special Cause Variation:** Sudden, externally induced, assignable fluctuation. Example: A batch of shafts oversize by 0.5 mm because the cutting tool broke and was not replaced. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** ESE Module-4 questions carry internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** A process produces bolts with target diameter 10.00 mm and specification $10.00 \pm 0.20$ mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size $n = 4$ are sampled. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 9.98$ mm, and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.08$ mm. Calculate the trial control limits for the $\bar{X}$-chart and R-chart, and state whether the process is in statistical control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the expected DPMO for the above process. Comment on process capability. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Control Limits**

From the constants table for $n = 4$: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$, $d_2 = 2.059$.

$\bar{X}$-chart limits:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = 9.98 + (0.729)(0.08) = 9.98 + 0.0583 = 10.0383 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = 9.98 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = 9.98 - 0.0583 = 9.9217 \text{ mm}
$$

R-chart limits:

$$
UCL_R = (2.282)(0.08) = 0.1826 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
CL_R = 0.08 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
LCL_R = (0)(0.08) = 0.0000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Decision:** The process is **in statistical control** as long as no plotted subgroup mean falls outside $[9.9217,\ 10.0383]$ and no plotted range exceeds $0.1826$. The fact that the center line $9.98$ lies *within* the spec window $[9.80, 10.20]$ is a *favorable* but separate issue — control is about stability, not capability. **(1 mark for interpretation)**

**Part (b) — Process Capability**

Estimate of $\sigma$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2 = 0.08/2.059 = 0.0389 \text{ mm}
$$

Compute $C_p$:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{10.20 - 9.80}{6 \times 0.0389} = \dfrac{0.40}{0.2334} = 1.714
$$

Compute $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_{pk,\ upper} = \dfrac{10.20 - 9.98}{3 \times 0.0389} = \dfrac{0.22}{0.1167} = 1.885
$$

$$
C_{pk,\ lower} = \dfrac{9.98 - 9.80}{0.1167} = \dfrac{0.18}{0.1167} = 1.542
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.885,\ 1.542) = 1.542
$$

Expected DPMO: For $C_{pk} = 1.542$, the one-sided tail area corresponds to $Z = 3 \times 1.542 = 4.626$ sigma. From the standard normal table, $P(Z > 4.626) \approx 1.84 \times 10^{-6}$. So

$$
DPMO \approx 2 \times 1.84 \approx 3.7\ \text{ppm} \approx 3700\ \text{DPMO}
$$

**Comment:** The process is in control and **highly capable** ($C_p > 1.67$ and $C_{pk} > 1.50$). However, since $C_p > C_{pk}$ slightly, the mean is mildly off-center (shifted toward LSL), so re-centering the lathe to bring $\bar{\bar{X}}$ from $9.98$ to $10.00$ would be a quick improvement.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the Western Electric Rules (or Nelson Rules) used to detect special cause patterns on a control chart. List any four patterns. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A call center receives on average $\bar{c} = 8$ complaints per day. Construct a 3-sigma c-chart for 30 days. A particular day shows 20 complaints. What action should the manager take? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Western Electric / Nelson Rules (7 marks)**

Developed in 1956 at Western Electric (now Lucent) to augment the basic 3-sigma rule, four key patterns are:

1. **Rule 1 — Beyond Limits:** One point outside the 3-sigma control limits. **(1.75 marks)**
2. **Rule 2 — Nine in a Row on One Side:** Nine consecutive points above (or below) the center line → process mean has shifted. **(1.75 marks)**
3. **Rule 3 — Six in a Row Trending:** Six consecutive points all increasing or all decreasing → systematic drift (tool wear, temperature rise). **(1.75 marks)**
4. **Rule 4 — Two of Three Beyond 2-Sigma:** Two out of three consecutive points beyond $\pm 2\sigma$ on the same side → warning of an approaching out-of-control condition. **(1.75 marks)**

> These rules reduce the **Average Run Length (ARL)** for a 1.5-sigma mean shift from ~250 points (Shewhart chart alone) to ~10 points (with Nelson rules), dramatically improving detection sensitivity.

**Part (b) — c-Chart Construction (7 marks)**

For a c-chart, the parameter $\bar{c}$ is given as 8 complaints/day.

$$
UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 8 + 3\sqrt{8} = 8 + 3(2.8284) = 8 + 8.4853 = 16.4853
$$

$$
CL_c = \bar{c} = 8
$$

$$
LCL_c = 8 - 8.4853 = -0.4853 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 \quad (\text{since } c \geq 0)
$$

So the trial control limits are: $LCL = 0$, $CL = 8$, $UCL = 16.49$.

**Action for 20 complaints:** Since $20 > UCL = 16.49$, this is a **special cause variation** (Rule 1 violation). The manager must:

1. **Stop and investigate** the day in question — was there a system outage, a billing error, a new product defect?
2. **Identify the assignable cause** using 5-Whys or fishbone diagram.
3. **Eliminate the cause** and re-establish limits if the process has fundamentally changed.
4. **Do not simply average 20 into the limits** — that would be "tampering" and would *hide* the special cause.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU examiners deduct marks on SPC questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up CL and SL** in the final sentence — examiners **always** look for the phrase *"the process is in control but **not** capable"* or vice-versa.
> 2. **Forgetting to set $LCL = 0$** when the formula gives a negative value for R-chart, c-chart, or u-chart. This is a **guaranteed 0.5–1 mark loss**.
> 3. **Using the wrong $A_2$ constant** — re-check the value of $n$ from the problem statement, not the data table. If $n=5$ is given, use $n=5$ constants, never $n=4$.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation** — 1–2 marks are reserved for "what does it mean?" Always end with a one-line conclusion.
> 5. **Reporting $C_{pk}$ without its two components** — show both the upper and lower terms before taking the min.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SPC = Shewhart's Brainchild** (1924) — separates common cause (noise) from special cause (signals).
- **Control Limits** = computed from data; **Specification Limits** = set by customer. Never confuse the two.
- **3-Sigma** limits give **0.27% false alarm** rate (α = 0.0027) — the universal engineering compromise.
- **Minimum 20–25 subgroups** are required to establish *trial* control limits (Shewhart's rule).
- **Use $\bar{R}/d_2$** to estimate $\sigma$ (R-method) — simplest, best for $n \leq 10$.
- **$C_p$** measures spread only; **$C_{pk}$** measures spread + centering. Always $C_{pk} \leq C_p$.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** = "Industry Standard Capable"; **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$** = "Six Sigma Territory".
- **Off-center process** (mean $\neq$ target) ⇒ $C_{pk} < C_p$. **Re-center first**, then improve spread.
- **Negative LCL** ⇒ set it to **zero** (for R, c, u-charts).
- **p-chart** for variable sample size, **np-chart** only for constant $n$.
- **Nelson Rules** reduce ARL from 250 to ~10 for a 1.5-σ shift — **always quote** in 14-mark answers.
- **SPC prevents tampering** (Deming) — never adjust a stable process.
- **In-control ≠ Capable.** A process can be perfectly stable (all points inside control limits) yet produce 50% defectives if $\sigma$ is too wide.
- **Six Sigma (3.4 DPMO)** corresponds to $C_{pk} \approx 2.0$ (with 1.5-σ mean shift) or $C_{pk} = 1.5$ (static).
- **DPMO formula:** $DPMO = (D \times 10^6) / (O \times n)$ — remember: $D$ = defects, $O$ = opportunities per unit, $n$ = units.

> 🎯 **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** "**CC-SS, 3, 25, 1.33**" — **C**ommon vs **S**pecial, **3-σ** limits, **25** subgroups, **1.33** minimum $C_{pk}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Charts for Variables and Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control Charts for Variables and Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Statistical Process Control (SPC) Charts?

A **Control Chart** is a graphical tool used in Statistical Process Control (SPC) to monitor, control, and improve a process over time by distinguishing between **common cause variation** (inherent to the process) and **special cause variation** (due to external assignable factors). It was first introduced by **Walter A. Shewhart** in 1924 at Bell Laboratories, and it forms the backbone of the **Measure** and **Control** phases of the DMAIC framework in Six Sigma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *"A Control Chart is a time-ordered plot of process characteristic values with a central line (CL) representing the process mean and Upper/Lower Control Limits (UCL/LCL), calculated typically as the mean ± 3 standard deviations, used to detect whether a process is in a state of statistical control."*

### 1.2 Classification of Control Charts

Control charts are broadly divided into two categories based on the type of data measured:

| Classification | Data Type | Typical Unit | Charts Available |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variables Charts** | Continuous (measured on a scale) | mm, kg, °C, volts | $\bar{X}$-R, $\bar{X}$-S, I-MR |
| **Attributes Charts** | Discrete / Categorical | Defectives (count) or proportion | p, np, c, u |

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous vs. Discrete Data:** Variables data gives more information per data point because it captures *magnitude* (e.g., diameter = 10.03 mm), whereas attribute data only classifies each item as *good/bad* or counts defects.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Heart Monitor" for a Process

Imagine a patient in a hospital connected to a heart monitor. The monitor plots the patient's heart rate over time with a **green safe zone** (say, 60–100 bpm). As long as the line stays inside the zone, doctors don't intervene — that's *common cause* (normal physiology). The instant the line spikes outside the zone, an alarm rings — that's *special cause* (potential heart attack).

A **control chart works identically for a manufacturing process**:
- The **Central Line (CL)** is the patient's resting heart rate → process average.
- The **UCL/LCL** are the alarm thresholds → ±3σ limits.
- An **out-of-control point** triggers an investigation → assignable cause found and removed.

### 1.4 The 3-Sigma Logic (Why "3"?)

Under the assumption of a **Normal Distribution** (Gaussian curve), approximately:
- **68.26%** of data falls within ±1σ
- **95.46%** of data falls within ±2σ
- **99.73%** of data falls within ±3σ

Hence, only **0.27%** (about **27 parts per 10,000**) of in-control data will trigger a false alarm. This probability is called the **Type I Error (α)** and is small enough to avoid frequent nuisance interventions while still detecting real shifts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The 3σ control limits are also called the **"Voice of the Process"** because they represent what the process is naturally capable of producing, not what we wish it would produce.

### 1.5 Variables vs. Attributes — Selection Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Use Variables Chart | Use Attributes Chart |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement cost | Cheap to measure | Expensive / destructive |
| Data type | Continuous (length, weight) | Discrete (pass/fail) |
| Information needed | Both mean & variability | Only proportion/count |
| Sample size | Small (n = 2 to 10 typical) | Often larger (n ≥ 50) |
| Speed of detection | Faster (more info/subgroup) | Slower (binary data) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Every Control Chart

Regardless of type, every control chart has **five mandatory components**:
1. **Data Points** plotted sequentially in time order.
2. **Central Line (CL)** — the grand average of the statistic being plotted.
3. **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** — CL + 3σ (process-level).
4. **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** — CL − 3σ (process-level).
5. **Control Limit Constants** — tabulated factors (A₂, D₃, D₄, B₃, B₄, etc.) derived from the standard normal distribution for small sample sizes.

### 2.2 Variables Charts: X̄-R (Most Common in KTU Exams)

Used when subgroup size **n ≤ 10** (typically n = 5). Two charts are constructed simultaneously:
- **X̄-chart** monitors the process **mean** (location).
- **R-chart** monitors the process **spread** (variability/dispersion).

#### 2.2.1 Formulas for X̄-R Charts

| Statistic | Central Line | UCL | LCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subgroup mean ($\bar{X}$) | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ |
| Subgroup range (R) | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |

Where **k** = number of subgroups and **A₂, D₃, D₄** are constants dependent on **n** (sample size per subgroup).

#### 2.2.2 Control Chart Constants Table (n = 2 to 10)

| n | A₂ | D₃ | D₄ | d₂ | D₂ | E₂ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 0 | 2.660 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 | 0 | 1.772 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 0 | 1.457 |
| **5** | **0.577** | **0** | **2.114** | **2.326** | **0** | **1.290** |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 0 | 1.184 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 0.205 | 1.109 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 | 0.387 | 1.054 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 | 0.546 | 1.010 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 | 0.687 | 0.975 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Sampling Rule:** Sample size n = 5 with k = 25 subgroups (total 125 readings) is the industry default and the most frequently tested combination in KTU papers.

### 2.3 Variables Charts: X̄-S Chart (Larger Subgroups, n > 10)

When **n > 10**, the range R becomes a poor estimator of process standard deviation. We use the **sample standard deviation S** instead.

| Statistic | Central Line | UCL | LCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$ |
| S | $\bar{S}$ | $B_4 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ |

Where $A_3 = \dfrac{3}{c_4 \sqrt{n}}$ and $B_3, B_4$ depend on n via the c₄ correction factor.

### 2.4 Variables Charts: I-MR Chart (Individual Measurements)

Used when:
- Each measurement is **expensive/destructive** (e.g., tensile testing of aircraft alloys).
- Production rate is **low** (one piece at a time, e.g., custom chemical batches).
- Subgroups of n > 1 are **impossible**.

The **Moving Range (MR)** for two successive observations is calculated, and the average MR ($\overline{MR}$) estimates σ via the relation $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\overline{MR}}{d_2}$ where $d_2 = 1.128$ for n = 2.

| Chart | Central Line | UCL | LCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Individuals (I) | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X_i}{n}$ | $\bar{X} + E_2 \overline{MR}$ | $\bar{X} - E_2 \overline{MR}$ |
| Moving Range (MR) | $\overline{MR}$ | $D_4 \overline{MR}$ | $D_3 \overline{MR}$ |

For n = 2: $E_2 = 2.660$, $D_4 = 3.267$, $D_3 = 0$.

### 2.5 Attribute Charts: The Big Four

#### 2.5.1 p-Chart (Fraction/Proportion Defective) — *Variable sample size allowed*

Used when each inspected unit is classified simply as **defective** or **not defective**, and the **sample size n can vary** from subgroup to subgroup.

$$ \bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i} $$

$$ UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} $$

$$ LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} $$

If LCL becomes **negative**, it is set to **zero** (you cannot have a negative proportion of defects).

#### 2.5.2 np-Chart (Number of Defectives) — *Constant sample size*

Used when sample size **n is constant** across subgroups. It plots the **count of defectives** directly.

$$ \overline{np} = n\bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{k} $$

$$ UCL_{np} = n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} $$

$$ LCL_{np} = n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} $$

#### 2.5.3 c-Chart (Number of Defects per Unit) — *Constant inspection unit*

Used when a single unit can have **multiple defects** (e.g., number of surface blemishes on a car door panel), and the **area/unit inspected is constant**.

$$ \bar{c} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{k} $$

$$ UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} $$

$$ LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} $$

#### 2.5.4 u-Chart (Defects per Unit) — *Variable inspection unit*

Used when multiple defects can occur per unit **AND** the inspection area (or opportunity) varies (e.g., fabric rolls of different lengths).

$$ \bar{u} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{\sum n_i} $$

$$ UCL_u = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}} $$

$$ LCL_u = \bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}} $$

### 2.6 Master Formula Cheat-Sheet (KTU Board Standard)

| Chart | Type | CL | UCL | LCL | Constants Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ | Variable | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$ | A₂ |
| R | Variable | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4\bar{R}$ | $D_3\bar{R}$ | D₃, D₄ |
| $\bar{X}$ (S) | Variable | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3\bar{S}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3\bar{S}$ | A₃ |
| S | Variable | $\bar{S}$ | $B_4\bar{S}$ | $B_3\bar{S}$ | B₃, B₄ |
| I | Variable | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} + 2.66\overline{MR}$ | $\bar{X} - 2.66\overline{MR}$ | E₂ = 2.66 |
| MR | Variable | $\overline{MR}$ | $3.267\overline{MR}$ | 0 | D₄ = 3.267 |
| p | Attribute | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | — |
| np | Attribute | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | — |
| c | Attribute | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | — |
| u | Attribute | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | — |

### 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Automotive Industry (Toyota, Ford):** X̄-R charts monitor piston ring diameters.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** I-MR charts monitor wafer thickness (costly destructive testing).
- **Software Quality:** p-charts track defect rate per 1,000 lines of code.
- **Healthcare:** c-charts track the number of hospital-acquired infections per ward per week.
- **Aviation (Boeing, Airbus):** X̄-S charts monitor turbine blade surface roughness across large samples.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why is the Range Estimator Constant d₂ = 1.128?

The expected value of the range R of a sample of size n drawn from a standard normal distribution N(0,1) is denoted **d₂**. It is computed as:

$$ d_2 = E[R] = \int_0^{\infty} r \cdot f_R(r) \, dr $$

where $f_R(r)$ is the probability density function of the range. For n = 2:
- The range is $R = \vert X_1 - X_2 \vert$.
- Both $X_1, X_2 \sim N(0,1)$ independently.
- The difference $X_1 - X_2 \sim N(0, 2)$.

$$ d_2 = E[\vert X_1 - X_2 \vert] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4}{\pi}} \approx 1.128 $$

This is why the unbiased estimator of σ is:

$$ \hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} $$

### 3.2 Derivation: 3-Sigma Control Limits for X̄

By the **Central Limit Theorem**, the distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is normal with mean μ and standard deviation:

$$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$

The 3-sigma control limits are:

$$ UCL = \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$

Substituting $\sigma \approx \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$:

$$ UCL = \mu + 3 \cdot \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} = \mu + A_2 \bar{R} $$

Therefore, the constant A₂ is defined as:

$$ A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: Worked Numerical Example (X̄-R Chart)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Problem]**

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing process produces steel shafts. Five subgroups of size n = 5 are drawn. The data are:

| Subgroup i | X̄ᵢ | Rᵢ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 35.2 | 4 |
| 2 | 36.0 | 3 |
| 3 | 34.8 | 5 |
| 4 | 35.5 | 4 |
| 5 | 36.1 | 2 |

Construct the **X̄ chart** and **R chart**. Is the process in statistical control?

#### Step 1: Compute Grand Averages

$$ \bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{35.2 + 36.0 + 34.8 + 35.5 + 36.1}{5} = \dfrac{177.6}{5} = 35.52 $$

$$ \bar{R} = \dfrac{4 + 3 + 5 + 4 + 2}{5} = \dfrac{18}{5} = 3.6 $$

#### Step 2: Retrieve Constants for n = 5

From the KTU constant table (n = 5):
- A₂ = **0.577**
- D₃ = **0**
- D₄ = **2.114**

#### Step 3: Compute Control Limits for X̄ Chart

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 35.52 + (0.577)(3.6) $$

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 35.52 + 2.0772 = \mathbf{37.5972} $$

$$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 35.52 - 2.0772 = \mathbf{33.4428} $$

$$ CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = \mathbf{35.52} $$

#### Step 4: Compute Control Limits for R Chart

$$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(3.6) = \mathbf{7.6104} $$

$$ LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(3.6) = \mathbf{0} $$

$$ CL_R = \bar{R} = \mathbf{3.6} $$

#### Step 5: Plot & Verify Control

- X̄ chart limits: [33.4428, 37.5972] — All 5 points fall inside ✓
- R chart limits: [0, 7.6104] — All 5 points fall inside ✓

**Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control**. No special causes detected.

### 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: p-Chart (Variable n)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Problem:** A factory inspects daily samples of varying size for defective items. Data:

| Day | nᵢ | dᵢ (defectives) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 8 |
| 2 | 120 | 10 |
| 3 | 90 | 6 |
| 4 | 110 | 9 |
| 5 | 130 | 12 |

Construct the **p-chart**.

#### Step 1: Compute Pooled Proportion $\bar{p}$

$$ \bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i} = \dfrac{8 + 10 + 6 + 9 + 12}{100 + 120 + 90 + 110 + 130} = \dfrac{45}{550} = 0.0818 $$

#### Step 2: Compute Day-Specific Limits

Since **n varies**, we calculate separate limits for each day:

$$ \sigma_{p,i} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}} $$

For Day 1 (n=100):
$$ \sigma_{p,1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0818 \times 0.9182}{100}} = \sqrt{0.000751} = 0.02741 $$

$$ UCL_{p,1} = 0.0818 + 3(0.02741) = 0.0818 + 0.0822 = \mathbf{0.1640} $$

$$ LCL_{p,1} = 0.0818 - 0.0822 = -0.0004 \to \mathbf{0} \text{ (truncated, since negative is meaningless)} $$

For Day 5 (n=130):
$$ \sigma_{p,5} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0818 \times 0.9182}{130}} = 0.02404 $$

$$ UCL_{p,5} = 0.0818 + 3(0.02404) = \mathbf{0.1539} $$

$$ LCL_{p,5} = 0.0818 - 0.0721 = \mathbf{0.0097} $$

#### Step 3: Check All Data Points

| Day | pᵢ = dᵢ/nᵢ | UCL | LCL | In Control? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0800 | 0.1640 | 0 | ✓ |
| 2 | 0.0833 | 0.1580 | 0 | ✓ |
| 3 | 0.0667 | 0.1664 | 0 | ✓ |
| 4 | 0.0818 | 0.1611 | 0.0025 | ✓ |
| 5 | 0.0923 | 0.1539 | 0.0097 | ✓ |

**Conclusion:** Process is **in statistical control**.

### 3.5 Analytical Walkthrough: c-Chart (Constant Inspection)

**Problem:** A textile roll is inspected for defects. 10 rolls inspected, defects observed: 3, 4, 5, 2, 6, 4, 3, 5, 4, 4. Construct c-chart.

#### Step 1: Calculate $\bar{c}$

$$ \bar{c} = \dfrac{3+4+5+2+6+4+3+5+4+4}{10} = \dfrac{40}{10} = 4 $$

#### Step 2: Compute Control Limits

$$ UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4 + 3\sqrt{4} = 4 + 6 = \mathbf{10} $$

$$ LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4 - 6 = -2 \to \mathbf{0} $$

#### Step 3: Verify

All 10 points (max = 6, min = 2) lie within [0, 10] → Process is **in control**.

### 3.6 Out-of-Control Patterns (Western Electric / Nelson Rules)

Even when all points lie inside the 3σ limits, specific **patterns** indicate an unstable process:

| Rule | Description | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | One point beyond ±3σ | Shift / spike |
| 2 | 9 points in a row on one side of CL | Mean shift |
| 3 | 6 points in a row trending up or down | Drift |
| 4 | 14 alternating points up-down | Systematic variation |
| 5 | 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond ±2σ | Strong shift |
| 6 | 4 of 5 consecutive points beyond ±1σ | Mild shift |
| 7 | 15 consecutive points within ±1σ | Stratification / over-control |
| 8 | 8 consecutive points beyond ±1σ (both sides) | Mixture of sources |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Rule 1 (single point beyond 3σ) alone is sufficient to declare the process out-of-control. Pattern recognition is for finer diagnosis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control Chart Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Data Available] --> B{Data Type?}
    B -->|Continuous Measurement| C[Variables Charts]
    B -->|Discrete Classification| D[Attribute Charts]
    C --> E{Subgroup Size n?}
    E -->|n less than or equal to 10| F[X-bar and R Chart]
    E -->|n greater than 10| G[X-bar and S Chart]
    E -->|n equals 1| H[Individual and Moving Range Chart]
    D --> I{Sample Size n Constant?}
    I -->|Variable n| J{One Defect Per Unit?}
    I -->|Constant n| K{Multiple Defects Per Unit?}
    J -->|Yes - Pass or Fail| L[p-Chart Proportion Defective]
    J -->|No - Count of Defectives| M[np-Chart Number Defective]
    K -->|Yes - Constant Area| N[c-Chart Defects Per Unit]
    K -->|No - Variable Area| O[u-Chart Defects Per Unit]
    F --> P[Apply 3 Sigma Limits]
    G --> P
    H --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{All Points Inside Limits?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Process In Statistical Control]
    Q -->|No| S[Investigate Assignable Causes]
```

### 4.2 Architecture of a Typical X-bar and R Control Chart (Block View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Process Data] --> B[Subgroup Sampling n = 5]
    B --> C[Compute Subgroup Mean X-bar]
    B --> D[Compute Subgroup Range R]
    C --> E[Compute Grand Average X-double-bar]
    D --> F[Compute Average Range R-bar]
    E --> G[Apply A2 Constant]
    F --> H[Apply D4 and D3 Constants]
    G --> I[X-bar Chart UCL and LCL]
    H --> J[R Chart UCL and LCL]
    I --> K[Plot X-bar Values]
    J --> L[Plot R Values]
    K --> M{Point Beyond Limits?}
    L --> M
    M -->|Yes| N[Out of Control Alarm]
    M -->|No| O[Process Stable]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: The 3-Sigma Control Band

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC[Process Behavior Over Time]
        UCL[UCL equals Mean plus 3 Sigma]
        CL[Central Line equals Process Mean]
        LCL[LCL equals Mean minus 3 Sigma]
        UCL --> CL
        CL --> LCL
    end
    subgraph ZONES[Sigma Zones]
        Z1[Zone A plus or minus 2 Sigma to 3 Sigma]
        Z2[Zone B plus or minus 1 Sigma to 2 Sigma]
        Z3[Zone C Mean to plus or minus 1 Sigma]
    end
    PROC --- ZONES
```

### 4.4 Variable Chart vs. Attribute Chart: Trade-off Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Variables Chart] --> B[Continuous Data]
    A --> C[More Information Per Point]
    A --> D[Smaller Sample Required]
    A --> E[Faster Detection of Shifts]
    F[Attributes Chart] --> G[Discrete Data]
    F --> H[Simpler Data Collection]
    F --> I[No Measurement Device Needed]
    F --> J[Cheaper for Large Volumes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:** *Differentiate between control charts for variables and control charts for attributes.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Variables Charts | Attribute Charts |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Continuous measurements (length, weight, temperature) | Discrete counts (defective/non-defective, no. of defects) |
| **Examples** | X̄-R, X̄-S, I-MR | p, np, c, u |
| **Information per Point** | Provides both magnitude & variation | Only classification or count |
| **Sample Size** | Small (typically 2–10) | Larger (often 50+) |
| **Sensitivity** | Higher (more information) | Lower |
| **Cost** | Higher (requires calibrated instruments) | Lower (visual inspection often sufficient) |

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:** *Define the term "process in statistical control" with respect to Six Sigma DMAIC methodology.*

**Model Answer:**

A process is said to be in **statistical control** when the only source of variation present in the process is **common cause variation** (i.e., inherent, random, predictable variation), and **no special cause variation** exists. This is identified when all data points on a control chart fall within the 3-sigma control limits (UCL and LCL) **and** no non-random patterns (such as trends, runs, or cycles — Western Electric Rules) are observed.
