## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Acceptance Sampling. List any two situations where it is preferred over 100% inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Acceptance Sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a random sample of items is drawn from a lot, and based on the number of defectives found in the sample, a decision is made to accept or reject the entire lot.

**Two situations (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Destructive Testing:** When inspection destroys the item (e.g., testing the tensile strength of a steel rod, crash-testing an airbag), 100% inspection is impossible.
2. **High Inspection Cost:** When the cost of inspecting every item is greater than the cost of passing some defectives to the customer (e.g., checking every single bolt in a batch of 100,000).
3. *(Alternative)* **Fatigue / Repetitive Inspection:** When 100% inspection leads to inspector fatigue and high error rates.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Producer's Risk (α) and Consumer's Risk (β) in acceptance sampling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Producer's Risk (α) | Consumer's Risk (β) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Probability of rejecting a *good* lot. | Probability of accepting a *bad* lot. |
| **Whose loss?** | Manufacturer / Producer. | Customer / Consumer. |
| **Linked to** | AQL (Acceptable Quality Level). | LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective). |
| **Typical value** | 0.01 to 0.10 (often 0.05). | 0.05 to 0.15 (often 0.10). |
| **Also called** | Type I error. | Type II error. |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** in detail. What does it represent? Sketch a typical OC curve and label AQL, LTPD, α, and β. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single sampling plan has **n = 80, c = 2**. The lot size is large. Compute the probability of acceptance for **p = 0.05**. If AQL = 0.04 and LTPD = 0.10, determine α and β. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OC Curve Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** is a plot of the probability of acceptance **Pa** versus the true fraction defective **p** in the lot. It describes the discriminatory power of a sampling plan.

**Key features (3 marks):**
- When **p = 0**, **Pa = 1** (perfect lots always accepted).
- When **p = 1**, **Pa = 0** (fully defective lots always rejected).
- The curve is **monotonically decreasing** (S-shaped or J-shaped).

**Sketch with labels (2 marks):** A typical OC curve shows AQL on the left (high Pa ≈ 1 − α), LTPD on the right (low Pa = β), and an inflection region in between where Pa drops sharply.

**Engineering utility (2 marks):** The OC curve helps quality engineers choose (n, c) such that the **operating ratio (LTPD/AQL)** is reasonable, providing simultaneous protection to producer and consumer.

**(b) Probability Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: **n = 80, c = 2, p = 0.05.**

Using binomial:
$$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{80}{d} (0.05)^d (0.95)^{80-d}$$

**Term d = 0:** $\binom{80}{0}(0.05)^0(0.95)^{80} = (0.95)^{80}$

$\ln(0.95) = -0.05129$, so $80 \times (-0.05129) = -4.1034$, thus $(0.95)^{80} = e^{-4.1034} \approx 0.01652$.

**Term d = 1:** $\binom{80}{1}(0.05)(0.95)^{79} = 80 \cdot 0.05 \cdot \dfrac{0.01652}{0.95} = 4 \cdot 0.01739 = 0.06957$

**Term d = 2:** $\binom{80}{2}(0.05)^2(0.95)^{78} = 3160 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot \dfrac{0.01652}{0.9025}$

$3160 \cdot 0.0025 = 7.9$

$7.9 \cdot 0.01831 = 0.1446$

**Sum:**
$$P_a = 0.01652 + 0.06957 + 0.1446 = 0.2307$$

$$\boxed{P_a \approx 0.231}$$

**For α at p = AQL = 0.04:** Re-compute Pa at p = 0.04.
$$P_a(0.04) = (0.96)^{80} + 80(0.04)(0.96)^{79} + 3160(0.0016)(0.96)^{78}$$

$(0.96)^{80} \approx 0.03876$, $(0.96)^{79} \approx 0.04038$, $(0.96)^{78} \approx 0.04206$.

$P_a(0.04) = 0.03876 + 3.2 \cdot 0.04038 + 5.056 \cdot 0.04206$
$= 0.03876 + 0.12922 + 0.21265 = 0.3806$

$\alpha = 1 - 0.3806 = 0.619$ (≈ 61.9% — *this is too high; the plan is too lenient*).

**For β at p = LTPD = 0.10:** $P_a(0.10) = 0.231$ (computed above).

$\beta = 0.231$ (≈ 23.1% — *also too high*).

**Conclusion:** This plan is unsuitable. We would need larger **n** or smaller **c** to bring α and β within acceptable limits (typically α ≤ 0.05, β ≤ 0.10).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the following with formulas: **AOQ, AOQL, ATI, and ASN**. Why is AOQL considered the most important metric in rectifying inspection? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectifying single sampling plan has **n = 25, c = 1, N = 500**. Compute AOQ for **p = 0.06** and hence find AOQL by evaluating AOQ at multiple p values. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Formulas (7 marks):**

**Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** — the expected fraction defective in the lots *after* rectifying inspection:
$$AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$$

**Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** — the **maximum** value of AOQ over all possible values of p:
$$AOQL = \max_{p} \left\{ \dfrac{P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} \right\}$$

**Average Total Inspection (ATI)** — average number of items inspected per lot:
$$ATI = n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$$

**Average Sample Number (ASN)** — for double sampling:
$$ASN = n_1 + (1-P_{a1}) \cdot n_2$$

**Why AOQL is most important (2 marks):** AOQL is a **guaranteed upper bound** on outgoing quality. No matter how bad incoming lots are, the rectified outgoing quality will never exceed AOQL. This is a *certificate of quality* — critical in regulated industries like pharma and aerospace.

**(b) AOQ and AOQL Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: **n = 25, c = 1, N = 500.**

For p = 0.06:
$$P_a = (0.94)^{25} + 25 \cdot 0.06 \cdot (0.94)^{24}$$

$(0.94)^{25} = e^{25 \ln 0.94} = e^{25 \times (-0.06188)} = e^{-1.5470} \approx 0.2130$

$(0.94)^{24} = 0.2130 / 0.94 = 0.2266$

$P_a = 0.2130 + 1.5 \cdot 0.2266 = 0.2130 + 0.3399 = 0.5529$

$$AOQ = \dfrac{0.5529 \cdot 0.06 \cdot (500-25)}{500} = \dfrac{0.5529 \cdot 0.06 \cdot 475}{500}$$

$$= \dfrac{15.757}{500} = 0.03151 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 3.15\%$$

**Computing AOQL** — evaluate AOQ at several p:

| p | Pa | AOQ (%) |
|---|-----|---------|
| 0.02 | 0.7872 | 1.495 |
| 0.04 | 0.6341 | 2.408 |
| 0.06 | 0.5529 | 3.151 |
| 0.08 | 0.4459 | 3.388 |
| 0.10 | 0.2719 | 2.583 |
| 0.12 | 0.1576 | 1.795 |

The **maximum** AOQ occurs near **p ≈ 0.08**, giving:

$$\boxed{AOQL \approx 3.39\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Even if incoming quality is poor, after rectifying inspection, the outgoing quality will never exceed 3.39% defective.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing α and β:** Many students mix up Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk. Remember: **P**roducer's risk affects the **P**roducer; **C**onsumer's risk affects the **C**onsumer.
> 2. **Forgetting the (N−n)/N factor in AOQ:** A frequent error is to write AOQ = Pa·p without accounting for the uninspected items.
> 3. **Not stating the OC curve inflection region:** A common 2-mark loss in long answers is failing to explain *why* the OC curve is steep in the middle — this is the "discrimination zone."
> 4. **Mixing up AQL with LTPD:** AQL is the *good* quality threshold; LTPD is the *bad* quality threshold. A good plan has **low Pa at LTPD** and **high Pa at AQL**.
> 5. **Ignoring sampling fraction rule:** Students often use binomial even when n/N > 0.10, leading to incorrect Pa values. KTU may explicitly test this.
> 6. **Skipping the AOQL maximization step:** Computing AOQ at only one p value does not yield AOQL. You must compute AOQ for several p values and find the maximum.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Acceptance Sampling Key Concepts**

- **Definition:** Sampling-based lot sentencing; *not* a process improvement tool.
- **Two Risks:** α (Producer's, at AQL) and β (Consumer's, at LTPD).
- **AQL** = Acceptable Quality Level; **LTPD** = Lot Tolerance Percent Defective.
- **OC Curve** = Pa vs p; decreasing S/J-shape; the steeper, the better the discrimination.
- **Pa formula** uses **binomial** (or **Poisson** if p is small, or **hypergeometric** if n/N > 0.10).
- **AOQ** = Pa · p · (N−n) / N; **AOQL** = max of AOQ.
- **ATI** = n + (1 − Pa)(N − n) for rectifying plans.
- **ASN** = n₁ + (1 − Pa₁)·n₂ for double sampling.
- **Plan types:** Single, Double, Multiple, Sequential — sequential has the smallest ASN.
- **Attribute vs Variables:** Attribute is Go/No-Go; variables uses measurements and needs smaller n.
- **Rectifying Plan** = rejected lots are 100% screened and defectives replaced.
- **Standards:** MIL-STD-105E / ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 (attribute); MIL-STD-414 / ANSI/ASQ Z1.9 (variables).
- **Operating Ratio (OR) = LTPD / AQL:** Higher OR = better discrimination but larger n required.
- **Sampling fraction rule:** n/N ≤ 0.10 → binomial; else hypergeometric.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the OC curve with labeled AQL, LTPD, α, β — it earns easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Acceptance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Acceptance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Acceptance Sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a predetermined number of items (sample size, **n**) are randomly drawn from a lot (batch) of size **N**, inspected, and based on the number of defectives (**d**) found, a decision is made to either **Accept** the lot (if $d \leq c$) or **Reject** the lot (if $d > c$), where **c** is the acceptance number.*

The **Design of Acceptance Sampling Plans** is the systematic statistical procedure used in Six Sigma projects to construct these sampling plans that simultaneously protect the **Producer** (low risk of rejecting good lots) and the **Consumer** (low risk of accepting bad lots), by choosing optimal values of **n** and **c**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Security" Analogy

Imagine you are the **Head of Airport Security**. You cannot frisk-check every one of the 10,000 passengers boarding a flight (that would be **100% inspection** — too slow, too expensive). Instead, you randomly pull aside **150 passengers (n = 150)** for a detailed check. You decide:

- If **≤ 3 dangerous items (c = 3)** are found in those 150 → **Let the flight go (Accept the lot)**.
- If **> 3 dangerous items** are found → **Ground the entire aircraft (Reject the lot)**.

This is exactly how an **Acceptance Sampling Plan** works. The "flight" is the *lot*, the "150 passengers" is the *sample*, and the "3 dangerous items" is the *acceptance number*. The sampling plan's job is to determine *how many* to check (*n*) and *how many defects to tolerate* (*c*) to keep both the airline (producer) and passengers (consumer) safe.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need Sampling Instead of 100% Inspection?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Engineering Justifications for Acceptance Sampling:**
> 1. **Destructive Testing** — e.g., testing the bursting strength of a cylinder, lifespan of a battery, or tensile strength of a rebar. Inspecting all items destroys them.
> 2. **High Cost of 100% Inspection** — e.g., checking every microchip on a wafer manually is economically infeasible.
> 3. **Fatigue & Error in 100% Inspection** — Human inspectors become tired, leading to higher inspector-error rates than sampling errors.

### 1.4 Classification of Acceptance Sampling Plans

| **Plan Type** | **Key Characteristic** | **Six Sigma Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Attributes Plan** | Item classified as Good or Defective | Most common in Six Sigma; uses *binomial* distribution |
| **Variables Plan** | Actual measurement (length, weight) used | Uses *normal* distribution; smaller sample size required |
| **Rectifying Plan** | Rejected lots are 100% inspected and defects replaced | Used in Dodge-Romig plans |
| **Non-Rectifying Plan** | Rejected lots are returned/scrapped as-is | Used in MIL-STD-105E plans |

### 1.5 Key Metrics in Acceptance Sampling

> [!NOTE]
> - **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot. Typically fixed at **0.05**.
> - **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot. Typically fixed at **0.10**.
> - **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level, $p_1$):** The maximum % defective that is considered *acceptable* to the producer.
> - **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective, $p_2$):** The % defective that the consumer wants to *reject* with high probability.
> - **OC Curve:** A graph showing $P_a$ (Probability of Acceptance) vs. incoming lot quality $p$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Single Sampling Plan (SSP) — Architecture

A **Single Sampling Plan by Attributes** is fully defined by three parameters: **(N, n, c)**
- **N** = Lot size
- **n** = Sample size drawn randomly
- **c** = Acceptance number (maximum allowable defectives)

**Decision Logic:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } d \leq c &\rightarrow \text{Accept the lot} \\
\text{If } d > c &\rightarrow \text{Reject the lot}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — The Heart of Acceptance Sampling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **OC Curve** is a plot of $P_a$ (Probability of Acceptance) on the Y-axis against the incoming lot quality $p$ (fraction defective) on the X-axis. It is the **fingerprint** of a sampling plan.

The probability of acceptance for a single sampling plan follows the **cumulative Binomial Distribution**:
$$
P_a(p) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{\,i} (1-p)^{\,n-i}
$$

Where $p$ is the true fraction defective in the lot.

### 2.3 Two Key Points on the OC Curve

| **Point** | **Lot Quality** | **Probability of Acceptance** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Point A (Ideal Point)** | $p_1$ = AQL | $P_a = 1 - \alpha = 0.95$ | Producer is protected: Good lots are accepted 95% of the time |
| **Point B (Ideal Point)** | $p_2$ = LTPD | $P_a = \beta = 0.10$ | Consumer is protected: Bad lots are rejected 90% of the time |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — Memorize This Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for solving KTU numerical problems. Each formula has been algebraically derived and verified.

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Purpose / Used For** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_a$ (Single Plan) | $P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$ | Exact probability of acceptance (Binomial) |
| 2 | $P_a$ (Poisson Approx.) | $P_a \approx \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{i}}{i!}$ | Used when $n \geq 20$ and $p \leq 0.10$ |
| 3 | $n$ (Sample Size) | $n = \dfrac{\left[ z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2(1-p_2)} \right]^2}{(p_2 - p_1)^2}$ | Solving for **n** given $p_1, p_2, \alpha, \beta$ |
| 4 | $c$ (Acceptance Number) | $c = n p_1 + z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} - 0.5$ | Solving for **c** (with continuity correction) |
| 5 | AOQ (Avg. Outgoing Quality) | $AOQ = \dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N - n)}{N}$ | Quality of lots *after* rectifying inspection |
| 6 | AOQL | $AOQL = \max_{p} \{ AOQ(p) \}$ | The *worst-case* average outgoing quality |
| 7 | ATI (Avg. Total Inspection) | $ATI = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a)$ | Avg. items inspected per lot |
| 8 | $z_\alpha$ (Std. Normal) | For $\alpha = 0.05 \Rightarrow z_\alpha = 1.645$ | Standard normal for producer's risk |
| 9 | $z_\beta$ (Std. Normal) | For $\beta = 0.10 \Rightarrow z_\beta = 1.28$ | Standard normal for consumer's risk |
| 10 | OC Curve (Normal Approx.) | $P_a \approx \Phi\left[ \dfrac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}} \right]$ | Used for large $n$ (Normal approximation) |

### 2.5 The Design Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $p_1$ (AQL), $p_2$ (LTPD), $\alpha$ (Producer's Risk), $\beta$ (Consumer's Risk)
> **Find:** $n$ and $c$ for the Single Sampling Plan

**Step 1:** Identify the standard normal values $z_\alpha$ and $z_\beta$ from the standard normal table.
**Step 2:** Compute $n$ using the variance-weighted formula (Formula 3 above).
**Step 3:** Round $n$ **upwards** to the next whole number (since we cannot sample fractional units).
**Step 4:** Compute $c$ using the linear formula (Formula 4 above).
**Step 5:** Round $c$ to the nearest integer.
**Step 6:** Verify by checking $P_a(p_1) \geq 0.95$ and $P_a(p_2) \leq 0.10$ using the exact binomial formula.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real Six Sigma Projects

| **Industry** | **Application of Acceptance Sampling** |
|---|---|
| Automotive (IATF 16949) | Incoming inspection of bought-out components (Tier-1 suppliers) |
| Pharmaceuticals (FDA) | Batch release of drugs; destructive sterility testing |
| Electronics (IPC) | Final QC of PCB assemblies before packaging |
| Food & FMCG | Batch inspection of packaged goods for weight, defects |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Characteristic Function (Binomial)

The number of defectives $X$ in a random sample of size $n$ from a large lot with true fraction defective $p$ follows a **Binomial distribution**:
$$
X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)
$$

The probability of finding exactly $i$ defectives in the sample is:
$$
P(X = i) = \binom{n}{i} p^{\,i} (1-p)^{\,n-i}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

The lot is **accepted** if the number of defectives $X$ is less than or equal to $c$ (the acceptance number). Therefore, the probability of acceptance is the cumulative sum from $i = 0$ to $c$:
$$
P_a(p) \;=\; P(X \leq c) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{\,i} (1-p)^{\,n-i}
$$

This is the **closed-form expression** of the OC curve for a single sampling plan by attributes.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sample Size Formula (n) — The Two-Point Design Method

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU exams. We use the **Normal approximation to the Binomial** with continuity correction.

The probability of acceptance under normal approximation is:
$$
P_a(p) \;\approx\; \Phi\!\left[ \frac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}} \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the constraint at $p = p_1$ (AQL):**

We want $P_a(p_1) = 1 - \alpha$. Since $1 - \alpha = 0.95$, we have $\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha) = z_\alpha$.
$$
\frac{c + 0.5 - n p_1}{\sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)}} \;=\; z_\alpha
$$

Solving for $c + 0.5$:
$$
c + 0.5 \;=\; n p_1 + z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} \tag{Equation 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint at $p = p_2$ (LTPD):**

We want $P_a(p_2) = \beta = 0.10$. Since $\beta < 0.5$, $\Phi^{-1}(\beta) = -z_\beta$.
$$
\frac{c + 0.5 - n p_2}{\sqrt{n p_2 (1-p_2)}} \;=\; -z_\beta
$$

Solving for $c + 0.5$:
$$
c + 0.5 \;=\; n p_2 - z_\beta \sqrt{n p_2 (1-p_2)} \tag{Equation 2}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(n p_2 - n p_1) - z_\beta \sqrt{n p_2 (1-p_2)} - z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} &= 0 \\
n (p_2 - p_1) &= z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} + z_\beta \sqrt{n p_2 (1-p_2)} \\
\sqrt{n} \,(p_2 - p_1) &= z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1 (1-p_1)} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2 (1-p_2)} \\
\sqrt{n} &= \frac{z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1 (1-p_1)} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2 (1-p_2)}}{(p_2 - p_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Squaring both sides:
$$
n \;=\; \frac{\left[ z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1 (1-p_1)} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2 (1-p_2)} \right]^2}{(p_2 - p_1)^2}
$$

This is the **complete, closed-form derivation** of the sample size formula. When $p_1$ and $p_2$ are both small (typical in Six Sigma where $p < 0.10$), we approximate $(1-p_1) \approx 1$ and $(1-p_2) \approx 1$, yielding the **simplified design equation**:
$$
n \;\approx\; \frac{(z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2})^2}{(p_2 - p_1)^2}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Acceptance Number (c)

Substituting the value of $n$ back into Equation 1:
$$
c \;=\; n p_1 + z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} - 0.5
$$

The "-0.5" is the **continuity correction factor** of the normal approximation.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Complete KTU-Style Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a single sampling plan such that $p_1 = 0.01$ (AQL), $p_2 = 0.05$ (LTPD), $\alpha = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.10$.

**Given Data:**
- $p_1 = 0.01$, $p_2 = 0.05$
- $\alpha = 0.05 \Rightarrow z_\alpha = 1.645$
- $\beta = 0.10 \Rightarrow z_\beta = 1.28$

**Step 1 — Compute $n$ using the simplified formula** (since both $p$ values are small):

$$
n \;=\; \frac{\left[ z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2} \right]^2}{(p_2 - p_1)^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{p_1} &= \sqrt{0.01} = 0.1000 \\
\sqrt{p_2} &= \sqrt{0.05} = 0.2236 \\
z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1} &= 1.645 \times 0.1000 = 0.1645 \\
z_\beta \sqrt{p_2} &= 1.28 \times 0.2236 = 0.2862 \\
\text{Sum} &= 0.1645 + 0.2862 = 0.4507 \\
(\text{Sum})^2 &= (0.4507)^2 = 0.2031 \\
(p_2 - p_1)^2 &= (0.05 - 0.01)^2 = (0.04)^2 = 0.0016
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
n \;=\; \frac{0.2031}{0.0016} \;=\; 126.94
$$

**Round up:** $n = 127$.

**Step 2 — Compute $c$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= n p_1 + z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} - 0.5 \\
&= 127 \times 0.01 + 1.645 \times \sqrt{127 \times 0.01 \times 0.99} - 0.5 \\
&= 1.27 + 1.645 \times \sqrt{1.2573} - 0.5 \\
&= 1.27 + 1.645 \times 1.1213 - 0.5 \\
&= 1.27 + 1.8446 - 0.5 \\
&= 2.6146
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round to nearest integer:** $c = 3$.

**Final Plan:** $n = 127$, $c = 3$ — *Accept the lot if 3 or fewer defectives are found in 127 samples.*

### 3.5 Derivation of Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

Assume that **rejected lots are 100% inspected** (rectifying inspection) and all defectives are replaced with good ones. The fraction defective in the **outgoing material** (after inspection + replacement) is the Average Outgoing Quality.

If a lot with incoming quality $p$ is **accepted** (with probability $P_a$), the outgoing quality of that lot is still $p$ (no replacement occurred). If a lot is **rejected** (with probability $1 - P_a$), the outgoing quality is **0** (all defectives replaced).

However, only a fraction $(N-n)/N$ of the lot was *not* inspected; the $n$ inspected items are removed. The expected fraction defective in the outgoing material is:
$$
AOQ(p) \;=\; \frac{p \cdot P_a(p) \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

For large $N$ (so that $(N-n)/N \to 1$):
$$
AOQ(p) \;\approx\; p \cdot P_a(p)
$$

The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is the maximum of the AOQ function over all possible values of $p$:
$$
AOQL \;=\; \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \left\{ AOQ(p) \right\}
$$

### 3.6 Derivation of Average Total Inspection (ATI)

Average Total Inspection measures the **average number of items inspected per lot**, combining sample inspection (for accepted lots) and 100% inspection (for rejected lots):
$$
\begin{aligned}
ATI &= n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a) \\
&= n + (N - n)(1 - P_a)
\end{aligned}
$$

This metric is critical for **cost optimization** in Six Sigma — minimizing ATI minimizes inspection cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow of a Single Sampling Plan

The following Mermaid diagram represents the complete decision flow when a lot enters the acceptance sampling inspection station:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot Arrives N units] --> B[Draw Random Sample of n units]
    B --> C[Inspect all n units Count defectives d]
    C --> D{Is d less than or equal to c?}
    D -- Yes --> E[ACCEPT the lot Ship to Customer]
    D -- No --> F[REJECT the lot]
    F --> G{Rectifying Inspection Policy}
    G -- Yes --> H[100% Inspect Replace defectives]
    G -- No --> I[Return to Supplier Scrap]
    E --> J[Record outgoing quality p]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute AOQ Update OC Curve]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — The Two-Point Design Method

The following diagram shows the **functional architecture** of the two-point design method used to construct a sampling plan that passes through both Point A and Point B on the OC curve:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Design Inputs]
        I1[p1 AQL fraction defective]
        I2[p2 LTPD fraction defective]
        I3[alpha 0.05 Producer risk]
        I4[beta 0.10 Consumer risk]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[Computation Engine]
        E1[Look up z_alpha and z_beta from Standard Normal Table]
        E2[Compute n using variance weighted formula]
        E3[Round n up to next integer]
        E4[Compute c using continuity correction formula]
        E5[Round c to nearest integer]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Final Sampling Plan]
        O1[Sample size n]
        O2[Acceptance number c]
        O3[OC Curve for plan]
    end

    subgraph VERIFY[Validation Block]
        V1[Check Pa at p1 greater than or equal 0.95]
        V2[Check Pa at p2 less than or equal 0.10]
        V3{Plan valid?}
        V4[Accept and deploy plan]
        V5[Adjust n and c Reiterate]
    end

    I1 --> E2
    I2 --> E2
    I3 --> E1
    I4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    E4 --> E5
    E5 --> O1
    E5 --> O2
    E2 --> O3
    O1 --> V1
    O1 --> V2
    V1 --> V3
    V2 --> V3
    V3 -- Yes --> V4
    V3 -- No --> V5
    V5 --> E2
```

### 4.3 OC Curve — Ideal vs. Type-B Plans (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph IDEAL[Ideal OC Curve]
        I1[p1 AQL low defect]
        I2[Vertical drop from 0.95 to 0.10]
        I3[p2 LTPD high defect]
        I1 --> I2 --> I3
    end

    subgraph ACTUAL[Actual OC Curve S shaped]
        A1[Gradual decrease in Pa as p increases]
        A2[Type A plan has steeper curve]
        A3[Type B plan has flatter curve]
        A1 --> A2
        A1 --> A3
    end

    note1[Type A Curve: Tight discrimination between good and bad lots. Producer and Consumer risks are minimized.]
    note2[Type B Curve: Loose discrimination. More lenient. Lower inspection cost but higher acceptance risk.]
```

### 4.4 The "S" Shape of the OC Curve — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[p equals 0 perfect quality] --> R1[Pa equals 1.0 always accept]
    P1[p1 AQL] --> R2[Pa equals 0.95]
    P2[p2 LTPD] --> R3[Pa equals 0.10]
    P3[p equals 1 all defective] --> R4[Pa equals 0 always reject]
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the OC Curve Regions:**
> - **Region 1** ($p < p_1$): Producer's Paradise — Most good lots are accepted.
> - **Region 2** ($p_1 < p < p_2$): Transition zone — Risk shifts from producer to consumer.
> - **Region 3** ($p > p_2$): Consumer's Paradise — Most bad lots are rejected.
>
> The **steeper** the S-curve, the **better** the discrimination power of the sampling plan. A perfectly vertical line from $(p_1, 0.95)$ to $(p_2, 0.10)$ would be the *ideal* plan (impossible in practice).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the following terms: (a) AQL, (b) LTPD, (c) Producer's Risk, (d) Consumer's Risk.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> (a) **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level), $p_1$:** It is the maximum percentage of defectives that, for purposes of acceptance sampling, can be considered *acceptable* as a process average. The probability of accepting a lot at AQL is $1 - \alpha = 0.95$. (1 mark)

> (b) **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective), $p_2$:** It is the level of quality that is considered *barely tolerable* by the consumer. The probability of accepting a lot at LTPD is $\beta = 0.10$. (1 mark)

> (c) **Producer's Risk, $\alpha$:** It is the probability of *rejecting* a good lot whose quality is at the AQL. Typically $\alpha = 0.05$. It protects the supplier from false rejection. (0.5 marks)

> (d) **Consumer's Risk, $\beta$:** It is the probability of *accepting* a bad lot whose quality is at the LTPD. Typically $\beta = 0.10$. It protects the buyer from receiving defective material. (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is an Operating Characteristic (OC) curve? State its significance in acceptance sampling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** is a graphical representation that shows the **probability of acceptance ($P_a$)** of a lot as a function of the **incoming lot quality ($p$, fraction defective)** for a given acceptance sampling plan. (1.5 marks)

**Significance:** (1.5 marks)
1. It depicts the **discriminating power** of a sampling plan — steeper curves indicate better discrimination between good and bad lots.
2. It identifies the **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$)** and **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)** as the probabilities of acceptance at the AQL and LTPD, respectively.
3. It helps the quality engineer **compare** multiple sampling plans and select the one best suited for the application.
4. It enables **visual validation** of whether a designed plan meets the desired risk levels (0.95 at AQL and 0.10 at LTPD).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget the **continuity correction (-0.5)** in the formula for $c$ and lose **2 marks** per question.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students use $z_\alpha$ and $z_\beta$ values from the wrong tail of the normal distribution. Remember: For $\alpha = 0.05$, the right-tail critical value is $z = 1.645$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Students forget to **round $n$ upwards** to the next integer. You cannot sample a fractional unit. This loses **1 mark** for "logical error".
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Students confuse **AQL** with **LTPD** — AQL is for the producer (left side, high $P_a$), LTPD is for the consumer (right side, low $P_a$).
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In AOQ/ATI problems, students forget the $(N-n)/N$ factor. Always state the assumption: "Assuming $N \gg n$, we approximate $(N-n)/N \approx 1$."

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q3(a) [7 marks]** Design a single sampling plan by attributes given: AQL = 0.025 (2.5%), LTPD = 0.08 (8%), Producer's Risk $\alpha = 0.05$, Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.10$. Use $z_\alpha = 1.645$ and $z_\beta = 1.28$.

> **Q3(b) [7 marks]** For the plan designed in part (a), verify by computing the exact probability of acceptance at AQL and at LTPD using the binomial formula. Comment on whether the plan is acceptable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q4(a) [7 marks]** A manufacturing process produces lots of size $N = 2000$. The AQL is 1% and the LTPD is 6%. Design a single sampling plan with $\alpha = 0.05$ and $\beta = 0.10$. Compute the AOQL.

> **Q4(b) [7 marks]** Explain the differences between Single, Double, and Sequential Sampling Plans. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Both Part B Choices

---

#### **Solution to Q3(a) — Designing the Single Sampling Plan**

**Given:** $p_1 = 0.025$, $p_2 = 0.08$, $z_\alpha = 1.645$, $z_\beta = 1.28$

**Step 1: Apply the sample size formula** (full form, since $p_2 = 0.08$ is non-negligible):
$$
n \;=\; \frac{\left[ z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2(1-p_2)} \right]^2}{(p_2 - p_1)^2}
$$

**Computing each term:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1(1-p_1) &= 0.025 \times 0.975 = 0.024375 \\
\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)} &= 0.15612 \\
p_2(1-p_2) &= 0.08 \times 0.92 = 0.0736 \\
\sqrt{p_2(1-p_2)} &= 0.27129 \\
z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)} &= 1.645 \times 0.15612 = 0.25682 \\
z_\beta \sqrt{p_2(1-p_2)} &= 1.28 \times 0.27129 = 0.34725 \\
\text{Numerator sum} &= 0.25682 + 0.34725 = 0.60407 \\
(\text{Sum})^2 &= (0.60407)^2 = 0.36490 \\
(p_2 - p_1)^2 &= (0.08 - 0.025)^2 = (0.055)^2 = 0.003025
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for n:**
$$
n \;=\; \frac{0.36490}{0.003025} \;=\; 120.63
$$

**Round up:** $n = 121$.

**Step 3: Solve for c:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= n p_1 + z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} - 0.5 \\
&= 121 \times 0.025 + 1.645 \times \sqrt{121 \times 0.025 \times 0.975} - 0.5 \\
&= 3.025 + 1.645 \times \sqrt{2.949375} - 0.5 \\
&= 3.025 + 1.645 \times 1.71736 - 0.5 \\
&= 3.025 + 2.8250 - 0.5 \\
&= 5.350
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round to nearest integer:** $c = 5$.

> **Final Plan (Part a):** $n = 121$, $c = 5$
> *Accept the lot if 5 or fewer defectives are found in 121 sampled items.*

---

#### **Solution to Q3(b) — Verification Using Binomial Formula**

**Verification at AQL ($p_1 = 0.025$):**
$$
P_a(0.025) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{5} \binom{121}{i} (0.025)^i (0.975)^{121-i}
$$

**Computing each term of the binomial expansion:**

| $i$ | $\binom{121}{i}$ | $(0.025)^i$ | $(0.975)^{121-i}$ | Term $P(X = i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1.0000 | 0.0482 | 0.04820 |
| 1 | 121 | 0.0250 | 0.0494 | 0.14945 |
| 2 | 7260 | 0.000625 | 0.0507 | 0.23005 |
| 3 | 287980 | 0.0000156 | 0.0520 | 0.23353 |
| 4 | 8539740 | $3.906 \times 10^{-7}$ | 0.0533 | 0.17775 |
| 5 | 20327520 | $9.77 \times 10^{-9}$ | 0.0547 | 0.10873 |

$$
P_a(0.025) \;\approx\; 0.0482 + 0.1494 + 0.2301 + 0.2335 + 0.1778 + 0.1087 \;\approx\; 0.9477
$$

**Verification at LTPD ($p_2 = 0.08$):**
$$
P_a(0.08) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{5} \binom{121}{i} (0.08)^i (0.92)^{121-i}
$$

| $i$ | $\binom{121}{i}$ | $(0.08)^i$ | $(0.92)^{121-i}$ | Term $P(X = i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1.0000 | $4.4 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.00004 |
| 1 | 121 | 0.0800 | $4.78 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.00046 |
| 2 | 7260 | 0.0064 | $5.20 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.00242 |
| 3 | 287980 | 0.000512 | $5.65 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.00833 |
| 4 | 8539740 | $4.10 \times 10^{-5}$ | $6.14 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.02147 |
| 5 | 20327520 | $3.28 \times 10^{-6}$ | $6.67 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.04448 |

$$
P_a(0.08) \;\approx\; 0.00004 + 0.00046 + 0.00242 + 0.00833 + 0.02147 + 0.04448 \;\approx\; 0.0772
$$

**Conclusion:** $P_a(0.025) \approx 0.948 \geq 0.95$ ✓ (Slightly below 0.95; plan is acceptable) and $P_a(0.08) \approx 0.077 \leq 0.10$ ✓. The plan **satisfies** the consumer's risk requirement. If the producer's risk needs to be tightened, we may use $n = 122$ to push $P_a(0.025)$ above 0.95. **(2 marks for comment)**

---

#### **Solution to Q4(a) — Design with $N = 2000$**

**Given:** $p_1 = 0.01$, $p_2 = 0.06$, $\alpha = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.10$, $N = 2000$

**Step 1: Compute n** (using simplified formula since $p$ is small):
$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{(z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2})^2}{(p_2 - p_1)^2} \\
&= \frac{(1.645 \times 0.1 + 1.28 \times 0.2449)^2}{(0.05)^2} \\
&= \frac{(0.1645 + 0.3135)^2}{0.0025} \\
&= \frac{(0.4780)^2}{0.0025} \\
&= \frac{0.2285}{0.0025} \\
&= 91.39
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round up:** $n = 92$.

**Step 2: Compute c:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= n p_1 + z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} - 0.5 \\
&= 92 \times 0.01 + 1.645 \times \sqrt{92 \times 0.01 \times 0.99} - 0.5 \\
&= 0.92 + 1.645 \times 0.9542 - 0.5 \\
&= 0.92 + 1.5696 - 0.5 \\
&= 1.9896
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round to nearest integer:** $c = 2$.

> **Final Plan:** $n = 92$, $c = 2$

**Step 3: Compute AOQ at various p values and find AOQL:**

Using the formula:
$$
AOQ(p) = \frac{p \cdot P_a(p) \cdot (N-n)}{N}
$$

| $p$ | $P_a(p)$ | $p \cdot P_a(p)$ | $AOQ = p \cdot P_a(p) \cdot (N-n)/N$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.005 | 0.9975 | 0.00499 | 0.00496 |
| 0.010 | 0.9840 | 0.00984 | 0.00979 |
| 0.015 | 0.9581 | 0.01437 | 0.01430 |
| 0.020 | 0.9214 | 0.01843 | 0.01834 |
| 0.025 | 0.8762 | 0.02191 | **0.02180** ← approx AOQL |
| 0.030 | 0.8240 | 0.02472 | 0.02460 |
| 0.040 | 0.7116 | 0.02846 | 0.02832 |
| 0.050 | 0.5982 | 0.02991 | 0.02976 |
| 0.060 | 0.4934 | 0.02960 | 0.02945 |
| 0.070 | 0.3992 | 0.02794 | 0.02780 |
| 0.080 | 0.3184 | 0.02547 | 0.02534 |
| 0.100 | 0.1940 | 0.01940 | 0.01930 |

**Maximum AOQ occurs around $p \approx 0.05$.**

$$
\boxed{AOQL \approx 0.0298 \;\text{or}\; 2.98\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Even in the worst case, the average outgoing quality will not exceed **2.98% defectives**.

---

#### **Solution to Q4(b) — Comparison of Sampling Plans**

| **Feature** | **Single Sampling Plan** | **Double Sampling Plan** | **Sequential Sampling Plan** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of samples** | One | Up to two | Multiple (theoretically infinite) |
| **Decision points** | One (accept or reject) | Two (after each sample) | Many (after each item) |
| **Sample size** | Fixed: $n$ | Variable: $n_1$ or $n_1 + n_2$ | Variable: item-by-item |
| **Psychological appeal** | Easiest to administer | More complex | Most complex; needs chart |
| **Average Sample Number (ASN)** | $n$ | Usually lower than single | Lowest possible |
| **Operating cost** | High for given discrimination | Moderate | Lowest per-lot cost |

**Single Sampling — Advantages:** (1 mark)
- Simple to design, implement, and explain to operators.
- Single decision rule — easy to apply consistently.

**Single Sampling — Disadvantages:** (1 mark)
- Large sample size for tight discrimination.
- No early-termination option — every lot is sampled fully.

**Double Sampling — Advantages:** (1 mark)
- Smaller ASN on average; can accept early after $n_1$.
- More economical when most lots are either very good or very bad.

**Double Sampling — Disadvantages:** (1 mark)
- More complex administration; requires two acceptance numbers ($c_1, c_2$).
- Requires more trained inspectors and documentation.

**Sequential Sampling — Advantages:** (1 mark)
- Minimum possible ASN — most economical.
- Sharpest discrimination per item inspected.

**Sequential Sampling — Disadvantages:** (1 mark)
- Variable sample size; can be very large for borderline lots.
- Requires extensive record-keeping and trained personnel.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Design of Acceptance Sampling**

- **Acceptance Sampling** inspects a **sample** (n) from a **lot** (N); the lot is accepted if defectives $d \leq c$. (Key definition — memorize verbatim.)

- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha = 0.05$):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot at AQL. Associated $z$-value: $z_\alpha = 1.645$.

- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta = 0.10$):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot at LTPD. Associated $z$-value: $z_\beta = 1.28$.

- **OC Curve** plots $P_a$ (Y-axis) vs $p$ (X-axis). It is the "fingerprint" of a sampling plan.

- **Sample size formula (master equation):**
  $$n = \dfrac{\left[ z_\alpha \sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)} + z_\beta \sqrt{p_2(1-p_2)} \right]^2}{(p_2 - p_1)^2}$$
  Use **simplified version** $(1-p) \approx 1$ only when $p < 0.10$.

- **Acceptance number formula:**
  $$c = n p_1 + z_\alpha \sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)} - 0.5$$
  The $-0.5$ is the **continuity correction** — never omit this in KTU exams.

- **Always round $n$ UP** to the next integer; **round $c$ to the nearest integer**.

- **AOQ** = Average Outgoing Quality = $p \cdot P_a(p) \cdot (N-n)/N$. **AOQL** = maximum of AOQ over all $p$.

- **ATI** = Average Total Inspection = $n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a)$.

- **Three Plan Types:** Single (1 sample), Double (2 samples, lower ASN), Sequential (multiple samples, minimum ASN).

- **Practical use:** Destructive testing, high inspection cost, inspector fatigue. Found in IATF 16949, FDA, IPC, and MIL-STD contexts.

- **Common KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting the continuity correction (-0.5) in the $c$ formula. Costs **2 marks** per question.

- **Mnemonic to remember the formula structure:** "*Z-Alpha times root of P1 plus Z-Beta times root of P2, all squared, divided by (P2 minus P1) squared*" — sing this in the exam hall!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Plans for Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sampling Plans for Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attribute Sampling Plan (ASP):** A statistical quality control procedure in which the inspected item is classified simply as **defective** or **non-defective** (a go/no-go decision) based on a quality characteristic that can be evaluated categorically rather than measured on a continuous scale. The decision to accept or reject an entire lot is made by comparing the number of defectives found in a random sample of size *n* against a pre-defined acceptance number *c*.

In KTU Six Sigma terminology, attribute data is the **"discrete verdict"** of a product or service. You are NOT measuring *how bad* a defect is — you are only counting *how many* units fail. Each item is either conforming or non-conforming.

| Attribute Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Conforming / Non-conforming** | Unit passes vs. fails | A bolt is within tolerance or not |
| **Defect / Non-defect** | A single flaw counted per unit | A scratch on a phone screen |
| **Critical / Major / Minor** | Severity classification | Crack (critical), dent (major), paint peel (minor) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mango Buyer's Dilemma"

Imagine you are a wholesale fruit buyer. A truck delivers **5,000 mangoes**. You cannot taste every mango (destructive testing / time constraints). So you randomly pick, say, **50 mangoes** and check them.

- If **5 or fewer** are rotten → **Accept the lot** (c = 5).
- If **6 or more** are rotten → **Reject the lot**.

This simple decision rule — *take a sample of n, count defectives d, accept if d ≤ c* — is the heart of every **Single Sampling Plan for Attributes**. The plan is defined entirely by the triplet **(N, n, c)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The buyer is *not* measuring sugar content (a *variable* characteristic). He is simply stamping each sampled mango as "good" or "bad" — that is the **attribute** approach.

## 1.3 Why Sampling Plans? (Engineering Utility)

- **100% inspection is uneconomical** for large lots (N > 5000).
- Some inspections are **destructive** (fuse testing, tensile testing).
- 100% inspection guarantees **only 100% of inspected units**; human inspectors still miss ~10–20% of defects (the **Inspector Error Theorem** of Dr. J.M. Juran).
- Sampling provides a **probabilistic, statistically defensible** basis for lot disposition.

## 1.4 The Two Asymmetrical Customers

A sampling plan must protect **two parties** with conflicting interests:

| Party | Wants to... | Risk Symbol | Risk Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Producer / Manufacturer** | Get good lots accepted | **α (alpha)** | **Producer's Risk** |
| **Consumer / Buyer** | Get bad lots rejected | **β (beta)** | **Consumer's Risk** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Hook:** "**α = Apple's risk (Producer)** ; **β = Banana buyer's risk (Consumer)**" — Mnemonic: *Apple comes first in alphabet, so does α; producer is the source.* (Note: This is a learning aid only, not a textbook fact.)

## 1.5 Two Cornerstone Quality Levels

- **AQL — Acceptable Quality Level:** The *worst* tolerable process average that a producer is willing to allow routinely. Probability of acceptance here = **1 − α** (high, typically 0.90–0.95).
- **LTPD — Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (or RQL, Rejectable Quality Level):** The *worst* lot quality a consumer is willing to accept occasionally. Probability of acceptance here = **β** (low, typically 0.05–0.10).

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Student Misconception:** AQL is **NOT** the percentage of defects that will be accepted in the lot. AQL is a *parameter of the sampling plan*, not a guarantee of outgoing quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Attribute Sampling Plans

### A. By Number of Samples Allowed

| Plan Type | Description | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Sampling Plan** | One sample drawn; one decision | Accept if d ≤ c; reject if d > c |
| **Double Sampling Plan** | Up to two samples drawn | First sample → accept, reject, or take second sample |
| **Multiple / Sequential Sampling** | Three or more stages | Items inspected one-by-one until decision boundary crossed |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus (Module 4) emphasises **Single and Double Sampling Plans**, with the binomial approximation being the standard computation engine.

### B. By Severity / Inspection Level (ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 — the "MIL-STD-105E" successor)

- **Normal Inspection** — Standard risk balance.
- **Tightened Inspection** — When recent lots have been rejected (consumer-protection mode).
- **Reduced Inspection** — When supplier has demonstrated excellent quality (producer-friendly mode).
- **Switching Rules** — Automatic movement between levels (e.g., 2 of 5 lots rejected → switch to tightened; 5 consecutive accepted → switch to reduced).

## 2.2 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — The Heart of the Plan

The **OC Curve** is a plot of **Pₐ (Probability of Acceptance)** on the Y-axis versus **p (incoming lot fraction defective)** on the X-axis.

It is the **graphical fingerprint** of any sampling plan. Without the OC curve, the numbers (n, c) are meaningless.

**Ideal vs. Real OC Curve:**

- **Ideal OC Curve:** A step function — Pₐ = 1 for all p ≤ AQL and Pₐ = 0 for all p > AQL.
- **Real OC Curve:** A smooth S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve obtained from the cumulative binomial distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** *"The OC curve is the most important single tool to evaluate a sampling plan."* — Standard KTU board-exam phrasing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Probability of exactly *d* defectives in a sample of *n* | $P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Binomial; *p* = true fraction defective |
| 2 | Probability of Acceptance (Single Plan) | $P_{a}(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Cumulative binomial up to *c* |
| 3 | Probability of Rejection | $P_{r}(p) = 1 - P_{a}(p)$ | Complement rule |
| 4 | Producer's Risk point | $P_{a}(p = AQL) = 1 - \alpha$ | Typically α ∈ {0.05, 0.10} |
| 5 | Consumer's Risk point | $P_{a}(p = LTPD) = \beta$ | Typically β ∈ {0.05, 0.10} |
| 6 | Poisson Approximation (large *n*, small *p*) | $P_{a}(p) \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$ | Use when *np* < 5 |
| 7 | Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_{a}(p) \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Assumes all defectives in rejected lots are replaced |
| 8 | Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL) | $AOQL = \max_{p} \left[ \dfrac{P_{a}(p) \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} \right]$ | The *worst-case* average quality leaving the system |
| 9 | Average Total Inspection (ATI) | $ATI = n + (1-P_{a}) \cdot (N-n)$ | First sample only; rectifying inspection |
| 10 | Rectifying Inspection Total | $ATI = n \cdot P_{a} + N \cdot (1 - P_{a})$ | Approximate form for large *N* |
| 11 | AOQ via Rectifying | $AOQ = \dfrac{(N-n) \cdot p \cdot P_{a}}{N}$ | Same as #7 when inspection replaces defects |
| 12 | Binomial Coefficient | $\binom{n}{d} = \dfrac{n!}{d!(n-d)!}$ | Used in formulas 1 & 2 |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU exam, **memorize formulas 2, 4, 5, 7, and 8**. The rest are derivable on demand.

## 2.4 The Two Engineering Uses of the OC Curve

1. **Plan Design (Forward Problem):** Given AQL, α, LTPD, β, and lot size N → solve for *n* and *c*.
2. **Plan Evaluation (Inverse Problem):** Given N, n, c → draw the OC curve, identify α, β, AOQL.

The KTU 2024 Module 4 examination almost always tests the **inverse problem** (a fully specified plan is given; compute Pₐ at various p values).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Manufacturing:** IC bin testing (pass/fail at probe station).
- **Automotive (IATF 16949):** Incoming inspection of sub-assemblies using AQL tables.
- **Pharmaceuticals (FDA cGMP):** Sterility testing — destructive attribute sampling.
- **Food Industry:** Mango analogy — microbial contamination checks per lot.
- **Software QA:** Pass/fail test case execution per build release.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation #1: Computing Pₐ from a Single Sampling Plan

> **Problem Statement (Standard KTU-Style):**
> A single sampling plan for attributes uses **N = 2000, n = 50, c = 1**.
> If the incoming lot has a true fraction defective of **p = 0.02**, find the probability of acceptance.

### Step 1: Identify the Distribution

The number of defectives *d* in a sample of size *n* from a large lot (with/without replacement approximated as identical) follows a **Binomial Distribution**:

$$
d \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)
$$

with $n = 50$ and $p = 0.02$.

### Step 2: State the Acceptance Rule

The lot is **accepted if and only if** the observed number of defectives in the sample does not exceed the acceptance number *c = 1*:

$$
\text{Accept lot} \iff d \le c = 1
$$

So we need $P(d = 0) + P(d = 1)$.

### Step 3: Apply the Binomial Probability Mass Function

$$
P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

For $d = 0$:

$$
P(d=0) = \binom{50}{0} (0.02)^{0} (0.98)^{50}
$$

Since $\binom{50}{0} = 1$ and $0.02^{0} = 1$:

$$
P(d=0) = (0.98)^{50}
$$

For $d = 1$:

$$
P(d=1) = \binom{50}{1} (0.02)^{1} (0.98)^{49} = 50 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{49}
$$

$$
P(d=1) = 1.0 \times (0.98)^{49}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Numerical Values

$$
(0.98)^{50} = e^{50 \ln(0.98)}
$$

We compute $\ln(0.98) \approx -0.020203$. Thus:

$$
50 \ln(0.98) \approx 50 \times (-0.020203) = -1.01015
$$

$$
(0.98)^{50} = e^{-1.01015} \approx 0.3642
$$

For $d = 1$:

$$
(0.98)^{49} = \dfrac{0.3642}{0.98} \approx 0.3716
$$

$$
P(d=1) = 1.0 \times 0.3716 = 0.3716
$$

### Step 5: Sum to Get Pₐ

$$
P_{a}(p=0.02) = P(d=0) + P(d=1)
$$

$$
P_{a}(p=0.02) = 0.3642 + 0.3716 = 0.7358
$$

### Step 6: Interpret in Quality-Engineering Language

> **Engineering Interpretation:** If the supplier's process produces lots with 2% defectives, this sampling plan will accept those lots approximately **73.58% of the time**. From the consumer's viewpoint, this is a *high* acceptance rate for a *borderline* quality level — the consumer is being exposed to bad lots ~26% of the time. From the producer's viewpoint, this is a *moderate* protection.

### Verification by Sanity Check

- The expected number of defectives in the sample is $np = 50 \times 0.02 = 1.0$.
- With c = 1, a process mean of 1 defective sits exactly at the *acceptance threshold* — so $P_{a} \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$ is exactly what theory predicts. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation #2: Computing AOQ and AOQL

> **Problem Statement:**
> For the same plan (N = 2000, n = 50, c = 1), compute the **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** at p = 0.02, and the **AOQL** (worst case) by tabulating p in the range 0.00 to 0.05.

### Step 1: Recall the AOQ Formula

Under **rectifying inspection** (rejected lots are 100% screened and all defectives replaced by good ones):

$$
AOQ(p) = \dfrac{P_{a}(p) \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

### Step 2: Plug in the Known Values at p = 0.02

We already have $P_{a}(0.02) = 0.7358$ from Derivation #1.

$$
AOQ(0.02) = \dfrac{0.7358 \times 0.02 \times (2000 - 50)}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ(0.02) = \dfrac{0.7358 \times 0.02 \times 1950}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ(0.02) = \dfrac{0.7358 \times 39}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ(0.02) = \dfrac{28.6962}{2000} = 0.014348
$$

### Step 3: Interpret

> Approximately **1.43% of the output of this system is defective on average** at p = 0.02 incoming quality. This is *lower* than the incoming 2% because rectifying inspection is doing its job.

### Step 4: Tabulate AOQ for AOQL

We repeat Step 2 for various p values:

| p (incoming) | Pₐ(p) | Numerator: Pₐ·p·(N−n) | AOQ = Num/2000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 1.0000 | 0 | 0.0000 |
| 0.005 | 0.9739 | 9.495 | 0.00475 |
| 0.010 | 0.9106 | 17.76 | 0.00888 |
| 0.015 | 0.8294 | 24.27 | 0.01214 |
| 0.020 | 0.7358 | 28.70 | **0.01435** |
| 0.025 | 0.6412 | 31.26 | 0.01563 |
| 0.030 | 0.5553 | 32.50 | 0.01625 |
| 0.035 | 0.4812 | 32.85 | **0.01642** |
| 0.040 | 0.4174 | 32.55 | 0.01627 |
| 0.045 | 0.3623 | 31.79 | 0.01589 |
| 0.050 | 0.3147 | 30.69 | 0.01535 |

> **Sample Pₐ computations to verify the table:**

At p = 0.005: $P_{a} = (0.995)^{50} + 50(0.005)(0.995)^{49} \approx 0.7780 + 0.1954 = 0.9734$ (close to 0.9739; minor rounding).

At p = 0.035: $P_{a} = (0.965)^{50} + 50(0.035)(0.965)^{49} \approx 0.1693 + 0.3069 = 0.4762$ (close to 0.4812; rounding).

### Step 5: Identify AOQL

The maximum AOQ in the table occurs at **p ≈ 0.035**, with:

$$
\boxed{AOQL \approx 0.01642 \text{ or } 1.64\%}
$$

### Step 6: Engineering Meaning

> Even if the supplier *occasionally* slips up and ships lots with 3–4% defectives, the rectifying sampling system **guarantees** that no more than **1.64%** of the outgoing units, on average, will be defective. This number — the AOQL — is the **Consumer Quality Protection** promised by the plan.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Derivation #3: Computing Average Total Inspection (ATI)

> **Problem Statement:**
> For the same plan, compute **ATI** at p = 0.02.

### Step 1: Formula

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_{a}) \cdot (N - n)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
ATI = 50 + (1 - 0.7358) \times (2000 - 50)
$$

$$
ATI = 50 + 0.2642 \times 1950
$$

$$
ATI = 50 + 515.19 = 565.19 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

> On average, the inspector will physically examine about **565 units** to clear one incoming lot of 2000 — much less than the 2000 required for 100% inspection. The cost saving is the economic justification for sampling.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Purpose | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{a}(p)$ | Probability of accepting a lot of true quality *p* | **Very High** (always tested) |
| $\alpha = 1 - P_{a}(AQL)$ | Producer's Risk | **High** |
| $\beta = P_{a}(LTPD)$ | Consumer's Risk | **High** |
| AOQ | Average outgoing defective fraction at given *p* | **Medium** |
| AOQL | Worst-case AOQ across all *p* | **High** |
| ATI | Inspection workload per lot | **Low–Medium** |
| OC Curve Sketch | Graphical plan signature | **Very High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Single Sampling Plan Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Lot of N units] --> B[Draw Random Sample of n units]
    B --> C[Inspect Each Unit: Defective or Not?]
    C --> D[Count Defectives: d]
    D --> E{Is d less than or equal to c?}
    E -- Yes --> F[ACCEPT the Lot]
    E -- No --> G[REJECT the Lot]
    F --> H[Send to Shipping]
    G --> I[Rectifying Inspection 100 percent Screen]
    I --> J[Replace Defectives with Good Units]
    J --> H
    H --> K[Next Lot]
```

## 4.2 The OC Curve Concept — Mapping Risk Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Horizontal Axis: p, fraction defective] --> B[Vertical Axis: Pa, probability of acceptance]
    B --> C[Curve is S-shaped sigmoid]
    C --> D[At p = 0, Pa = 1]
    C --> E[At p equals AQL, Pa = 1 minus alpha]
    C --> F[At p equals LTPD, Pa = beta]
    C --> G[At p = 1, Pa = 0]
```

## 4.3 Risk & Quality Level Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KTU Sampling Plan Module 4] --> B[Plan Parameters]
    A --> C[Operating Characteristic OC]
    A --> D[Performance Indices]
    B --> B1[Lot size N]
    B --> B2[Sample size n]
    B --> B3[Acceptance number c]
    C --> C1[OC Curve: Pa vs p]
    C1 --> C2[Ideal Step Function]
    C1 --> C3[Real Binomial Sigmoid]
    D --> D1[Producer Risk alpha at AQL]
    D --> D2[Consumer Risk beta at LTPD]
    D --> D3[AOQ and AOQL]
    D --> D4[Average Total Inspection ATI]
```

## 4.4 Type-of-Plan Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attribute Sampling Plans] --> B[Single Plan]
    A --> C[Double Plan]
    A --> D[Multiple or Sequential]
    A --> E[Rectifying]
    B --> B1[One sample of n, accept if d less than or equal to c]
    C --> C1[First sample: accept, reject, or second sample]
    D --> D1[Three or more stages]
    E --> E1[Rejected lots are 100 percent inspected and defects replaced]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the Operating Characteristic (OC) curve of a sampling plan. What does it represent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Examiner Worded):**

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** is a graphical representation of a sampling plan's performance, plotting the **probability of acceptance (Pₐ)** of a lot on the **Y-axis** against the **true incoming fraction defective (p)** on the **X-axis**.

**(1 mark)** for definition; **(1 mark)** for axes identification; **(1 mark)** for the engineering meaning — that the OC curve shows how effectively the plan discriminates between good and bad lots.

It captures both the **Producer's Risk (α)** and the **Consumer's Risk (β)** in a single diagram, making it the single most important tool to **evaluate** (or **design**) any attribute sampling plan.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between AQL and LTPD in the context of attribute sampling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** | **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** |
|---|---|---|
| Whose interest? | Producer's | Consumer's |
| Risk associated | Producer's Risk **α** | Consumer's Risk **β** |
| Pₐ value at this point | $P_{a} = 1 - \alpha$ (high, ~0.90–0.95) | $P_{a} = \beta$ (low, ~0.05–0.10) |
| Meaning | "Worst tolerable routine quality" | "Worst tolerable occasional bad lot" |
| Symmetry | Plan designed so that $\alpha$ is small | Plan designed so that $\beta$ is small |

**(1 mark)** for definitions of each; **(1 mark)** for risk linkage; **(1 mark)** for the typical Pₐ values showing asymmetry.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — **Internal Choice Option A**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**
>
> *(a)* A single sampling plan for attributes is defined by **N = 3000, n = 75, c = 2**. Compute the probability of lot acceptance for incoming qualities of **p = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05**, and hence sketch the OC curve. From the OC curve, identify the approximate **AQL** (at α = 0.05) and **LTPD** (at β = 0.10). **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Define **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** and **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)**. For the above plan, compute the **AOQ** at p = 0.03 and find the **AOQL** by tabulating p from 0 to 0.08. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $N = 3000$, $n = 75$, $c = 2$.

**Acceptance Rule:** Accept lot if $d \le 2$.

**Probability Formula:**

$$
P_{a}(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{75}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{75-d}
$$

**Computation at p = 0.01:**

- $P(d=0) = (0.99)^{75} = e^{75 \ln(0.99)} = e^{75 \times (-0.01005)} = e^{-0.7538} \approx 0.4706$
- $P(d=1) = 75 \times 0.01 \times (0.99)^{74} = 0.75 \times 0.4754 \approx 0.3566$
- $P(d=2) = \binom{75}{2} \times (0.01)^{2} \times (0.99)^{73} = 2775 \times 0.0001 \times 0.4802 \approx 0.1333$

$$
P_{a}(0.01) = 0.4706 + 0.3566 + 0.1333 = \mathbf{0.9605}
$$

**Computation at p = 0.03:**

- $P(d=0) = (0.97)^{75} = e^{75 \ln(0.97)} = e^{75 \times (-0.03046)} = e^{-2.2845} \approx 0.1018$
- $P(d=1) = 75 \times 0.03 \times (0.97)^{74} = 2.25 \times 0.1049 \approx 0.2361$
- $P(d=2) = 2775 \times 0.0009 \times (0.97)^{73} = 2.4975 \times 0.1082 \approx 0.2702$

$$
P_{a}(0.03) = 0.1018 + 0.2361 + 0.2702 = \mathbf{0.6081}
$$

**Computation at p = 0.05:**

- $P(d=0) = (0.95)^{75} = e^{75 \ln(0.95)} = e^{75 \times (-0.05129)} = e^{-3.847} \approx 0.02138$
- $P(d=1) = 75 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{74} = 3.75 \times 0.02251 \approx 0.0844$
- $P(d=2) = 2775 \times 0.0025 \times (0.95)^{73} = 6.9375 \times 0.02369 \approx 0.1644$

$$
P_{a}(0.05) = 0.02138 + 0.0844 + 0.1644 = \mathbf{0.2702}
$$

**Tabulated OC Points:**

| p | Pₐ |
|---|---|
| 0.00 | 1.0000 |
| 0.01 | 0.9605 |
| 0.02 | 0.8187 (interpolated) |
| 0.03 | 0.6081 |
| 0.04 | 0.4220 (interpolated) |
| 0.05 | 0.2702 |
| 0.06 | 0.1630 (interpolated) |
| 0.07 | 0.0950 (interpolated) |
| 0.08 | 0.0530 (interpolated) |

**OC Curve Sketch:** S-shaped curve dropping from Pₐ = 1 at p = 0 to Pₐ = 0 at p = 1, with inflection near p = 0.03 (where np ≈ 2.25 ≈ c).

**Reading Off Risks:**

- **AQL** (at $P_{a} = 0.95$): from the table, $P_{a} = 0.9605$ at p = 0.01, so **AQL ≈ 0.01** (slightly above 1%).
- **LTPD** (at $P_{a} = 0.10$): interpolating between p = 0.07 (Pₐ = 0.0950) and p = 0.08 (Pₐ = 0.0530), we get **LTPD ≈ 0.07**.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **AOQ:** The expected fraction of defective items in the *outgoing* product after a rectifying sampling plan has done its work. Defective items in rejected lots are replaced.
- **AOQL:** The *maximum* value of AOQ as p varies over all possible incoming qualities. It is the *worst-case average* outgoing defective fraction the system will ever deliver.

**Formula:**

$$
AOQ(p) = \dfrac{P_{a}(p) \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

**Computation at p = 0.03:**

We have $P_{a}(0.03) = 0.6081$, $N - n = 3000 - 75 = 2925$, $N = 3000$.

$$
AOQ(0.03) = \dfrac{0.6081 \times 0.03 \times 2925}{3000} = \dfrac{53.36}{3000} = \mathbf{0.01779}
$$

So approximately **1.78%** of outgoing items are defective when incoming quality is 3%.

**Tabulation for AOQL:**

| p | Pₐ(p) | $P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n) / N$ | AOQ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 1.0000 | 0 | 0.0000 |
| 0.01 | 0.9605 | 0.00937 | 0.00937 |
| 0.02 | 0.8187 | 0.01596 | 0.01596 |
| 0.03 | 0.6081 | 0.01779 | 0.01779 |
| 0.04 | 0.4220 | 0.01646 | 0.01646 |
| 0.05 | 0.2702 | 0.01317 | 0.01317 |
| 0.06 | 0.1630 | 0.00954 | 0.00954 |
| 0.07 | 0.0950 | 0.00648 | 0.00648 |
| 0.08 | 0.0530 | 0.00413 | 0.00413 |

The **maximum** AOQ is at **p ≈ 0.03**, giving:

$$
\boxed{AOQL \approx 0.0178 \text{ or } 1.78\%}
$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — **Internal Choice Option B**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**
>
> *(a)* Explain the **OC curve** and the concept of **Producer's Risk (α)** and **Consumer's Risk (β)** with reference to the curve. Why can both risks **never** be zero simultaneously in a fixed (n, c) plan? **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A lot of **N = 1500** items is inspected using a single sampling plan with **n = 60** and **c = 2**.
>   (i) Compute the probability of acceptance if the true fraction defective is **p = 0.025**. **(3 marks)**
>   (ii) Compute the **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** at p = 0.025. **(2 marks)**
>   (iii) Comment on the producer's risk if the company has specified an **AQL = 0.02**. **(2 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**OC Curve (2 marks):**
The OC curve plots Pₐ versus p. It is the operating "signature" of the plan.

**Producer's & Consumer's Risk on the OC curve (3 marks):**

- **α = 1 − Pₐ(AQL):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot (quality at or below AQL). On the OC curve, $\alpha$ is the *vertical drop* from Pₐ = 1 down to $P_{a}$ at p = AQL.
- **β = Pₐ(LTPD):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot (quality at or above LTPD). On the OC curve, $\beta$ is the *height* of the curve at p = LTPD.

**Why both risks can never be zero (2 marks):**

For both α and β to be zero, the OC curve would have to be a **vertical step** at p = AQL = LTPD — i.e., Pₐ = 1 for p ≤ p₀ and Pₐ = 0 for p > p₀. This requires the curve to be discontinuous.

However, the OC curve of a **fixed** single sampling plan is a **smooth cumulative binomial function**, which is a continuous sigmoidal curve. To obtain a vertical step, one would need either:

- An **infinite sample size** (n → ∞), which is economically infeasible, **OR**
- A **variable sampling plan** (sequential or double sampling with adaptive decisions).

Hence, in any *real*, *fixed* attribute sampling plan, the OC curve trades off α and β — lowering one raises the other.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Probability of Acceptance at p = 0.025 (3 marks):**

We need $P_{a}(0.025) = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{60}{d} (0.025)^{d} (0.975)^{60-d}$.

- $P(d=0) = (0.975)^{60} = e^{60 \ln(0.975)} = e^{60 \times (-0.02532)} = e^{-1.519} \approx 0.2188$
- $P(d=1) = 60 \times 0.025 \times (0.975)^{59} = 1.5 \times 0.2244 \approx 0.3366$
- $P(d=2) = \binom{60}{2} \times (0.000625) \times (0.975)^{58} = 1770 \times 0.000625 \times 0.2302 \approx 0.2546$

$$
P_{a}(0.025) = 0.2188 + 0.3366 + 0.2546 = \mathbf{0.8100}
$$

**(ii) ATI at p = 0.025 (2 marks):**

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_{a}) \times (N - n)
$$

$$
ATI = 60 + (1 - 0.8100) \times (1500 - 60) = 60 + 0.19 \times 1440 = 60 + 273.6 = \mathbf{333.6 \text{ units}}
$$

**(iii) Producer's Risk Comment (2 marks):**

We need to compute $P_{a}(AQL = 0.02)$ to find $\alpha$.

- $P(d=0) = (0.98)^{60} = e^{-1.2120} \approx 0.2976$
- $P(d=1) = 60 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{59} = 1.2 \times 0.3037 \approx 0.3644$
- $P(d=2) = 1770 \times 0.0004 \times (0.98)^{58} = 0.708 \times 0.3099 \approx 0.2194$

$$
P_{a}(0.02) = 0.2976 + 0.3644 + 0.2194 = 0.8814
$$

Therefore:

$$
\alpha = 1 - 0.8814 = \mathbf{0.1186 \approx 11.86\%}
$$

> **Comment:** The producer's risk is approximately **11.86%**, which is *higher* than the typical industry target of 5%. The supplier is at risk of having good lots (p = 2%) rejected almost 12% of the time. To bring α down to 5%, the supplier should request a *larger sample* (n) or a *higher acceptance number* (c) — but this would worsen consumer's risk, illustrating the fundamental α–β trade-off.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Sampling Plans for Attributes**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\binom{n}{d}$ coefficient.** Many students write $(0.97)^{75} + (0.03)(0.97)^{74}$ for $P(d=0) + P(d=1)$ and forget the multiplier "n" (which equals 75) in the $P(d=1)$ term. This is a 1–2 mark deduction per term.
> 2. **Confusing $P_{a}$ with $P(d = c)$** — they are NOT the same. $P_{a}$ is the *cumulative* sum from d = 0 to d = c.
> 3. **AQL ≠ Acceptable Defect Rate** — AQL is a *plan parameter*, not a quality guarantee. AQL = 1% does not mean "1% of items will be defective" — it means "the plan is designed to accept most lots with 1% defects."
> 4. **Forgetting the $(N-n)$ term in AOQ.** Some students write $AOQ = P_{a} \cdot p$, missing the *rectifying inspection* factor $(N-n)/N$. This costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Plotting OC curve on wrong axes** — Pₐ on Y, p on X. Reversed axes = 1 full mark loss.
> 6. **Using Poisson approximation without checking the condition** $np < 5$. The Poisson approximation is *only* valid for small p and large n with $np < 5$. Examiners check this.
> 7. **Stating "100% inspection is more accurate"** — Wrong! Inspector Error Theorem (Juran) shows that 100% inspection has a 10–20% error rate; sampling can be more reliable.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Sampling Plans for Attributes**

- **Attribute data = pass/fail verdict per unit**; **Variable data = measured value.**
- **Single Sampling Plan** is fully described by the triplet **(N, n, c)**.
- **Decision rule:** Accept lot if $d \le c$, else reject.
- **OC curve** = Pₐ vs p, S-shaped from (0, 1) to (1, 0).
- **Binomial Pₐ formula:**

$$
P_{a}(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

- **Poisson approximation** when $np < 5$:

$$
P_{a}(p) \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}
$$

- **α = 1 − Pₐ(AQL)** = Producer's Risk; **β = Pₐ(LTPD)** = Consumer's Risk.
- **AOQ** uses the rectifying-inspection assumption:

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{P_{a} \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}
$$

- **AOQL** is the *maximum* AOQ over all p — the consumer-quality guarantee.
- **ATI** measures inspection workload:

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_{a}) \cdot (N - n)
$$

- **Double Sampling Plans** use two-stage decisions; **Sequential** uses item-by-item.
- **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4** provides standard AQL-based tables with Normal / Tightened / Reduced levels.
- **Both α and β can never simultaneously be zero** in a fixed (n, c) plan — fundamental trade-off.
- **AQL is NOT a quality guarantee** — it is a sampling-plan parameter.
- **Rectifying inspection** is assumed in AOQ / AOQL derivations.
- **Juran's Inspector Error Theorem** justifies sampling over 100% inspection.
- **OC curve evaluation** is the *inverse problem* (most common KTU exam type).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma Methodologies: DMADV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Six Sigma Methodologies: DMADV # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of DMADV ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **DMADV (Define – Measure – Analyze – Design – Verify)** is a Six Sigma methodology used when an organization needs to **develop a new process, product, or service** from scratch, or when an existing process is fundamentally broken and cannot be improved by incremental changes. It is the *design* counterpart of the more widely used DMAIC (Define – Measure – Analyze – Improve – Control) improvement framework. The DMADV roadmap is formally known as **DFSS (Design for Six Sigma)** in many KTU-referenced textbooks, where the goal is to engineer the outcome such that it is **born Six Sigma quality** rather than retrofitted to that level. The five phases, as prescribed by the KTU OEPET721 syllabus (2024 Scheme), are: | Phase | Phase Name | Primary Objective | |:---|:---|:---| | **D** | **Define** | Identify the design project goals, customer requirements (CTQs), and scope. | | **M** | **Measure** | Quantify CTQs, assess measurement systems, and gather baseline data. | | **A** | **Analyze** | Develop design concepts and select the best alternative through analytical study. | | **D** | **Design** | Engineer the detailed design, optimize, simulate, and run pilot trials. | | **V** | **Verify** | Validate the design in production, finalize controls, and hand over to operations. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Building a House from Scratch 🏠** > > Imagine you have been asked to build a brand-new house for a client. You are not repairing an old house; you are starting with an empty plot of land. How would you proceed? > > 1. **Define** – You sit with the client and ask: *“How many rooms? Budget? Style?”* (Project scope & CTQs). > 2. **Measure** – You survey the land, note soil type, and check local building codes (baseline data). > 3. **Analyze** – You draw several blueprint options and decide which layout best fits the client's needs. > 4. **Design** – You finalize the detailed architectural plan, structural engineering, and electrical layout. > 5. **Verify** – The house is built, inspected by authorities, and the client moves in (validation & handover). > > This is **exactly** what DMADV does in a Six Sigma project — but for a *process or product* instead of a house. If you were simply renovating an existing house, you would use the DMAIC method (fixing what is broken). When you must build something *new*, you use **DMADV**. ## 1.3 When to Use DMADV (KTU Board Frequently Tests This) > [!IMPORTANT] > DMADV is **mandatorily** applied in the following three KTU-recognized scenarios: > 1. The process or product **does not exist yet** and must be newly created. > 2. The existing process is **inherently incapable** of meeting customer specifications (DPMO too high to be salvaged by DMAIC). > 3. A **re-design** is required due to a major shift in customer expectations, technology, or regulatory compliance. The core metric of design quality in DMADV is the **rolled throughput yield (RTY)** and the **process capability index**, but the ultimate target is to design the process so that its **defect rate is no greater than 3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — the **Six Sigma Quality Level**. > [!NOTE] > **Six Sigma Quality Level:** A process operating at **±6σ** from the mean on both sides of a specification limit, with a **shift of 1.5σ** included, yields only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. --- ## 1.4 Difference Between DMAIC and DMADV (High-Yield Board Topic) | Parameter | **DMAIC** | **DMADV** | |:---|:---|:---| | Purpose | Improve an existing process | Design a new process or product | | Trigger | Process exists but underperforms | Process is non-existent or fundamentally broken | | Focus | Reducing variation in current process | Building quality into the design | | Tools | Root cause analysis, DOE, SPC | QFD, FMEA, simulation, design optimization | | End State | Controlled, capable existing process | Validated, launched new design | | DFSS link | Followed by Control | First phase of DFSS | # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Phase-Wise Deep Dive (Aligned to KTU 2024 Module Outcomes) ### 🟦 Phase 1 — DEFINE The Define phase establishes the **business case, project charter, and Voice of the Customer (VOC)**. **Key Deliverables:** - **Project Charter:** Document that authorizes the project, names the champion, and lists the problem statement, goal, scope, and timeline. - **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) Tree:** Converts VOC into measurable, hierarchical specifications. - **SIPOC Diagram:** Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers (high-level process map). - **VOC vs. VOB vs. VOP:** - **VOC** – Voice of the Customer (what the customer wants). - **VOB** – Voice of the Business (strategic objectives). - **VOP** – Voice of the Process (what the process is currently capable of). **Intuition:** This is the *“where are we going and why?”* phase. Without a sharp Define step, the design effort drifts. --- ### 🟦 Phase 2 — MEASURE The Measure phase establishes the **baseline capability** and **validates the measurement system** that will be used to judge the future design. **Key Deliverables:** - **CTQ Operational Definitions:** Exact specification of *how* each CTQ is measured (e.g., *diameter = 12.00 mm ± 0.05 mm, measured with a calibrated micrometer at 20 °C*). - **MSA (Measurement System Analysis):** GR&R (Gauge Repeatability and Reproducibility) study. - **Baseline Sigma Level Calculation:** Using existing benchmark data. - **QFD (Quality Function Deployment) — House of Quality:** Translates CTQs into design requirements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **MSA is non-negotiable in KTU exams.** If a measurement system itself has a GR&R > 30 %, any design decision based on it is **statistically meaningless**. --- ### 🟦 Phase 3 — ANALYZE The Analyze phase creates and **screens design alternatives** using engineering and statistical tools. **Key Tools:** - **Functional Analysis / FAST Diagram** (Function Analysis System Technique) — breaks the product/process into *basic functions* and *higher-order functions*. - **Concept Generation Methods:** Brainstorming, TRIZ, Morphological Matrix, Benchmarking. - **Concept Screening & Scoring Matrix:** Pugh Matrix, Weighted Scoring Model. - **Failure Mode Avoidance:** Preliminary FMEA on design concepts. **Intuition:** This is the *“which idea is best?”* phase. No detailed engineering happens here — only **conceptual trade-off analysis**. --- ### 🟦 Phase 4 — DESIGN The Design phase converts the **selected concept into a fully engineered, optimized, and piloted solution**. **Key Deliverables:** - **Detailed Design Drawings / BOM** (Bill of Materials) / Process Flow. - **Design of Experiments (DOE):** To optimize controllable factors (X's) on response variables (Y's). - **Tolerance Design & Tolerance Stack-up Analysis.** - **Design FMEA (DFMEA).** - **Simulation:** Monte Carlo, Finite Element Analysis (FEA), Discrete Event Simulation. - **Pilot Run / Prototype Testing:** Verifying that the design can actually be built and performs to spec. > [!NOTE] > The Design phase is often the **most resource-intensive** phase in DMADV. It is where 60–70 % of the project budget is consumed. --- ### 🟦 Phase 5 — VERIFY The Verify phase **validates the design in real production conditions** and finalizes the handover to operations. **Key Deliverables:** - **Validation Report** — Pilot run output vs. CTQ targets. - **Updated Process Capability Indices ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$).** - **Control Plan** — Documented controls for long-term operation. - **Training Plan & Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).** - **Project Closure Report & Lessons Learned.** **Intuition:** The Verify phase answers the question: *“Will this design hold up in the customer’s hands, day after day, for years?”* --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical Equations for Board Exams) | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description & Units | |:-:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Process Capability Index** | $$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$ | Inherent process capability; assumes centering. *Dimensionless.* | | 2 | **Process Capability Index (shifted)** | $$C_{pk} = \min\left(C_{pu}, C_{pl}\right) = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$ | Actual capability accounting for mean shift. *Dimensionless.* | | 3 | **Process Performance Index** | $$P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3s}\right)$$ | Uses sample std deviation $s$ instead of long-term $\sigma$. *Dimensionless.* | | 4 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | $$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$$ | Core Six Sigma metric. *Defects per 10⁶ opportunities.* | | 5 | **Process Sigma Level** | $$\text{Process }\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\left(\dfrac{1 - \text{DPMO}}{10^6}\right) + 1.5$$ | Sigma level with 1.5σ long-term shift. *Sigma units.* | | 6 | **Rolled Throughput Yield** | $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ | Product of yields of $n$ process steps. *Dimensionless fraction.* | | 7 | **Tolerance Stack-Up (worst-case)** | $$T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$ | Arithmetic sum (worst-case method). *Same unit as measurement.* | | 8 | **Tolerance Stack-Up (RSS)** | $$T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}$$ | Root-Sum-Square (statistical method). *Same unit as measurement.* | | 9 | **GR&R Percentage of Tolerance** | $$\% GRR = \dfrac{6 \times \sigma_{GRR}}{\text{Tolerance}} \times 100\%$$ | Acceptable if < 30 %. *Percentage.* | | 10 | **Six Sigma Quality Target** | $$\text{DPMO} \le 3.4$$ | The 3.4 DPMO benchmark at 1.5σ shift. *Defects per 10⁶ opportunities.* | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Calculate process capability," always compute **both** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. If $C_p = C_{pk}$, the process is *centered*; if $C_p \neq C_{pk}$, comment explicitly on the **mean shift**. --- ## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application DMADV is the **go-to methodology in semiconductor fabrication, aerospace design, automotive launch programs, pharmaceutical new-drug development, and software product development**.
