Any process where the *cost of failure in the field* is so high that you cannot afford to *discover* defects after launch — you must *engineer them out* during the design phase. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Example:** When Toyota developed the **Prius Prime** (4th-generation plug-in hybrid), it used a DMADV approach to engineer a brand-new powertrain platform that did not exist before, ensuring a Six Sigma level of design robustness before the first production unit rolled out of the factory. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Process Capability Calculation (DMADV Verify Phase) **Problem Statement:** A newly designed shaft has specification limits **LSL = 9.95 mm** and **USL = 10.05 mm**. After a pilot run, the **sample mean** $\bar{X} = 10.025$ mm and the **sample standard deviation** $s = 0.008$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the **process sigma level**, assuming a long-term shift of 1.5σ. --- ### 🧮 Step 1: Compute the Tolerance Spread $$USL - LSL = 10.05 - 9.95 = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$ ### 🧮 Step 2: Compute $C_p$ (Process Capability — Centered) $$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$$ $$C_p = \dfrac{0.10}{6 \times 0.008}$$ $$C_p = \dfrac{0.10}{0.048} = 2.083$$ *Interpretation:* A $C_p > 1.33$ indicates the process is **highly capable** even under worst-case centering. ### 🧮 Step 3: Compute $C_{pu}$ (Upper Capability) $$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3s} = \dfrac{10.05 - 10.025}{3 \times 0.008}$$ $$C_{pu} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.024} = 1.0417$$ ### 🧮 Step 4: Compute $C_{pl}$ (Lower Capability) $$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3s} = \dfrac{10.025 - 9.95}{3 \times 0.008}$$ $$C_{pl} = \dfrac{0.075}{0.024} = 3.125$$ ### 🧮 Step 5: Compute $C_{pk}$ (Minimum of $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$) $$C_{pk} = \min(1.0417, 3.125) = 1.0417$$ *Interpretation:* Since $C_{pk} \neq C_p$, the process is **not centered** — it is drifting *upward* toward the USL. ### 🧮 Step 6: Compute Process Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift) $$\text{Process }\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\left(\dfrac{Yield}{2}\right) + 1.5$$ For a 2-sided spec, the *tail defect rate* per side is: $$\text{Tail} = 1 - \Phi\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{s}\right)$$ $$z = \dfrac{0.025}{0.008} = 3.125$$ $$\text{Tail} = 1 - \Phi(3.125) \approx 0.000888$$ $$\text{Total Yield} = 1 - 2 \times 0.000888 = 0.998224$$ $$\text{DPMO} = (1 - 0.998224) \times 10^6 = 1776 \text{ DPMO}$$ $$\text{Process }\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(0.999112) + 1.5 = 3.125 + 1.5 = 4.625\,\sigma$$ *Conclusion:* The process is operating between **4σ and 5σ**, **below the Six Sigma target**. The Design phase would need to either **(a)** shift $\mu$ closer to 10.00 mm, or **(b)** reduce $\sigma$ via better process control to push the process above 4.6σ and ultimately to 6σ. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for Multi-Step Design **Problem Statement:** A DMADV-designed assembly has **3 sequential steps** with first-pass yields of $Y_1 = 0.97$, $Y_2 = 0.95$, and $Y_3 = 0.92$. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield** and the **total DPMO** for the line. --- ### 🧮 Step 1: Compute the Rolled Throughput Yield $$RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3$$ $$RTY = 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.92$$ $$RTY = 0.847658 \approx 84.77\%$$ ### 🧮 Step 2: Compute Total Number of Defects per Million Total yield loss $= 1 - 0.847658 = 0.152342$ $$\text{DPMO}_{line} = 0.152342 \times 10^6 = 152{,}342 \text{ DPMO}$$ ### 🧮 Step 3: Convert to Process Sigma Level $$\text{Process }\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\left(\dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 - 152{,}342}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) + 1.5$$ $$\text{Process }\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(0.847658) + 1.5$$ $$\text{Process }\sigma = 1.02 + 1.5 = 2.52\,\sigma$$ *Interpretation:* The line is at a **2.52σ level — far from Six Sigma**. The Analyze/Design phases must focus on improving the weakest step ($Y_3 = 0.92$). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tool-to-Phase Mapping (Critical for KTU Part B) | Tool | Define | Measure | Analyze | Design | Verify | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Project Charter** | ✔ | | | | | | **SIPOC Diagram** | ✔ | ✔ | | | | | **VOC / CTQ Tree** | ✔ | ✔ | | | | | **QFD (House of Quality)** | | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | | | **MSA / GR&R** | | ✔ | | ✔ | ✔ | | **FAST Diagram** | | | ✔ | ✔ | | | **Pugh Matrix** | | | ✔ | | | | **DFMEA** | | | ✔ | ✔ | | | **DOE (Design of Experiments)** | | | | ✔ | ✔ | | **Tolerance Stack-Up** | | | | ✔ | | | **Simulation (Monte Carlo/FEA)** | | | | ✔ | ✔ | | **Control Plan** | | | | | ✔ | | **Pareto / FMEA** | | | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | | **Hypothesis Testing** | | ✔ | ✔ | | ✔ | | **SPC Charts** | | | | | ✔ | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The DMADV Phases ```mermaid flowchart TD A["D - Define
Project Charter, VOC, CTQ, SIPOC"] --> B["M - Measure
Baseline Data, MSA, QFD"] B --> C["A - Analyze
Concept Generation, Pugh Matrix, FAST"] C --> D["D - Design
DOE, DFMEA, Simulation, Pilot Run"] D --> E{"V - Verify
Validation Successful?"} E -- Yes --> F["Control Plan
SOPs, Training, Handover"] E -- No --> C F --> G["Project Closure
Lessons Learned"] style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style D fill:#d62728,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style E fill:#9467bd,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style F fill:#8c564b,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style G fill:#17becf,color:#000,stroke:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **feedback loop from Verify → Analyze** is critical. If the pilot run fails validation, the team must re-iterate through Analyze and Design — **not** re-do the entire project from Define. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of DMADV Deliverables ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Define_Phase["DEFINE PHASE"] D1["Project Charter"] D2["Voice of Customer VOC"] D3["CTQ Tree"] D4["SIPOC Diagram"] end subgraph Measure_Phase["MEASURE PHASE"] M1["QFD House of Quality"] M2["MSA / GR and R"] M3["Baseline Sigma Level"] end subgraph Analyze_Phase["ANALYZE PHASE"] A1["Concept Generation TRIZ"] A2["Pugh Selection Matrix"] A3["Preliminary FMEA"] end subgraph Design_Phase["DESIGN PHASE"] De1["Detailed Engineering Drawings"] De2["Design of Experiments DOE"] De3["Tolerance Stack-up RSS"] De4["DFMEA"] De5["Prototype Build"] end subgraph Verify_Phase["VERIFY PHASE"] V1["Pilot Run Validation"] V2["Cp Cpk Calculation"] V3["Control Plan"] V4["Project Closure Report"] end D1 --> M1 D2 --> M1 D3 --> M1 D4 --> M1 M1 --> A1 M2 --> A2 M3 --> A2 A1 --> A2 A2 --> De1 A2 --> De2 A2 --> De3 A2 --> De4 A2 --> De5 De1 --> V1 De2 --> V1 De3 --> V1 De4 --> V1 De5 --> V1 V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 V3 --> V4 style Define_Phase fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#000 style Measure_Phase fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#000 style Analyze_Phase fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#000 style Design_Phase fill:#ffebee,stroke:#000 style Verify_Phase fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — DMADV Decision Tree (When to Use DMADV vs DMAIC) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["Process or Product Already Exists?"] -- Yes --> Q2["Current Capability Cp above 1.0?"] Q1 -- No --> DMADV["Use DMADV - Design for Six Sigma"] Q2 -- Yes --> DMAIC["Use DMAIC - Incremental Improvement"] Q2 -- No --> Q3["Re-engineering Cost less than Cumulative Scrap Cost?"] Q3 -- Yes --> DMADV Q3 -- No --> DMAIC style DMADV fill:#d62728,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style DMAIC fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff,stroke:#000 ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q1. Distinguish between DMAIC and DMADV. Under what conditions is DMADV preferred over DMAIC? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** DMAIC is used to *improve* an existing process that is underperforming but still exists, focusing on root-cause analysis and control. DMADV is used to *design* a new process or product so that it is born at Six Sigma quality, focusing on concept generation, detailed design, and validation. DMADV is preferred when **(i)** the process does not yet exist, **(ii)** the existing process is fundamentally incapable of meeting specifications, or **(iii)** a major re-design is required due to shifts in customer/regulatory requirements. *(3 marks — board examiners expect the trigger conditions to be mentioned explicitly.)* --- > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q2. List the five phases of DMADV and write the main objective of the Verify phase. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The five phases are **Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify**. The Verify phase aims to **validate the new design in a production environment** through a pilot run, confirm that CTQs are met, compute updated $C_p$ / $C_{pk}$ values, finalize the **Control Plan and SOPs**, and formally hand over the validated process to operations for long-term sustainability. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 Choice A]`** ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **DMADV methodology** in detail. Discuss the deliverables, tools, and outputs of each of the five phases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A newly designed printed circuit board (PCB) has trace width specification **LSL = 0.18 mm, USL = 0.22 mm**. A pilot run of 50 samples yields a sample mean $\bar{X} = 0.205$ mm and a sample standard deviation $s = 0.005$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the **process sigma level** assuming a 1.5σ long-term shift. Comment on whether the design meets Six Sigma quality. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution #### Part (a) — DMADV Methodology Detail (7 Marks) | Phase | Objective | Key Tools | Deliverables | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Define** | Frame the design problem | VOC, CTQ, SIPOC, Project Charter | Charter, CTQ Tree, SIPOC | | **Measure** | Quantify baseline & validate measurement | QFD (House of Quality), MSA/GRR | CTQ operational defs, QFD matrix | | **Analyze** | Generate & screen design concepts | FAST, Pugh Matrix, TRIZ, Preliminary FMEA | Selected concept, scoring matrix | | **Design** | Engineer, optimize, pilot | DOE, DFMEA, Tolerance Stack-up, Simulation | Drawings, BOM, prototype, DFMEA | | **Verify** | Validate & hand over | Pilot run, Cp/Cpk, Control Plan, SPC | Validation report, control plan, SOPs | **Important:** Mention the **feedback loop** between Verify → Analyze (1 mark) and the **linkage of DMADV with DFSS** (1 mark). --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Tolerance Spread** $$USL - LSL = 0.22 - 0.18 = 0.04 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — $C_p$** $$C_p = \dfrac{0.04}{6 \times 0.005} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.03} = 1.333$$ **Step 3 — $C_{pu}$** $$C_{pu} = \dfrac{0.22 - 0.205}{3 \times 0.005} = \dfrac{0.015}{0.015} = 1.000$$ **Step 4 — $C_{pl}$** $$C_{pl} = \dfrac{0.205 - 0.18}{3 \times 0.005} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.015} = 1.667$$ **Step 5 — $C_{pk}$** $$C_{pk} = \min(1.000, 1.667) = 1.000$$ **Step 6 — Process Sigma Level** The limiting $z$ value is at the upper side: $$z = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{s} = \dfrac{0.015}{0.005} = 3.00$$ Using standard normal: $$1 - \Phi(3.00) = 0.00135 \text{ per tail}$$ Total defect rate $\approx 2 \times 0.00135 = 0.0027$ $$\text{DPMO} = 0.0027 \times 10^6 = 2700$$ $$\text{Process }\sigma = 3.00 + 1.5 = 4.5\,\sigma$$ **Comment:** The PCB process is operating at **4.5σ**, which is **below Six Sigma (6σ)**. Since $C_{pk} = 1.0$ (exactly at the lower bound of acceptability), the design must be improved — either by **re-centering the process** ($\bar{X} = 0.20$ mm would equalize $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$) or by **reducing variation** ($s < 0.005$ mm via tighter process control). --- > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 Choice B]`** ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **House of Quality (QFD)** matrix in detail. How is it used in the Measure phase of DMADV? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DMADV project designs a 3-stage chemical process with first-pass yields $Y_1 = 0.98$, $Y_2 = 0.96$, and $Y_3 = 0.94$. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield**, **total DPMO**, and the **process sigma level** with a 1.5σ shift. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution #### Part (a) — House of Quality (QFD) (7 Marks) **Definition:** The House of Quality is the first matrix of **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**. It translates **customer requirements (WHATs)** into **engineering characteristics (HOWs)**. **Components (must list all for full marks):** 1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** – left wall: VOC items. 2. **Engineering Characteristics (Hows)** – ceiling: measurable design specs. 3. **Relationship Matrix (Roof)** – center: strong/medium/weak correlations between Hows. 4. **Correlation Matrix (Ceiling)** – top: shows interactions among engineering characteristics. 5. **Technical Importance Ratings** – right wall. 6. **Target Values & Units** – room. 7. **Competitive Benchmarking** – bottom. **Use in DMADV Measure Phase:** QFD is applied in Measure to **operationalize CTQs** — converting qualitative customer desires into quantitative engineering specifications. It ensures that the eventual design (Design phase) is *customer-driven*, not *engineer-driven*. The output feeds directly into the **Analyze phase** as design constraints. *(7 marks)* --- #### Part (b) — RTY & Sigma Level Numerical (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Rolled Throughput Yield** $$RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 = 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.94$$ $$RTY = 0.884352 \approx 88.44\%$$ **Step 2 — Defect Rate** $$\text{Defect fraction} = 1 - 0.884352 = 0.115648$$ **Step 3 — DPMO** $$\text{DPMO} = 0.115648 \times 10^6 = 115{,}648 \text{ DPMO}$$ **Step 4 — Process Sigma Level** For a single opportunity per unit: $$\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{115{,}648}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.884352$$ $$\text{Process }\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(0.884352) + 1.5 = 1.205 + 1.5 = 2.705\,\sigma$$ **Conclusion:** The chemical process is at only **2.7σ** — far from Six Sigma. The weakest step is $Y_3 = 0.94$, so the **Design phase must prioritize improvements to Stage 3** to raise RTY above 99.99966 % (the Six Sigma threshold). --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks)** > > 1. **Confusing DMAIC and DMADV:** Examiners award marks for explicitly stating the *trigger conditions* that justify using DMADV. Writing "DMADV is for improvement" loses 1–2 marks instantly. > 2. **Skipping the Mean-Shift Check in $C_{pk}$:** Always compute **both** $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$ and take the *minimum*. Just writing one value and calling it $C_{pk}$ will lose 2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the 1.5σ Shift:** Sigma-level questions in KTU **always** assume a 1.5σ long-term shift. A student who reports $\sigma = 3.0$ instead of $4.5$ for the PCB question above loses 2 marks. > 4. **Wrong QFD Terminology:** Calling it "Kano Model" or "FMEA" instead of "House of Quality" results in zero marks for that sub-part. Be precise. > 5. **No Mention of Feedback Loop in DMADV:** The *Verify → Analyze* feedback loop is a high-yield KTU point. Omitting it costs 1 mark in the theory question. > 6. **Rounding Errors in NORMSINV:** Use $\text{NORMSINV}(0.99865) \approx 3.0$, $\text{NORMSINV}(0.999) \approx 3.09$, etc. Wrong z-value cascades into wrong sigma level. --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **DMADV = Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify** — used for *new* processes/products. - ✅ **DMAIC = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control** — used for *existing* processes. - ✅ DMADV is the first step of **DFSS (Design for Six Sigma)**. - ✅ The **3 trigger conditions** for DMADV: (i) process doesn't exist, (ii) process is inherently incapable, (iii) major re-design mandated. - ✅ **Six Sigma Quality Level** = 3.4 DPMO with a 1.5σ long-term shift. - ✅ **Define phase outputs:** Project Charter, VOC, CTQ Tree, SIPOC. - ✅ **Measure phase outputs:** QFD (House of Quality), MSA/GRR, baseline sigma. - ✅ **Analyze phase outputs:** Concept alternatives, Pugh Matrix, Preliminary DFMEA, FAST diagram. - ✅ **Design phase outputs:** DOE results, DFMEA, Tolerance Stack-up (worst-case or RSS), prototype, $C_p$/$C_{pk}$ for the *new* process. - ✅ **Verify phase outputs:** Validation report, updated Control Plan, SOPs, Project Closure. - ✅ The **Verify → Analyze feedback loop** allows iteration if the pilot run fails. - ✅ **$C_p \geq 1.67$** is the Six Sigma target for the *designed* process. - ✅ **$C_{pk} = C_p$** means the process is **centered**; $C_{pk} < C_p$ means **mean shift**. - ✅ **RTY** = product of all stage yields; the *real* yield of a multi-step process is always less than the lowest individual yield. - ✅ Always **re-check MSA/GRR** before making design decisions — *"garbage-in, garbage-out"*. - ✅ **Pugh Matrix** is a screening tool, not a scoring tool; use **Weighted Scoring** for detailed ranking. - ✅ **DOE** in the Design phase typically uses **Taguchi L9 / L18 orthogonal arrays** for robust design optimization. > [!NOTE] > **Final Board Tip:** If a KTU question says "design a new process" or "create a new product," the answer is **always DMADV**. If it says "improve" or "reduce defects in existing process," the answer is **DMAIC**. Master this distinction and you secure **3 marks instantly** in Part A.

topics.title: DMADOV
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DMADOV

# DMADOV — The Design for Six Sigma (DFSS) Roadmap

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DMADOV** stands for **Define – Measure – Analyze – Design – Optimize – Validate**. It is the **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** methodology used to develop *new* products, services, or processes so that they are born capable of meeting Six Sigma quality levels (≤ **3.4 DPMO**) from inception, rather than being fixed after the fact.

In contrast to **DMAIC** (used to *improve* existing processes that are below spec), DMADOV is used when the process or product **does not yet exist**, or when a fundamental redesign is required. The 2024 KTU syllabus places DMADOV as the logical successor to DMAIC in Module 4, because it represents the *proactive* counterpart to DMAIC's *reactive* approach.

The standard DFSS variants recognized globally are:

| Acronym | Phases | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| DMADV | Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify | Motorola / GE |
| IDOV | Identify, Design, Optimize, Validate | SBT/GE variant |
| **DMADOV** | **Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Optimize, Validate** | **KTU 2024 / Academic variant** |
| DMEDI | Define, Measure, Explore, Develop, Implement | Six Sigma newcomers |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — Building a house vs. renovating a kitchen**
> - **DMAIC** is like a renovation team fixing the leaks, cracks, and uneven tiles in an *existing* kitchen. The walls, ceiling, and plumbing are already there.
> - **DMADOV** is like an **architect** sitting at a blank plot, designing the *entire* kitchen — layout, materials, plumbing paths, electrical loads — so that it is structurally defect-free from day one.
> 
> Just as an architect uses blueprints, structural analysis, and load tests *before* construction, a DFSS project uses voice-of-customer data, simulation, tolerance analysis, and pilot validation *before* full-scale launch.

### Why DMADOV Exists — The "Why" Behind the Methodology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core principle:** *"It is cheaper to design quality in than to inspect it in."*
> 
> Statistics from GE and Motorola indicate that **70 %–80 %** of a product's life-cycle cost is committed during the design phase. Correcting defects in design costs **10×–100×** more than correcting them in concept.

### Key Metrics and Constants Used in DMADOV

- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — primary defect metric.
- **Sigma Level (Z)** — short-term and long-term capability.
- **Process Capability Indices** — **C<sub>p</sub>**, **C<sub>pk</sub>**, **P<sub>p</sub>**, **P<sub>pk</sub>**.
- **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** — multiplicative yield across N stages.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — output units without rework ÷ input units.
- **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** — used in FMEA during the *Design* phase.
- **Design Margin (k·σ)** — distance from nominal to nearest spec limit, expressed in sigma multiples.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Phases of DMADOV — Step-by-Step Logic

#### **Phase 1 — DEFINE**
- **Goal:** Identify the *business case*, *customer CTQs* (Critical-to-Quality), and the *project charter*.
- **Why?** Without a clear problem statement, the design effort drifts.
- **Key Tools:**
  - Project Charter (problem, goal, scope, team, timeline)
  - **VOC** (Voice of Customer) — surveys, interviews, focus groups
  - **CTQ Tree** — converts customer needs into measurable specs
  - **SIPOC** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers)
  - **Kano Model** — classifies features as Must-Be, One-Dimensional, Attractive
- **KTU Key Takeaway:** The Define phase produces a *signed-off project charter* — no design work proceeds without it.

#### **Phase 2 — MEASURE**
- **Goal:** Quantify the *current state* (baseline) and the *critical-to-quality* parameters; gather baseline data.
- **Why?** You cannot improve what you cannot measure. Even for new designs, you measure the *gap* between customer expectations and the existing best-in-class benchmark.
- **Key Tools:**
  - **MSA (Measurement System Analysis)** — Gage R&R, bias, linearity
  - **Process Mapping** (As-Is for benchmarking)
  - **Baseline DPMO** calculation
  - **QFD (Quality Function Deployment)** — House of Quality maps CTQs to engineering specs
- **Critical Output:** A list of measurable CTQ variables with target values, tolerances, and measurement methods.

#### **Phase 3 — ANALYZE**
- **Goal:** Identify the *root causes* of the gap and the *design drivers*.
- **Why?** Before designing, you must understand *which variables actually drive* the CTQ outcomes.
- **Key Tools:**
  - **DFMEA (Design FMEA)** — failure modes ranked by RPN
  - **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa)** Diagram
  - **Hypothesis Testing** (t-test, ANOVA, Chi-Square)
  - **Regression Analysis** — links design parameters to CTQs
  - **Pareto Analysis** — 80/20 prioritization
- **Critical Output:** A *short list* of design drivers (Xs) that significantly influence the CTQ (Ys).

#### **Phase 4 — DESIGN**
- **Goal:** Create the *detailed design* of the new product or process.
- **Why?** This is the core creative engineering step. Multiple design concepts are generated and the best one is selected.
- **Key Tools:**
  - **Pugh Matrix** — concept selection
  - **Tolerance Stack-Up Analysis**
  - **DOE (Design of Experiments)** — full/fractional factorial, RSM
  - **FMEA refinement**
  - **CAD / FEA / Simulation models**
- **Critical Output:** A *detailed design specification* with tolerances, materials, control plan draft.

#### **Phase 5 — OPTIMIZE**
- **Goal:** Fine-tune the design to achieve the *robust optimum* (insensitive to noise factors).
- **Why?** A design may meet specs at nominal conditions but fail under variation. Optimization seeks the *Taguchi robust* point.
- **Key Tools:**
  - **RSM (Response Surface Methodology)** — second-order models
  - **Tolerance Optimization**
  - **Robust Design (Taguchi)** — S/N ratios
  - **Mistake-Proofing (Poka-Yoke)** in design
- **Critical Output:** Optimized parameter settings and tightened tolerances.

#### **Phase 6 — VALIDATE**
- **Goal:** Prove that the *final design* works as intended in the real world (pilot run, full launch).
- **Why?** Even optimized designs can fail at scale. Validation closes the loop before mass production.
- **Key Tools:**
  - **Pilot Run / Prototype Testing**
  - **Hypothesis Test on Pilot Data** (verify capability)
  - **Control Plan finalization**
  - **PPAP / Production Part Approval Process** (automotive standard)
  - **Cost-Benefit Analysis** and **Lessons Learned**
- **Critical Output:** Sign-off, control plan, training, and transition to steady-state operations.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Six Sigma Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas appear regularly in KTU ESE questions for the Six Sigma course. Memorize the relationships between DPMO, Yield, and Sigma Level.

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DPMO** | $\dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$ | defects / million opportunities | D = defects, U = units, O = opportunities per unit |
| 2 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\dfrac{U_{\text{passed}}}{U_{\text{input}}}$ | dimensionless (0–1 or %) | Units completed without rework |
| 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\prod_{i=1}^{N} FPY_i$ | dimensionless | N = number of process steps |
| 4 | **Normalized Yield** | $Y_{\text{norm}} = \ln(FPY)$ | dimensionless | Used in long-term sigma conversion |
| 5 | **Process Capability — C<sub>p</sub>** | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{\text{ST}}}$ | dimensionless | ST = short-term |
| 6 | **Process Capability — C<sub>pk</sub>** | $\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Accounts for centering |
| 7 | **Process Performance — P<sub>p</sub>** | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{\text{LT}}}$ | dimensionless | LT = long-term (uses sample std-dev) |
| 8 | **Defects per Unit (DPU)** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | defects / unit | Building block of DPMO |
| 9 | **Sigma Level (short-term)** | $Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5$ | σ (with 1.5σ shift) | The *1.5σ shift* is industry convention |
| 10 | **RPN (FMEA)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | score | Severity × Occurrence × Detection |

**Standard DPMO → Sigma Level Conversion (for KTU exams):**

| DPMO | Sigma Level |
|---|---|
| 3.4 | 6.0 |
| 233 | 5.0 |
| 6,210 | 4.0 |
| 66,807 | 3.0 |
| 308,537 | 2.0 |
| 690,000 | 1.0 |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of DMADOV

- **Automotive:** New EV battery platform design (Tesla, Tata Motors).
- **Healthcare:** Designing a new surgical instrument sterilization process.
- **Software:** Designing a new fraud-detection algorithm in banking.
- **Manufacturing:** New semiconductor wafer fab process.
- **Service:** Designing a new airline boarding process for minimum missed flights.

The unifying theme: **every scenario where the process does not exist yet** — DMADOV is preferred over DMAIC.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of DPMO from Raw Process Data

**Given:** In a DMADOV pilot, a printed circuit board (PCB) assembly line produces 500 units/day for 5 days. Each unit has 12 soldering points (opportunities). Total defects recorded = 75.

**Step 1 — Total Units (U):**

$$
U = 500 \times 5 = 2500 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Defects (D):** Given as 75.

**Step 3 — Opportunities per Unit (O):** 12 soldering points.

**Step 4 — Apply the DPMO formula:**

$$
DPMO = \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{75 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{2500 \times 12}
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{75{,}000{,}000}{30{,}000} = 2500 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Yield:**

$$
Yield = 1 - \frac{DPMO}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975 \;\; (99.75\%)
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift):**

$$
Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(Yield) + 1.5
$$

For Yield = 0.9975, $\Phi^{-1}(0.9975) \approx 2.81$.

$$
Z_{ST} \approx 2.81 + 1.5 = 4.31 \sigma
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical KTU 14-mark question style. The full calculation chain is: defects → DPU → DPMO → Yield → Sigma level. Practice all four conversions.

### 3.2 Derivation of Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

A DMADOV-designed assembly process has 4 sequential stages. The First Pass Yields of the four stages are: 0.98, 0.95, 0.92, 0.99.

**Step 1 — State the RTY formula:**

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{4} FPY_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each value:**

$$
RTY = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.99
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplication chain:**

$$
0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.9310
$$

$$
0.9310 \times 0.92 = 0.85652
$$

$$
0.85652 \times 0.99 = 0.84795
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to %:**

$$
RTY \approx 84.80\%
$$

**Step 5 — Cost of Poor Quality Insight:** If 1,000,000 units enter the line, only ~848,000 emerge as perfect first-pass units — the *other 152,000* must be reworked, scrapped, or warranty-replaced.

### 3.3 Derivation of C<sub>pk</sub> and C<sub>p</sub>

**Given (post-DMADOV validation):** A CNC lathe produces a shaft with specification LSL = 24.90 mm, USL = 25.10 mm. Sample of 100 shafts yields:
- Mean $\mu = 25.03$ mm
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 0.015$ mm (short-term)

**Step 1 — Compute C<sub>p</sub>:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{ST}} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.20}{0.09} \approx 2.22
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the two-sided indices for C<sub>pk</sub>:**

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.03}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.07}{0.045} \approx 1.56
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.03 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.13}{0.045} \approx 2.89
$$

**Step 3 — C<sub>pk</sub> is the minimum of the two:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.56, \; 2.89) = 1.56
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> C<sub>pk</sub> = 1.56 > 1.33 ⇒ process **capable**. For Six Sigma (target 6σ with 1.5σ shift), C<sub>pk</sub> must exceed 1.5. The lower value (1.56) is the bottleneck because the process mean is shifted toward the **upper** spec limit — the design team must recenter the lathe.

### 3.4 DPMO and Sigma Conversion (Alternate Path — Z-table Based)

**Given:** A hospital laboratory wants to design a new testing process. Their pilot data shows DPU = 0.04 defects per test, and each test has 5 opportunities.

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
DPMO = 0.04 \times \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{5} = 0.04 \times 200{,}000 = 8000 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Defect Rate:**

$$
p = \frac{DPMO}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.008
$$

**Step 3 — Look up Z for Yield = (1 − 0.008) = 0.992:**

$$
\Phi^{-1}(0.992) \approx 2.41
$$

**Step 4 — Add 1.5σ shift:**

$$
Z_{ST} = 2.41 + 1.5 = 3.91 \sigma \approx 4.0 \sigma
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check with the standard table:** DPMO = 6,210 corresponds to 4.0σ. Our 8,000 DPMO is slightly worse (between 3.9 and 4.0σ) — matches the calculation.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — DMADOV vs DMAIC

| Parameter | DMAIC | DMADOV |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Improve existing process | Design new process/product |
| **Starting condition** | Process exists, has defects | Process does not exist (or is being reinvented) |
| **Phases** | 5 (D-M-A-I-C) | 6 (D-M-A-D-O-V) |
| **Typical tools** | Regression, ANOVA, SPC, FMEA | QFD, FMEA, DOE, RSM, Robust Design |
| **Output** | Improved process at ≤ 3.4 DPMO | New process that *starts* at ≤ 3.4 DPMO |
| **Project trigger** | Problem statement | Customer need / new opportunity |
| **Cost orientation** | Cost of repair | Cost of design (often 70-80% of LCC) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DMADOV Phase Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["PHASE 1 DEFINE Project Charter and CTQs"]
    phase2["PHASE 2 MEASURE Baseline Data and VOC"]
    phase3["PHASE 3 ANALYZE Design Drivers and Root Causes"]
    phase4["PHASE 4 DESIGN Detailed Design and DOE"]
    phase5["PHASE 5 OPTIMIZE Robust Optimum and RSM"]
    phase6["PHASE 6 VALIDATE Pilot Run and Capability Proof"]
    deliverable1["Signed Project Charter"]
    deliverable2["CTQ Specification and QFD"]
    deliverable3["FMEA and Pareto of Drivers"]
    deliverable4["Design Specs and Tolerance Stack"]
    deliverable5["Optimized Parameter Set"]
    deliverable6["Control Plan and Pilot Sign Off"]

    phase1 ==> deliverable1
    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 ==> deliverable2
    phase2 --> phase3
    phase3 ==> deliverable3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 ==> deliverable4
    phase4 --> phase5
    phase5 ==> deliverable5
    phase5 --> phase6
    phase6 ==> deliverable6

    classDef phaseStyle fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef delivStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    class phase1,phase2,phase3,phase4,phase5,phase6 phaseStyle
    class deliverable1,deliverable2,deliverable3,deliverable4,deliverable5,deliverable6 delivStyle
```

### 4.2 DMADOV vs DMAIC — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DMAIC[REACTIVE PATH DMAIC]
        A1["Define Problem"]
        A2["Measure Current"]
        A3["Analyze Root Cause"]
        A4["Improve Process"]
        A5["Control Sustain"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    end

    subgraph DMADOV[PROACTIVE PATH DMADOV]
        B1["Define CTQs"]
        B2["Measure VOC and Bench"]
        B3["Analyze Design Drivers"]
        B4["Design New Solution"]
        B5["Optimize Robustly"]
        B6["Validate Pilot"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6
    end

    A5 -. Transfers Lessons .-> B1

    classDef reactive fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c00000,color:#000000
    classDef proactive fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 reactive
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6 proactive
```

### 4.3 DFMEA Risk Reduction Loop (Used in DESIGN Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify Failure Modes in New Design"]
    score["Assign S, O, D Scores 1 to 10"]
    rpn["Compute RPN equals S times O times D"]
    threshold["RPN greater than 100? Action Required"]
    action["Redesign or Add Control"]
    recheck["Recompute RPN"]
    accept["Document and Accept Residual Risk"]
    next["Move to Next Failure Mode"]

    start --> score --> rpn --> threshold
    threshold -- YES --> action --> recheck --> threshold
    threshold -- NO --> accept --> next
    next --> score
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**  
*What is the DMADOV methodology? In which scenario is it preferred over DMAIC?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
DMADOV is a **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** framework comprising six phases — **Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Optimize, Validate** — used to develop *new* products, services, or processes that meet Six Sigma quality targets from inception. It is preferred over DMAIC when the process or product **does not yet exist**, when the existing process is fundamentally flawed (requires re-engineering), or when a new design opportunity is being pursued. DMAIC, in contrast, is used to *improve* an existing in-place process. (3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for phases, 1 mark for scenario.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**  
*Define DPMO. A process produces 2,000 units with 60 defects, each unit having 4 opportunities. Calculate the DPMO and the equivalent Sigma level.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **DPMO definition (1 mark):** Defects Per Million Opportunities — the number of defects normalized per million opportunities for defect occurrence.
- **DPMO calculation (1 mark):**

$$
DPMO = \frac{60 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{2000 \times 4} = \frac{60{,}000{,}000}{8000} = 7500 \text{ DPMO}
$$

- **Sigma level (1 mark):** Using the standard conversion table, 7,500 DPMO ≈ 3.95σ ≈ **4.0σ**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**  
*(a)* Explain in detail the **six phases of the DMADOV methodology** with the tools used in each phase. *(7 marks)*  
*(b)* A new injection-moulding process is being designed via DMADOV. During the pilot, 1,200 moulded parts are inspected. Each part has 8 critical dimensions (opportunities). A total of 96 defects are observed. Compute (i) DPU, (ii) DPMO, (iii) First Pass Yield, and (iv) the equivalent short-term Sigma level. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Phases of DMADOV (7 marks — 1 mark per phase + 0.5 mark for tools):**

1. **Define** — Identify the business case, customer CTQs, project scope. Tools: Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, Kano Model.
2. **Measure** — Quantify baseline performance and CTQ parameters. Tools: MSA, Gage R&R, QFD, baseline DPMO.
3. **Analyze** — Identify root causes and key design drivers. Tools: DFMEA, Fishbone, Hypothesis Testing, Regression, Pareto.
4. **Design** — Develop the detailed new design. Tools: Pugh Matrix, DOE, Tolerance Stack-Up, CAD/FEA.
5. **Optimize** — Refine parameters for robustness against noise. Tools: RSM, Taguchi S/N ratios, Mistake-Proofing, Tolerance Optimization.
6. **Validate** — Pilot run, capability verification, control plan finalization. Tools: Pilot testing, PPAP, hypothesis test on pilot data, cost-benefit analysis.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — full marks distribution shown):**

- **Given:** U = 1,200 units, O = 8 opportunities/unit, D = 96 defects.

- **(i) DPU (1.5 marks):**

$$
DPU = \frac{D}{U} = \frac{96}{1200} = 0.08 \text{ defects/unit}
$$

- **(ii) DPMO (1.5 marks):**

$$
DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} = \frac{96 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1200 \times 8} = \frac{96{,}000{,}000}{9600} = 10{,}000 \text{ DPMO}
$$

- **(iii) First Pass Yield (2 marks):**

$$
FPY = 1 - \frac{DPU}{O} = 1 - \frac{0.08}{8} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 = 99\%
$$

*(Or equivalently: Defect rate = DPMO/10⁶ = 0.01; FPY = 1 − 0.01 = 0.99.)*

- **(iv) Short-term Sigma Level (2 marks):**

$$
Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(0.99) + 1.5 = 2.326 + 1.5 = 3.826 \sigma \approx 3.83\sigma
$$

*(Cross-check via table: 10,000 DPMO corresponds to ≈ 3.8σ — consistent.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**  
*(a)* Compare **DMADOV and DMAIC** methodologies. Under what conditions is each selected? *(7 marks)*  
*(b)* A new bottle-filling line has four sequential stations with First Pass Yields of 0.97, 0.93, 0.95, and 0.98. Compute the Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) and the expected number of defect-free bottles if 500,000 bottles enter the line. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DMADOV vs DMAIC (7 marks — table + 1 mark per explanation):**

| Aspect | DMAIC | DMADOV |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Improve existing process | Design new process |
| When used | Process exists & has chronic defects | Process absent or requires redesign |
| Number of phases | 5 | 6 |
| Tools | SPC, regression, FMEA | QFD, FMEA, DOE, RSM |
| Outcome | 3.4 DPMO capability achieved | New design born at 3.4 DPMO |
| Cost focus | Cost of poor quality | Cost of design (70-80% of LCC) |

**Selection rule:** If the process exists, has data, and is fixable → **DMAIC**. If no process exists, the existing one is too constrained, or a new product is being created → **DMADOV**.

**(b) RTY Calculation (7 marks):**

- **RTY formula (1 mark):**

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{4} FPY_i = 0.97 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \times 0.98
$$

- **Step-by-step multiplication (3 marks):**

$$
0.97 \times 0.93 = 0.9021
$$

$$
0.9021 \times 0.95 = 0.85700
$$

$$
0.85700 \times 0.98 = 0.83986
$$

- **RTY result (1 mark):**

$$
RTY \approx 0.8399 = 83.99\% \approx 84\%
$$

- **Defect-free bottles from 500,000 input (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Defect-free units} = 500{,}000 \times 0.8399 = 419{,}950 \text{ bottles}
$$

- **Conclusion (0 mark extra — implicit in the answer):** About 80,050 bottles (≈ 16%) will require rework, scrap, or warranty recovery.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgot the 1.5σ shift.** Many students write $Z = \Phi^{-1}(Yield)$ without adding **+1.5σ**. This loses 1–2 marks. The 1.5σ shift is a Six Sigma *industry convention* and is mandatory unless the question explicitly says "long-term."
> 2. **DPU vs DPMO confusion.** DPU is defects **per unit**; DPMO is defects **per million opportunities**. Mixing these up invalidates the entire numerical chain.
> 3. **C<sub>p</sub> vs C<sub>pk</sub>.** C<sub>p</sub> uses only the spec width; C<sub>pk</sub> uses the *min* of the upper and lower indices. C<sub>pk</sub> ≤ C<sub>p</sub> **always**.
> 4. **Misstating DMADOV phases.** The order is **D-M-A-D-O-V**, not D-M-A-D-V-O. Examiners will deduct marks if Validate is placed before Optimize.
> 5. **Skipping the "+1" in Z calculation.** When using the formula $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - DPMO/10^6) + 1.5$, students sometimes forget the leading 1 inside the bracket, giving an inverted probability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DMADOV** = **D**efine, **M**easure, **A**nalyze, **D**esign, **O**ptimize, **V**alidate — the **DFSS** methodology for *new* designs.
- Use DMADOV when the process **does not exist**, or when a fundamental redesign is required.
- The **DPMO formula** is $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$.
- The **1.5σ shift** is a Six Sigma convention; always add it to $\Phi^{-1}(\text{Yield})$ to get short-term Z.
- **RTY** is the product of all stage FPYs; it represents the probability a unit emerges defect-free through *all* stages.
- **C<sub>p</sub>** measures spread capability; **C<sub>pk</sub>** measures spread *and* centering. C<sub>pk</sub> is the gold-standard metric.
- **RPN** in DFMEA = S × O × D; action required when RPN > 100 (or the company's threshold).
- **QFD (House of Quality)** is the most important Measure-phase tool for translating VOC into engineering specs.
- **DOE and RSM** are the dominant Design- and Optimize-phase tools — be ready to explain factorial design and second-order response surfaces.
- **Pugh Matrix** is the most commonly tested concept-selection tool in KTU exams.
- **Pilot + capability proof** in Validate must show C<sub>pk</sub> ≥ 1.5 (or whichever threshold the question specifies) before sign-off.
- **Cost insight:** 70–80% of life-cycle cost is fixed in design — DMADOV attacks this *upstream* cost.
- **Comparison line to remember:** DMAIC fixes; DMADOV *prevents*. DMAIC is reactive; DMADOV is proactive.
- **Common standard values:** 3.4 DPMO = 6σ; 233 DPMO = 5σ; 6,210 DPMO = 4σ — these are table-ready for the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roles and Responsibilities in Six Sigma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Roles and Responsibilities in Six Sigma # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **Six Sigma methodology**, *Roles and Responsibilities* refer to the formally defined hierarchical structure of personnel deployed to execute, govern, and sustain a Six Sigma initiative. The framework follows a **Top-Down Deployment** strategy integrated with a **Bottom-Up Execution** mechanism, ensuring that strategic quality objectives defined at the executive level are translated into measurable, project-level deliverables by trained practitioners. Each role carries a **defined authority, accountability matrix (RACI)**, training certification level, and a specific position within the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) project lifecycle. As per KTU OEPET721 syllabus standards, the Six Sigma organization is built on a **Belt System** analogous to martial arts ranking — the color of the belt represents the expertise level and corresponding responsibility weight. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Expectation):** > Six Sigma roles constitute a *structured human-resource architecture* that assigns accountability for process improvement, project leadership, statistical analysis, and cultural transformation across all organizational tiers. Without this architecture, DMAIC projects collapse into uncoordinated activities. ## 1.2 The Seven Standard Six Sigma Roles (Hierarchical Pyramid) The universally accepted Six Sigma governance model defines the following **seven primary roles**, ranked from strategic to operational: 1. **Executive Leadership** (CEO, COO, Senior VPs) 2. **Champions** (Senior Managers / Project Sponsors) 3. **Master Black Belts (MBB)** — Strategic resource 4. **Black Belts (BB)** — Full-time project leaders 5. **Green Belts (GB)** — Part-time project leaders / team members 6. **Yellow Belts (YB)** — Workgroup members 7. **White Belts** — Awareness-level personnel ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Production Analogy Imagine Six Sigma as a **blockbuster film production**: | Six Sigma Role | Movie Production Equivalent | Why This Analogy Works | |---|---|---| | **Executive Leadership** | The **Studio Head / Producer** | Approves the budget and greenlights the film; doesn't get into the daily shooting details | | **Champions** | The **Executive Producer** | Selects the script, removes bureaucratic obstacles, ensures the project stays aligned with studio goals | | **Master Black Belts** | The **Director of Photography / Chief Mentor** | A senior technical expert who trains the core crew and resolves technical deadlocks | | **Black Belts** | The **Director** | Leads the actual film shoot — full-time, hands-on, responsible for the final output quality | | **Green Belts** | The **Assistant Directors & Key Crew** | Run smaller parallel scenes (sub-projects) while still assisting the main shoot | | **Yellow Belts** | The **Department Heads & Junior Crew** | Execute specific tasks in their domain (lighting, costumes) under direction | | **White Belts** | The **Set Assistants / Interns** | Aware of the process, contribute basic-level support, but not decision-makers | > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as a film cannot be made without a clear chain of command, a Six Sigma initiative without defined roles will have overlapping responsibilities, finger-pointing, and project failure. The role architecture prevents *organizational entropy* — the natural tendency of complex projects to descend into chaos. ## 1.4 The Underlying Philosophy — Why Roles Matter Three foundational principles justify the existence of the Six Sigma role structure: - **Principle of Accountability (RACI Clarity):** Every DMAIC deliverable must have exactly one *Responsible* person, one *Accountable* owner, multiple *Consulted* experts, and appropriately *Informed* stakeholders. - **Principle of Capability Matching:** Tasks must be assigned based on certified statistical and project management capability — not on seniority. - **Principle of Cultural Embedding:** Quality is a *horizontal* concern (touches every department), so roles must be *vertical* (ranked by expertise) to create a clear career path that sustains the initiative long-term. > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick-Recall Trick:** Remember the hierarchy as **"E-C-M-B-G-Y-W"** = *Evenly Cooked Maggi Boils Gently Yet With Heat*. Sound silly? It will save you 2 minutes in the exam hall. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Role-Responsibility Matrix ## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Role Below is a structured, deep-dive into the rationale, training level, time commitment, and deliverables of each role. This forms the **analytical backbone** for any 14-mark KTU question on this topic. ### A. Executive Leadership (Strategic Apex) - **Why this role exists:** Six Sigma needs budget, visibility, and cultural air-cover. Without CEO-level sponsorship, the initiative is treated as a "flavor of the month." - **How they operate:** Approve the *Six Sigma deployment plan*, allocate capital, remove organizational resistance, link Six Sigma goals to **strategic business objectives (revenue, cost, market share)**. - **Time commitment:** ~5% of available time. - **Key deliverable:** *Strategic Quality Council charter* and *enterprise-wide KPI dashboard*. ### B. Champions (Tactical Bridge) - **Why this role exists:** Executives cannot mentor 50 Black Belts personally. Champions are the *translators* between strategy and execution. - **How they operate:** Identify projects, select and mentor Black Belts, validate project charters, approve *Go/No-Go* gates between DMAIC phases, ensure project alignment with business case. - **Time commitment:** ~15-20%. - **Key deliverable:** *Project portfolio review* and *ROI validation reports*. ### C. Master Black Belts (Technical Authority) - **Why this role exists:** Six Sigma is statistical; organizations need a senior internal expert to train, audit, and rescue stalled projects. - **How they operate:** Train and certify Black Belts and Green Belts, mentor multiple Black Belts simultaneously, lead *enterprise-wide* projects, maintain the **Six Sigma tool/technique library**. - **Time commitment:** 100% dedicated to Six Sigma (full-time). - **Key deliverable:** *Training curriculum*, *statistical methodology audits*, *complex multi-departmental projects*. ### D. Black Belts (Project Leaders) - **Why this role exists:** The *workhorses* of the initiative. They turn abstract project charters into measurable improvements. - **How they operate:** Lead 2-3 projects concurrently, apply DMAIC rigorously, use advanced statistical tools (regression, ANOVA, DOE), coach Green Belts, document project savings. - **Time commitment:** 100% dedicated to Six Sigma. - **Key deliverable:** *Completed DMAIC project reports* with verified *sigma shift* and *hard/soft savings*. ### E. Green Belts (Part-Time Practitioners) - **Why this role exists:** Embeds quality thinking into the *operational fabric* of the organization. Most improvements happen here. - **How they operate:** Lead smaller, department-scoped projects, participate as team members on Black Belt projects, apply basic statistical tools in their daily work. - **Time commitment:** ~25-50% (the rest is their regular job). - **Key deliverable:** *Local process improvement projects* and *validated root-cause analyses*. ### F. Yellow Belts (Team Members) - **Why this role exists:** Quality is everyone's job — not just specialists. Yellow Belts bring *domain expertise* to teams. - **How they operate:** Participate in brainstorming, data collection, process mapping, basic 5-Whys and fishbone analysis. They do not lead projects. - **Time commitment:** ~10% — task-specific involvement. - **Key deliverable:** *Workgroup-level process data* and *SOP updates*. ### G. White Belts (Awareness Layer) - **Why this role exists:** Creates a *quality-literate workforce* and supports cultural change. - **How they operate:** Understand basic Six Sigma vocabulary, support improvement teams, identify improvement opportunities in their daily work. - **Time commitment:** Awareness-only; no project work. - **Key deliverable:** *Improvement idea submission* and *basic process compliance*. ## 2.2 The Six Sigma Training & Certification Metrics | Belt Level | Typical Training Hours | Statistical Rigor | Statistical Tools Mastered | Avg. Project Savings (USD) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **White Belt** | 4-8 hrs | Awareness | Basic 7QC tools | N/A | | **Yellow Belt** | 16-40 hrs | Foundational | Pareto, Fishbone, Control Charts (basic) | \$25K - \$75K | | **Green Belt** | 80-160 hrs | Intermediate | Hypothesis testing, Regression, Control Charts | \$75K - \$200K | | **Black Belt** | 160-240 hrs | Advanced | DOE, ANOVA, Advanced Regression, Cpk | \$200K - \$500K+ | | **Master Black Belt** | 240+ hrs + mentorship | Expert | All above + Nested/Nested DOE, GLM, predictive modeling | Strategic/Enterprise-wide | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No mathematical formula is canonical for this topic — it is governance and HR architecture.** The "formula" in a KTU context is the **RACI Logic**: > > $$R + A = 1, \quad C \geq 1, \quad I \geq 0$$ > > where *R*esponsible, *A*ccountable, *C*onsulted, and *I*nformed define the minimum stakeholder footprint for every DMAIC deliverable. Every task must have *exactly one A* (the buck stops here) and *one or more R* (who does the work). ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Manufacturing Industries:** Black Belts reduce *defect-per-million-opportunities (DPMO)* in assembly lines. - **IT & Software:** Green Belts apply DMAIC to *defect density reduction* and *mean-time-to-resolution (MTTR)*. - **Healthcare:** Champions drive *hospital-acquired infection reduction* and *patient wait-time optimization*. - **Banking & Services:** Yellow Belts improve *loan processing cycle time* using basic SIPOC analysis. --- # SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Role Deployment Lifecycle & Project Interaction Model ## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Role Deployment Sequence In any enterprise Six Sigma rollout, roles are deployed in **five sequential phases**. This is the high-yield analytical framework KTU examiners test in 14-mark questions. ### Step 1: Strategic Mandate - **Action:** Executive Leadership announces the Six Sigma initiative and defines the *business case* (cost of poor quality, customer CTQ requirements). - **Output:** Six Sigma Deployment Charter + Annual Quality Goals. - **Logic:** *Why first?* Because without executive mandate, no middle manager will allocate resources. ### Step 2: Infrastructure Setup - **Action:** Executive Leadership appoints Master Black Belts; Master Black Belts design the *training curriculum* and *certification rubric*. - **Output:** Curriculum document, certification exam blueprint, selection criteria for Champions. - **Logic:** *Why now?* Tools and standards must exist before humans are assigned. ### Step 3: Project Identification & Sponsorship - **Action:** Champions scan the business for problems aligned with strategic goals; they select *project candidates* and pair each with a Black Belt. - **Output:** Approved Project Charters, expected ROI estimates. - **Logic:** *Why this step?* Projects without sponsorship die in the *Define* phase. ### Step 4: Project Execution (DMAIC) - **Action:** Black Belts lead DMAIC; Green Belts assist; Yellow/White Belts provide data and feedback; Champions unblock resources; MBBs audit statistical validity. - **Output:** Validated solutions, control plans, savings reports. - **Logic:** *Why split tasks?* Each role contributes its *highest and best use* — Black Belts on statistics, Yellow Belts on domain knowledge, Champions on politics. ### Step 5: Closure & Sustainment - **Action:** Champions validate savings; Black Belts hand off *control plans* to **Process Owners**; MBBs update the *lessons-learned library*. - **Output:** Signed-off project closure, control plan in operation, project savings booked to the P&L. - **Logic:** *Why this final step?* Improvement without *Control* phase is a *one-time event*, not a *sustained capability*. ## 3.2 The Analytical Role-Interaction Matrix (RACI × DMAIC) This is the **single most important table** for KTU 14-mark answers. Memorize the pattern. | DMAIC Phase | Executive | Champion | MBB | Black Belt | Green Belt | Yellow Belt | White Belt | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Define** | I | A, R | C | R | R | C | I | | **Measure** | I | A | C | R | R | R | I | | **Analyze** | I | I | C | A, R | R | C | I | | **Improve** | I | I | C | A, R | R | C | I | | **Control** | I | A | I | R | C | R | I | *Legend: R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed* ### Key Inferences from the Matrix: 1. **Champions are Accountable in Define, Measure, and Control** — these are the *business boundary phases*. They own the *What* and *Why*. 2. **Black Belts are Accountable in Analyze and Improve** — these are the *technical core* phases. They own the *How*. 3. **MBBs are never Accountable, but always Consulted in D-M-A-I** — they are the *technical auditors*, not the *project owners*. 4. **Yellow Belts are Responsible (not Accountable) in Measure and Control** — they own the *data integrity* and *operational discipline*. 5. **White Belts are only Informed** — they are *aware* but not *active*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common mistake students make is writing "Black Belts lead the entire DMAIC project." This is **incorrect**. Black Belts are *Accountable* only in *Analyze* and *Improve*. The Champion is Accountable for *Define, Measure, Control*. Writing this distinction correctly can earn 3-4 extra marks in a 14-mark question. ## 3.3 Numerical Justification — Typical Role Ratios A well-staffed Six Sigma organization follows the following **standard ratio benchmarks** (used in 7-mark sub-parts): $$\text{White : Yellow : Green : Black : MBB : Champion : Exec} \approx 1000 : 50 : 20 : 10 : 2 : 5 : 1$$ ### Worked Example Calculation: A company has **5000 employees** rolling out Six Sigma. - White Belts: $\frac{5000}{1000} \times 1 = 5$ wait, that formula needs calibration. Let me restate using the canonical industry benchmark: **Standard industry ratios (per MBB reference):** - 1 MBB per 1000 employees - 1 Black Belt per 100 employees (some sources cite 1 BB per 250) - 1 Green Belt per 50 employees - 1 Champion per 100-300 employees For 5000 employees: - MBB: $\frac{5000}{1000} = 5$ Master Black Belts - Black Belts: $\frac{5000}{100} = 50$ Black Belts - Green Belts: $\frac{5000}{50} = 100$ Green Belts - Champions: $\frac{5000}{200} \approx 25$ Champions (using midpoint) > [!NOTE] > This numerical example is exam-friendly. A typical KTU 7-mark sub-part asks: *"For a company with X employees, calculate the recommended number of Black Belts and Green Belts."* Use the ratios above. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Six Sigma Organizational Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[Executive Leadership
CEO COO VP] --> B[Champions
Senior Managers] B --> C[Master Black Belts
Strategic Technical Experts] C --> D[Black Belts
Full-time Project Leaders] D --> E[Green Belts
Part-time Project Leaders] E --> F[Yellow Belts
Workgroup Members] F --> G[White Belts
Awareness Layer] A -.->|Approves Budget| H[Six Sigma Deployment Charter] B -.->|Project Portfolio| I[Project Charters] C -.->|Trains Certifies| J[Trained Belt Pool] D -.->|DMAIC Project| K[Project Closure Report] E -.->|Sub Projects| L[Local Improvements] F -.->|Domain Data| M[Validated Data Sets] G -.->|Process Ideas| N[Improvement Suggestions] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = direct reporting/training authority. Dotted arrows = deliverable/artefact flow. This is the **organizational backbone** of any Six Sigma deployment. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Role Interaction Across DMAIC Phases ```mermaid graph LR P1[Define] --> P2[Measure] P2 --> P3[Analyze] P3 --> P4[Improve] P4 --> P5[Control] P1 -.->|Champion Accountable
Black Green Responsible| R1[Role Activity Block D] P2 -.->|Champion Accountable
Black Green Yellow Responsible| R2[Role Activity Block M] P3 -.->|Black Belt Accountable
Green Responsible
MBB Consulted| R3[Role Activity Block A] P4 -.->|Black Belt Accountable
Green Responsible
MBB Consulted| R4[Role Activity Block I] P5 -.->|Champion Accountable
Black Yellow Responsible| R5[Role Activity Block C] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Role-Deployment Workflow ```mermaid graph TD S1[Step 1: Executive Mandate] --> S2[Step 2: MBB Recruits Trains] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Champions Identify Projects] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Black Belts Lead DMAIC] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Champions Validate Savings] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Process Owner Takes Control Plan] S1 -->|Inputs| A1[Strategic CTQs] S2 -->|Outputs| A2[Certified Belts] S3 -->|Outputs| A3[Signed Project Charters] S4 -->|Outputs| A4[Validated Solutions] S5 -->|Outputs| A5[Approved Savings] S6 -->|Outputs| A6[Sustained Improvements] ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks "Draw the role hierarchy of Six Sigma" (4-7 marks), reproduce the first diagram and annotate the **time commitment %** and **training hours** next to each role. That extra annotation typically fetches full marks. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` **Define the role of a "Champion" in Six Sigma. List any three of his/her key responsibilities.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Champion** is a senior manager (typically a Director or VP-level executive) who acts as the *tactical bridge* between executive leadership and project-level Black Belts. They are responsible for: 1. **Project Selection & Sponsorship** — Identifying Six Sigma projects aligned with strategic business goals and matching them with qualified Black Belts. 2. **Resource Removal & Project Unblocking** — Removing organizational, budgetary, and political obstacles that impede the project's progress. 3. **Phase-Gate Approval** — Reviewing and approving the *Go/No-Go* decision at the end of each DMAIC phase (especially at the end of *Measure* and *Improve*). 4. **ROI Validation** — Verifying the claimed financial savings before booking them to the P&L. *(Award 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any three well-explained responsibilities.)* --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]` **Differentiate between a Black Belt and a Green Belt in Six Sigma. (Any three points.)** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Black Belt | Green Belt | |---|---|---| | **Time Commitment** | 100% full-time dedicated to Six Sigma | 25-50% part-time, balance with regular job | | **Project Scope** | Leads 2-3 cross-functional projects simultaneously | Leads 1 smaller, department-scoped project OR supports BB as team member | | **Statistical Skill** | Advanced (DOE, ANOVA, regression, Cpk analysis) | Intermediate (basic hypothesis testing, control charts, Pareto) | | **Reporting To** | Master Black Belt / Champion | Black Belt (functional) and own manager (administrative) | | **Typical Position** | Dedicated quality role, often lateral hire or promoted internally | Regular employee with Six Sigma added to responsibilities | *(Award 1 mark per well-articulated difference; full 3 marks for a clear comparative table.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam]` **"Six Sigma success depends critically on a clearly defined role architecture, not just on statistical tools." Discuss this statement with reference to:** **(a)** The hierarchical role structure of Six Sigma, with the responsibilities of any four roles. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The RACI accountability distribution across the five DMAIC phases, with a justification for why accountability shifts between Champions and Black Belts. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks The Six Sigma role architecture is a *top-down deployment* model with a clearly demarcated hierarchy. The seven canonical roles and their responsibilities are: 1. **Executive Leadership:** Approves the Six Sigma strategic plan, allocates annual budget for training and projects, links Six Sigma goals to enterprise-wide revenue/cost targets, and provides organizational air-cover. They own the *Six Sigma Deployment Charter*. 2. **Champions:** Function as the tactical bridge. They select projects aligned with strategy, appoint and mentor Black Belts, approve phase-gate reviews (especially at the *Measure → Analyze* transition), and validate claimed savings. They are the *project sponsors* and the *business owners* of the improvement. 3. **Master Black Belts (MBB):** The senior technical authority. They design the training curriculum, certify Black and Green Belts, mentor multiple Black Belts, and audit statistical methodology on stalled or complex projects. They are *Consulted* in almost every project but rarely *Accountable*. 4. **Black Belts:** Full-time project leaders who apply the DMAIC methodology end-to-end. They own 2-3 concurrent projects, deploy advanced statistics (DOE, regression, hypothesis testing), and coach Green Belts. They are the *technical engines* of the initiative. *(Award 1.5 marks for each well-explained role + 1 mark for an introductory statement on hierarchy = 7 marks.)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks The RACI distribution across DMAIC is not uniform — accountability **shifts** between phases based on whether the activity is *business-boundary* (Define/Measure/Control) or *technical-core* (Analyze/Improve). | DMAIC Phase | Accountable Role | Responsible Roles | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | **Define** | **Champion** | Black Belt, Green Belt | Project framing is a *business* decision (scope, goals, CTQs), hence Champion owns it. | | **Measure** | **Champion** | Black Belt, Green Belt, Yellow Belt | Data-collection infrastructure must align with the *business case*; Champion must validate metric definitions. | | **Analyze** | **Black Belt** | Green Belt, Yellow Belt | Root-cause analysis is a *technical* task requiring statistical expertise that Champions do not possess. | | **Improve** | **Black Belt** | Green Belt, Yellow Belt | Solution design and pilot testing is a *technical* activity; Champion approves the final selection but does not design. | | **Control** | **Champion** | Black Belt, Yellow Belt | Sustainment and handoff to the process owner is a *business* activity; Champion owns the savings validation. | **Why the Shift is Logical:** A Champion operating without statistical depth would misguide the *Analyze* phase. A Black Belt operating without business context would misalign the *Define* phase. Therefore, accountability follows *who best understands the phase's dominant knowledge domain*. This *role-phased accountability* is what makes Six Sigma uniquely effective compared to other quality methodologies like TQM (where accountability is diffuse). *(Award 1 mark for each row of the table + 1 mark for the synthesis/justification paragraph + 0.5 mark for opening statement + 0.5 mark for logical conclusion = 7 marks.)* --- ### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]` **(a)** Explain the role and training requirements of a **Master Black Belt** and a **Black Belt**. How do they differ in terms of time commitment, project ownership, and statistical expertise? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A manufacturing firm employs 4000 workers and is launching a Six Sigma initiative. Using the standard industry ratios, calculate the recommended number of Master Black Belts, Black Belts, and Green Belts. Justify your staffing plan with a deployment timeline. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Master Black Belt (MBB):** - **Role:** Senior Six Sigma expert who combines *technical mastery* with *program-level governance*. They are the *internal consultants* who train, certify, mentor, and audit. - **Training:** 240+ hours of formal training, plus 1-2 years of post-certification mentoring. They typically hold advanced degrees in Statistics, Industrial Engineering, or Operations Research. - **Time Commitment:** 100% dedicated to Six Sigma, but the *output* is *program-wide*, not project-specific. - **Project Ownership:** Leads *enterprise-level* or *cross-departmental* projects of high strategic value; otherwise *Consulted* across all projects. - **Statistical Expertise:** Expert — Design of Experiments (full factorial, fractional, response surface), Advanced Regression, General Linear Models, Nested Designs, Predictive Modeling. **Black Belt (BB):** - **Role:** Full-time project leader who executes DMAIC from charter to closure. They are the *technical engine* of the initiative. - **Training:** 160-240 hours of formal training with rigorous certification exam. - **Time Commitment:** 100% dedicated to Six Sigma, but the *output* is *project-specific*. - **Project Ownership:** Leads 2-3 concurrent cross-functional projects, typically 4-6 months each. - **Statistical Expertise:** Advanced — DOE (introductory to intermediate), ANOVA, Regression, Capability Analysis (Cpk), Hypothesis Testing. **Key Differences Table (Summary):** | Aspect | Master Black Belt | Black Belt | |---|---|---| | Time commitment | 100% (program-level) | 100% (project-level) | | Projects led | 1-2 large/strategic | 2-3 medium/tactical | | Statistical depth | Expert (advanced DOE, GLM) | Advanced (introductory DOE, ANOVA) | | Mentorship | Mentors BBs and GBs | Mentors GBs and YBs | *(Award 1 mark per well-articulated role + 1 mark for the comparison table + 1 mark for concluding synthesis = 7 marks.)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** - Total workforce $N = 4000$ employees - Standard ratios: - 1 MBB per 1000 employees - 1 Black Belt per 100 employees - 1 Green Belt per 50 employees **Calculations:** Number of Master Black Belts: $$N_{MBB} = \frac{N}{1000} = \frac{4000}{1000} = 4 \text{ MBBs}$$ Number of Black Belts: $$N_{BB} = \frac{N}{100} = \frac{4000}{100} = 40 \text{ Black Belts}$$ Number of Green Belts: $$N_{GB} = \frac{N}{50} = \frac{4000}{50} = 80 \text{ Green Belts}$$ **Deployment Timeline (Justification):** | Quarter | Activity | Roles Mobilized | |---|---|---| | **Q1** | Strategic announcement; recruit 4 MBBs from external/Internal pool | Executive Leadership | | **Q2** | MBBs design curriculum; train first cohort of 10 Black Belts | MBBs | | **Q3** | Remaining 30 Black Belts trained; first 40 Green Belts begin training | MBBs + BBs | | **Q4** | Black Belts launch ~80-100 projects; Green Belts assigned as team members | All belts | | **Year 2** | Remaining 40 Green Belts trained; project portfolio review and ROI validation | Champions + MBBs | **Staffing Plan Rationale:** The 4 MBBs can each mentor ~10 Black Belts (totaling 40), and each Black Belt can mentor ~2 Green Belts (totaling 80), so the recommended staffing is **mutually consistent and operationally feasible** for a 4000-employee firm. *(Award 2 marks for calculations + 3 marks for timeline table + 2 marks for rationale = 7 marks.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Six Sigma Role Questions:** > > 1. **Treating "Champion" and "Executive" as synonyms** — They are NOT the same. Executives set strategy; Champions operationalize it. Mixing them up costs 2-3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the "Master Black Belt" role entirely** — Many students jump from Champion → Black Belt, skipping MBB. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. > 3. **Writing "Black Belts lead the entire DMAIC project"** — This is factually incorrect. Black Belts are Accountable only in *Analyze* and *Improve*. Champion owns the rest. > 4. **Not using the standard ratio for numerical questions** — A 7-mark numerical sub-part almost always requires the *1 MBB : 10 BB : 20 GB* per 1000-employee ratio. Skipping the formula loses 3 marks. > 5. **Confusing "Yellow Belt" and "White Belt"** — Yellow Belts are *active* team members; White Belts are *awareness* only. This distinction appears in nearly every KTU paper. > 6. **Writing definitions without examples** — A 3-mark answer with a real-world industry example (e.g., "Black Belts reduce defect rate in an automotive assembly line from 4-sigma to 5-sigma") typically scores 1 mark higher than a definition-only answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets (Read before entering the exam hall):** - **Roles in descending order of authority:** Executive → Champion → MBB → BB → GB → YB → WB - **Full-time roles:** MBB and BB (100% dedicated) - **Part-time roles:** Champion (~15-20%), GB (~25-50%), YB (~10%), WB (awareness only) - **Accountable in Define/Measure/Control:** **Champion** - **Accountable in Analyze/Improve:** **Black Belt** - **Always Consulted (never Accountable):** **Master Black Belt** - **Active data contributors (Measure & Control):** **Yellow Belts** - **Awareness only, no project work:** **White Belts** - **Standard ratio per 1000 employees:** 1 MBB : 10 BB : 20 GB - **Typical Black Belt project savings:** \$200K - \$500K+ per project - **Training hours (approx.):** WB 4-8, YB 16-40, GB 80-160, BB 160-240, MBB 240+ - **Key tools per role:** BB → DOE, ANOVA, Regression; GB → Hypothesis testing, Pareto; YB → 5-Why, Fishbone - **RACI rule:** Every task = exactly 1 Accountable, 1+ Responsible, 1+ Consulted, 0+ Informed - **Analogy to remember:** Six Sigma = Movie Production → Studio Head = Executive, Executive Producer = Champion, Director = Black Belt, Assistant Director = Green Belt, Junior Crew = Yellow Belt, Intern = White Belt - **Mnemonic for hierarchy:** *E-C-M-B-G-Y-W* = "Evenly Cooked Maggi Boils Gently Yet With Heat" - **Top-down + Bottom-up:** Strategy flows DOWN (Executive → WB), execution data flows UP (WB → Executive). - **Process Owner (often missed in answers):** A *post-project* role, not a belt. The department manager who takes over the control plan after project closure. ---

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET721 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are "Applications" in Six Sigma?

In the context of KTU's OEPET721 Six Sigma syllabus, **Applications** refers to the practical deployment of the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology, **Lean Six Sigma** tools, and **statistical process control** techniques across diverse industrial, service, and transactional environments. It is the bridge between theoretical Six Sigma knowledge and real-world problem-solving.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> *Six Sigma Applications* is the systematic implementation of DMAIC and DMADV (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify) frameworks across manufacturing, service, healthcare, BPO, banking, and IT/software industries to achieve measurable improvements in quality, cost, delivery, and customer satisfaction metrics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room**
>
> Imagine a hospital ER where patient wait times are inconsistent. A Six Sigma "Black Belt" team is deployed. They **Define** the problem (average wait = 4 hours, target = 1 hour), **Measure** current process times at every stage (triage, registration, doctor consultation, discharge), **Analyze** root causes (under-staffing during peak hours, redundant paperwork), **Improve** by redesigning workflow and adding a fast-track lane, and **Control** with a digital dashboard that flags delays in real-time. This is a Six Sigma Application — structured, data-driven, and result-oriented.
>
> Think of Six Sigma Applications as a **universal Swiss Army knife** — the same blade (DMAIC) cuts problems in a Toyota factory, an Amazon warehouse, a hospital, or a software company.

### 1.3 Classification of Six Sigma Applications

Six Sigma Applications are broadly classified into three domains:

| Domain | Primary Focus | Typical KPI Improved | Example Sector |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing Applications** | Defect reduction, cycle time, yield | DPMO, First Pass Yield | Automotive, Electronics |
| **Service Applications** | Transaction accuracy, wait time, customer satisfaction | Sigma Level, NPS Score | Banking, BPO, Telecom |
| **Transactional/IT Applications** | Process efficiency, software defects, project delivery | Defect Leakage, Schedule Variance | IT Services, Software Development |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Used in Applications

- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — the universal Six Sigma quality metric.
- **RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)** — cumulative yield across process stages.
- **Sigma Level (Z)** — capability index derived from DPMO.
- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** — financial quantification of waste.
- **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** — customer-driven specifications.
- **PPM (Parts Per Million)** — defect rate metric.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question on "Applications" appears, always anchor your answer in **DMAIC phases** + a **specific industry case** + **quantified benefits** (e.g., "reduced defects from 25,000 DPMO to 3,400 DPMO, saving ₹2.3 crore annually"). Examiners reward numbers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DMAIC Application Framework — Theoretical Breakdown

Every Six Sigma application, regardless of industry, follows the **DMAIC roadmap**. Here is a structured, phase-by-phase breakdown aligned with KTU Module 4 expectations:

#### **Phase 1: DEFINE**
- Identify the **project** (aligned with business goals, customer CTQs, and problem statement).
- Create the **Project Charter** (problem, goal, scope, team, timeline, benefits).
- Develop the **SIPOC** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) high-level map.
- Identify the **Voice of Customer (VOC)** and translate to CTQs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this phase matters:** Without a clearly defined problem, even the best statistical tools produce noise. The Define phase is the *compass* of the entire DMAIC journey.

#### **Phase 2: MEASURE**
- Establish baseline performance of the CTQs.
- Create **Operational Definitions** for defects.
- Build a **Data Collection Plan**.
- Validate the **Measurement System** using **Gage R&R** and **MSA (Measurement System Analysis)**.
- Calculate current process **Sigma Level** and **DPMO**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this phase matters:** You cannot improve what you cannot measure. This phase produces the *baseline data* against which improvement is judged.

#### **Phase 3: ANALYZE**
- Apply **root cause analysis** tools: **5 Whys**, **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram**, **Pareto Analysis**, **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**.
- Use statistical tools: **Hypothesis Testing**, **Regression**, **ANOVA**, **Correlation Analysis**.
- Identify the **Vital Few X's** (independent variables) that drive the **Y** (CTQ / dependent variable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this phase matters:** 80% of problems stem from 20% of causes (Pareto). This phase isolates the *leverage points* for maximum impact.

#### **Phase 4: IMPROVE**
- Generate solutions via **Brainstorming**, **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, **Kaizen**.
- Pilot solutions, validate improvements with statistical significance.
- Optimize using **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** if needed.
- Document **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this phase matters:** This is where the *actual change* happens — processes are redesigned, and solutions are stress-tested.

#### **Phase 5: CONTROL**
- Implement **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts (X-bar, R, p, c charts).
- Create **Control Plans**, **Visual Management** boards, **Standard Work** documents.
- Hand over to the **Process Owner** with a **Reaction Plan**.
- Sustain gains via **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) and ongoing monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this phase matters:** Without control, improvements regress to the mean (Hockey Stick Effect). Control locks in the gains permanently.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Applications Module

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million opportunities | Dimensionless |
| **Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | First-pass yield at one stage | Decimal (0 to 1) or % |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times \dots \times Y_n$ | Cumulative yield across $n$ stages | Decimal (0 to 1) or % |
| **Sigma Level (Short-term)** | $\text{Sigma} = \text{NormInv}(1.5 + Y) + 1.5$ | Z-score including 1.5σ shift | Sigma |
| **Sigma Level (Long-term)** | $\text{Sigma} = \text{NormInv}(Y)$ | Z-score without shift | Sigma |
| **COPQ** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Cost of Poor Quality | ₹ / $ |
