| **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process spread vs. spec spread | Dimensionless |
| **Process Capability (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Capability accounting for centering | Dimensionless |
| **Pareto 80/20** | $\text{Vital Few} = \text{Top } 20\% \text{ causes causing } 80\% \text{ of defects}$ | Prioritization rule | Qualitative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall Alert:** In the table above, notice the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` for absolute values. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks when students confuse Cp (capability) with Cpk (centered capability). Cp ≥ Cpk always.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Six Sigma Applications

- **Manufacturing:** Reduces scrap, improves OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness), lowers warranty claims.
- **Healthcare:** Reduces patient wait times, surgical site infections, medication errors.
- **Banking/Finance:** Reduces loan processing cycle time, error rates in account opening, fraud detection time.
- **IT/Software:** Reduces post-release defects, improves on-time delivery, lowers cost per ticket.
- **Government/Public Services:** Reduces permit processing time, citizen grievance resolution time.
- **Supply Chain:** Reduces inventory variance, improves supplier on-time delivery, lowers logistics cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight for KTU 2024:** Companies like **General Electric, Motorola, Toyota, Amazon, Wipro, Infosys, Apollo Hospitals**, and **HDFC Bank** are flagship Six Sigma adopters. Referencing these in answers yields bonus impression points.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Exam Pattern):**
> A call center processes 8,400 customer calls in a week. Each call has 3 opportunities for defects (wrong information, long wait, dropped call). In one week, 504 defects are recorded. Calculate the DPMO, RTY, and Sigma Level (long-term and short-term).

#### **Step 1: Calculate DPMO**

We use the formula:
$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$$

Substituting the values:
$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{504}{8400 \times 3} \times 10^6$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{504}{25200} \times 10^6$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 0.02 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 20{,}000}$$

#### **Step 2: Calculate Yield (Y)**

The yield is computed from DPMO:
$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{20{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - 0.02$$

$$\boxed{\text{Yield} = 0.98 = 98\%}$$

#### **Step 3: Calculate Long-Term Sigma Level**

Long-term sigma excludes the 1.5σ shift:
$$\text{Sigma}_{\text{long}} = \text{NormInv}(Y)$$

Where NormInv is the inverse standard normal CDF. For Y = 0.98:
$$\text{Sigma}_{\text{long}} = \text{NormInv}(0.98) \approx 2.0537$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sigma}_{\text{long}} \approx 2.05\sigma}$$

#### **Step 4: Calculate Short-Term Sigma Level**

Short-term sigma includes the 1.5σ shift (Motorola standard):
$$\text{Sigma}_{\text{short}} = \text{Sigma}_{\text{long}} + 1.5$$

$$\text{Sigma}_{\text{short}} = 2.0537 + 1.5$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sigma}_{\text{short}} \approx 3.55\sigma}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A short-term sigma of 3.55 means the call center is operating at roughly 27,000 DPMO equivalent — far below world-class (6σ = 3.4 DPMO). A Six Sigma project would target moving this to 4σ or higher.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) in a Multi-Stage Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A laptop manufacturing line has 4 stages with yields: Stage 1 = 95%, Stage 2 = 92%, Stage 3 = 98%, Stage 4 = 97%. Calculate RTY and the cost of poor quality if 10,000 laptops are produced monthly and each defective unit costs ₹3,500 to rework.

#### **Step 1: Compute RTY**

$$\text{RTY} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \times 0.97$$

Performing stepwise multiplication:
$$\text{RTY} = 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.874 \times 0.98 = 0.85652$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.85652 \times 0.97 = 0.8308244$$

$$\boxed{\text{RTY} \approx 0.8308 = 83.08\%}$$

#### **Step 2: Compute Effective Defect Rate**

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - \text{RTY} = 1 - 0.8308 = 0.1692$$

$$\text{Defective Units per Month} = 10{,}000 \times 0.1692 = 1692 \text{ units}$$

#### **Step 3: Compute Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**

$$\text{COPQ} = 1692 \times 3500$$

$$\text{COPQ} = ₹5{,}922{,}000 \text{ per month}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COPQ} \approx ₹59.22 \text{ Lakhs/month}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice how each stage's yield loss *multiplies*. Even small per-stage losses (e.g., 5%) snowball into a 17% overall defect rate. This is the "Hidden Factory" concept — the largest leverage for improvement often lies in the *worst-performing stage*.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Six Sigma Tools Used in Each DMAIC Phase (Application Map)

| DMAIC Phase | Primary Tools Used | Application Context | Statistical Tool Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Define** | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ Tree, Kano Model | Problem scoping, stakeholder alignment | Qualitative / Survey |
| **Measure** | Data Collection Plan, MSA, Gage R&R, Capability (Cp, Cpk), DPMO Calculation | Baseline establishment, data quality | Descriptive Statistics |
| **Analyze** | Pareto, Fishbone, 5 Whys, FMEA, Regression, ANOVA, Hypothesis Testing | Root cause identification | Inferential Statistics |
| **Improve** | DOE, Response Surface Methodology, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, Pilot Studies | Solution generation and validation | Design of Experiments |
| **Control** | SPC Charts (X-bar, R, p, c), Control Plans, Visual Management, SOPs | Sustain improvements | Statistical Process Control |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping:** Course Outcome CO4 (Apply Six Sigma tools to real-world problems) maps directly to this matrix. Always structure your answer using this DMAIC-to-tools mapping when a question says "Explain how Six Sigma is applied in [industry]."

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — DMAIC Application Flow (Universal Across Industries)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Business Problem / VOC] --> B[Define Phase: Project Charter and SIPOC]
    B --> C[Measure Phase: Data Collection and Baseline Sigma]
    C --> D[Analyze Phase: Root Cause Analysis and Pareto]
    D --> E[Improve Phase: Pilot Solutions and DOE Validation]
    E --> F[Control Phase: SPC Charts and Control Plan]
    F --> G[Sustained Improvement and Cost Savings]
    G --> H[Process Owner Handover and Closure]

    stepA[Manufacturing Example] -.-> B
    stepB[Service Example] -.-> B
    stepC[Healthcare Example] -.-> B
    stepD[IT Software Example] -.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The central spine (A→B→C→D→E→F→G→H) is the universal DMAIC flow. The dotted lines (stepA, stepB, stepC, stepD) indicate that the *same* DMAIC framework is applied across different industry verticals — only the *tools* and *CTQs* change.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Six Sigma Application Architecture in an Organization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Champions: Senior Leaders] --> L2[Master Black Belts: Mentors]
    L2 --> L3[Black Belts: Project Leaders]
    L3 --> L4[Green Belts: Team Members]
    L4 --> L5[Yellow Belts: Awareness Level]

    L1 -.-> APP1[Strategic Project Selection]
    L2 -.-> APP2[Tool Training and Coaching]
    L3 -.-> APP3[DMAIC Project Execution]
    L4 -.-> APP4[Data Collection and Analysis]
    L5 -.-> APP5[Process Improvement Ideas]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** This is the **Belts Hierarchy** that drives Six Sigma applications in organizations. Each belt level corresponds to a depth of competence and a scope of application. KTU students must know this hierarchy — it is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Application Decision Tree (Choosing DMAIC vs DMADV)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does the process already exist?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the problem with an existing process?}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[Use DMADV: Design a new process]
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use DMAIC: Improve the existing process]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Are we designing something new?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R1
    Q3 -- No --> R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** This decision tree is *extremely high-yield* for KTU exams. **DMAIC** = improvement of existing processes; **DMADV** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify) = design of new processes/products achieving 6σ from the start. Examiners love to ask: *"When would you use DMADV instead of DMAIC?"*

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Six Sigma Application (Manufacturing Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
    I1[Raw Materials Supplier]
    I2[Customer Order]
    I3[Design Specifications]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Process Layer DMAIC]
    P1[Define CTQs from VOC]
    P2[Measure Baseline Sigma and Cp Cpk]
    P3[Analyze via Fishbone and FMEA]
    P4[Improve via DOE and Kaizen]
    P5[Control via SPC and Poka Yoke]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
    O1[Defect Free Product]
    O2[Customer Satisfaction Metric]
    O3[Cost Savings COPQ]
    end

    INPUT --> PROCESS --> OUTPUT
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    P5 -.Feedback.-> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** This block-level architecture shows the **Input → Process → Output** flow with the DMAIC cycle embedded in the Process layer. The dotted feedback arrow from P5 (Control) back to P1 (Define) represents the *continuous improvement loop* — Six Sigma applications are not one-time events but cyclical.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four industries where Six Sigma has been successfully applied and state one CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) parameter for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Industry | Example CTQ Parameter |
|---|---|
| Automobile Manufacturing | Defects per Vehicle (DPV) |
| Banking & Financial Services | Loan Processing Cycle Time |
| Healthcare | Patient Wait Time in Emergency Department |
| IT/Software Services | Post-Release Defect Density |

Six Sigma's DMAIC framework is industry-agnostic and has been applied across **manufacturing, healthcare, BPO, banking, IT services, retail, and government** sectors, always focusing on quantifiable improvements in **quality, cost, delivery, and customer satisfaction**.

*(Full marks for 4 industries + 4 CTQs, presented in a structured format.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between DMAIC and DMADV. When is DMADV preferred over DMAIC?*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DMAIC | DMADV |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control | Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify |
| **Purpose** | Improve an existing process | Design a new process/product |
| **When Used** | Process exists but underperforms | New product launch or process redesign |
| **Outcome** | Incremental improvement | Step-change / breakthrough design |
| **Tool Emphasis** | Root cause analysis, SPC | DOE, QFD, Robust Design |

DMADV is preferred when a **new process or product is being designed from scratch** and the goal is to achieve **6σ capability from inception**, avoiding the cost of retrofitting quality later. Example: designing a new semiconductor fabrication process.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the DMAIC methodology with a suitable application in the manufacturing industry. Discuss the tools used in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
DMAIC is a five-phase data-driven problem-solving methodology that forms the core of Six Sigma applications. It is applied systematically to improve existing processes in manufacturing.

**Phase 1 — Define (1.5 marks):**
- Identify the problem (e.g., high rejection rate in a CNC machining line — 8% rejection vs. target 1%).
- Tools: Project Charter, SIPOC diagram, VOC, CTQ Tree.
- Example CTQ: *Outer Diameter of bearing race must be 50.00 ± 0.05 mm*.

**Phase 2 — Measure (1.5 marks):**
- Establish baseline: DPMO, Cp, Cpk.
- Validate measurement system using Gage R&R.
- Example: Baseline Cpk = 0.85 (incapable).

**Phase 3 — Analyze (1.5 marks):**
- Identify root causes via Fishbone (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment).
- Apply Pareto to identify *Vital Few* causes (e.g., tool wear accounts for 60% of variation).
- Use hypothesis testing to confirm statistically significant factors.

**Phase 4 — Improve (1.5 marks):**
- Implement solutions: optimized tool change schedule, in-process gauging, operator training.
- Pilot run shows Cpk improves from 0.85 to 1.45.
- Use DOE to optimize cutting parameters (speed, feed, depth).

**Phase 5 — Control (1 mark):**
- Deploy SPC X-bar and R charts at the machining station.
- Create Control Plan with reaction rules.
- Hand over to Process Owner; Cpk sustained at 1.45 over 3 months.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
This DMAIC application reduced rejection from 8% to 1.2%, saving approximately ₹45 lakhs annually — a clear demonstration of Six Sigma's manufacturing impact.

---

**Q.A. (b)** A process produces 1,200 units/day. Each unit has 4 defect opportunities. The daily defect count is 96. Calculate DPMO, RTY (assuming 3 sequential stages with equal yields), and the short-term Sigma Level. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: DPMO Calculation (2 marks)**

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{96}{1200 \times 4} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{96}{4800} \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 20{,}000}$$

**Step 2: Yield per Stage (2 marks)**

$$Y_{\text{overall}} = 1 - \dfrac{20{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.98$$

For 3 equal stages:
$$Y_{\text{stage}} = (0.98)^{1/3}$$

Computing stepwise:
$$0.98^{1/3} = e^{\frac{\ln(0.98)}{3}} = e^{\frac{-0.0202}{3}} = e^{-0.00673}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{\text{stage}} \approx 0.9933 = 99.33\%}$$

**Step 3: RTY (1.5 marks)**

$$\text{RTY} = (0.9933)^3 = 0.98 \approx 98\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{RTY} = 98\%}$$

**Step 4: Short-Term Sigma Level (1.5 marks)**

Long-term Sigma:
$$\text{Sigma}_{\text{long}} = \text{NormInv}(0.98) \approx 2.05$$

Short-term (with 1.5σ shift):
$$\text{Sigma}_{\text{short}} = 2.05 + 1.5 = 3.55$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sigma}_{\text{short}} \approx 3.55\sigma}$$

**Interpretation:** The process operates at 3.55σ — equivalent to ~27,000 DPMO. To reach 4.5σ (world-class for many industries), defect rate must drop to ~3,400 DPMO.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a)** Discuss the application of Six Sigma in the healthcare industry. Explain with a real-world case study how DMAIC reduced patient wait times. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Healthcare is one of the fastest-growing adopters of Six Sigma globally. Hospitals apply DMAIC to reduce wait times, medication errors, surgical site infections, and readmission rates — directly impacting patient outcomes and cost.

**Define (1.5 marks):**
- Problem: Average Emergency Department (ED) wait time = 4.5 hours; target = 1 hour.
- CTQ: Door-to-Doctor Time (DTDT).
- SIPOC: Patients → Triage → Registration → Doctor Consult → Treatment → Discharge.

**Measure (1.5 marks):**
- Collected 30 days of timestamp data at every stage.
- Baseline: DTDT averaged 90 minutes; Sigma Level ≈ 2.8.
- Validated timestamp accuracy via MSA.

**Analyze (1.5 marks):**
- Fishbone revealed: peak-hour understaffing (Man), redundant registration forms (Method), lab report delays (Material).
- Pareto: 78% of delays came from *peak-hour understaffing* + *redundant paperwork*.

**Improve (1 mark):**
- Implemented fast-track lane for non-critical patients.
- Digitized registration (eliminated 2 paper forms).
- Added a flex-nurse during 6 PM–10 PM peak.

**Control (0.5 mark):**
- Real-time ED dashboard (Visual Management).
- Daily huddle to review wait time metrics.
- Reaction plan if DTDT > 60 min for 3 consecutive days.

**Result:** ED wait time reduced from 4.5 hours to 1.2 hours; Sigma Level improved from 2.8 to 4.1; patient satisfaction (NPS) rose by 32 points. Annual cost savings: ₹1.8 crore from reduced LWBS (Left Without Being Seen) rates.

---

**Q.B. (b)** A hospital admission process has 5 stages with yields: 96%, 94%, 99%, 97%, 95%. Compute RTY, total defect rate per 1,000 admissions, and suggest which stage to prioritize for improvement using the **Vital Few** concept. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: RTY (2.5 marks)**

$$\text{RTY} = 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.99 \times 0.97 \times 0.95$$

Stepwise:
$$0.96 \times 0.94 = 0.9024$$
$$0.9024 \times 0.99 = 0.893376$$
$$0.893376 \times 0.97 = 0.866575$$
$$0.866575 \times 0.95 = 0.823246$$

$$\boxed{\text{RTY} \approx 0.8232 = 82.32\%}$$

**Step 2: Defect Rate (1.5 marks)**

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - 0.8232 = 0.1768 = 17.68\%$$

Per 1,000 admissions:
$$\text{Defective admissions} = 1000 \times 0.1768 = 176.8 \approx 177$$

$$\boxed{\approx 177 \text{ defective admissions per 1{,}000}}$$

**Step 3: Identify Worst Stage (3 marks):**

Stage yields and corresponding defect rates:
- Stage 1: 96% → 4% defect
- Stage 2: 94% → **6% defect** ← *worst*
- Stage 3: 99% → 1% defect
- Stage 4: 97% → 3% defect
- Stage 5: 95% → 5% defect

**Application of Vital Few (Pareto) Concept:**
Sorting in descending order of defect contribution:
- Stage 2: 6% (cumulative 6%)
- Stage 5: 5% (cumulative 11%)
- Stage 1: 4% (cumulative 15%)
- Stage 4: 3% (cumulative 18%)
- Stage 3: 1% (cumulative 19%)

The **Vital Few** are Stages 2, 5, and 1, which together account for **15% out of 19% total stage defects (~79%)**. Per the 80/20 rule, **Stage 2 (94% yield) must be prioritized** for Six Sigma improvement — even a 2% improvement here (94% → 96%) would lift overall RTY by ~1.5 percentage points, far exceeding the impact of improving Stage 3 (already at 99%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse DPMO with DPV** — DPMO uses *opportunities*, DPV uses *vehicles*. Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always show stepwise calculation** for RTY — examiners award partial marks for each correct multiplication step.
> 3. **The 1.5σ shift** applies only to *short-term* sigma. Many students incorrectly add it to long-term sigma. State explicitly: *"Long-term sigma = 2.05; adding the Motorola 1.5σ shift gives short-term sigma = 3.55."*
> 4. **For Vital Few questions**, do NOT just name the worst stage — apply the **cumulative Pareto logic** and show the percentage contribution.
> 5. **Industry case answers** must include **quantified before-and-after metrics** (e.g., sigma level, DPMO, cost savings). Vague answers ("improved quality") fetch ≤ 50% marks.
> 6. **Diagram recall**: Examiners in KTU 2024 Scheme often award 1 mark for a correctly drawn **SIPOC** or **DMAIC flowchart**. Practice these.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Six Sigma Applications (Module 4)**

- ✅ **DMAIC** = the universal application framework: **D**efine → **M**easure → **A**nalyze → **I**mprove → **C**ontrol.
- ✅ **DMADV** = used for designing *new* processes; preferred when no existing baseline exists.
- ✅ **DPMO** formula: (Defects ÷ (Units × Opportunities)) × 10⁶ — commit to memory.
- ✅ **Sigma Level (long-term)** = NormInv(Yield); **Short-term** = Long-term + 1.5σ (Motorola standard).
- ✅ **RTY** = product of all stage yields — *multiplicative*, not additive.
- ✅ **Vital Few** (Pareto 80/20): prioritize the top 20% of causes that drive 80% of defects.
- ✅ **Industries where Six Sigma is applied**: Manufacturing, Healthcare, BPO, Banking, IT/Software, Retail, Government, Supply Chain.
- ✅ **Belts Hierarchy**: Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt.
- ✅ **CTQ** = Critical-to-Quality — the *Y* in the Six Sigma equation Y = f(X).
- ✅ **COPQ** = Internal Failure + External Failure + Appraisal + Prevention costs.
- ✅ **Always quantify** application benefits with metrics (DPMO, Sigma Level, RTY, Cost Savings).
- ✅ **SPC Charts** (X-bar, R, p, c) are the primary Control-phase tools.
- ✅ **Gage R&R** validates measurement systems in the Measure phase — required for trustworthy data.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing; a key Lean Six Sigma tool for sustainable Control.
- ✅ **Real-world references**: GE (Jack Welch), Motorola (1986, Bill Smith), Toyota, Amazon, Apollo Hospitals, Wipro — using these in answers boosts examiner confidence.
- ✅ **Hidden Factory**: the cumulative effect of yield losses across stages — *RTY reveals it*.
